U70wAj9AUuk.summary:
* Citizen Steve Willard presented data showing unequal exhaust citation enforcement in Holland Township compared to Georgetown Township, requesting formal action from the board to address the sheriff's office performance
* State Representative Brad Brinks discussed Selfridge Air Force Base runway upgrades ($158M), doctor licensing compact, and upcoming legislative focus on healthcare costs, energy mandates, and state property taxes
* Legal counsel Ron Bolche provided comprehensive summary of Lawn Avenue litigation history (2017-2020), confirming settlement agreement dismissed all claims with prejudice; cautioned board on Open Meetings Act compliance, employee complaint procedures, and email communication protocols
* Board unanimously approved 1.75 mills public safety millage renewal (7-0) for 2027-2030 to fund fire, sheriff, and emergency medical services
* Zoning ordinance map amendment for Quincy Street property (Ag to commercial) approved unanimously (7-0)
* Four community service organizations approved for funding: American Legion Band ($3,000), Hospice of Holland, Holland Community Theater ($500), Zeeland Historical Society
* Two quitclaim deeds for Ottawa Street right-of-way abandonment approved (7-0 each) conveying township interest to adjacent property owners
* Critical water main replacement project on Lakewood Boulevard scheduled May-September creating significant traffic disruption on major township corridor

all claims by Trustee DeYoung against the township were dismissed with prejudice. The matter was done...this is now Trustee DeYoung's 3rd attempt to get Lawn Avenue. He didn't get it in negotiations. He didn't get it in litigation.

The problem comes in if there is a response, particularly if the response is copied to all, because now we have an exchange of ideas going on that's outside the public view...Reply all is a very bad practice among public body members.

the recommendation before you this evening is to have a millage rate for the next cycle here of 1.75 mills...the voters have been very receptive to this millage. The last one, I looked it up, it passed 70 to 30

the water main underneath Lakewood Boulevard needs replacement...the result of that is that the eastbound lanes on Lakewood Boulevard...will be closed. So traffic will be reduced to one westbound lane...it is a critical one. Obviously, all emergency services are being coordinated.
after 11 years, the parking is still not being enforced equally at 610 North Shore Drive, nor anywhere else...We have a selective enforcement problem here. We have a dereliction of duty issue.

we're trying to get the $158 million so that we can start being used here in the state before President Trump is gone...Once it's up and operational, then that won't be true

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: My name is Steve Willard. I live at 3568 Jayden Court in Holland Township. My car was in the shop this week. Just got it out today. Muffler replacement.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Karma.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: The bill is $1,200. I believe in following the law, even when it's expensive. I'm not here asking for something I'm unwilling to do myself. The law is not being applied equally in Holland Charter Township, and I have the data to prove it. In March, I received a response to a FOIA request for every exhaust citation in Ottawa County issued by the Sheriff's Office countywide over 12 months. The response contained 42 records. Holland Township received 4. Georgetown Township received 17 in the same period by the same agency under the same statute. That 17 is not random. It's driven almost entirely by one deputy who runs proactive exhaust enforcement patrols in his Holland Township has no equivalent. And here's what makes it even worse. There were zero exhaust citations in Holland Township between May 2025 and January 2026. 230 days. During that window, I personally made over 14 documented dispatch calls about a single address 2 doors from me. I sent the full data to Sheriff DeBoer and Captain Douglas on April 2nd. No response. I sent it to Lieutenant Knott, who's here, who oversees the western.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: He's not here.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: West District. He replied, he said, in quote, continues to stress proactive traffic enforcement to supervisors and deputies in his district. Stressing it and deploying it are not the same thing. The data shows the gap is still there. I also asked Lieutenant Knapp for a simple monthly summary citation counts by township so I wouldn't have to file a FOIA request every month just to track whether anything changed. No response. This board contracts law enforcement from Ottawa County Sheriff's Office. Holland Township generated the highest exhaust complaint volume in Ottawa County during the 12-month period. We received the least enforcement. That is a contract performance issue. This board has the standing and the obligation to say something about it. I'm asking this board to formally raise this disparity with Ottawa County Sheriff's Office command Request proactive exhaust enforcement patrols in Holland Township and request the Ottawa County Sheriff's Department provide the township with a regular enforcement reporting by jurisdiction. You are the contracting party. You should have that visibility without residents having to FOIA for it. I've submitted the full FOIA data sheet with written comment tonight. The numbers are there. The ask is straightforward. And you guys should have gotten that data.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thank you, Steve.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Greg.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thank you, Mr. Supervisor and board. Good to see all of you again. So just a couple of thoughts for you. Just wanted to— just had an opportunity. I thought, hey, I'd better come make sure that I talk to you folks. First of all, thank you for the work that you do, continuing to do, because I know that being — in government, no matter what level it is, takes work, effort, and is not necessarily easy. So, thank you for the work that you guys continue to do. I wanted you to hear a little bit about the Suffrage Air Base upgrade and things that are going on. I don't know what you guys have heard, but the Governor and the Speaker of the House had connected with President Trump and gotten permission and approval to move That Suffrage Air Force Base was going to be kind of taken down. They were going to get rid of the A-10s, which is the style of aircraft that is there right now. That was going to be gone in the next few years, and they were going to replace that someplace else with F-15-somethings. I don't know what the thing is, but it's at the top of the list. The thing that we found out now is we, we as a state need to upgrade the runway to be able to make sure that these aircraft get actually installed and Yes. We're trying to get the $158 million so that we can start being used here in the state before President Trump is gone. Because it's potential that when he's no longer there, that the President could say, well, we're going to move it to some other location. Once it's up and operational, then that won't be true. So we've got— we're working really hard right now with our Democrat counterparts to ensure that we get the $158 million that we need to upgrade the runways. 'Cause that's the key that will unlock the planes coming into our area, is having the runways updated. So anyway, that's where we're at with the suffrage area process. One of the other things that you may have heard or may not have heard is we legislatively, along with the governor, put through a doctor compact. There were some real concerns about doctors not having a So, if you're licensed in one state, you can't necessarily work in another state unless there's a compact between the states. We approved that compact. We had about 8,000 doctors who were licensed in another state, but actually working in Michigan, that we had the potential of losing all of those. And so, we needed to make sure that that compact had gone through. That did get taken care of, and we now have that so that we can employ them here, or they can move other places as they need to. And then just a couple ideas for where we're headed as a legislature between now and year end. One is the budget. First of the budgets came out today from the House, and I think they were just gonna— I don't know if they were coming out today or they're gonna come out first of next week from the Senate. So we'll start to see how those dovetail or don't, as the case might be. So they're being rolled out. I think 12 went today, and then we've got another 8 or so that go next week that will be done. And then things that we're really going to be focusing on, I think, for the next few weeks are going to be cost of healthcare. That's a continuing issue. We need to continue to try and address it. Energy cost and availability. We're not in favor of— I think it's 235, or at 235, which is the Forcing mandates about green energy and those kinds of things. And we really think that we need to try and move back from that if we can. So we'll be working on that. And then the other piece of it is property taxes, the state part of property taxes, not the local unit property taxes. We're not looking to change that at all. But we are looking to see if there's a way that we can get rid of the state side of property taxes. So it's one of the things that you'll see us at least be working toward if we can. And with that, I'd be happy to answer questions. I know that that isn't necessarily part of the township's deal, but you can if you'd like.

So, I know that there are some doctors that are licensed in, you know, Puerto Rico, Pennsylvania. They are licensed here. They don't live here.

_utterances.text: And then operational reports.

_utterances.text: Yeah, 3 of them were included in your agenda packet. Just to quickly note those, the first being the March Building activity, building permit activity, good March, almost hit $10 million. And I think the striking thing, if you had a chance to look at the detail associated with the activity report, is that, you know, there wasn't a major project. You know, it was really spread across all the different building categories. So, you know, for first quarter, be a little over $20 million. I think, you know, 2026 is off to a good start. Happy to answer any questions regarding that report. If none, the fire department has a new monthly report, has a new look. You'll see here that in addition to that chart on the second page that we've always received, Chief Tibbetts has implemented kind of a short report that talks about everything else besides running the calls that is incorporated into that chart that you see there. So In this particular report, talked a little bit about the training that department personnel have been doing. You know, certainly a number of activities that the fire department undertakes regarding community risk reduction listed there as well. And then certainly, you know, we have a lot of talented folks on the department. And the second page there, you know, listed in part some of the training and certifications that some members of the Fire department personnel have undertaken and successfully completed. So, kind of rounds out besides the chart that you see there. And again, another you know active month for the department as well. And we're seeing you know tracking a little bit ahead of last year in terms of you know that first quarter that has come through. So, happy to answer any questions regarding the fire department report. And then lastly, the sheriff. Department. You saw the calls for service there for March, 1,216. And I would want to direct your attention to the last page where it lists a number of different categories and specifically direct your attention to those 2 asterisks at the bottom of that chart there. We sat down with Lieutenant Knott and Supervisor Tesla and kind of looked through the categories that we were reporting on and made some modifications to that. So you'll see that The false alarm reporting category, as well as animal complaints or calls, have been removed, and some new categories have been added. And those would be disorderlies, as well as retail fraud, stolen vehicle, and criminal sexual conduct. So, change that up a little bit. Certainly, I always— I'm sure I can speak for the lieutenant that if there are other categories the board would like to see, we can certainly talk about adding those as well.

_utterances.text: All right then, a quick review of the March check disbursement. You'll see that disbursements are a little over $3 million, $3.1, almost $3.2 million. Just to point out a couple of the larger expenditures under a couple of the larger funds for this particular month. Certainly the general fund, Fund 101, that $549,000. We had a couple of bills from the county for law enforcement services. The February and March bills came through totaling $363,429. So certainly the majority of that $549,000 that you see there. Expenditures in Fund 405, the building and site capital projects, fund of $485,000. You know, we have some different projects in various stages at this point. The community center was $71,483 of that total of $485,000, and I believe that is the last one. I think that was the retainage payment, so that one has wrapped up. Another project that is coming along very nicely is the improvements at the Township Hall, the east wing. The Community Development Department, and expenditures for the month of March were $204,533. And then lastly, the last payment on the exterior improvements at the Township Hall with the parking and the garages and so forth, $177,157. So that's the vast majority of those expenditures in Fund 405. And then a pretty significant Disbursement for Fund 590, the sewer operating, a little over $1 million of that, and the majority of that was a payment on the digester in the amount of $621,598. And then of course our typical wastewater treatment bills from our friends in the City of Zeeland, $49,911, and the City of Holland at $253,135. So, I'll be happy to answer any questions regarding that.

_utterances.speaker: I

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right, thank you, Steve. And we also have the agenda from March 19th, 2026, to consider as part of this package. Look for a motion to approve the consent agenda.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Then I've got on here legal counsel. I just want to introduce Ron Bolche. Asked him to come here tonight and maybe over the next few meetings as well. Maybe one of his staff members would be here as well to make sure we If we have any legal questions, guidance on our format of our meeting, that that is being cared properly. And I also asked him to kind of give a summary of our Lawn Avenue history that's been brought up a couple of times in the past and asked him for clarification on that, as well as just some board function.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Thanks, Russ. Yeah, early— I think it was earlier this year, but a couple months, a few months ago, I received an inquiry from Steve and from Russ basically about Lawn Avenue. And frankly, it struck me as a blast from the past. I hadn't thought about this one for a long time. Like 15 years or so. I did have a discussion at that time and gave my thoughts almost kind of like I reviewed it for the first time. I'm glad that my advice at that time was consistent with my advice before and any advice I'll give you tonight. When I was asked to consider attending this meeting tonight, I was also asked to check out a couple of videos of prior meetings, and I've realized that Lawn Avenue has continued to be a very hot topic with you all, and which was a bit surprising to me. So given that, I walked through my files and documents that were returned to me or provided to me and refreshed my memory on a number of things. And I won't— it's been a number of years. I'll not go through item by item because you don't have the time for that. But I will just give you a summary of, uh, of what happened from my standpoint. Um, back in 2017 or '18, uh, Trustee DeYoung and his attorney at the time, Bill Sickel, claimed that the Ottawa County Road Commission vacated Lawn Avenue incorrectly and improperly deeded the property to the township. And their claim at that time was that the land should have been deeded to the property owners, including Trustee DeYoung. Had discussions, I did, with the Road Commission attorney at the time. The Road Commission was adamant they had done things correctly. We had multiple meetings, lots of meetings, long meetings, discussions, a lot of emails between Trustee DeYoung and his attorney and me and the township officials, specifically the Community Development Department at the time, Corey and John Sedd. The other property owners as well, Walters in particular. The Road Commission never backed off its position that it had properly vacated the property and deeded it to the township. Other property owners in the area other than Trustee DeYoung did not want the property. There was no unanimity there. The township ultimately refused to recommend or pursue abandonment of the property as it was deeded to the township. In May of 2018, the Community Development Department, specifically in a written memo, made a recommendation to the board That the demand to vacate Lawn Avenue be denied. I wasn't at the meeting subsequent to that. My file ends at that point on that issue. I assume that the board accepted that recommendation, and and the matter, that phase of the matter stopped then. However, quite soon after that, Trustee DeYoung sued the township. With a different attorney at this point, and there was a lawsuit filed demanding that ownership of Lawn Avenue be provided to the adjacent property owners, including Trustee DeYoung. Back in 2020, the matter went to mediation with former Circuit Court Judge Ed Post, and a settlement agreement was reached at that time. The settlement agreement says the township will not restrict the DeYoung use of Lawn Avenue to access property. The township will give the DeYoungs an access easement over Lawn Avenue. The township will remove barriers and signs adjacent to Lawn Avenue. The township will not enforce parking regulations against the DeYoungs differently from how Enforces parking regulations against other members of the public. The DeYoungs may use Lawn Avenue in a way that does not interfere with other persons using Lawn Avenue. And the DeYoungs were to pursue a permit, if necessary, to bring the shed and a retaining wall into compliance with zoning and other ordinances. There were some setback issues and buildings over property lines, et cetera.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: And finally, all claims by Trustee DeYoung against the township were dismissed with prejudice. The matter was done.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: It's over.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So therefore, Trustee DeYoung has no further claim against the township for the vacation of or the ownership of Lawn Avenue. And One of the 2 meetings that I listened to from the past couple of months, there were repeated references by Trustee DeYoung that there have been discussions with me and I had told the township it was time to vacate Lawn Avenue. Absolutely untrue. There have been no discussions like that. I have never said that. So I I don't know where they came from, but I can tell you they are absolutely 100% not true. According to what I heard and what I observed in the memo, the meetings that I read or viewed since then, this is now Trustee DeYoung's 3rd attempt to get Lawn Avenue. He didn't get it in negotiations. He didn't get it in litigation. His claims were dismissed with prejudice. They cannot be brought back again. And now at the March 19 meeting, he said the reason he's on the board is to get Lawn Avenue deeded to him. I guess I'll leave that at that point. On that score, it's obviously disconcerting to me that that claims are being made about what I've said, what I've recommended in the past, and then there's the the evident self-interest involved here. I will say, however, that. With regard to what I have perceived is going on in the past about criticisms of staff members being made by township board members, I would recommend that there be a bit of a chain of command followed in that regard. If there is a township board member who has a complaint against a staff member, that should go to the supervisor of that person and ultimately, obviously, to the superintendent of the township. And if that cannot be properly addressed at that point, or if the township board member with a complaint doesn't get satisfaction, I would recommend that then if any complaint is going to be brought against a township employee, that the employee be given notice, an opportunity to respond, an opportunity to be present at a board meeting when his or her performance will be discussed, and an opportunity to ask for a closed session under the Open Meetings Act, which is a statute right of public employees if their job performance is going to be critiqued by their employer. So surprise or unannounced or unnoticed criticisms of township employees just should not happen at a township board meeting on what seems to me to be a rather regular basis. The last thing that Supervisor TeSlaa just asked that I would address would be the Open Meetings Act and electronic communication. And, and this may or may not be a problem, but it's just he asked me to give a reminder. With our emails and texting capabilities, there are ways in which public board members such as you and other public bodies can have communication outside the public, and that needs to be carefully monitored. It is not a problem for a township board member to email another township board member or to even to email all township board members. That first email is not a problem. You could send a letter by first-class mail. And there would be no problem. You can send communication by email, and there's no problem. The problem comes in if there is a response, particularly if the response is copied to all, because now we have an exchange of ideas going on that's outside the public view and outside the presence of the public, and we're conducting deliberations potentially, and that would be a violation of the Open Meetings Act. So just for what it's worth, I'm happy to ask questions, answer questions about that. But the supervisor just asked me to address that. Just something to keep in mind. If there's an exchange of ideas going on, stop it right there. Reply all is a very bad practice among public body members. So just avoid it. I'm not sure if you're doing it, but if you are, Take a look at that. Consider it carefully. So that's that's my walk down memory lane and and my response to the supervisor, and I'm happy to answer questions.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right, thank you, Steve. We got the consideration of a resolution concerning the public safety Safety millage proposition that's planned to put on the August 4th primary election ballot.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: That's right. As the COVID memo indicates, the township for a very long time has had a millage to support public safety services. It started out, you know, supporting the E-Unit program, which is the primary medical response that was operated by the Sheriff's Department. And for a number of years it was that. Currently we're under a levy that was approved by the voters of 1.5 mills, and that authorization from the voters goes through the end of 2026. So we're again looking at a renewal of the millage for public safety. This will cover the time period 2027 through 2030 inclusive. It'll impact the budgets 2031 through yeah 2028 through 2031. So the levy is in front of the budget year in which it is expended. So as the second paragraph indicates, and you've all been around the table as we've taken this journey from the unit being dissolved by Sheriff Kempker. The board decided that we wanted to continue to provide that service. It came into the fire department, and I think that transition went very well. We are our primary medical response is certainly one of the best in the county in that regard. When we talk to people who were involved in the provision of those services, so that transition went quite well. We also made. As part of transitioning that, we went to a full-time fire department. So that was a major transition in the way we provide emergency fire services as well. The board made the decision to maintain staffing levels for the Sheriff's Department at its current level. So there was no deduction in staffing levels for the Sheriff's Department when the primary medical response was transitioned To the fire department, that was a good thing because surprise, surprise, calls didn't go down on the sheriff's side. In fact, you'll see if you had a chance to look at the memo that we are you know holding well not steady. Those call volumes are continuing to grow as the township continues to grow. So given that, staff went through an analysis, looked at historical cost factors for the provision of now I'm using the term public safety. Services. And if you had a chance to look at the proposed ballot language, it too, you know, references that. And we are anticipating, you know, some cost increases certainly as we go along. And those were identified at the top of page 2 of the memo as our best projection as to what costs were going to be for the fiscal years that would be impacted. by the millage, that FY 2028 through FY 2031. And as we've talked about in the last few budget cycles, you know, the public safety wedge of the budget pie continues to increase, and obviously critical services being provided to our residents, and therefore they need to be staffed appropriately and funded appropriately. The staff prepared different scenarios for The millage, millage levels and so forth, discuss those with the executive committee. The recommendation before you this evening is to have a millage rate for the next cycle here of 1.75 mills. And so a logical question that is, well, what does that generate in terms of revenues? And some— making some assumptions both on the terms of costs as well as what the taxable value of the township will be at the time that millage is levied. That's what you see in the middle of page 2 of my memo in terms of what it generates. And of course, if you look at that set of projected costs, when you look at the millage proceeds, there is a gap yet that still needs to be filled by the general fund to meet those anticipated costs. And that's what that chart there at the bottom of the memo indicates. So that's kind of an overview of, you know, looking forward. Obviously, we want to make sure we have adequate public safety services. And certainly the voters have been very receptive to this millage. The last one, I looked it up, it passed 70 to 30, 70% to 30%. So the recommendation is to renew that.

_utterances.text: So, the highest traffic volume roadway in the township The first is the River Avenue Bridge. The second is Lakewood Boulevard from roughly the Westco gas station to 120th and actually a little farther east as well. Unfortunately, the water main underneath Lakewood Boulevard needs replacement, and that is going to commence in early May, and the result of that is that the eastbound lanes on Lakewood Boulevard from roughly Corwell House— next to that is Pump Station 2, and that's where the project will begin— and proceed easterly up to but not into the intersection of 120th and Lakewood. So eastbound lanes are going to be closed. And of course, to provide safety to the workers who are in there and One of the westbound lanes is going to be closed. So traffic will be reduced to one westbound lane from essentially Courwell House to 120th Avenue. When this phase— that's the first phase of the project— when that has been completed, the intersection of 120th and Lakewood will be closed. So obviously in our community, with only a couple of river crossings, When you take one of the river crossings out, it does create some disruption. Certainly, law enforcement will help us to the extent they can, making sure that you know everyone still drives in a prudent manner when they sometimes get a little frustrated when they're in when they encounter road closures of this nature. It is a critical one. Obviously, all emergency services. are being coordinated. We'll take care of that as well. And then the project— and then, you know, hopefully we can get through that intersection as expeditiously as possible. And then the project will proceed north on 120th past the Township Hall up to James. So there's going to be a little pain this summer starting in the near future and going right up to the end of paving season. But it needs to be done. We either have, we either have reliability problems, capacity problems, or both. And the time has come to do that. So I hate to be the bearer of bad news, you know, let your friends and neighbors know because that is going to create some disruption to normal traffic patterns. So that's the bad news I have for this evening. That's all I have.

And you don't have to look at all of this here tonight, but I would ask that you read through it. And the bottom line is, after 11 years, the parking is still not being enforced equally at 610 North Shore Drive, nor anywhere else. Mr. Bulthuis, you mentioned as part of your presentation that parking should be equally enforced, and that is not happening in Holland Township. We have a selective enforcement problem here. We have a dereliction of duty issue. Mr. Willard has— it took him 6 months to get anything accomplished, and it only happened because Mr. Bush stepped in to do it. It would not have happened had he not spent 6 months of his life attending these meetings requesting that it got done, but it did finally get done. And Thank you for that, Mr. Bush. I know that last time there was a similar measure in front of the House, I think he was on The other side of it. Right? And, so, I would like to know. And, it interests me that, I think it is kind of at the top of the agenda for townships. And so, I think we should make sure it's at the top of his agenda, too.

And maybe there'll be more information.

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yeah, and I know that, you know, there's all kinds of meetings on Thursday nights when we have our trustee meetings, and I know that he's going to a lot of them, but I would like to hear more from him.

_utterances.speaker: E

_utterances.speaker: F

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Exactly, yeah. So, he's accessible, but I don't think how we feel as a township always gets to that state level. Or, that's what you say?

_utterances.speaker: I

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: J

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: F

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Thanks for coming.

Crds8gPkKoo.summary:
- Added agenda item #13 on selective enforcement by township staff at DeYoung's request; motion passed unanimously
- Approved conditional zoning for 188 North Jefferson/West McKinley (I-2/R-2 to C-2), roll call 6-0-1
- Approved parks and recreation plan update with MCSA Group for $14,500; motion passed unanimously
- Approved fire department Cadet Explorer program resolution; motion passed unanimously
- Approved mobile oxygen generating system purchase from Novair for $83,489.20, roll call 5-1-1 (DeYoung opposed)
- Approved Enviroclean cleaning services contract for $341,811 annually, roll call 6-0-1
- Approved vehicle replacement (5 half-ton pickups) from Barber Ford for $181,130, roll call 6-0-1
- Extensive public comment and board discussion on selective enforcement allegations involving modified exhaust violations, parking ordinances, and code enforcement practices; staff performance concerns raised

highlights.quote: Deputy was posted on Greenlee Street, March 16th, 2026. The driver agreed to use a different vehicle. 6 months, 20+ calls, 2 FOIAs resolved in one conversation once someone showed up.

3 cases, same outcome. The exhaust violation was confirmed. Vehicle was confirmed. North Shore Drive, it's been confirmed. Formal complaint filed? Yep. Director's response, one sentence: will review. No action.

Corey Brosma's official response: Thank you for copying the Ottawa County Sheriff Department on this message. Let that sink in. 17 calls deep about being woken. No. Multiple phone calls. This is his only reply. No action, by the way.

I have given specific examples of the selective enforcement and targeted harassment of my family by our staff. I've been documenting that information for the entire board now in official written correspondence. The selective enforcement at my house has come to a screeching halt since I became an elected official.
99% of residents park or have parked in the right-of-way in front of their homes, but the only enforcement of that ordinance is at my family's home.

I submitted a 24-page code enforcement analysis to this board. 15 violations were found in a half-mile radius. 3 months, 1 resident. The township verified all this in an official FOIA response. It was 100% reactive. Zero proactive inspections documented.
I see that we didn't know the protocol or who was— where it lied. Okay? He's the public. He expects us to know. Okay? It was bounced around. We can all admit that, right? It was bounced around.
I appreciate that you care enough about our community to stick with it this long and not give up. I do. I really appreciate that. You're the voice for a lot of people. I apologize that we failed you.

I would prefer to wait for whatever update we're getting internally. I wouldn't rule it out. But I, if I were to make a motion, I would— well, I'm not making one, but I'm not going to support that today because I think we haven't heard something that sounds like it's in progress.

Given the amount of time that has passed and the concerns that have been raised by residents and employees, I believe the public deserves to understand what occurred and how these issues were handled. We owe it to the people to find the truth.

dave_segments.text: I do. I move to add agenda item number 13, labeled Update on Selective Enforcement by Holland Charter Township Staff.

I just want to make sure they, especially if we're getting grants, I'd like to look at the waterfront there at Dutton Park and what might be able to be done with that, because I just think it's a huge waste of Waterfront.
If they're going to do—what's that? Just get a hold of Tiffany and tell her.
So what do we spend on oxygen for 1 year? That'd be simple math to do here if we had—
And these are not any type of paid position, is that right? They're just volunteers as part of the— Classes.
So, somebody's mathed this all out to figure out that it's worth spending $83,000 instead of having just people bring the tanks in and out over 15 years, is that right?
Yes.
Yeah, it's all pretty well laid out in here.
I agree with that, and I also agree with your revoking of the permit suggestion, because I think there's some people who are going to handle this really well, and I think there's some that are not.
I don't think we would be here without him. Without his work, we wouldn't be here. We wouldn't be talking about it at all. We would be doing the same as what we've always done, which is nothing.

We have a storage shed wherein we will we were required to follow the setback requirements of a permanent structure, including requiring us to spend money to acquire a survey to prove that our shed was not located within 10 feet from the nearest side yard and not within 15 feet from the backyard setback. There are literally hundreds, if not thousands, of storage sheds in Holland Township that are placed in the back corners of their property and are not forced to follow any setback requirements.

I have over the last dozen years received multiple letters by mail, multiple harassing and threatening emails, citations the Sheriff's Department dispatched to our house over 9 times. Township employees sent to our home and tasked to remove personal items from the road right-of-way, throwing them in our yard while the entire neighborhood has personal items in the road right-of-way. Selective enforcement.

The Lawn Avenue road abandonment, or better stated, the lack thereof, has been handled unlike any other road abandonment ever in Holland Charter Township. We all know it is the only one that exists out of at least 20 road abandonments where the township has kept the land, despite the fact that it goes against Michigan case law, despite the fact that the township landlocked our property, and despite the fact that township legal counsel advised staff to turn over the land to the adjoining landowners.

I believe if we are serving our citizens with courtesy, integrity, and efficiency, they deserve to have our board address this ongoing concern. Simply hiring another enforcement agent does not address nor solve this problem. It seems a very specific pattern of behavior from our staff is becoming increasingly clear.

Whether it be persistently ignoring the ordinances at some locations or targeted over-enforcement of ordinances at others. If this pattern of behavior, when our staff just doesn't like you, is to target and harass our citizens— and we now know it is— one can't help but wonder if that exact same thing doesn't happen to our employees.

I believe that our staff was straight and honest and would never allow such a thing as was described in the complaint by that former employee. But based on everything that has been revealed since then and everything that I have seen and experienced personally from our staff, I fear I may have been wrong, very wrong.

I will be making a motion that the board determine whether the need for confidentiality no longer exists and that the board allow discussion in open session of the broader policy issues related to the consistent and fair application of township ordinances and personnel policies to the extent permitted under the Michigan Open Meetings Act.

I will further be moving that the board authorize an independent third-party investigation into township enforcement and personnel complaint practices in order to determine whether policies have been applied consistently and without disparate treatment or retaliation.
No, it didn't get away. He's ignoring it.
It's not complicated at all.

Along the line of digging in a little deeper, I believe there are many more examples of selective enforcement by our staff, and I intend to keep exposing them. If you believe that you have experienced selective enforcement from our staff, I want to hear from you.

Selective enforcement happens when a government agency chooses to enforce enforcement of a law or regulation only against certain businesses or individuals while ignoring others who are violating the same regulation, leading to unequal treatment under the law.

So as the memo indicates, and as this renewal proposal from the consultant. Indicates they will undertake an online survey that residents and others can fill out with, you know, express their preferences and provide other information there. And then we'll certainly have some public input, in-person public input meetings as well as part of that process. When all that information has been, in this case, updated, you know, the results of the input have been analyzed, They'll compile that all in an updated parks and recreation plan for us, at which time, if approved by the board, would then be submitted to the Michigan Department of Natural Resources. And that's important because without an approved plan, the township is not eligible for MDNR funding. So, and funding not only from that state agency, but some other state agencies as well as the federal government as well. So we certainly want to get this updated and reached out to MCSA Group. They have been updating our plan for a number of years, so very intimate knowledge of our park system. And they presented the proposal that was included in the agenda packet and would complete those tasks that I just spoke with in terms of the public input, The demographics, the new needs analysis, and so forth, for an amount of $14,500, which when I saw that struck me as being—I think that's lower than it was when we did it five years ago. So I did dig into the records, and it is actually a reduction of $2,000 from the last time they did it. And so I think that speaks to the fact that they have information that you know they can use as a base to work from, and then of course have to update. And do the other elements of it as well. But some of the base maps and so forth that were created for the last plan remain for this one as well. So we were pleased with MCSA's performance with this project the last time they did it. So staff's recommendation is to approve the attached proposal to MCSA in the amount of $14,500. Thank you.

_utterances.text: Yeah, so Chief Tibbetts brought forth this idea to the fire committee, you know, Certainly. We've talked around this table many times about keeping a properly staffed fire department and the challenges associated with that, especially on the part-paid side. And certainly that continues to be an ongoing issue and concern that we always want to make sure— obviously we have a full-time department now, but we want to make sure we still have a robust core of part-pays. As well. So one strategy that communities across the state, actually nation, have been using is to partner with entities like Career Align Tech Center to organize and employ a cadet program which allows departments to, if authorized by their governing boards, to offer this to students who are interested in a career, either in our case it would be fire department or first response since we have both services within our department. And if you had a chance to look at the handbook that has been created, you know, it speaks to their participation, their performance, I mean, within, you know, the scope of their abilities, appearance, you know, responding to calls, how that's to be done. And so forth, permission from their parents or guardians, and so on and so forth. Because these are people, young people who are 16, 17 years old. It does allow even for some limited participation on the department once they reach 18, if they are you know doing well at career line type as well. So Chief Tibbetts is you know very excited about. You know, this opportunity, he feels— he sees that as a great recruitment tool because if you get them early, get them into the fold and, you know, get to know the other firefighters and our processes and so forth, you know, these could potentially be future employees of the township, either in a part-paid role or potentially a full-time role if the case might arise.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: The first one there is a request from the fire department for the purchase of a mobile oxygen generating system. So the capital improvement program for this year called for the replacement of the self-contained breathing apparatus or SCUBA breathing air compressor at the department. And as Chief Tibbetts took a look at that and worked With other representatives of that equipment, it was determined there are several good years before it reaches the end of its useful life. So he said it made no sense to him to replace that piece of equipment at this time. But since we've taken on the primary medical response, there is a new need in the fire department to replenish oxygen tanks that he used Primarily when medical calls are made, and, you know, we're doing 150 to 200 of those a month. And so he has requested that that funding that had been designated for the scuba compressor, air compressor, be reallocated to the purchase of one of these oxygen, mobile oxygen generating units.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Systems. So the fire committee said go ahead and proceed and get some quotes with that. And so he has done so, and you see that 2 bids were received for this system, one being from the Novair in the amount of $83,489, the other from Grainger at $110,785. So the recommendation is to proceed with the purchase of the Novair USA system in the amount of $83,489.20.

In fact, I would challenge our staff to find even one other storage shed that was required to follow those same setbacks that were required at our home. We were selectively enforced. Number 2, and the one that I continue to highlight, is the no parking in the road— road right-of-way ordinance, which has been proven is not being enforced anywhere else in the township. In fact, both the Ottawa County Sheriff Department and the Ottawa County Road Commission both recently stated that they do not and will not enforce that ordinance, despite staff trying to pass the buck to them. Meanwhile, my family has over the last dozen years received multiple letters by mail, multiple harassing and threatening emails, citations the Sheriff's Department dispatched to our house over 9 times. Thank you. Selective enforcement.

_utterances.text: I believe I am one extremely strong example of the selective enforcement being conducted in our township against our citizens. His documentation shows that he has been continually blown off and discarded by our staff. Only recently, and only because of board involvement, has his concern begun to be addressed and apparently resolved. While I very much appreciate all the work that has been done recently in hiring an additional employee to assist with the enforcement of our ordinances, we continue to ignore the selective enforcement that is being conducted by our staff. This is a much larger issue, whether it be persistently ignoring the ordinances at some locations or targeted over-enforcement of ordinances at others. I believe if we are serving our citizens with courtesy, integrity, and efficiency, they deserve to have our board address address this ongoing concern. Simply hiring another enforcement agent does not address nor solve this problem. It seems a very specific pattern of behavior from our staff is becoming increasingly clear. If this pattern of behavior, when our staff just doesn't like you, is to target and harass our citizens— and we now know it is— one can't help but wonder if that exact same thing doesn't happen to our employees. At least it sure made me wonder. Therefore, I took some time to meet with a handful of our former employees to listen to their stories. And I have to say, especially after the bias that our staff has continually shown over the last year, that the stories from our former employees are very consistent with the harassment stories from our citizens. As a matter of fact, the stories from our employees appear to be highly credible, which leads me to selective enforcement. Point example number 3. Back on January 16th, 2025, we had a closed session portion of the board meeting where we were presented with a legal complaint from a former Holland Charter Township employee. Please understand, I am not looking to discuss any details from that closed session for now. However, just like most of you, at that time I was very confident and placed 100% trust in our staff. I believe that our staff was straight and honest and would never allow such a thing as was described in the complaint by that former employee. But based on everything that has been revealed since then and everything that I have seen and experienced personally from our staff, I fear I may have been wrong, very wrong. Is it possible that we all fell for staff's Mr. Rogers' Neighborhood facade? Yes. Unfortunately, that former employee's story sounds very similar to my story. It sounds very similar to Mr. Willard's story, and coincidentally, very similar to many other stories that I have heard from other township residents. I have read and reread that legal complaint many times now. For each of you, if you have not recently reread that complaint, I highly encourage you to do so. Specifically, from this newly found perspective. I am guessing that you may be as shocked as I am by how familiar that story is. I highly regret voting for the information in that legal complaint to have been hidden from our citizens in a closed session. I highly regret that the final resolution of that closed session, as I am now quite convinced that as a board we have made— we may have assisted with a miscarriage of justice toward one of our own employees. I hope I hope and pray I'm wrong, but I don't think so. What I do know for sure is that moving forward, we owe it to the people to find the truth. We owe it to our citizens who elected us to represent them. We owe it to our employees. We owe them transparency. In summary, when you step back and look at the broader picture, citizens' complaints about selective enforcement on one side and internal concerns involving disparate treatment or potential retaliation toward an employee on the other, it raises a bigger question about whether township policies are being applied fairly and consistently across the board. The public deserves transparency, transparency on that broader issue. Given the amount of time that has passed and the concerns that have been raised by residents and employees, I believe the public deserves to understand what occurred and how these issues were handled. For that reason, I think it's appropriate for this board to determine whether continued confidentiality is still justified. Prior to board discussion, I would like to read through the motion that I will be presenting so it is clear what is being requested. I will be making a motion that the board determine whether the need for confidentiality still exists regarding the closed session that occurred on January 16th, 2025, and that the board allow discussion Discussion in open session of the broader policy issues related to the consistent and fair application of township ordinances and personnel policies to the extent permitted under the Michigan Open Meetings Act. I will further be moving that the board authorize an independent third-party investigation into township enforcement and personnel complaint practices in order to determine whether policies have been applied consistently and without disparate treatment or retaliation. And now I would like to open it up to any board discussion. Anybody have anything they want to say on that, or— go ahead, Gretchen.

IH95NH2imog.summary:
- **Code Enforcement Discussion**: Supervisor TeSlaa presented findings on noise ordinance enforcement, highlighting the effective use of 'affirmative relief' (court-ordered corrective actions) that began in September 2025. Current process shows 94-96% compliance after initial warning letters. No ordinance changes proposed; existing ordinance deemed adequate with proper enforcement mechanisms.
- **Zoning Amendment (188 N Jefferson Ave)**: Introduction of conditional zoning map amendment from I-2 industrial to C-2 commercial (mixed with R-2 residential). Planning Commission unanimously approved (7-0). Community concerns addressed. Returns for vote on March 19th.
- **Lost City LLC Liquor License**: Approved 5-0 for quota liquor license application. Contingent on written agreement that if applicant ceases operations in township, license returns to township, or alternatively township receives right of first refusal on any sale. License limited to beer, wine, and canned cocktails with restricted service areas and staff training requirements.
- **Federal Grants Management Policy**: Formal policy adopted governing management of federal grant funding, including approval process, implementation, budget monitoring, and record keeping.
- **Building Security & Infrastructure**: Security improvements ongoing at Township Hall including window installations at public service desks. Non-motorized pathway network resurfacing program approved for 2026 (7 segments, 4+ miles). Four retaining walls to be replaced.

highlights.quote: affirmative relief is a court-ordered requirement that the property owner take specific action to correct a code violation. This goes beyond paying a fine and requires physical corrections, actions such as removing debris or junk, correcting an unpermitted use, repairing or securing a structure, restoring the property to ordinance compliance, repairing or replacing a loud or modified vehicle muffler that violates noise ordinance.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So what— if you look at the days here, it's basically once a complaint's received, we go into an evidence submitted to the code enforcement. That can be verbal, video, email, or a phone call. So if we're going to deal with the sound issues, video would be the ideal way to let code enforcement know of something going on. Once that has been submitted to the code enforcement officers, we would open a case and the ordinance would be reviewed and these procedures would begin. And then if there's, if there's enough evidence that something went on, we would send a letter out in 7 days. And then we find within that, when that letter is sent out, compliance is at 94 to 96% of the time. The case is closed. If there is another issue that goes on, we send out a citation. And these are— we can only, as a township, only enforce property. Property is all we can enforce if it's on someone's property. So with the civil citation, what I found is I talked to our code enforcement officer, Jenny, and she is using something that is very, very effective. She's putting a fine and she's using something called, which I had never heard of, affirmative relief. So when she issues a ticket, she can put out a fine and affirmative relief. So when that happens, if you look on the thing here, you have a court reviewing— reviews it, and then the court enforcement— the code enforcement would monitor it. So, so what I'm basically saying is our current ordinance works. Citations are being issued. 94 to 96% of compliance. And fines and affirmative relief is a way that is actually working to correct problems. For if you're like me that didn't— have never heard of affirmative relief, it's on— affirmative relief is a court-ordered requirement that the property owner take specific action to correct a code violation. This goes beyond paying a fine and requires physical corrections, actions such as removing debris or junk, correcting an unpermitted use, repairing or securing a structure, restoring the property to ordinance compliance, repairing or replacing a loud or modified vehicle muffler that violates noise ordinance. So we have noise ordinances on the books. So what I'm suggesting is that if there's a muffler violation, you send a video of it going on to the code enforcement officer. The code officer will review it. They will issue, if need be, a letter to come into compliance. And if that case, they will issue a fine and affirmative action. So that's what we're currently doing. And it's— we started, I think it's September, they started affirmative relief. Have you heard of affirmative relief?

So that's what my findings are on this ordinance of the mufflers and other things that we've talked about. It's been a lot of work and a lot of due diligence, but I think it's a good solution. And thanks to our code enforcement officer that has had good luck with it. So that's kind of my spiel. I'd love to have more discussion if need be, but that's— I think things are working the way they're working. And unfortunately, Mr. Willard's gone to the point where he's done a lot of due diligence, and I appreciate that. As our business continues to grow, we're requesting approval for a liquor license that would allow us to responsibly offer beer, wine, and select canned cocktails. Over the past several years, we've made exhaustive efforts to acquire an existing local license but have not received responses to any of our inquiries. As a result, we respectfully request your consideration and approval to move forward so we may continue expanding in a way that benefits both our business and Holland Township. Over the past several years— or, I'm sorry, obtaining this license would allow us to enhance our food and beverage offerings Increase our open hours, host corporate events and private gatherings, provide new catering opportunities, and expand our team beyond our current 15 employees. Our primary goal is not simply to add alcohol sales, but to compete with existing entertainment centers outside of Holland Charter Township. We're confident this step will strengthen our long-term sustainability and improve the permanence and attraction to the Holland Town Center in which we're located. We're committed to responsible service, regulatory compliance, and maintaining the family-friendly environment that's defined our, defined our business I'd like to ask Jim Storey, our liquor licensing consultant, to outline the liquor license environment in Ottawa County, and both Jim and I will be available to take any questions you have afterward.
