_utterances.text: Well, good evening, I guess, temporary Chair Busch, and members of the Board of Trustees of Holland Charter Township. As referenced by Ryan, I am Jim DeGraaf. I'm of Storyline Connections. I'm a liquor licensing consultant. I've done that for about 20— no, not that long, about 15 years. I appreciate, really, the opportunity to visit with you on this important economic development request Lost City, which is a very popular entertainment destination for both residents and visitors, and very important for you, is uniquely located in Holland Charter Township. In all of the Mackinac region, it's here. The Lost City is asking for your economic development support by formally advising the Michigan Liquor Control Commission of the township's designation of the Lost City as the first among all others For the remaining quota license under your control. For a small business, the quota license is important because the cost of all other licenses issued by the Liquor Control Commission, for which Lost City is eligible, are beyond economic viability for most small businesses. For example, the category called resort licenses, which are limited to five issued annually by statewide. Are set by statute at $20,000. Class C liquor by the glass transferable licenses cost a market minimum of $70,000 in Ottawa County. By comparison, if the commission approves Lost Cities' quota license application after your action, the cost is a maximum of $1,300 annually in licensing fees, in addition to the $500 already already paid to the township for the application. For large national and international hospitality operations, such as those owning On the Border, Logan's, Burger Dave's, and so on, the high-cost licenses are but a blip on their capital budget sheet. For a small, locally owned business in the hospitality area, the virgin quota license in your possession is a lifesaver for the business and their employees. Holland Township charter government— excuse me— Holland Charter Township government has historically been friendly to economic development. The law city does not seek a tax abatement, often granted to large corporations, or waiving of fees. Granting access to the quota license in your hands is the no-cost-to-the-township aid that will help this Again, thank you for the time. I'd be happy, at your direction, Chair Bush, to respond to questions.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Well, thank you everyone for— did anyone else like to speak on behalf of Law City? I know you got some other people that came in the back. Are they just here for support? Okay. Do we have some questions as board members? Any comments, Steve?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Well, yep, as the staff report that was included in your agenda packet indicates, you know, we took a look at the various ordinance criteria enumerated there, 1, 2, 3, 4. Didn't come up with any means, you know, the automatic disapproval. There were no conditions with this application or its ownership regarding that under the discretionary items. Likewise, there was nothing that would lead staff to say that this should not be approved. And then, Lastly, it just provides some information on the priorities as well. So it did go through the staff review process with the building official. He didn't have any concerns. Planning and zoning looked at their respective component of it, didn't have any concerns. And again, my review of the full application, it was complete to form, and I didn't have any concerns with the information provided either.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thanks.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: How many total liquor licenses How many charter licenses do we have available yet before the—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: One.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: This is it?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: This is it. Next census.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: When were you guys here before? It seems like it's been a couple years. Is that right? What did we do back then?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: We actually, you guys had conditionally granted us a license based on the purchase agreement. But then, Edwards Realty, who owned the Town Center, had sold it. They basically said they don't want to sell us one building, they want to sell the whole center. And so, they ended up going a different route, which Rendered that kind of a loophole, I guess.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: So at that time you were not asking for a quota liquor license, is that right?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: No, we were.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: You folks had actually granted it to us upon receipt of the building purchase agreement.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: They were in negotiations to buy the building. We made that part of the contingent. I saw.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay. You were going to do a lot of modifications had you gotten that, were able to purchase it.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: It would have been about a About $5 million, right, in total, I guess, investment in total from us. And at this point, with us not owning the building, we're not necessarily pursuing that, but, you know, we are still attempting to purchase the building actively from the current ownership too.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay, so Edwards, who formerly owned it, wouldn't sell that portion to you. They sold the whole thing to one owner?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay, and you're negotiating with them right now?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, we have been in negotiations with them recently and Yeah, I mean, at this point there's not much happening, but yeah, we still are actively pursuing it as that's kind of the long-term goal.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah, I hope so. Yeah, I thought your drawings, and it was really— it would have been— it's going to be a great place.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: So you are currently leasing the building then, correct?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: So what happens with the liquor license if they don't renew your lease?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Uh, well, long term— well, first of all, we hope they do.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Well, of course. I would imagine, but—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: We've been in that location for 25-plus years. Yeah. And, uh, as of right now, we have a good relationship with the ownership group and do intend to extend after our current lease is up. There is always the option that that happens, but if that does happen, we would prefer to try and stay within Holland Charter Township.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: And then, Jim, you— that liquor license can float anywhere in Holland Township, is that right?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Pardon?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: That liquor license just has to stay in Holland Township, right? How does that work? Is it based on the address?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Well, as a licensee yourself, you might know that once once the court license is issued to an owner, that owner can transfer it any place in Ottawa County. Okay. Any place beyond except Ottawa County.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: They can't sell that either, can they?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I don't know.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Can they sell that?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I don't know.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I mean, instead of transferring it, can they sell it?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Well, it's considered Supreme Court. The court in Michigan has issued a ruling 25 years ago that the liquor— once a liquor license is issued to an owner, you, me, anybody, is considered personal property. So it can be sold to another owner.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Is there a way for us to keep it in Holland Township?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Legally?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Legally.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I don't know of any. Because state law allows it to be transferred within the county of where it is. So, I don't think you can overrule state law. Just a question. No, I'm just saying.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: That I don't know of any— a number of other localities with which I have dealt have attempted to do that. Really hasn't been defensible. And, candidly, it's never been an issue once the license has been issued. Clients that I've dealt with. Now, I can't— I mean, there are 13,000 liquor licensees in Michigan, so I can't speak for all of them, the environment there.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: How do you plan to monitor the alcohol control with the— it looks like it's— I mean, I've been in there with my sons. It's a fun place to hang out for kids. Um, so kids are obviously everywhere.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And if alcohol is everywhere, they're probably— this could be a problem. How do you How does that come into play?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, so I think it's multifaceted. I think we have to have management on site most of the time, if not all the time that we're serving alcohol. I think proper training would mean tips-approved staff. And I guess most importantly, policies for— I guess policies for drink maximums too. We don't intend to overserve anyone. This is strictly meant to be an addition to a good time, not something that you come to seek out at the Lost City. We don't want to be a bar, frankly. We don't want to be a place where, you know, grown adults come and hang out like that. We prefer to have kids around. We just want to offer something that the adults can enjoy while they're there.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Can I supplement that?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: You know, the Michigan law is pretty clear. You get an on-premise, and now I guess even off-premise, it's been expanded to, you need to, within 180 days of the license being issued by the Liquor Control Commission, you have to complete An alcohol management class for anyone who sells or serves alcohol. And of course, that pretty much covers the whole area, honestly. So, and that's a condition that'll be in the licensing order from the commission when it comes. And I can tell you, they do enforce it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Sure, go ahead, Mr. Church.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Number one, so yeah, my kids, as well as everyone else's, have enjoyed hanging out at your business. And I've been there with them, and I think I would have enjoyed having a beer while they were playing. That just seems very natural to me.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Mm-hmm.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: It occurs to me that with a lot of kids, the one thing you'd want to prevent is kids imbibing. And what do you propose to do It seems to me in some places they will restrict the serving of alcohol to a certain area. Of course, that wouldn't be necessary if you're having a corporate event where it makes a ton of money.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: If you're entertaining entirely adults to do something fun for an afternoon, you can serve it liberally and wherever. While kids are the primary users of the space, what are your—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: what is your concept for monitoring who's Again, I think it's, I think it's a multifaceted approach. I think it's having trained staff knowing what to look for, having management on site that's watching, you know, what's actually going on. I mean, we have typically on a busy Saturday, let's say, you know, we may have 10 employees on the floor at one point, and we're at all points, I have my assistant manager or myself are walking the floor constantly just looking for, you know, potential issues and things like that. So diligence and I think training are really going to be the two Cornerstone to that.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Is there a primary area where alcohol is served? Is there a bar, for instance?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: So right now we don't have a bar. We do have a concession stand. That will be the starting point. And long-term, you know, we've considered expanding the seating and maybe adding a— I don't know if I want to call it a bar, but an area, you know, that parents may sit that isn't necessarily associated with the open play areas all around.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Does it make sense to restrict alcohol to those areas?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So for instance, would there be some spatial types of restrictions that would make sense? Like here but not there?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, so there would be some. For example, like in laser tag, I wouldn't want any alcohol in the laser tag arena at all. It would probably be contained to party rooms, to the general seating area, the game floor, which would mainly just be parents walking around, you know, holding their beverage. But there's no tables, there's no places to set any of that down or You know, so it'd be in somebody's hand at all times. And mini golf would be the last place where, again, the parent might monitor their kid over there while, you know, maybe having a beer in hand, but for the most part would be restricted to the seating areas and party rooms, I think.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Trustee Church, can I add to that?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Of course.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: When I was on the Liquor Control Commission as a hearing officer, there are 3 areas where you can, if you If you're a licensee, you lose your license. Serving to minors, allowing drugs to hang around licensed premises, and cheating in your liquor order. So, it is incumbent on the licensee to make sure that alcohol doesn't touch the lips of kids.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Serving to minors is just—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: But it is where the commission, under successive commissions, The commissioners have been very diligent in saying, you do that, you may get one chance after that, but if it ever happens again, the license is gone. And when you're talking about something that is going to be important to the business to survive, that's the incentive. Make sure it doesn't get hit.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: It doesn't get hit. Because if it does, you're out. Your license is lost. Understood. So, I have one other question that goes to something that Dave was mentioning and others, which is, you are a long-term business operation in Holland Township. And I heard from what you said that you're interested in staying and being part of the development that you're part of, being part of the township, and we want that too.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I do, uh, think of the idea of it leaving Holland Township and not liking that. Not liking that. And I know you said those are hard to claw back, but can they be given back by agreement? So for instance, can the owner agree that In the event the owner leaves the township to establish elsewhere, there is an agreement in place that it'll be given back to the township. I also like the idea that it should be granted to businesses while they are operating and present in Holland Township, and that there be an agreement in place that if one of those 3 is not true, that it come back.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: You asked me hypotheticals. Can they do that?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I suppose you could try, but I don't know how enforceable that would be.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Because—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Is there a first right of refusal that we could have on it? Say, let's just— I'm just trying to go down the road here. You know better than me with your expertise. No one in the room is going to know more than you do on this topic. So—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'm sorry you had to qualify that.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah. Keep it above board. Keep it above board.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Above board.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Above board.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So let's just go down the road of Lost City. Hopefully they've been in business for hundreds of years.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: But if there's an opportunity for it, it's not working out, something happens, is there a way that the township could have first right of refusal on it? Some type of recorded document for the next board that's here in 20 years says— well, I'm just thinking, I'm just trying to be creative, and maybe there's not a way, I don't know.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: You could write anything.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'm just saying that under current— my understanding of current law, I'm not an attorney, but I have administered the law enough to know what the Attorney General's Office says, and the Liquor Control Commission's attorney is the Attorney General, is that you— they give to local governments— let me put it under the umbrella of what the role of local government is. The role of local The role of local government in the licensing system is to advise the commission. And the commission is pretty jealous about defending what it has been given to it by the legislature.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: To run.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: To run. And it's actually in the Michigan Constitution, if you look at it. Is once the Liquor Control Commission has been created, which it was in 1933, it has Exclusive power to regulate the sale of alcoholic beverages in the state of Michigan. They guard that pretty jealously. So, you can write it. Whether a future Liquor Control Commission would let you get away with it, I don't know. I'm just giving you the history.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I think that's an important comment. But even though I'm restricted from driving, I'm not going over 70 miles an hour on the highway, and I think the state government has exclusive authority to set those rules. Maybe the federal government, but some government entity does. I can agree with my insurance company to drive 55. I can agree. And if I drive more than 55, then my insurance company will have some recourse against me, like taking away my insurance. So I think that in this case, everyone wants the same thing. For the liquor license to benefit this business in Holland Township while it's operating, and I think there's no harm in putting an agreement in place that says yes, we're willing to recommend this. I think that's our role, right? To recommend that that license be granted, and then the Liquor Commission will act on that. But that we're willing to recommend it if the recipient is willing to return it should they cease operating in Holland Township. I think that's completely. Fair, and I would like to see this be the way we deal with this type of license going forward. I think that if it's to benefit the township, this is how we accomplish that. It may not be enforceable, I agree. I understand that it could be challenged, but I like 2 things. One is what I just said, and the other is that if that fails, that the applicant or the recipient agree to give the township a right of first refusal to purchase. I think those are 2 good options for us to have, even if they ultimately prove to be unenforceable, I would like them to be enforceable. That's my thought on it. That's all I have to say.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Sure. Yeah, that's fine.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I think the right of first refusal is the best of the 2 options that you have outlined there.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I mean, no doubt.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: But, of course, I would say, you know, you have plenty of businesses in Holland Charter Township that have quota licenses to which that doesn't apply.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: It doesn't apply.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: And they have done it. They have sold these quota licenses out of the township to other businesses. It has been, it has happened.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: As we've been told. That's what I'm saying. Of other municipalities.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Transferred in.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, exactly.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: For example, Fat Burrito transferred the City of Holland license into the township. That's a former, whatever, Italian restaurant. I can't remember the name of it.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Johnny Craino's?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, that's it. That's a City of Holland quota license that got transferred into the township.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I'm 100%, we have not done this in the past. But, and that's another reason I'm raising the question, is can we keep them here? I would much rather see them in Holland Township. So if there's a way to—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Well, I think it's a solid— excuse me for interrupting.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: But it's a solid fact, 30 years.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I'm totally getting it. Here's the thing, we want—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I want— They live in the township, they live here.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: That business ain't going anywhere. And, you know, think about this, of these kind of indoor entertainment businesses, the other The other major one is in the city of Holland. Lucky Strikes. So, this is one that is unique, like I said, to Holland Charter Township. So, I think, in there, they looked at your master plan for the town center. I don't know what the current name of it is.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Town Center.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Town Center. They're in total agreement with that. Want to be an anchor for it. Steve and I, in a couple of our pre-meetings, I've outlined some ways you can use existing liquor law to kind of expand that as a, if you will, an entertainment district. By creating either a principal shopping district, or a downtown development authority. And, in those cases, with those kind of licenses, you're in a much better driver's seat for keeping it there.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I think we should try, and here's why. Because, our primary responsibility is to Holland Township. Okay, and that means others in Holland Township as well. And, I agree that, if all the things you've asserted are true, and I have full confidence in that, but I think it's a good idea that, If they turn out not to be true, that that come back, and we have the opportunity to give it to others in Holland Township who would also benefit from it. I think that I would like to see businesses in Holland Township benefit from that now and in the future. And, you know, if Lawson is here in perpetuity, that is my wish too. That is my wish too. I don't see this as obstructing anything you want to do. I only see it acting in favor of the other residents of Holland Township in the event that that turns out differently. That's all. I feel like that's our responsibility.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I tend to agree with Jim. I like the idea of the right of first refusal. I don't think that would negatively impact us in any way if we did choose to move. Then we'd have to figure out a new liquor license or give us another opportunity to pursue that then. But yeah, as of right now, I don't see that being a roadblock for us. And at the end of the day, I mean, there's always the potential that it's not enforceable anyways.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So yeah, I'd like to add I'm disappointed that the owner did not allow that. I understand maybe why, but I hope that the new owner does see the benefit of you adding to this, because if he— if you do are able to purchase it and you make those changes, it's just going to make the area that much better.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I think so too. And per the master plan, which we reviewed, I mean, I've seen potential drawings of, you know, adding a park and maybe some standalone housing and some other things. And I just, I think having a community hub like that there, that's been there forever, I mean, it can only benefit that area, I think, anchor it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Well, there's no question. I think everyone wants to see your business excel, and I'm sure it will. I think, like Elliot said and Dave, it's just, they're trying to do the best thing for Holland Township. Holland Township thing is that if you could, does it hinder you on the application with the state if we put in a first right of refusal, whatever, you know, put some language into that?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: And that isn't something the state would consider one way or the other.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: They don't care.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: They don't care.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's your problem. That's your—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: that's the agreement between you guys.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Fine.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: We definitely do not want to hinder you. I think we all want to give you this license. Uh, let me ask you one more question.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Um, how long is your current lease for, and is there options to renew?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, so currently I think it— 2 more years from now, and it was basically left under, yeah, we'll renegotiate as rates have been You know, climbing and falling.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, massively so. Yeah, I will make a motion unless somebody has any questions.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Any more questions?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I think the motion should be contingent upon putting an agreement in place.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We'll make how you want to read, or you, Dave, one of you.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Go ahead, Mr. Attorney.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So I, I move that we approve subject to the The formation of an agreement between the applicant and the township that in the event the applicant ceases to operate in the township, that the license will be returned. Or if that proves to be unenforceable, that the applicant— that the township will have a right of first refusal as to any sale that is contemplated.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: For my own personal Could a township own the license after they've distributed it?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: They can buy it back.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: They could, okay.

_utterances.speaker: B

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Do you have enough information, Mr. Clerk, for the motion? Okay, so you're, you're moving for approval and that the township put an agreement in place.

_utterances.speaker: G

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: B

_utterances.speaker: D

EmCciTPf2X8.summary:
- **Public Comment**: Steve Willard presented escalating ordinance for modified exhaust penalties (escalating fines $250-$1,000, mandatory repair verification, impound authority). Requested township move enforcement from Sheriff's Office to code enforcement with escalating penalties.
- **County Administrator/Deputy Introductions**: New Ottawa County Administrator Patrick Centen (24 years local government experience; previously Hudsonville city manager, Grand Haven Charter Township planning director) and Deputy Administrator Brian Bissett (former Berrien County Administrator, South Haven City Manager) presented. Discussion of county priorities: Marshall Aquifer drawdown, farmland pressure, affordable housing, and PA 233 solar/battery storage legislation.
- **County-Township Collaboration**: County commissioners discussed strategic partnerships, state lobbying opportunities for capital projects, MDOT drain improvements ($60k per drain, MDOT funding), and past relationship concerns with previous administrator.
- **Road/Maintenance Approvals**: Approved 2.9 miles resurfacing ($759,338.10) by 6-0 vote; approved up to 3 cycles road mowing/trimming ($20,000 estimated); approved chloride dust control ($1,800 for 2 applications on gravel roads).
- **Administrative Updates**: Community center bingo event (100 attendees, monthly third Wednesday scheduling); sheriff January activity report (1,370 calls); winter tax collection at 96% ($9.8M billed).
- **Personnel**: Treasurer's cashier Jackie Kruse retiring after 18 years; Jodi Bauman hired to replace her; manager search underway with final candidates expected end of April/early May.
- **Modified Exhaust Ordinance Status**: Draft language under attorney review with sheriff input; revisions ongoing; timeline targeting April agenda with possible final solution. Board discussed constraints, escalating penalties, and enforcement mechanisms.

highlights.quote: In December, I presented this board with draft ordinance language for modified exhaust penalties with escalating fines. I was told end of January. Last meeting I was told it was being worked on. Tonight is, is the last meeting of February and there's nothing on the agenda. Since September, every weekday the same vehicle disrupts my neighborhood twice a day.

I handed this board draft language in December. Amendment B: escalating fines, $250, $500, $1,000 plus impound. Mandatory repair verifications. Real consequences that get worse. Not a flat fine that drivers Shrug off twice.

My background is local government. I've got about 24 years of experience. I was a city manager in Hudsonville for a good long stint, 12 years, so I really cut my teeth there. I was in Grand Haven Charter Township as the planning and zoning director, dealing with code enforcement, so I know that job well too.

The Marshall Aquifer is the primary groundwater drinking source for Ottawa County residents. It is capped by a pretty large layer of clay, and that clay prevents water from quickly recharging it. And we have now kind of passed that tipping point where we have more demand, more straws, more wells into that aquifer than it can recharge. So we're seeing a negative drawdown on the water table.

We put in $6 million in a public safety building. We had to come buy the building. There was no relationship at all. And then you send a contract for us for services, which the county does a great job in their police services. But kind of remember that we are paying our— we've paid our own share for a long time. I've been on this board for 20 years, and I have never ever really seen the county do much for Holland Township.

You guys don't have a township lobbyist, but we've got county lobbyists and it's budget season right now. And a lot of you know my work in Lansing when I'm not on the commission. But if there are big capital projects that you have coming up to take up, you know, let Patrick know, let us know. And that's something that we can work with our partners at the state to bring awareness to.

We had MDOT there and we were talking to them about a lot of the chemicals that comes off the vehicles as they, you know, travel up and down the highway through Holland Township, Zeeland Township, all the way across. They have money in their budget to improve those drains there to handle the chemicals. And it costs about $60,000 per drain, but they have money where they will— they're willing to foot the bill as long as it's reasonable.

highlights.topic: Escalation Problem and State Law Limits
highlights.quote: There is— the courts do have some say in what the fines are. They can What fines are, but that, that's a process with the state and with the courts. The local court can have some say on what the fine is. But that is not a function of the township. That's a function of the 58th District Court of Ottawa County.
It seems to me it's always inadequate. Every year, when I drive along any major trunk line, I just see weeds. And I think a It looks terrible. I think 2 has always been inadequate. I'd like 3 cycles.
I have that. I'll give that to you tonight. Let's end the discussion. If we can give money to other—

It's just maybe the next time you come in to do your presentation, if I could get a card from each of you, I would appreciate that. I'm just as bad. I'm a real estate agent. I never have mine either.

I will say I've heard good things as well, and I did take a little tour because I hadn't been through the final product. But I've heard very good things, and people just saying it's really nice, like. They expected more of a YMCA, and I've never been to one of those, but I've heard really good things about it.

Yeah, I just wanted— wondered if we could go over the, um, the process for hiring a new manager. How does that work? Who actually— does the board make that decision ultimately? The executive board? Who, who's looking right now? I know we're looking, but yeah.
Who's we when you say that?
So after that process is all done, is it a board that makes the decision that votes on that, or how?

_utterances.text: Steve Willard, 3568 Jaden Court. Good evening, board members. In December, I presented this board with draft ordinance language for modified exhaust penalties with escalating fines. I was told end of January. Last meeting I was told it was being worked on. Tonight is, is the last meeting of February and there's nothing on the agenda. Since September, every weekday the same vehicle disrupts my neighborhood twice a day. At 6:30 in the morning, he starts his car and it sounds like a shotgun going off. That's what wakes me up. At 3:15 in the afternoon, he comes home and revs his engine again. Every weekday. 6 months. I've reported this vehicle to the Sheriff's Office over 20 times. The first sergeant brushed me off until I filed an internal affairs complaint. Only then did he write one ticket. A new sergeant, Sergeant Tubergen, took over. I had to start the process all over. He issued a second citation on February 4th, 2 tickets total. The driver still does it every single weekday. Last week I called Sergeant Tubergen again. His response: he'll try, time allowing, to sit in the area around 3:15 to hear for himself. Try. Time allowing. That is the ceiling of what the Sheriff's Office will do. Modified exhaust is not a political win for them. There are no headlines in a muffler ticket. It will never be a priority, and 2 citations with zero behavior change proves it. But Township Code Enforcement officers do get wins from quality of life and Enhancements, improvements. That's their mission. When code enforcement cleans up a neighborhood, the board hears about it. It matters here in a way that it'll never matter at the county level. So why are we still outsourcing this to an agency that doesn't prioritize it with penalties that clearly don't work? I handed this board draft language in December. Amendment B: escalating fines, $250, $500, $1,000 plus impound. Mandatory repair verifications. Real consequences that get worse.

_utterances.text: County is a different animal. It's, it's definitely a different animal than the cities, and Brian will share, he was a city manager as well. City level, and at the township level too, you're very connected with the residents day to day. You're hearing Road needs to be plowed, water's not working. County is a little higher level, and a lot of people don't really understand at the, at the county level the things that happen. I often say a lot of the services that counties provide, most people don't use, or a lot of people don't use. You think about your jails, your courts, the services that are there by state statute that are required to be provided. We're excited. We're in one of the fastest growing counties in the state, as I'm sure you all know. And so we're looking to work with our board on a lot of the growth-related challenges that we're facing, namely pressure on farmland, the demand for more affordable and attainable housing, and also our biggest issue and concern environmentally right now is the drawdown on the Marshall Aquifer, which is in a specific kind of central eastern portion of the county. But if you don't know, the Marshall Aquifer is the primary groundwater drinking source for Ottawa County residents. It is capped by a pretty large layer of clay, and that clay prevents water from quickly recharging it. And we have now kind of passed that tipping point where we have more demand, more straws, more wells into that aquifer than it can recharge. So we're seeing a negative drawdown on the water table. And we're at the county level trying to figure out how do we address those challenges through coordinated land use control, smart growth, education on smart water conservation and different alternatives to watering your yard. And then also looking at municipal water systems and opportunities how to connect those at a high level. So, our board is about to engage in a strategic planning retreat in March. _utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I do in my truck.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I can run out and get them.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: It's just maybe the next time you come in to do your presentation, if I could get a card from each of you, I would appreciate that. I'm just as bad. I'm a real estate agent. I never have mine either.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I don't know.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Horrible idea.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I have a couple questions. I'm sorry, go ahead first. So, the questions that I have for you is that we had a county administrator, first John Shea, that we had a great relationship with. That went by the wayside. You're back on the train here. So, it's important for us to have a relationship with the county. We put in $6 million in a public safety building. We had to come buy the building. There was no relationship at all. And then you send a contract for us for services, which the county does a great job in their police services. But kind of remember that we are paying our— we've paid our own share for a long time. I've been on this board for 20 years, and I have never ever really seen the county do much for Holland Township. We've kicked into the kitty; we haven't asked for much. So when you have your strategic planning, it would be nice to have you come back with a solution for us, some type of infrastructure solution for us. We've paid our own way for a long time. And then for aquifer ideas, Commissioner Water Resource Commissioner Joe Bush has done a great job of informing us on what this it's almost. crisis mode. I hate to use that, but I really appreciate anything you can do with Joe. He does an outstanding job. So that's my—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Can I ask just a follow-up? When you're talking about coming alongside with infrastructure, what specifically are we talking? Roads and sewer? Are we talking police and fire?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Well, I can tell you, like, when the Road Commission, the first millage that passed, we funded the Road Commission $1 million to do Riley, thinking it was going to pass. So we're willing to be creative. We're willing to do what we can do. Our 42,000 residents that we have in Holland Township, we're willing to do what we can do to be creative But it would be nice to hear back from your side, is there infrastructure that goes on in Holland Township that can be looked at? It took a long time to get stuff done with Hawthorne Park. That was a big deal to try to get that whole project done. Just little stuff. I'm not trying to be critical of what you're doing. I'm excited that you're back. John Shea and I were having a great relationship and that got stripped away for really no reason.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So, that's what I have to say.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I appreciate it. And I'd love to have a follow-up with you just to kind of better understand what those issues are because As you know, Road Commission is roads and different functions.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yeah, I get what you do.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I'd love to hear more.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Just my two cents.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Well, I want to jump in too, given the collaborative nature of government from township to county, but also we've got a relationship from county to state, from township to state as well.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: And we have a—

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: to my knowledge, you guys don't have a township lobbyist, but we've got county lobbyists and it's budget season right now. And a lot of you know my work in Lansing when I'm not on the commission. But if there are big capital projects that you have coming up to take up, you know, let Patrick know, let us know. And that's something that we can work with our partners at the state to bring awareness to and, you know, potentially do something in the budget or have some sort of collaboration with multiple layers of, you know, local and state funding. So, you know, just keep those in mind.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Depending on us.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I appreciate you saying that, because we've never really done that. We've kind of pulled our own weight and paid our own way.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Most haven't.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I mean, most don't even know that's an option, right? You have this huge capital project, and you look in the state budget, all sorts of other counties and cities and townships have, you know, line items right there that the state's paying for. It's an option.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: It is an option.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: You know, we can't make any promises. the whole budget process is a big negotiation. But, you know, we might as well ask. That's the least we can do, right?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, counties have a unique role. We can convene, we can help find solutions, and we can advocate. And that's maybe a role that we can do a better job for you guys.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I appreciate that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: One?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Oh, yeah. For those who are less informed, can you describe the reporting relationships? So, who do you report to? And who reports to you?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep. Okay.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: So, I have 11 bosses.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: You report to the commissioners?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I do, directly.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: And then, uh—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: He's our only employee.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: All right then.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, so, we want to make sure it's done right.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: And then, uh, and then, Brian and I are in the administration office, and then we've got 1,200 employees in about 24 different departments. 5 of those are elected officials. So, you've got your treasurer, your clerk, your sheriff, Your drain commissioner, and those, those agencies. So, those are departments, but I don't have direct oversight over them as elected officials. So, it's a very subtle dance that we do to try to stay on the same page with the direction that we're headed. But, if you had an issue with an elected official, for example, I couldn't hold that elected official accountable, if that makes sense.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: It does. So, the sheriff does not report to you?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: But we have to work together because the funding for the Sheriff Department and all the other electeds flows through the Administrator's Office from a budgetary standpoint.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I was just going to give you a quick fun update. So I sit on the Drain Board with Joe Bush, and you guys were mentioning the good work that he's doing, and there's a lot to talk about there that I'm not going to get into tonight that's very positive in relation to what might be happening with getting the groundwater situation addressed. What I wanted to mention is in our meeting today up at Fillmore Complex, we had MDOT there and we were talking to them about a lot of the chemicals that comes off the vehicles as they, you know, travel up and down the highway through Holland Township, Zeeland Township, all the way across. They have money in their budget to improve those drains there to handle the chemicals. And it costs about $60,000 per drain, but they have money where they will— they're willing to foot the bill as long as it's reasonable. It's not a million dollars because we have to work with some of our entities, and we haven't talked about that yet. But it's nice because it's not coming out of our budget and it's helping Holland Township, right? It's keeping our water cleaner as it comes off the road and back into our aquifers and downstream. So these are ways that we're working that You don't always get to see, you know, but we are advocating for our spaces. Josh Bruger, the chair, was also in the meeting, so he's advocating for, you know, his area, Grand Haven. And I'm like, okay, give me Holland Township, Zeeland. Let's get this done. Let's get this money in first. And, you know, that's where the road is. So yeah, there's a lot of these little benefits that I hope to be bringing to you soon, you know, the next time you see us and say, yeah, we got that.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: And speaking of advocacy, and to Commissioner George's point on the lobbyists, so we, we have regular meetings with legislators and the lobbyists. We have a little more clout as a county. The legislators, you know, we have their ear a little bit more. Yeah, we pay very close attention to the legislation that's coming across the desk in Lansing. Any legislation that we feel threatens local control or local choice, We will send Bob to Lansing to advocate against. So, we're looking out for the interests of the local units in regards to making decisions. PA 233 is a good example. That's the new law that effectively bypasses local decisions on energy, large-scale energy developments, the battery storage or the solar. We actually hosted a forum, an educational forum, that we They invited all the local units in to say, here's what you can do, here's what you can't do. But, that's a piece of legislation that Ottawa fought against, and it went through. So, that's an example of how we're kind of working behind the scenes on things on your behalf that you may not always know about.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Russ and I were at that. You did a very good, you guys did a very good job on that. It was very informative, I think, Russ. We got a lot of information out of it. So, we really appreciate you doing that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Thanks again for showing up tonight.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Absolutely.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: In force.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: May I make one comment?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, I just want— since you're all here, I want to give a shout out to the county park staff, in particular Kurt Terhaar and Aaron Bodvile-Mast. Yeah, so when we were working on the Hawthorne Park project, you know, jointly, and we decided we were going to go after a DNR grant, and the decision was made that the township would Put in for that so that you could pursue other opportunities with them as well. Those guys spoon-fed that process to me. You know, they are just experts at that system, know exactly what the DNR is looking for. They were tremendous to work with. I owe them a debt of gratitude for buttoning that all up. You know, I signed it, but they did 99% of the work.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Wow.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So I just want to shout out to those 2 guys. Really helped us a ton.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, I will do that.

_utterances.speaker: G

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, they're good guys.

_utterances.text: They've written a few grants on there.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: That's right.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: They've got it down to a science. So, and we got the full amount. Anyways, thank you.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay. So, thanks again.

_utterances.text: What a great guy John Shea was. And for those that don't know, Patrick was John's deputy at that point, and we lost him. But then we were able to. To John Shea. John Shea brought him over to be his number two over there.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We poached him back, so we did our job.

And it's kind of interesting, that last page there, we do have of course just one month of data since we began the new year, but You know, the preceding years as well, and it kind of shows where 2025 ended up. We talked about that a little bit the last time we were together as well. So we do have Lieutenant Knott here this evening.

_utterances.speaker: I

And so this year, the estimate for per application of chloride treatment is $1,800.

_utterances.text: All right, thanks, Steve. We'll do these one at a time. The first one is a new motion for 2.9 miles of resurfacing existing pavements and restoring driveways and shoulders in the township on the attached list that we have, for a total of $759,338.10. I'll move for approval.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G

_utterances.speaker: B

_utterances.speaker: L

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Mr. TeSlaa?

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: L

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Mr. Chairman?

_utterances.speaker: B

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Do we have the option of requesting a third, if it seems necessary? It seems to me it's always inadequate. Every year, when I drive along any major trunk line, I just see weeds. And I think a It looks terrible. I think 2 has always been inadequate.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Look to— hello? If you want to adjust the motion up to 3.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Do you want to do a trimming and a road— what do you want to do, trimming and mowing, or both?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I don't think we have to adjust the motion.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: You don't have to adjust the motion.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: We just have to—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Steve has to request it.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: What do you want to do, Elliott, mowing and trimming?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I guess I'd like 3 cycles. It seems to me every year, in the middle of the summer, when I drive along the roads that are 4 lanes, they look surprisingly unkempt. It's my opinion that we should up it to 3, at a minimum. I would move that we double this. Okay.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So, you're moving for 3 cycles? Up to 3 cycles?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Up to 3 cycles. One is somehow included, and then this would be a second, and for another $10,000, a third. So, I think this changes the estimated cost to $20,000.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Oh, it would be $10,000 per—

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So, yeah, up to 30. I would move—

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I do move that we approve 3 cycles of bone marrow.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I support it.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: All in favor, say aye.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Any opposed? Same sign? Okay, we'll see if we can get those scheduled in. Then there's the chloride treatment program. This is for 2 applications of liquid calcium chloride spot treatment in front of homes on gravel roads during the summer of 2026, and the request is for $1,800 to spread 2,000 gallons of material. Look for a motion on that.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: What exactly does that do?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: It's dust control on gravel roads.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay, so only road applications?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep, and I think that's always been adequate. We don't get any other complaints that I know of. Steve?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, you know, it's one of those things, again, weather dependent. When we have a really dry summer, you know, then sometimes we hear from Property homeowners more saying, hey, can they hit it again? And so, yeah, again, very weather dependent. I mean, I can remember a couple years, I think we've done 3 or 4.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So we do do more if there's the need.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: The plan is for 2, but we do extra if there's more.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: How many gravel roads do we have?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Very few.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, the tail end of Mason Avenue from 104 East by Ridge Point Church. And 124th Avenue from Ransom to Quincy.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I was thinking we have what, so that's less than 2 miles? Oh yeah, yeah, less than 2 miles.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: See, that makes us better. Oh, I support that motion.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: She's going to make it.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: I'll make the motion. I'm sorry, I apologize.

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I'll support it.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Supported by Vince. Okay, all in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Any opposed, same sign. Okay, administrative report. Steve.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, you're second.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Everything went to sleep. So, you know, when we, um, envisioned the community center, we said from day one it was going to be a gathering place for all. So, you know, certainly, you know, people are out playing pickleball and using the exercise equipment and walking the track, but we intentionally incorporated other spaces that would be used for non-athletic activities. And what you're looking at here is yesterday's bingo event in the community center room. So 100 people, very broad cross-section of The community came together for Bingo Day. So $5 to cover the pizza lunch that we provided. Staff worked to get, you know, small gifts from a number of different businesses, which were the prizes in the bingo. And I just thought this event just encapsulated so nicely the, the other part of that vision, the non-athletic part of the vision of the community center. So, just wanted to share that with you.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Thank you. I didn't know that we did that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, just started. I think this was the second event of bingo.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: But now it's been scheduled once a month.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Every third Wednesday.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Third Wednesday.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I will say I've heard good things as well, and I did take a little tour because I hadn't been through the final product.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Oh.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: But I've heard very good things, and people just saying it's really nice, like. They expected more of a YMCA, and I've never been to one of those, but I've heard really good things about it.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So thanks, Dave.

_utterances.speaker: K

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: It was kind of after hours.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: You already did your pump?

_utterances.speaker: F

_utterances.text: Anything else, Steve?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's it for me.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Dave?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Uh, yeah, I just wanted— wondered if we could go over the, um, the process for hiring a new manager.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: How does that work?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Who actually— does the board make that decision ultimately? The executive board? Who, who's looking right now? I know we're looking, but yeah.

_utterances.text: So we've posted it on the MTA board as well as Michigan Municipal League and collecting resumes for Basically up to the next month, we'll be reviewing resumes over by the end of. I didn't take my agenda along with me.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So we've got Mike, myself, Vince, Randy Quartering, and then my missing Warren Smith and Warren Smith.

_utterances.text: Finance director, yeah. So that's on there. There'll be a first round where we try to narrow it down to 4 to 5 candidates. And we need a board presence there because That has to be recorded, has to be an official meeting. And so that— I'm hoping that that can happen sometime end of April, first part of May.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.text: So after that process is all done, is it a board that makes the decision that votes on that, or how? Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah. You have to make the final approval.

_utterances.text: All right.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: As a board.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: All I could do is recommend.

_utterances.speaker: F

_utterances.speaker: K

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Quiz away.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: C
