_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Pass.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Vince?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So, as you know, the winter tax bills went out. We sent out 12,892 tax bills. bills this year. We billed $9,832,268 for just the Holland Township, and we collected 96% of that. And then we send out 700 courtesy notices, which will take us to 98% collected. So that's kind of the update on the winter tax season. Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Thank you for those.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Is it low?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Is it okay for you?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: We can talk to the assessor.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Is that okay?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, thanks.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Does he get the employee discount?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yeah, Dave gets one.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, yeah.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: And then on a different note, uh, our cashier Jackie Kruse has been there 18 years and she will actually be retiring at the end of February. And Rose Borst, who's been a saint, that she retired from the city of Zeeland, she's been our like go-to helper if we ever need anything. She'll be also retiring and We have found another person by the name of Jodi Bauman that will be starting on Monday, and she's got a lot of accounting and customer service, and we're pretty excited to have her. I think it'll be a seamless transition. So, that's an update from the Treasurer's Office.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Thanks, Vince. Gretchen?

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Can I ask a question?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay, I just want to know where we're at with Mr. Willards, and I'm sorry, I maybe Missed it or don't remember. How are we going to help this man get this resolved? Is it— it's in between like the county, right?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: No.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Attorney?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Well, it's that we've had attorneys review, okay, and we've done different renditions of that, okay. Um, we submitted that to, um, the sheriff, okay, and we've had some discussions there, some other feedback input. For some other revisions. So that's where we're at at this point.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Revisions to the ordinance?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Correct, of what we had submitted and looked at.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Just for—

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: so we don't—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: best way that we can enforce it.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: There's— we can't go ticket this gentleman who has a slow car?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We submit those to the sheriff.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, we have code enforcement, but because I get calls All over from everybody that—

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: from business owners that get, you know, citations and whatnot. And they seem to respond pretty quickly.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: You know, some people do respond faster than others.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: So what is— what is the—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I think they have given him a ticket.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Pardon me?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I thought they gave him a ticket. Didn't you say that, Mr. Will, that they gave a ticket to the guy?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: They gave 2 tickets.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: 6 months.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So what we're looking for is— and he's asking about escalation, so that's the next step of what's best options there.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay. I thought we did have a noise ordinance though, right?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep, we had a generic one.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Gotcha.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: And now we need to— this is the one, this is the—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We went to a whole bunch of detail, but now we're paring that back. To—

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: thank you—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: somewhat of a generic but would have some more teeth in it.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay. Yeah, thank you. So that'll give us more of a—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Hopefully be more effective.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Effective to get this guy to stop doing it, or does he just need to be caught by a police officer revving his engine?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I think that's what—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: that's what we have some Yeah, he's had some stakeouts to do that.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Oh, he has been?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah. And he's been issued citations.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: So he just pays them and he keeps on doing it? Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: What's the reason for that?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Well, the problem is a lot of people will spend a lot of money to make their cars sound that way. So this person may have spent a couple thousand dollars on his exhaust. And I don't know, I haven't seen the car. Yeah, but from past history, people will spend thousands of dollars on their exhaust to make it sound a certain way. So a ticket here and there is just part of the price.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: They don't care.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: It's, it's, yeah, laughable if you get a $150 ticket as opposed to another $2,000 to fix your exhaust.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: So we're just kind of in a loophole.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We'll try to close that loophole.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yeah, it's going to be closed.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: So there's going to be a law enacted and your name is going to be on it.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, there you go. Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Origin story.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yeah. So what is the time frame going forward? It seems like a lot of times passed, and things are happening evidently. What are we anticipating in terms of further revisions and further review? And I think the request here is to get an ordinance in front of the board to vote on. What's the timeframe for that? What's the timeframe? Do we have— do we have a timeframe?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I don't have an exact one.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: What do you think? I mean, what's it looking like? What's the next step? What are we waiting on at this moment? And what would be the step after that?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I would hope that in April.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I've seen— just not to interrupt you, but I've seen that the attorneys have reviewed it.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: The county is trying to make sure that we can enforce what's on the books, because they're the ones enforcing it.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: And our code enforcement, we're looking at a new person there. So, we've got— everything's lining up. I'm sorry it's not happening as fast as you want, and I feel bad for you.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: But it's moving. It's coming to the end.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay, it's moving at the pace of government, as fast as it can go.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So this must be frustrating.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, we're sorry.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Well, if I received a ticket once or twice or 3 times, if that ticket could escalate to like, if you spend $2,000 on a modification, and that you're going to get another ticket, and the next one you get, you get put in jail. Is there anything like that? Because I'll tell you, if I was— if I lived next door and I heard that every morning, I would be livid. In fact, on my way here on 136, I was at the light here turning, and someone was turning off of Riley to go onto 136, and it probably was that guy.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: And he—

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: it was so loud that it— when he accelerated, I mean, it It jolted me, and I can't— I hate, you know, if it was any of our senior citizens that drive, or that's— it's just, I think it's unacceptable. If he's just getting a ticket and it's $250 or whatever, I mean, how many tickets do you get before you get in trouble? Like big trouble, you have to serve jail time. I mean, what is there? We don't have anything like that, do we?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So, It's no different than a speeding ticket. You get stopped for speeding, you get a fine, and you get a point.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: I like what you just said, because if I speed enough, I'm going to get my license taken away, right? Can we take— is that—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Under the state law, this is not a moving violation.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Gotcha.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: It's an equipment violation. It's actually a repairable ticket. So if you fix it, bring it into the court, and the court says, yep, you quieted it down, Adequately, they'll waive that fine. You have the option of just paying the fine and going on with your day, or you can fix it, make modification, and you don't get that fine assessed to you.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Which is a $100 fine, so it's nothing to that for a $2,000 model.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: So that's a state-regulated fine? Yes.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So we need Michigan Motor Vehicle Code.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: So we need to go up the chain too, don't we, a little bit? And tell the state they need to make these fines higher as well, correct?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: The state controls some of that. The courts have a say on what the fines are as well.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay, so locally the courts—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: The courts can.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: I'm not trying to beat you up, I'm just trying to educate myself.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I'll be completely transparent.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: No, I appreciate— I appreciate this so much, you have no idea.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: There is— the courts do have some say in what the fines are. They can What fines are, but that, that's a process with the state and with the courts.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay, not the local court. It would be that—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: The local court can have some say on what the fine is.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: But that is not a function of the township. That's a function of the 58th District Court.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Of Ottawa County.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay. It's kind of confusing on all of the, the township, the chain of command.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Township laws are completely separate from what the state laws are. State law is— say, so even with the loud exhaust the way it is now, my guys can only write that ticket if that vehicle is traveling on the roadway. If it's sitting in his driveway, I can't write him under state law because I can't enforce state law on private property.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So until that vehicle leaves the driveway, accelerates down the street, then I can make a traffic stop on him under the way that the state law is written and issue that citation, whether it's a fixed ticket or he decides to pay the fine.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: So he obviously is educated and he knows these things.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I don't know if he is or he isn't. I mean, you heard one coming here.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: We get constant complaints about loud exhaust.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: I'm sure you do.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: It's a huge issue out at the beach, you know, in Park Township.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: And we'll sit there and we'll write the same people over and over again or we'll write everybody. It just depends. But it never ends. Because of the amount of cars that are out there with that exhaust. I mean, look at how much traffic we have in Holland Township. It's just like speeding, it's just like registration, it's just like cars with no insurance. They're all out there. That one just grabs everyone's attention because it's loud. And we do our best to enforce it and stop cars and write tickets when we can.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: I appreciate you saying that because this gentleman's been to several of our meetings and obviously Obviously, this is videoed and people hear it or they watch it and they don't know how the procedure is and who's responsible. So, your explanation helps me understand it better, and I'm sure those out there that will be watching this as well. It's not that we're not doing our jobs, our hand— we've got to go through these certain steps.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: So, I appreciate that explanation and I'm— I'm thankful that you're here tonight.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I'll second that. And Russ, so as for the township, are we doing everything we can with all possible haste to come up with a solution?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yes. Okay.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: And we're anticipating having something on the agenda?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: At least an update in April. I don't know if it'd be a final solution, but I would like it to be on the agenda then.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Does that help you at all? Did that give you any, any, any more information?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yeah, but if you're asking for my opinion, the township code can change to $250, $500, and $1,000. That is legal, and they can enforce a repair legally, and they can force an impoundment. You can write that in your code. It can be law. That can happen, and they know it, and they want they want you to do that because they don't want to deal with it anymore.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Well, I think their hands are tied, sir. Yeah, I understand. That's why.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yeah, he didn't come through.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Yeah, I think I just think we need to know who what we all can do to help this get resolved.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: If we go the sound route, I'm very anxious to get this.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Me too.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I want to be able to sleep in without thinking about it.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Would there be a way that we could see the draft text of the amendment sooner than April? Um, I think, I think so. Mr. Willard is asking for certain things, and we're understanding that the, the Sheriff's Office cannot do certain things without the ordinances being changed. It would be nice if we could see a draft. I think everyone would like to understand What, what is being proposed, and does it, does it match at all what is being asked for? I would like to see that as soon as possible.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I would too.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: There's a draft out there, whatever condition it's in. I think we ought to at least have it in, at least have it in the packet next time around, just, just to see what's on, what's on the menu right now. I mean, maybe it needs to be changed. We could then at least discuss how it compares to what's being asked for and the constraints that the Sheriff's Office is under It would be nice to understand what we're dealing with. There's there's enough attention on behalf of the board, given that it's gone on a long time. I think we want to see what progress is being made.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay. And you know, he spent money on a FOIA to get this information. I did tell him I was going to refund him his information because I think all of the time that he spent to research this and to bring this to our attention, and there's other. We hear from the lieutenant that people are complaining about the noise ordinance. If we can give money for economic development or something, isn't there something in our cookie jar that we could— because he's, he's bringing this up. This is going to make our township better. So if we can't refund him that money that he spent to get this information, to bring this to our table—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I want to go to Do that?

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Pardon me?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I wouldn't be able to do that, no.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Is there anything— there's nothing that we can do?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I just—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: it's the taxpayers' money, so it's their money. It's 42,000 residents' money, but it's just one person asking for it. So he had to— he paid the FOIA. How much did you pay for it? $150.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: I have that. I'll give that to you tonight. Let's end the discussion.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yeah, there you go.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: If we can give money to other—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Contract for services. We don't give money to anyone. We contract for services.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Anything else, Elliot? No, thank you.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Look for a motion to adjourn. I'd like a motion to adjourn. Okay. Thanks, Steve.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Questions, Dave?

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Yeah, why don't we go— uh, they haven't played back any of my questions.

6mueb0PK9KA.summary:
- **Public Comment on Code Enforcement**: Citizen presented detailed 24-page analysis documenting enforcement gaps at 11813 Greenleaf Street, requesting formal performance review and potential termination of Community Development Director Corey Broersma for incomplete and inconsistent enforcement over 5 months.
- **Road Commission Updates**: Curt Kirkland reported summer projects including Butternut Drive resurfacing, roundabout installation at 120th and Port Sheldon, bridge work on 104th over Black Creek, and funding from new road tax ($13 million promised but not yet received). Road Commission over budget by $430,000 on snow removal; out of salt but can purchase additional allocation.
- **IFEC Tax Abatement - Next Generation Services**: Approved motion (unanimous) for 12-year industrial facilities tax exemption worth $2,435,672.70 for oil reclamation company; $2.44M capital investment, 8 new jobs, 24 retained positions.
- **IFEC Transfer - VoltaGen LLC**: Approved motion (unanimous) to transfer exemption certificate from Volta Power Systems to VoltaGen (new ownership); 15 employees with median wage of $130,000/year; company-wide profit sharing program implemented.
- **MacIntyre Resource Center Funding**: Approved motion to increase funding from $56,000 to $76,000 (additional $20,000 for mortgage principal); serves 3,000+ people annually; kitchen licensing completed; new Black Sheep Kastani entrepreneurship program launching.
- **Road Resurfacing**: Staff recommends 15 subdivision street segments totaling ~$760,000 and 2.86 miles for 2026 resurfacing; $1M total budget also covers striping, dust control, mowing.
- **Brownfield Authority**: Warren Smith reappointed; intergovernmental agreement approved for Ottawa County Brownfield Authority to manage program paperwork and reporting; 3-4 brownfields currently in township.
- **Crane Truck Purchase**: Approved motion (6-0 roll call) for Michigan Kenworth purchase at $220,209.91 (low bid).
- **Code Enforcement Report**: 1,277 complaints in 2025 (grass/weeds 397, property condition); staffed by 1.55 FTEs. Board discussion on selective enforcement concerns, compliance metrics, staffing (moving to 2 full-time), and partnership with Sheriff's Department.

highlights.topic: Code Enforcement Complaint – Selective Enforcement Pattern
highlights.quote: 5 months, same property, same pattern, same director...His own photo shows flat tires and registration expired over 2 years. His own evidence proves non-compliance by his own stated criteria. He acknowledged my January 13th evidence. He said he would review it. Then he responded 3 weeks later as if it never existed.
We used to have a lot of T-bone intersections, collisions there, and I think we've dropped them down to none.

highlights.topic: Next Generation Services – Tax Savings & Wages
highlights.quote: It's probably $35,000 to $40,000 a year in taxes...with that $35,000 to $40,000 in savings, are you willing to give every one of your employees a $5 an hour raise?
Last month we rolled out a company-wide profit share program to all of our employees. So as we continue to grow and expand, that goes right back to our employees.

We help over 3,000 people...Nonprofits have access to spaces they cannot afford. They also have access to mentorship and support, shared development opportunities, more efficient use of public dollars, safer neighborhoods.

My thought process is that he's talked about it for maybe the last 2 years about reducing that mortgage...I'd like to increase that to $76,000, and he can do what he wants with it. But I would appreciate if just the suggestion that you put it right to the principal of your mortgage.

She inspects 2,600 rental units that we have on a cycle...she's got a lot. With just one person driving around...their goal is that they drive every street in the township. The goal is once a month. Right now, it's probably more like once every 2 months.

highlights.topic: Lawn Avenue Road Abandonment – Legal Dispute
highlights.quote: anyone who knows anything about real estate knows appurtenant easements run with the land...staff is just very unhappy and very unwilling to accept the outcome of Mr. Bulthuis' legal advice, perhaps even to the point of defiance. It also appears that it's even more possible that staff just doesn't like Dave DeYoung.

I feel like that needs to be documented because this is incomplete for me. I don't want to know how many investigations we've done...compliance rate seems important. And maybe the time lag between report and compliance, that also seems important as a measurement of effectiveness.

We 100% approve these. We've never said no. We've never said no. I just feel like we have sort of a lack of criteria. Just an ask is always good enough...I feel like it would be nice to raise the bar a little bit, or at least so we can feel like it's justified.

dave_segments.text: I think that even a bigger problem that we have is not just the enforcement of the code, but the fact that some situation, homeowners have a continuous violation and they are totally ignored. That situation is totally unenforced over and over and over. Even if there's complaints that are brought up, the situation is totally ignored. In other situations, ordinances are enforced that they are not enforced on anyone else. This is not just a compliance issue. This is a selective enforcement issue.

It's probably $35,000 to $40,000 a year in taxes. And you have how many current employees?...with that $35,000 to $40,000 in savings, are you willing to give every one of your employees a $5 an hour raise?

I would say I'm very reluctant to give away money, I will say that. From the township's money. I'm surprised that you're not asking for more, I guess, is what— even with the, just the cost of living and how everything has gone up, how you're still doing that.
dave_segments.topic: Tax Abatement – Reluctance to Give Away Money

dave_segments.text: I do think what you're saying, though. I want to see that capital campaign get started, too. To pay off that mortgage, right?...I will make a motion to approve the $76,000.

I would also draw over to 610 North Shore Drive, which there have been and are continuing violations over there. It would really be great if we could get Mr. Broersma to spend some time over there checking those out instead of blaming that on the Ottawa County Road Department...There's a violation of a second driveway that was installed improperly, illegally.
dave_segments.topic: Code Enforcement – North Shore Drive Violation

dave_segments.text: They continued to enforce it at my house because the sheriff was continually called by the people that live at 610 North Shore Drive...I will bet you that nobody else has.

I would say I'm very happy that he's getting some attention on his situation, so I would like to make sure that the attention is also [given to others]...Even with the, just the cost of living and how everything has gone up, how you're still doing that. I'm agreeing with everybody here that $56,000 with what you do with that is pretty amazing.
I guess more curiosity about what, what makes the decision between a roundabout and a red light or a green light, a traffic light? And I'm just thinking that 120th and Port Sheldon.

staff is just very unhappy and very unwilling to accept the outcome of Mr. Bulthuis' legal advice, perhaps even to the point of defiance. It also appears that it's even more possible that staff just doesn't like Dave DeYoung. And I am just fine with that. However, because I do not like someone is not a valid reason for such targeted and aggressive behavior from Holland Charter Township staff toward a citizen.
And might you have any plans to share the wealth, so to speak?...How many employees do you have?...15? I'm sorry, did I miss that?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay, we're going to stand and have a pledge of allegiance, and Vince Bush will lead us in opening prayer. I pledge allegiance to the flag of the United States of America, and to the republic for which it stands—one nation under God, indivisible, with liberty and justice for all.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Shall we pray? Heavenly Father, thank you for the day, and thank you for the the board that we try to do the best that we can for your kingdom, Lord, and everything we do is in the glory. glorify you. Be with our first responders, be with our paramedics as we're in the fire hall today, be with our firemen, and bless everything, like I said, that we do to glorify you. In Jesus' name we pray, amen.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Amen. Thanks, Vince. We got time for citizen comments.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, before I start, I sent I have a comment to Steve. He wasn't able to print it off, so I forwarded it all to everyone on the—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Good evening, board members. In November, I presented this board with a 24-page analysis documenting systemic enforcement gaps. Page 14 of that report, which you all received, features Case Study 3, incomplete enforcement about 11813 Greenleaf Street. Tonight, I'm here to tell you that that same property with the same director It is now the basis of my request for formal performance review for Corey Broersma and consideration of termination. 5 months, same property, same pattern, same director. Let me walk you through it. September, I reported 2 unlicensed vehicles at 11813 Greenlee. Mr. Broersma declared it's in compliance on September 24th, but his inspector only checked if vehicles were off jacks, never verified the registration. October, I followed up explicitly asking him to verify registration on those same unlicensed vehicles.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Mr.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Brusman's response, quote, 11813 Greenlee was notified and was in compliance on September 24th. He ignored my request and defended incomplete enforcement. November, the township was forced to open a new case for the same violations that were never actually resolved. December, that case was closed without verification again. January, on January 9th, Mr. Brusman told me in writing what constitutes a violation, quote, Improperly licensed or visible damage that would make the vehicle inoperable. On January 13th, I submitted photos showing exactly that. 2 flat— well, all of them, all flat tires and expired registration. His response that day, we will review. February 4th, yesterday, I asked for an update. Mr. Broersma's response, he reinforced or he referenced only his January 12th Inspection conducted before I submitted evidence and declared the property in compliance. He attached his own inspection photo as proof. Board members, his own photo shows flat tires and registration expired over 2 years. His own evidence proves non-compliance by his own stated criteria. He acknowledged my January 13th evidence. He said he would review it. Then he responded 3 weeks later as if it never existed. This is not a training issue. This is not a one-time mistake. This is 5 months of documented failure on a single property, a property you were specifically warned about in November. Mr. Broersma is the Community Development Director. He sets the standard, and that standard, based on documented evidence, is Declare compliance regardless of facts, ignore citizen evidence, and defend incomplete enforcement. I request the board direct the township manager, Steve, to initiate formal performance review of Mr. Broersma with a consideration for termination. Thank you.

So, but if we can get that money through, one of the projects we're looking at is doing B-line in Green Lake from Riley to 120th. That's what we'd move up from 2027. B-line from Riley Street to 120th. Okay. We also have scheduled this summer that you may be interested in, a roundabout's going to be installed at 120th And, Port Sheldon. So, we'll have a little traffic interruption. We'll be doing a lot of work on 120th, with road improvements. If you look up above, we've got— where is it? From Taylor Street to M-45, we'll be also repaving this year. One of the bridges that we will be working on is the 104th Bridge over the Black Creek. So, those are the projects we're doing. We're also interested in learning from the township what areas you're having significant growth in. Because, up to now, we've always had a practice of just repaving roads. And now we're looking at, we have additional funding. We're looking at improving roads. What roads need to be 3 lanes, 5 lanes, 4 lanes? What intersections need to be improved, roundabouts, and stuff like that. So, if you could share that information for us, that would be great.

NextGen Services is an established company providing oil reclamation services for industrial manufacturers, helping local businesses reduce operating costs, improve equipment reliability, and lower environmental impact. Their services directly support the efficiency and sustainability of West Michigan's manufacturing base. The company is proposing an approximate $2.44 million capital investment in a new facility located at 13023 Dryzinga Drive here in Holland Charter Township. This investment will support continued growth and investment in the region. The project will look to create 8 new jobs and retain 24 existing positions over the next 3 years. And the new facility will allow the company to accommodate current demand while positioning them for long-term growth here in Holland Charter Township. And I have Jim here to answer any specifics about his business or the project.

And so, as the memo indicates, it begins with Road Commission staff sending us roadways, subdivision streets within the Township that have a PACER, and that's a surface road condition rating of a certain amount. It's usually 3 and 4, which is your poor and fair categories. Staff takes those, works with the township engineer. We look at ones that, you know, might be impacted by developments or utility projects, you know, set those aside. We don't want to go out and resurface the road. tear it up a year or 2 later. So we, you know, once we get that Road Commission list, we go through that, you know, make those modifications, and then we go out and do a field review. And so, you know, as the memo indicated, I think there were 37 this year, 35 street segments that were field reviewed, and then the ones that staff is recommending were highlighted in In yellow there, although board has asked to see that entire list of 35. So you'll see the highlighted ones that are noted there, and I believe that's 15 segments or so, totaling, you know, roughly $760,000 is the estimate. Those segments represent about 2.86 miles of resurfacing.

_utterances.text: Are you still waiting on a vote on amendments to the code? Yeah, that's why I'm still here.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And I just saw another— I just saw another draft last week that we have to the lawyer to give us final feedback on.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Randy, great discussion. I think you I was wondering if you guys thought of an aging report. Anything over 90 days that it hasn't been resolved, that's what the board ought to be focused on, not a 30-day issue.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We can look at it.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Why is it 90? Pick 120 out of here.

_utterances.speaker: N
_utterances.text: But to me, you ought to be looking at the aging report of these complaints, not just the pure number.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yep. Can we do that? That sounds like Okay, great, great idea. How do we do that?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I mean, the software that we use to track investigations, you know, certainly has a timeline in it.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: The other thing that concerns me is simply the time lag. Should it be 5, 10, 15 days instead of 30, 60, 90, 120? That seems It seems like just tolerance to me. It seems like long-term tolerance. We're going to tolerate this instead of doing— why is it not— why is the turnaround time not very rapid? Why is it not 3, 6, 9 days? Here's your notice, and there's a ticket 6 days later.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: His specific example is because of legal review. He knows that. So, the legal review is the big thing. They were waiting to figure out what's going on, to make sure that The amendments put in for noise and sound that you want done, it has to go through the attorneys, and the deputies have to look at it too. So, there's an email that I just got while we're sitting here from Corey that's addressed that, what the legal opinion is. I haven't read it. But, so, he knows what— that's what he's wanting. He wants an ordinance in place to effectively help or disallow a certain sound violation. And, that's what we're working on.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Through the lawyers. Understands that. Employers and the parking.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah. And then do we also—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: am I right, Steve? 100%.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay. And then do we also need to change our— the timing of enforcement investigation? It seems to me that 120 days is a long time to wait for something that has teeth, right?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: It's all different. I think it's different at different levels. So it's not all 120.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah. But, depending on what the steps are legally, with the court filings and everything else, some things do take that long. But, like Steve said, most of the resolution happens when you do a follow-up. You don't ever get to the citation. You're just doing an awareness, this needs to be fixed. They're given a timeframe to do it, it's completed. But to get some more of the detailed reporting that you'd like to see, we have to work on that part. I don't have that at the tip of my fingers.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Elliot, I encourage you— I'm sorry, I encourage you to read through the analysis. It's got all the information that you're looking for. It's got all sorts of recommendations.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: All right then. Cool.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I'm just very happy that he's getting some attention on his situation, so I would like to make sure that the attention is also Yes. And I would also draw over to 610 North Shore Drive, which there have been and are continuing violations over there. It would really be great if we could get Mr. Broersma to spend some time over there checking those out instead of blaming that on the Ottawa County Road Department, which— thank you, now that you're here, that would— if you could Maybe check out 610 North Shore Drive. There's a violation of a second driveway that was installed improperly, illegally. If we could have you look at that, sir, I would really appreciate it.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: 610 North Shore Drive?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: North Shore Drive, correct.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: And they're parking— and are they parking in the street?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's the added driveway, I think, is the That's what Dave is referring to.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Oh, okay.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: They added a driveway. They park illegally in the road right-of-way, which apparently that's a law. You're not allowed to do that, at least not at my house. Everybody else can, but not at, not at my house. I have a citation. I did get a citation on that, Elliot. They do hand them out. I did get that. So—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: How long did it take you to get that?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Uh, quite a while. I, I don't know, it was a long time ago, but okay. Yeah, and let me just tell you how that went. So the sheriff shows up, okay, and they say, you, you're not allowed to park here. There's— and I, I asked the sheriff, so you are not allowed to park alongside the road? And he said, no, you're not allowed, you're not allowed to do that, but we don't enforce it.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Because everybody does it. But they continued to enforce it at my house because the sheriff was continually called by the people that live at 610 North Shore Drive. And so I did get a citation for that very reason, but I will bet you that nobody else has.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Who wrote that citation?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: The sheriff or the deputy. Did you have to pay a ticket?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I didn't pay it because we ended up in a lawsuit over it.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Mm-hmm. So I'm looking to get a motion to receive this report for information. I'll make that motion. I'll support it. All in favor say aye. Aye. Any opposed? Thank you. Administrative reports.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Steve, another thing to be proud of.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: The University of Michigan at Dearborn has again recognized the township for its successes and efforts in contributing to Michigan's entrepreneurial growth and economic development. So we were identified as a 4-star community along with 93 other communities across the state. And I think this is just again affirmation of the type of business-friendly environment the township has created and tries to maintain. So, very nice that others are recognizing that as well. That's all I have.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thanks, Steve. Dave?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I do have an update.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Several of the board members already know this. But some of you may not know that our chairman, Mr.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: TeSlaa, since July has been leading up the charge preparing the Lawn Avenue Road abandoned property for the final transfer of the property to the adjoining landowners.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I find it appropriate and important to update the entire board as to make sure everybody is aware of the current status.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: On January 29th, I met with Chairman TeSlaa in his office.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Mr.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: TeSlaa stated he had, he had been meeting with a new attorney who specializes in real estate. Here are the factual statements from that meeting. Mr. TeSlaa said that he had just spoken with the lawyer that morning about options. I asked who the attorney is that he's speaking with to confirm it is Mr. Bolchi. Mr. TeSlaa stated that is not Mr. Bolchi, but he could not remember who the attorney is. The chairman stated that a new easement option An abandonment process is a lot like a legal nightmare involving trying to rewrite everything. He said this nightmare is not in the best interest of the township and that the new attorney is hesitant to move forward with the current easements, the split, trying to divide it, and putting new easements on top of the existing ones. Mr. TeSlaa said they discussed putting the road through, but he stated that it is not possible because there's not enough room. The chairman then proposed a non-motorized permanent bike path in its current location as an alternative option. The chairman also stated that Ms. Van Ness next door does not currently have an easement over township property to access her property and that her property is currently landlocked. Mr. TeSlaa then offered an easement from the township and said he would offer to pay for surveys and and so on to build the walking path. I stated that I appreciate him offering options, but anything short of deeding the property over to the adjoining landowners is not acceptable. I then asked to revisit why the land can't be deeded over to the adjoining landowners, as there was no real answer given other than it might be a legal nightmare. Mr. TeSlaa stated, well, it can be, but the easement part is the problem because the easements do not run with the land. Quite shocked by that statement, I asked for clarification on what he said. Did you just say that the attorney told you that easements do not run with the land? Mr. TeSlaa stated, correct, that is the risky part, that the easements do not go with the land. The concern is that the easements the township had previously granted to my family for us to gain our legal access back to our home would be Mr. TeSlaa then confirmed that Karen Van Ness does not want the land and that she would need an easement unless the land was deeded over to the property owners. Just wonder if anyone else sees the irony here. Mr. TeSlaa stated that his priority is to make sure there is no liability for the adjoining landowners and that he did not think we would want the liability. I stated that I expect the liability, as any property owner would, as I have previously stated. Mr. TeSlaa stated that 80 to 90% of previous road abandonments did not have easements, and that is why this one can't be completed. Mr. TeSlaa stated that he would find the law or ordinance that the attorney is referencing which states that easements do not run with the land. He stated he would also share the name of the attorney who gave him that advice. Mr. TeSlaa stated, we also still have concerns about the utilities that run under The land, but we can take action on the pathway this year. I stated that the township should not move forward with paving the path as a possible satisfactory solution. I then stated that it does not make any sense in preparing the abandonment solution previously. Mr. Ron Bolche, working on behalf of the township, prepared easements in conjunction with our attorney Bill Sickel and made a statement that it was now time to turn over the land to the adjoining landowners. If easements do not run with the land, this makes zero sense. I thanked our chairman for his work and stated that I look forward to moving forward with the road abandonment. And that was the end of that meeting. Back in July, I had made several suggestions and recommendations to Mr. TeSlaa and staff. I was told they would need to run those past the attorney prior to deeding the land over. I assumed it would be a few weeks for Mr. Bulthuis to do a quick review of what was already approved. But months passed. Little did we know that staff was out scrambling to find a different attorney who would help them find any way possible to keep the land from the DeYoungs. And now, after 6 months of staff meeting with a fresh new real estate specialist attorney paid for by our citizens, staff concluded its findings and informed Chairman TeSlaa that he should set up a meeting and explain to me in no uncertain terms the following. Summary. This is how it went. Unfortunately, Dave, the final transfer of the land over to the adjoining landowners is just not possible. First, it's not in the best interest of the township because it would just be a real legal nightmare for the attorneys. By the way, all the legal paperwork has been completed and approved by one of the most respected township attorneys in Michigan. Secondly, Dave, it's just not possible because it would be This is really risky as easements do not run with the land. You might lose the very easements that we had to grant you.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: You know, the easements that staff forced your family to obtain in order to receive your legal access back to your home? Yeah, those— that access, Dave.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: The staff stole from you by keeping your land. Oh, and just so the board is aware, anyone who knows anything about real estate knows appurtenant easements run with the land. Thirdly, Dave, it's just not possible because land splits and divisions are really difficult. They're just too hard. Another side note, the survey containing all of the required easements has already been drafted. New legal descriptions for each parcel have already been written, and everything has been approved by township legal counsel. Finally, Dave, it's just not possible because we wouldn't want you to have to incur the liability. So I guess it's probably in the township's best interest then just to leave the taxpayers liable. One would almost think that the township's best interest should really be the interest of its citizens, and when I say that, I do mean all of our citizens. But apparently our staff does not agree with that. So here we go again, board members. We have been in this exact same position before. We currently have the full board support, and we have the support from legal counsel, just like we did the first time around when Mr. Bolche advised staff that it was time to deed the property over. Every member of this board has made their support clear over the years. Just this past July, Chairman Teslaw sent out an email expressing the support of the executive board and its interest in deeding the land over to the adjoining landowners. More specifically and individually, Mr. Bush has encouraged several times to just get it on the agenda and we will pass it.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I've been told by other board members—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I don't know if I said that.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Excuse me?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I don't know if I said that. I said get it on the agenda, but I don't know if I said pass it.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I've been told by other board members that Mr. Bush has shared the same statement with them as well. So Mr. Bush, I thank you for your support. Mr. TeSlaa, you have stated it several times in writing as well as verbally that it is well past time to get this completed. Obviously, that support is even clearer in your current leadership and drive to do so. I appreciate your support in your current effort. In my opinion, Mr. Dahlman has always been pretty reserved regarding this issue, but of course we do have Mr. Tesla's recent confirmation of your support, as well as Chairman Nienhuis's confirmation of your support several years ago during the first finalization attempt. Thank you, sir, for your consistent support. Mr. Church, you voiced your support being contingent on any and all township conditions being met first. You were adamant that my family adhere to every one of the township's requirements before the land would be deeded over, and if we did not comply, the land would never be deeded over. As I admitted to you a couple years ago, I was very unhappy with you at that time for making that demand.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Mr.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Church, At the same time, I shared with you that I have since come to respect you for that decision. First of all, it just makes logical sense. Second, I realized that you were just trying to get the final abandonment in a position to move forward, and I respect you and appreciate you for that. As we are all aware by now, those demands were met. 4 specific appurtenant easements were toiled over for more than a year between attorneys Ron Bolche, legal counsel for Holland Charter Township, and Bill Sickel, attorney for the Aaron DeYoung Trust. Ultimately, those easements were created and approved by Ron Bolche, who, by the way, is also the attorney and legal counsel for multiple local municipalities, and he has been for many, many years. Obviously, he is very well respected, and governing bodies all over West Michigan trust Mr. Bolche's legal work and counsel. Thank you, Mr. Church, for your support.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Ms.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Malatke, you have expressed your strong support over the years and have been adamant about deeding this land over from the very beginning. Your vision has been clear from the start that this should never have been withheld in the first place. I appreciate your support and I appreciate you. Finally, in regard to our former chairman, Mr. Nienhuis has been very empathetic and extremely supportive over the last several years, working vigorously to push the final deeding forward, especially in his final year as supervisor. He has written statements during the first abandonment attempt that showed full executive board support and approval even way back then. Mr. Nienhuis worked feverishly toward the abandonment despite staff fighting him. Mr. Nienhuis has made it clear that we are well beyond a reasonable timeframe to finalize this abandonment. I thank Mr. Nienhuis for his support. Once again, on behalf of myself and my family. I want to thank each one of you for your continued support. So my fellow board members, this is where we are at. This is what our staff has come up with this time to dismantle and derail and redirect the board's intentions. Our staff loaded up the chairman with a bunch of nonsense and nudged him off into a meeting to try to explain it. I have to tell you, Mr. Chairman, I actually felt sorry for you being pushed into that meeting having to sell that load of garbage on behalf of the staff. It does make one wonder why a new attorney would be needed at all. It appears all of a sudden the staff believes Mr. Bulthuis is no longer respected and trusted. I can't help but wonder who came up with that idea, and I'm not talking about those who may have been led and guided into agreeing with the idea. I wonder who initiated it. I would imagine Mr. TeSlaa, as a fairly fresh new supervisor, would likely have been looking for some guidance and experience. It appears quite possible that staff is just very unhappy and very unwilling to accept the outcome of Mr. Bulthuis' legal advice, perhaps even to the point of defiance. It also appears that it's even more possible that staff just doesn't like Dave DeYoung. And I am just fine with that. However, because I do not like someone is not a valid reason for such targeted and aggressive behavior from Holland Charter Township staff toward a citizen. At this point, my family is not looking for any further assistance from staff in trying to move the Lawn Avenue abandonment road— abandoned road forward to final dissolution. In fact, we respectfully request that staff stop helping at all. It is glaringly obvious that staff's intention is to make damn sure that the final deeding of this land to the DeYoung family and the adjoining landowners Does not happen. With all of this said, my family will be adding this to the agenda in the very near future. However, we do find it important to hear from each board member. Therefore, we will be waiting for Mr. Nienhuis's return to do so. To be clear, we will be moving forward with the final transfer of the land using the exact same easements that were previously prepared and approved by Holland Charter Township legal counsel Ron Bolchi. There has been no change in facts, no change in requirements, no change in legal procedures since that approval. In fact, there has been no change at all other than it has now been proven that road abandonments have been fully approved even when the boardroom is full of citizens who are adamantly opposed. This was the exact case with our very last road abandonment for the Culver's folks. Therefore, the very weak and final objection that Staff previously recommended denial over during the first road abandonment attempt has been eliminated. Once again, speaking directly to staff, please cease and desist from attempting any further assistance in the board's desire to to deed the abandoned Lawn Avenue property over to the adjoining landowners. My family, as citizens of Holland Charter Township, has had more than enough of your service with integrity, efficiency, and courtesy. In conclusion of this update, on multiple occasions over the last handful of years, I have written official letters via email to this board requesting your focus and attention towards selective enforcement that may be happening at the hands of our staff in Holland Charter Township. As a quick reminder, selective enforcement is defined as when a government agency chooses to enforce a regulation only against certain businesses or individuals while ignoring others who are violating the same regulation. This can be seen as discriminatory and unfair. Overall, selective enforcement undermines the rule of law and can lead to unequal treatment under the law. It is important for law enforcement agencies to enforce laws fairly and impartially without discrimination or bias. Basically, selective enforcement Selective enforcement happens when a governing body treats citizens completely differently. It is when a governing body treats situations and procedures completely differently. Selective enforcement is when a governing body applies laws and ordinances unequally and unfairly, and in this case, quite possibly to an extreme. So there it is, folks, the current update on Law Avenue, the road saga. Other than I just, other than that, I just want to say congratulations to Steve on his recent resignation announcement. We are super happy that you're moving on to your next adventure, and we wish you all the best wherever that may take you.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Thanks, Dave.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'll pass. Anything? I was, uh, the business owners on, uh, Donnelly reached out to me, Steve, that perhaps we They wanted to know if we could look at a bike path between Riley and Quincy, on John F. Donnelly. They seem to be getting a lot of people that are walking on that street, and they wanted to know if we could put that in a kind of— however that starts, with a survey, or funds available, or whatever the case is. That's what I got. Yep. Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Gretchen?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I'm going to save mine for our next meeting.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Elliot?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yeah, just one thing. So I share the opinion that we should have some criteria that is more robust for approving tax Okay. We discuss this from time to time, and they come in front of us and we get some minimal set of facts, and basically we get no facts. We get some advocacy for a tax abatement, and it's for a lot of time, and we just don't have a lot to go on. It seems to me that we could reasonably ask for more, like report back I mean, so they make some projections, but people don't come back. We don't know if they've ever accomplished those projections.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We don't even know if they're realistic. Yeah, it's state regulated. They have to report.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: They have to report back. So, they make some projections.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Not to us.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: But to the state.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We're the flow-through person.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay. So, there is some double-checking. All right. I think there should be some criteria. I think the question about wages is a fair one, right, to ask. So we're giving you this benefit, right, you're getting, what, $500,000 over the course of 10 years, it's a lot. And to have some ready set of questions that they're ready to answer makes sense to me. I feel like asking for taxes— I don't get tax abatements.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I do.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I've invested money in the township, right? Build a house, and I don't have a tax abatement. I'd like one. I would expect to answer a lot of questions before I got it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: The SCV value is usually 50% of what the value of the house is. But do you want to call that an abatement or not?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: And then homestead exemption. Yeah, it's fair.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: It's fair. But I do feel like we should be a little more probing with, with this type of request. Someone's asking not to pay taxes. To me, that seems like a serious question. And we ask— I think we should be getting more information about the worthiness.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I think the state is basing it on growth. So, that's why it's always employees and what do you expect the growth from this to happen.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: And they are paying taxes.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So what if they don't? What if they don't achieve that? What if they come in and say, we're going to have 8 more employees, and they never do? What happens? Do we stop abating the taxes or not?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I don't know what the state follow-up is. I'd have to check.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I feel like I'd like to know. I just, I always have a— and I think you were alluding to this, Dave. You said you always approve these. We 100% approve these. We've never said no. We've never said no. I just feel like we have sort of a lack of criteria. Just an ask is always good enough. It doesn't matter how many employees they're going to have or how few. I feel like it would be nice to raise the bar a little bit, or at least so we can feel like it's justified. I don't know how to put that into words, but I'll leave that right there.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: With you, Mr. Chairman, as a persistent source of—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Maybe that's a— I'll bring that up at the MTA as well and just see what other communities, you know, if they have other criteria besides growth in their employee count. You know, this is going to result in 10% growth. We heard 50% growth today, which is amazing. Yeah, small business is, you know, able to do that a little better than large ones.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: But if you could also report back at just what it is that the state is verifying from your end, what are the consequences of noncompliance?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay, thank you. Okay, thank you. Appreciate everybody coming tonight. I'll just look for a motion to adjourn. So moved. So moved. Thanks, Mike.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: My pen just died and my computer died. I don't have a plug.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Good timing then, right?

keMxNF-8xfY.summary:
- **Consent Agenda**: Approved building permit activity report showing $103M construction value in 2025 (up from $76M in 2024); approved December disbursements of $3.1M including sheriff payments and water purchases; approved monthly call service report with discussion of inflated crime statistics due to new coding of hunting shots and vehicle backfires as 'shots fired'
- **Citizen Comment**: Anna White introduced herself as candidate for Ottawa County District Court Judge, replacing retiring Judge Canole
- **Lakeshore Advantage Update**: Mandy Cooper presented economic development work including 128 startups navigated, $4.8M in capital activated; Impact Fab president Ross shared business growth story; motion approved for $55,000 annual support (unanimous)
- **Tulip Time Funding**: Approved $15,000 annual support (unanimous)
- **Drain Matters**: Approved resolutions for Gritter Industrial Park drain adoption and Summerlin South detention pond riprap erosion control project ($12,828); discussed developer responsibility timelines and assessment processes
- **Township Hall Renovations**: Approved $447,497 for east wing improvements including design, construction ($363K), and contingency; goal to complete by early May; 4 in favor, 3 absent
- **Budget Amendment**: Approved amendment to correct omission of $551,850 in capital projects and add $99,926 inter-fund transfer to community center fund
- **Comprehensive Plan**: Approved master plan update incorporating housing study; discussed owner-occupied housing emphasis and slowing rental development trend
- **Committee Reports**: Community enhancement grants totaling ~$12K each to Gateway Mission, Boys and Girls Club, and Route for transportation vans; water species study on Black River; community center attendance exceeded 900 in one day with 1,300 at New Year's Eve event

highlights.quote: The numbers are inflated the last 3 months...shots fired during hunting season...a car that backfires, somebody thinks it's a shot and they'll call in

We did an expansion almost 5 years ago now, and the first phone call I made was to Lakeshore Advantage...when we're looking at, do we move closer to Grand Rapids...it became pretty clear, no, we want to reinvest right here in Holland Township
We look for EMTs, we have water and sewer...we have code enforcement people that we need...I don't think I've ever talked about asking about looking to try to help us navigate that career line
Projects have been down across both counties this past year...the number of projects is significantly down across our entire region...they're larger and more complex right now
When—at what point does this become Holland Township's responsibility? I mean, that retaining pond was created by the developer of that project, right?

highlights.topic: Community center New Year's Eve success
highlights.quote: They had 1,300 people come in for that. They were expecting 600. They had 1,300. So they ran out of hot chocolate and they ran out of marshmallows
A $245,000 house requires a $68,000 annual income...we got to get some below $245,000...average income is 50 grand
Have we ever...has this report ever said stop building?...We all know where it's going every year. Why are we paying for a study?

dave_segments.text: I think, like, just tonight I'm showing a house and people were asking, what's the crime like here? And I always say, go look at the statistics, right?
It doesn't make sense to add those to the crime, right? That doesn't, that doesn't look so good to me
Is the community center disbursement, is that still paying final bills on? That project, or where does that come— where's that going?
The 60 is pretty much then operational so far, right?

When— at what point does this become Holland Township's responsibility? I mean, that retaining pond was created by the developer of that project, right? And you're also saying this is somewhat of a self-created problem
Here's the thing: it's not the it's not the people living in that unit right there, right? But I like the property manager. If they're causing a problem, I think there's some responsibility there too
Do you do anything on the grant side of things for seeking those out for entrepreneurs?
We actually might be able to use that in our building, you know, with our audio system. We could get some lights and stuff to maybe enhance what we got going over there
Have we ever—I've only been here a couple years, but has this report ever said stop building?...We all know where it's going every year. Why are we paying for a study?
You weren't at the whatever meeting that came up the last time, but my question is, why do we have to keep growing?
Or slow it down tremendously
I was looking at those. Those got to be about $25,000 apiece or something. 400 square feet. Some small ones
I'll make a motion...All right. Appreciate it
I'll make the motion to adjourn

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We'll stand and have the Pledge of Allegiance, and I will open us up with a word of prayer. I pledge allegiance to the flag of the United States of America and to the Republic for which it stands, one nation under God, indivisible, with liberty and justice for all. Our Father in heaven, Lord, we give you thanks for the many blessings that you have provided for us and even for this day and this opportunity in the meeting to conduct the business of our township. Lord, we just ask for your guidance in our discussion and our deliberation. We also just pray for our first responders. We're here in their building, but Lord, keep them in safety as they are out on emergency calls on a daily, hourly basis. We just ask for safety for them as well as our citizens around this township. Just bring us home safely tonight as well. We ask this in Jesus' name. Amen. Amen. All right, we have a chance for citizen comments if anybody is here, or if you're all here for the business that you're ready to discuss tonight, we'll move on to the consent agenda. Steve, any communications?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yep, just a note here from the League of Women Voters that they're going to be joining us either online or in person as observers.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: A couple of them for you this evening, and they happen to have, you know, it's the December reporting period, so we have year-end totals to kind of look at with these reports as well. The first one being the building permit activity report. I think we can all be pleased with where we ended up in 2025. Construction value a little bit over $100 million at $103 million. So you can see that certainly eclipsed that total of 2024 of a little over $76 million. Didn't quite match our banner year back in 2023, but, you know, very strong finish. Very strong December at about $14.4 million. If you had a chance to look at the detail from the activity report, December's activity report, you know, really that A project going on at MSA Lakeshore, as well as another phase of Lakewood Place Apartments, is what really drove most of that $14 million that you saw there.

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yep, we had disbursements in the month of December of $3.1 million. $1 million, roughly. Just a couple of the more major expenditures and disbursements for December. We had a couple of bills from the Sheriff's Department in the amounts of $174,717 and $199,406. So, you add those together, that's—

_utterances.speaker: D

Another significant expenditure in the general fund for December was the bill from the contractor who does our spring and fall cleanup days, which are very popular with our residents. That came in at $23,812. Looking at the fund 405, the building and site capital project, that $288,000 disbursement there, all or Nearly all of it going to GDK Construction. $111,429 for the community center and $159,991 for the township hall improvements. So different projects going on and expenditures related to those. And then lastly, disbursements a little higher, $1.24 million for Fund 591, the water operating fund. Most of that going to our friends in the city of Wyoming for water purchases. Water purchases, we had 2 months that got billed out in December, $397,936, $498,006 going for water purchases. Again, a significant portion of that monthly disbursement. Happy to answer any questions regarding the disbursement report.

_utterances.text: So, that's for expenses, mostly operational. There may be small capital expenses in there. The site, the 405 fund, building site capital, that had an expenditure for the construction of the community center yet of $111,429. Yep, so we're still wrapping that up.

_utterances.text: But Greg, do you—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: the 60 is pretty much then operational so far, right?

_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right, thanks, Steve. And we had the— Monthly call service, did you want to cover that too, or did you—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: On the sheriff?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, just briefly, just want to point out again, another year-end total. And so, I would just direct your attention to the 3rd page there, where it looks at various year-to-date totals. Since this is the December report, we can kind of get a snapshot of what the entirety of the year looked like. And when you look through those various classifications there, it's kind of a mixed bag. Some are up, some are down, but I think we can all agree, you know, our deputies are staying very busy.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, I do want to make a note that I did talk to our Lieutenant Nick Knott just because the shots and stabbing number is like triple, but I got a mixed answer from him because it's, it's how you code it, and I think they are coding some things differently than what they had in the past. So he said even A person hearing shots fired, but during— most of these happened in October to November hunting season. So, he said right now, they are coding those as shots fired, even though it's not a firearm necessarily at a person, but somebody heard a shot and they can't— you know, deputy went out and said, oh, you know, there's woods over there, it's probably a hunter. They didn't have any other calls or anything like that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So, the numbers are inflated the last 3 months. Um, he, um, can get us more detail on exactly— there were some arrests that were made, and, you know, there's a gun in the glove box, so that's actually called a— just, it's not necessarily a shot fired, but it's a handgun that's confiscated. It's a whole scene. So, those are coded under that at this time.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: That doesn't look good for our statistics though. That should probably be something we should peel out of there.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Or just that if that's the way they're going to code them, that we know what's happening, and then from years on now, we can track them the same way, just so we know significantly what's happening. Otherwise, I can see if there's enough—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I think, like, just tonight I'm showing a house and people were asking, what's the crime like here? And I always say, go look at the statistics, right?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right, yep. It's a good question, Dave. I can see once— I'll follow up with Nick again. And, um, because I know they're, they're actually still trying to hire a new person that does the final reports.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah. Um, but I don't want— it doesn't make sense to add those to the crime, right? That doesn't, that doesn't look so good to me.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right, that's true.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Especially if it's just a hunting shot or someone hunting or something and it's a shot fired. Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Or he said even, you know, backfire, a car that backfires, somebody thinks it's a shot and they'll call in and And they'll say, oh, it must have been, you know, a car. But that's how some of the— that's why those codes are so high at this time. Then we had the minutes from our December 18th meeting that were up for review. If there's no changes there, I'll just ask for a motion to approve the consent agenda.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I'll make a motion to do that. Bush?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'll support. And supported. All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any opposed, same sign. Okay, that's unanimous. Um, we did change our location. I saw somebody come in a little bit later. We had our citizen comment ready, but if you came here for that, I'll give you an opportunity. Yeah, I, I'll put it back up there for you to— if you want to come up, state your name and address, and we'll give you 3 minutes.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Thank you so much.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: My name is Anna White. I'm 1537 Perry Street. I'm actually a Park Township resident. I'm here tonight because I'm running for Ottawa County District Court Judge this fall, so I wanted to introduce myself, meet some of you, and just let you know who I am. I'm currently the Deputy Public Defender for Ottawa County, so I've been living and working in Ottawa County for over 20 years now as an attorney. Before that, a social worker, worked at Resilience. With victims of domestic and sexual assault. So a lot of experience in this community. When you talk about crime rates, I am very familiar with what's happening around here. And I'm running for judge because I know that we have a legacy of really phenomenal judges in our county. I want to continue that. But I've also practiced in other places, so I know how important it is to have people run for judge who know the area, know the people here, and who are willing to create a courtroom where people are listened to, treated with dignity. And also held accountable when they need to be held accountable. So, I have some materials with me tonight. If you have questions afterwards, I'd be happy to chat.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So, Canole's seat, is that what you're going for?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yes, he's retiring at the end of this year.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
