_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Judge Bocanegra.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Oh, okay.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: So, it would be right in Holland, right here.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yep, yep, yep.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So, yeah.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Wonderful.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Thank you so much.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: And, I also really value punctuality.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So, I really hated coming in late tonight.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: One demerit.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: When I saw the sign, I thought, oh, this is a great way to start off.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, you're welcome. Thank you. Then we have Mandy Cooper is here to give us an update from Lakeshore Advantage.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Thank you. Good evening. My name is Mandy Cooper. I'm the Vice President of Strategic Initiatives at Lakeshore Advantage. I think last year you saw Jennifer Owens, who's our president. She wasn't able to be here tonight, but sends her regards. Appreciate the opportunity to come just give you an update on our work. As most of you, I think, are aware, we are the nonprofit economic development organization that serves all of Ottawa and Allegan Counties, but tonight especially Holland Charter Township as well. Our mission is to ensure a healthy economy here on the lakeshore where people want to live and work, and we do that by staying pretty laser-focused on a specific segment of our economy.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: What is that?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: We work to make sure that our primary employer base can stay healthy and can grow and thrive here. And when I say primary employer base, I'm talking about a specific kind of employer. So these are businesses that export more goods and services outside of the region than can be consumed inside of the region. So think of like a manufacturer or a tech company, agribusiness. They're selling around the country or the world, and we they sell, those dollars come back here. And the reason why they're so important for our economy is because they really provide the foundation for our local economy. As I mentioned, they bring dollars in, and then hopefully they're getting spent here locally through our retailers, restaurants, their supply chains, which all help keep those dollars here. They also tend to pay higher wages, and they become a really valuable tax base for our local communities. Again, such as Holland Charter Township. So when we talk about our work, really, again, our main goal is to make sure that those employers can stay here and thrive here. I'd say around 80 to 90% of our work is really focused on the employers that are already here. We've had a thriving economy here on the Lakeshore. Our local businesses have been growing over the last 23 years where we've been here serving for 23 years, and so We have the privilege of really being able to support them first and foremost and equip them. So with that said, I'm going to have a little presentation. Our vision, our 10-year target if you will, is to be a business and talent magnet. We know if businesses want to locate here and if talent wants to locate here, we're winning as a Lakeshore region and our economy will be helping rapidly. So again, at the end of the day, When we go, how are we doing? This is what we're measuring ourselves against. As far as how we do our work, we really talk about grow, plan, solve. Economic development is all about proactive effort, making good decisions today to secure a long-term healthy future. And so we kind of do that in 3 buckets, which I'll talk through in just a minute. For reference, I mentioned those primary employers. There's about 700 of them between Ottawa and Allegan Counties alone. And we try to meet with about half of them every year, some more in-depth than others, but that, that's kind of just to give you the scope of the work. Part of our work is to also always be bringing the business voice to the table. We know advocating for what our employers need to be successful and to thrive and grow is an important piece of our work in making sure that our communities, our partners are educated on what employer needs are. Doesn't mean we can always meet all of them, but at least to say here's what our employers are telling us and what we should consider when we think about how to create a healthy business ecosystem. One example, Wendy was just talking to me about this, Angela Guzman, our COO, sits on the Holland Township Planning Committee. So many of us serve on various planning committee public boards, but also private or nonprofit boards as well. Again, because we know how important it is to really bring the business voice. And with that said, tonight I actually brought the business voice with me. So if Ross can step up. We are really grateful. Ross is a great partner. He's president of Impact Fab, and he agreed just to share a little bit about his experience working with us. Better to hear from him than myself.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Thank you so much for allowing me to be here tonight. And, when Mandy emailed asking if I'd be willing to join, it was a no-brainer. Moved back to the Holland area about 20 years ago, after growing up here, and Lakehurst Advantage was one of the first organizations I engaged with. And now, proud to be second-generation business owner and president of Impact Fab. So, we are one of the primary employers. We're a manufacturer, steel fabrication, Where we're building parts that go across the country and even beyond, depending on what the product is. But we also employ 56 people now full-time that are pretty much to the person the primary breadwinner for their home. So we're able to provide good benefits for them and a good wage as well. Even our, I think our high school kids who've been out make more than minimum wage. So it's a very competitive industry we're in. And so therefore, Lakeshore Advantage has been a fantastic partner. We did an expansion almost 5 years ago now, and the first phone call I made was to Lakeshore Advantage. And they did such a wonderful job helping navigate the process with us, opening up doors with some of the connections that they have that I didn't know who to talk to, helping think through things, working on that. And so when we're looking at, do we move closer to Grand Rapids with our business, because that's where a lot of our main customers are, or do we relocate elsewhere, It became pretty clear, like, no, we want to reinvest right here in Holland Township. We're literally just around the corner on John O'Donnelly. And so that's been a fun thing to really put our roots deeper down here in the community. So that's on the business side. On the personal side, Lakeshore Advantage has done wonderful just programs and trainings. And so really has been that spot as a young business leader to get a lot of mentorship from other business owners. The motor generation. But even this past fall, I was able to participate in an AI cohort. I was trying to demystify AI, how do we apply it, how do we make it work well for our businesses. And it was fantastic. It just really took a lot of fear out of that, gave some really practical ways of helping run business well. So as we look to that, they had a campaign this fall— or this summer, I guess it was—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: For reinvesting into Lakeshore Advantage, and that was a very easy yes, we want to be a part of that. Come back to that as a small piece of the bigger cog. You know, we're not Gentex or a major corporation, but we're really proud of the work we do and being part of this community. And just very grateful for Lakeshore Advantage for how they partner. They do a yearly survey that even a small employer like myself gets to be a part of and say, hey, these are the struggles we're having, these are the things that are going well. And they listen and take all that information back and then they get to share that out. So truly appreciate the work that they do.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Ross, I got a question for you. What is your business? I know what your business is, but what do you guys do? Like end users and what do you do?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yeah, so steel fabrication. We are building basically industrial equipment in a lot of different sectors. So it's either working with the automation companies in the area for custom automation or working with a lot of different material handling And like the robotics space as well, for GPS-guided fork truck systems, is a big customer of ours. We get into furniture actually, and then outdoor furniture is kind of another big primary thing that leaves our region. So right now we're finishing up a huge project for YouTube's campus. We did a bunch of things that went to the Apple 1 campus in California, all custom fabrications, metal componentry that goes goes in outdoor seating, pavilions, furniture, and things of that nature. So, wow, pretty, pretty neat stuff to be a part of. And it's, uh, uh, then, you know, working with other local suppliers like Plascore, who's building, uh, aerospace components, rocket components, SpaceX components. Um, it is pretty wild when you think of the little— our little business and how it has ties to some different things.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So your dad founded it?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: He did.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Then what was their— what was his background? What— how did he get into business?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So he is a welder. He didn't graduate from high school. He's just a hard worker. He's your classic American dream entrepreneur, uh, willing to take a risk and started the business in our garage with a welder and a drill press. And, uh, really, he's really smart and cagey, so lots of credit for, uh, for seeing that. And, uh, but just a true— to this day, the hands-on. You're going to see him on a fork truck on the floor. You're not going to see him in the office.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: How many employees you have?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: 56 full-time employees.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: What's your sales, kind of?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So, we just finished last year over $10 million.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Oh, good for you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Have you spent some time at the new building Lakeshore has on 7th Street?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I have.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: It's been fantastic. Like, the cohort I was part of this fall was housed there. Just a really great space. Another thing that I didn't touch on, but the work they do on entrepreneurship, really being that economic driver for us for the next big ideas that come through that we get to be a little piece of too.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Nice. Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Thanks for letting me share.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Thanks, Ross. And that's the presentation, I think. What I need to say though about Ross is, you know, the reality is the work we do, we're trying to help employers. We're trying to assist them, help fill gaps, navigate them. We can only do that if we know how to do it or we have partners who are willing to share how to do it. And Ross has come to such a great example of being willing to share what they've learned, either with us or with other companies directly, to say, here's how we did it, here's how we're integrating AI, Industry 4.0, automation, and all of those things to help make them successful. So again, you know, our business ecosystem is built also on collaboration among our business owners, which is something don't always see. So just huge thank you to Ross for coming tonight, obviously, and for the work he's doing in economic development by participating. So with that said, I'll go pretty quickly through the rest of this since you just heard what this all shows up like, which is the best case. No, that's what we hope for. It's way more interesting than listening to me. So Grow, we mentioned. As Russ said, we do these business intelligence interviews where we sit down with around 150 40, 120 companies each year, and we ask them pretty in-depth questions about how they're doing. We ask them about opportunities, challenges, do they have new products, do they plan to expand in the next few years, and that helps us understand what's happening in our business climate here locally and if there are shared needs that we need to lean into. And so I actually brought copies of the results of those surveys. It's our industry trends We can't share the individual interview responses because those are confidential. The businesses are very open with us so that we can learn from them, but the industry trends report, you'll see some aggregated responses there. A lot of the trends we've been hearing include things we've heard before over the past year, 2, 3, you know, access to talent continues to be a challenge, rising costs, related to supply chain uncertainty around tariffs. These are all barriers, challenges right now from our companies. One of the ones that has been increasing year over year as far as how loud they're getting about it is access to housing, saying, what are you doing about it, Lakeshore Advantage? Can you help at all? Because we need housing so that we can attract and retain our young talent. And housing is something Holland Charter Township is doing a lot of, so thank you from an economic development perspective, for that from the regional perspective. The other thing we're doing as we look, I mentioned the types of employers we worked in, we've identified 3 main growth sectors. So these are the sectors within the primary employer base that they may not be the single largest, but we see that they have a substantial presence and we believe they're going to keep growing. So we're leaning in to better understand them and try to figure out what we can do specifically in each of these sectors to support them. AES, advanced energy storage, which each of these sectors is actually represented in Holland Charter Township by primary employers as well. The P in the GPS plan, here's where we'd like to talk a little bit about that entrepreneurial startup support. So as Ross mentioned in I think you're well aware, we opened the NEXT Center just over a year ago and have been able to locate our business incubator called Surge on the first floor. That's in the Smart Zone, supported by Holland Charter Township. When we started, we had hoped to have 50 members of that business incubator by this time. We're over that. We have 53 members and we have a waiting list.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So it's actually exceeded our wildest expectations as far as engagement goes in that space and the connections we're making. Can you go to the next slide? Over the past year, we've actually navigated 128 startups. Now, not all of those are SmartZone startups. SmartZone supports high-tech, high-growth companies, and those are the ones that mostly make up our SURGE membership. But if we get calls from other startups, we try to at least send them in the right direction. We also help them activate around $4.8 million in capital and leveraged funding. So again, whether that's funding to secure services like legal services, product design, general capital from venture capitalists, from accelerator funding, there's been $4.8 million secured for our startups. And that's typically Typically one of the key barriers as they're trying to launch. And then finally we talk about solve.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Please, Steve.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: When we think about our region, the question we're always asking, again, that economic development kind of core question, what decision can we make today that will help us secure our future long-term? You know, we mentioned smart manufacturing as one of those sectors that Ross is in as well as many of our local manufacturers. Here on the lakeshore. When we talk with them, their highest barrier is really securing the talent they need that have the skills they need. So it's either a quantity issue or they can't find the talent that actually has the specific skills they want. As far as what those careers are, those are the 4 top-in-demand careers we've identified through various research datasets. Anything related to skilled trades is in high demand. Software and systems continues to be in demand. You know, Ross mentioned AI and what that's doing for companies as they're trying to figure out what does integration look like, how do they use it for optimization. Industrial technicians and engineers. So, Ross, head nod, does this resonate? Okay, just checking. You know, so there, there are still ample opportunities in our companies, many of which are willing to train. However, we know that there are a few pockets where there aren't clear training paths or on-ramps if you don't opt in early. So right now we're working to— with our education partners and manufacturers to identify the engineering pathways. And again, engineering is a broad category, but to say, are there any gaps or opportunities here where we could fill them, create better on-ramps so we can get more skilled people into into these roles in the future. So again, just one example. You know, how this kind of comes to light is we were talking with a local employer here in Holland Charter Township this past year. They had a need for chemists, but they actually didn't need degreed chemists. They needed people who knew how to work in a lab. And we were able to connect them with GRCC, who offers a chemistry class here at the Lakeshore Campus, students learning how to work in a lab, and now they can be employed at this company. So again, sometimes it's simple connecting the dots for the company in a moment of need, and sometimes we're working systemically to try to solve long-term and close gaps and identify opportunities. And then as far as our work with our public partners comes in, at the end of the day, we really depend on our public-private partners partnership work to activate economic development. So, you know, I get to sit in a seat where I'm our main liaison with our local units of government on a day-to-day basis. So Steve and I get to meet quarterly. I meet quarterly with a lot of our local units of government. We share what's going on, what challenges, are there properties becoming available that we should be sharing with our business partners, do you have infrastructure needs. You might say, Steve, there's some funding, I don't know if it will fit, is this a need? But that back and forth conversation and staying up to date with each other is really key. And then also willingness to invest when it makes sense to say, hey, you know, and again I'll reference housing, Holland Charter Township, you've been saying yes to a lot of housing. My understanding from what Randy just shared is that you're on track on the rental side especially, but being a community that says, hey, We're willing to do what we can to support growth that makes sense for our community, and we understand it's needed for the broader Lakeshore region. It's always really important. And then finally, just recognizing economic development is a long-term game. And I say game, that's probably the wrong word, but it's a long-term investment. We love to see results year to year. The reality is most of the work we do, it's a 2, 3, 4, 5-year window to get to see the actual finished product. So again, having a partner who's been with us for so long such as Holland Charter Township is incredibly valuable, and that support allows us to continue to work like that. So we're grateful for that. Last plug I want to give before I can take questions if you have them, we are hosting our annual meeting on January 29th. You are all invited to attend, and you can scan the QR code, which I think is probably in your packet as well. Go on our website, but we'd love to have you. We'll be talking about what's coming in the next year, what some of those opportunities and challenges are that we've been facing, and what we're hoping to do about them. So we'd love to have you join us if you're available. And with that, I'm happy to answer questions. I had. We had sent a packet with some data points in there in advance. I didn't run through all of those in the presentation since you already have them, but I can answer about what we discussed or the information that we shared.

They call it a ball drop for kids, and that was hosted by Herrick Library, but they did it on the basketball court. And they had 1,300 people come in for that. They were expecting 600. They had 1,300. So they ran out of hot chocolate and they ran out of marshmallows. But they tried to keep as many of the kids happy, and the adults had to sit on the side and maybe not partake in the beverage. But yeah, just a lot of exciting stuff happening at the community center. Of course, I'm there.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We've been playing pickleball a lot, and there's people from all over the county coming in to play pickleball. And we just get a lot of rave reviews of how nice the facility is from the users that are there. So, the attendance is up, and, yeah, we have them now focusing on the rentals, the rooms that we have up in the front, as well as the big room that's Community room, I think, is what they call that in the back. That would be more like for wedding receptions and that kind of thing, or large birthday parties over that area. So that's all I got. Let's look for a motion to adjourn tonight.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I'll make the motion to adjourn. All right, thanks, Steve.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thanks for putting up with our New setting here for a couple of months. What's that?

3T7ktA6P08I.summary:
- **Drainage Projects & GIS Updates**: Water Resources Commissioner Joe Busch presented updated drainage maps for the township showing approximately 1,000 drains countywide, with Holland Charter among the highest. Two major 2026 projects discussed: Sleepy Hollow (Mason & 24th) and Hunters Creek North.
- **Road Abandonment**: Motion to recommend abandonment of Ottawa Street (Paper Street) passed unanimously (6-0-1). School and neighboring property owners signed off; matter forwarded to Ottawa County Road Commission.
- **2026 Hardship Exemption Resolution**: Approved to implement federal poverty guidelines for property tax exemptions (up to 50% relief). Resolution allows automatic annual updates without requiring board reapproval each year.
- **Cemetery Plot Fee Increases**: Approved raising resident burial plot fees from $350 to $500 and non-resident fees from $1,750 to $2,000, aligning with regional benchmarks.
- **Year-End Budget Amendment**: Approved with $2 million net increase in fund balance. Notable items: $1 million interest revenue boost from strong market performance; $335,000 transfer from building department back to general fund; $600,000 transfer to Hawthorne Park MAC improvement project (township acting as banker).
- **Capital Equipment Purchases**: Approved three Parks Department items—Exmark mower ($32,663), Aerovator for field maintenance ($13,761), and major water/sewer main replacement project on 106th Avenue/Paw Paw/112th (contract to K&R for $8.486 million with total authorization of $10.6 million including contingencies and engineering).
- **Planning Commission Annual Report**: 449 units approved in 2025; 261 were single-family homes. Motion to receive report and express gratitude approved.
- **Code Enforcement Update**: 437 rental inspections conducted (light year; average is 800). Township implementing rolling quarterly inspection model with large complexes to level-load workload. Budget seeks to hire 2 full-time code enforcement staff (currently 1 full-time) by January.
- **Administrative Notice**: Facility relocating to Fire Station No. 1 training room (131 Riley Avenue) January–April 2025 for eastern building renovations. First meeting at new location: January 15th.
- **All votes passed unanimously (6-0-1 absent) unless otherwise noted.**

_utterances.text: Right, correct. That's why we do inspections. But yeah.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: How many people do we have that actually— we go out and do that? Because we've grown so much. Do we have enough staff?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's why in our budget we're actually looking at, instead of another part-time person, to have 2 full-time people.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: We have one full-time now.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: And even when we had the part-time person, I thought they were— we weren't getting to everything personally. Anybody else think that?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Well, there— it's what, January?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: So do we need—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Our goal is—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Do we need 2 full-time people doing that job?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And that's what's in the budget, and that's what we're looking to hire hopefully in January. This is just for rental inspections, right? This doesn't— is that right or no? This is for all enforcement? All enforcement, yeah.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: This is the other enforcement. Yep, so they kind of plan out their week and day of, okay, how many, you know, rental units am I going to inspect? But then there's also the complaint calls that come in and they go out, and then it's all the follow-ups that have to happen. Because I asked Jenny, I said, well, on some of these things that she writes up, There can be a 3-month or more legal process to go through because you've got to meet with the judge, or maybe the police become involved. But before any action can be taken on some of these properties, there has to be a magistrate approval.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Because they can— they're deputized to write tickets, right? And they write a ticket, and then they have to—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: They have to submit those to get actually authorized by the court.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: To get signed by the judge or magistrate.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: You know how that works, right, Dave?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yep. So, yeah, there's some of these, it's a long process to actually get process changed, because then if a car has to be towed, that's usually a 3 or more month process to Actually get to that level if—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: It's unbelievable.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: The violator hasn't corrected it on their own. And then, of course, there's a— on some things, there's a fee to be paid. You know, a lot of things in the summertime, it's code enforcement of, you know, they didn't mow their grass. But they get notified, and then we have a set service that goes out and mows somebody's lawn because it's, you know, too long, and then it's a fee that they have to pay.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: We were talking about that, and there's a couple people that have a natural lawn that—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, there might be—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: They get away with, you know—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Depending on the grasses that they have. I forgot what the article was used, but there was a—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Native grasses? Wildflowers?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: It's a hot mess is what it is.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Can I make it, uh, Mr. Boldheiss, can just feedback to, uh, code enforcement. I see that Traditions has inspections coming up and ongoing. Can we, when we have a pilot program, which they're one of them, can we get a report back on how those units are being, uh, maintained? Because we've talked as a board, just kind of trying to make sure that they're doing what they have on their half of the end.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I'd like to see that because they have been— I mean, I—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Just—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: yeah.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So what I—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Horrible.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: One feedback on that is to have them come back and give a little more detailed update. It would have another column on this inspection report to say what the outcome was, because that's not on just this basic inspection report. So we would get that then. That information on, you know, pass/fail or what— I don't know if we want to get into the detail of what all needed to be corrected, but whether it passed or failed may be the first overview for us to understand, you know, how much the land— how responsive the landlords are in the correction process. Well, I just—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: on the pilots is the reason, because we're giving them 50% tax break.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: The other ones, I don't— I could—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: that would be fine. But those pilot-specific ones—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, just when a pilot project comes up.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, what is the—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: They're getting a break to say they're doing great things for the residents of their housing.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, I never ever got a response. I don't think they ever responded back to the township. This was several years ago when I was— the first term I served, when they had— Traditions had severe mold problems, windows blowing out. Oh yeah, I mean, it was bad. I witnessed it. I don't think they ever So, we did talk about it. I think maybe Corey maybe went and checked it, or somebody did, but I don't think we ever got a follow-up on what had happened. And they had just changed, I think, owners. So, I really think we need to—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, and they have— I mean, those new owners have been making improvements, yeah.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Accountable, because that's—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, I think they're doing what they can do.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yep, Washington-based group.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, so this was for information, and I'll get another detailed update. The goal is to have it ready in February. Administrative reports, Steve?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yes, I want to let you all know that we are not going to be meeting in this— our friendly confines here for a few months. As you are aware, the eastern end of the building where the Community Services Development Department is located is going to go under some fairly extensive renovations. And after looking at phasing plans and construction schedules and so forth, the decision was made to have several of the employees' workstations relocated so that the contractor can come in there and have free rein to move forward with the planned improvements. So that relocation of those employees is going to be to this space. So that's going to displace us, as well as the Zoning Board of Appeals and the Planning Commission. We're going to be moving over to the friendly confines of the training room at Fire Station Number 1 on 131 Riley Avenue, just west of the railroad tracks there. And the contractor is going to stay on schedule and be done by the beginning of May, so Mike can move in and run his election. So our first meeting in January, January 15th, will be at that location. So please note it, and we'll send out some reminders as well. But just to let you know that we'll be out of this space from January through April. Yep, next to the North Branch.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: 7 o'clock sharp.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: That's all I have. You know, Merry Christmas, everyone.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Dave, any other committee updates?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Pass.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Mike?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Merry Christmas, everyone.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yep, Merry Christmas.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Uh, the Brownfield is starting to get a little more action with the TIF program, the new state law that passed last year. I think we're up to 3 possible programs coming down with some housing for that, so it's starting to be traction-wise.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So stay tuned. I don't have a Happy blessed holiday. Merry Christmas to folks. Best wishes. I'll pass.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, same. Happy holidays.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right. Well, I do appreciate everybody on the board and the actions that you've taken this past year, involvement in taking care of the business of our community. I think we've made some Huge updates this year. One is for the safety in our community with a full-time fire department. I think we're seeing an improved response time and just the level of service that we get out of that group. And then obviously opening up the community center at the end of summer was a huge—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: It's a great takeaway for us after many years of planning and 2 years of— about of construction and remodel, and to see the response from the community and area people, probably a 20-mile radius potentially of individuals that have come to use that facility. I think it still looks like brand new, and it's had lots of use for the last 6 months. But I know just last week, I think he hit the milestone of like 640 people in one day coming in to utilize the facility. So—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: It's kept a lot of people busy. When I go there and play, I also work and help monitor and ask Ben how things are going today, because it's a different day every day over there. But yeah. So, Merry Christmas, Happy New Year. Look forward to next year at the Riley Street building. I think we'll try to put that in big, bold red letters when we send those out. But, uh, looking for an adjournment. I'll make that motion, Mr. Chairman. All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Aye.

9lhDx2BYaLc.summary:
• **PUD Amendment - MacIntyre Legends**: Planning Commission unanimously approved final development plan amendment adding 1 lot (69 total units in this phase) with lot size adjustments for transition between dense development and golf course. Includes sidewalk connection to park and 2-year deferred paving arrangement for top course to minimize construction damage.
• **Zoning Ordinance Text Amendment - Wetland Mitigation**: Second reading and adoption passed 6-0-1 (one absent), updating language and reference points for front yard requirements.
• **Fire Department Capital Expenditures**: (1) Chevrolet Tahoe vehicle purchase from Berger ($61,448 not-to-exceed, matching existing medical units for racking uniformity); (2) Stryker LifePak patient monitor/defibrillator ($60,000+) for new chief's vehicle to maintain ALS certification and equipment compatibility with AMR transport. Both approved unanimously.
• **Code Enforcement Policy Development**: Board scheduled meeting for December 9 to review Stephen Willard's 24-page enforcement data report and draft ordinance amendments addressing overnight parking in R-2 zones and modified exhaust penalties. Target implementation by January with coordination through sheriff and road commission.
• **Building/Operational**: November building permits at 4.6 million; total November disbursements $3.973 million including final Quincy Pathway payment ($255K), Township Hall improvements ($291K), pump station 31 force main project completed for winter.
• **Board Appointments Approved**: Steven Haberkorn reappointed as ZBA alternate; Russ TeSlaa reappointed as MAX representative.

highlights.quote: I didn't just complain, I proved there's a systemic problem. My small neighborhood, about half percent of the township, had 15 vehicle code violations in three months. Every single one of them came from my complaints, zero from township proactive enforcement.
I am now a resident with data and solutions. I'm committed to working with the board to make these changes happen.

The Save the Campbell plan to replace Consumers with a locally controlled electric cooperative in Ottawa County can be an effective strategy to thwart the misguided net-zero agenda and to make sure AI data centers only go in if they make sense for local residents.

What they're actually proposing to do is to build the infrastructure for this entire phase, but they're only going to construct the roadway for the eastern half of this phase, and then the western half is going to be an emergency access only.

What Eastbrook likes to do is that we actually put a thicker base course down than what is required and we're allowed to defer the paving of the top course until within a period of 2 years after obtaining the first building permit. It allows a lot of the construction traffic to be on that base course rather than on the top.

Since we're outfitting the chief's vehicle as a med vehicle as well, this is a piece of vital equipment that will be utilized in that vehicle. Purchasing this maintains uniformity within the department and everyone is already trained on this unit.

Part of that report says that the project will not be detrimental to health, safety, or welfare through the excessive production of traffic noise, smoke, fumes, and glare. This sounds like exactly the quality of life type of concern that Mr. Willard was addressing.

highlights.topic: Noise ordinance discovery and decibel measurement
highlights.quote: If they get pulled over and there's modified exhaust, you don't have to listen to it. If it's been modified, that's a violation. The fact that they don't have decibel gauges on their cars is not an excuse.

The McIntyre Resource Center has received $21,000 from our last meeting to enhance their kitchen as well as repair roof over their big area on one section there. We're supporting our community with the dollars that we're giving in.

If you haven't been there today, what a tremendous response we're getting on the rec center. It just makes your heart warm to see that happen. We all were a part of that, planning it for so many years.

speaker_map.H: Steve (fire chief or staff - last name unknown)

_utterances.text: Well, then I guess we don't have time.

_utterances.text: Dear God, we give thanks for all the blessings Thank you, Lord, for all the gifts that you have provided unto us. And as we move into this winter season, we think about the greatest gift that you did give to us. Help us always to live and treat one another as you want us to do so. Be with us this evening as we go about the work of the township. May our deliberations and discussions be pleasing in your sight. Watch over all of us here. Return us home safely in the evening. We ask that you would bless us as we go through the evening and take care of those we love, as well as our first responders. Keep them out of harm's way. We ask this all in your Son's name.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Amen.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thank you, Steve. Before we actually start the meeting, um, on item 10, other business, I do have 2 board appointees that I'll bring up at that time. We'll always have time for citizen comments.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Good.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: You know the drill. State your name and then three minutes. Oh yeah, for the other gentleman behind us, so he knows.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yeah, Stephen Willard, Holland Charter Township. Do you need my address?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Please.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Three five six eight Jaden Court. Good evening. Before I begin, I want to thank Supervisor Teslaw. For his email response earlier this week, he confirmed that the township is already working on policy updates in several of the areas: legal language, signage, staff procedures, and proactive enforcement. That's encouraging, and I appreciate that, and I appreciate your leadership tonight. I'm here to support that work with specific recommendations and draft ordinance language that can help accelerate the process. I've been before this board twice in recent months raising concerns about parking violations and noise issues in residential neighborhoods. After those comments, I decided to stop complaining and start documenting. I spent three months systematically monitoring code violations in my neighborhood. I filed a FOIL request, spent $150 of my own money to get the township's enforcement records. I analyzed every case. The result is a 24-page report that I submitted to every one of you. Here's what the data shows: My small neighborhood, about half percent of the township, had 15 vehicle code violations in three months. Every single one of them came from my complaints, zero from township proactive enforcement. If that pattern holds across the township, we're looking at 1,500 to 3,000 violations that go unaddressed simply because nobody reports them. I didn't just complain, I proved there's a systemic problem. Tonight, I'm asking the board to address the root issues I've been raising. First, implement overnight parking restrictions in R-2 residential zones. The data shows long-term street parking correlates with code violation and neighborhood degradation. Second, increase penalties for modified exhaust violations to create meaningful deterrence. Current fines don't reflect the intentional costs of modifications. Both policies are common in comparable townships, revenue positive, and address the systemic enforcement gaps I've documented. I provided draft ordinance amendment language for the board's consideration, which I submitted to Steve.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: This isn't criticism, this is collaboration. The supervisor said it correctly, it starts with policy. I'm here to finish that— finish what you started. I started as a concerned resident. I am now a resident with data and solutions. I'm committed to working with the board to make these changes happen.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thanks, Steve. Appreciate that.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Joseph Parnell McCarter, Georgetown Township volunteer leader of the Save the Campbell effort. In the first round of Campbell resolutions, 7 township boards passed a request for Campbell closure delay, as did the Ottawa County Board. They were forwarded to the Trump administration, and it executed the delay. Consumers Energy has consented to keep Campbell open as long as required by the Trump administration, but no longer. In the second round of Campbell resolutions, 7 township boards— the sheet says 6, but since then there— one has been added. 7 township boards, including Georgetown, Zeeland Township, and 5 others, have passed them already, and we are getting positive indications from others. This second round establishes a committee of township representatives to explore the replacement of Consumers Energy in Ottawa County with our own locally controlled electric cooperative, seeking the assistance of the Trump administration and perhaps partnering with an AI data center to use a portion of the Campbell's Electric to help with financing and residential rate reductions. Zeeland's Electric Cooperative is shown a locally controlled electric cooperative can be successfully run at half the price of Consumers Energy residential rates. Grand Haven, Holland, and Great Lakes Electric Cooperative are also successful area examples with lower pricing. The CEO of North American Electrical Liability Corporation, NERC, the premier organization for grid monitoring, says we have a 5-alarm fire situation with our grid. Because generating capacity is not keeping up with demand. We are adding energy-intensive data centers which require electric 24/7, but using unreliable solar, wind, battery to fill much of the gap. It not only does not provide reliable electric, but it also wastes our valuable farmlands and imposes dangerous lithium-ion battery plants on unwilling communities like Blendon Township. Using Michigan PA 233 to impose it. This Consumers Energy direction is also making electric very expensive for residents. Consumers Energy is asking for the highest rate increase in decades, irrespective of the Campbell. The Save the Campbell plan to replace Consumers with a locally controlled electric cooperative in Ottawa County can be an effective strategy to thwart the misguided net-zero agenda and to make sure AI data centers only go in if they make sense for local residents. and do not hurt the reliability of our grid. Now is the window of opportunity to pursue this while we can get the Trump administration assistance. I have handed you the second round of Campbell resolutions which have already been passed by 7 other boards. Please consider placing them on your agenda and passing them like the other township boards have already done and more expected. It is a matter of good due diligence to gather information Using the exploratory committee, which is what these aim for, an exploratory committee. It's simply information gathering. It doesn't bind any township to anything. It's gathering information. Does an exploratory committee— I mean, does locally controlled electric cooperative make sense for those parts that are on Consumers Energy? Thank you so much.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thank you very much. Then we have a public hearing for the amendment to a PUD, final development plan, at the MacIntyre Legends.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Great.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: John?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Good evening, board. John Tempest with Dreesing and Associates, 12330 James Street. I am here representing Eastbrook Homes for the PUD amendment at MacIntyre Legends that is before you. We've been in front of the Planning Commission and I think you have a copy of the staff report in your packet. I'll give you just a quick summary of the changes that were involved in this Planned Unit Development Amendment, and then I'm happy to answer any questions you have. So, I don't know if there's a copy of the drawing that could be put up on the screen, but you've got it in front of you as well. Here we are. The changes for this planned unit development, in terms of the layout, are actually very minor. We've got— so the section at the bottom that's kind of— it's in blue, but then outlined in red. That is the area of the development that we're talking about tonight. And the amendment from the prior versions of this PUD include the addition of one lot to the development at the far southwest corner of that— That's the one that's outlined in red. red outlined area. So we're at 69 units within this phase. And we've made some adjustments to the lots throughout so that the— all of the lots on the north side of that road got a little bit wider to allow for some of the larger footprints that Eastbrook has now available in their portfolio. And then the lots on the south side of the road actually got slightly smaller. And the reason we did that, it was kind of on purpose to provide some transition.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: between the more dense development to the south and then the golf course to the north. And Eastbrook has some nice units that will go well together while, at the same time, providing a variety of price points and options to their buyers within that area. And the planned unit development, other than those changes I mentioned, we've got a sidewalk connection to the park that's at the far west end of this blue area. And we've provided some additional information to comply with township ordinance requirements in terms of utility layout, landscaping, and some of the other details and information that are now required by your ordinance. So that's really the, the sum total of the changes that we're proposing here. And I think we had pretty good dialogue and feedback with the Planning Commission. Generally speaking, we're continuing to meet some of the commitments that we've met with prior phases as far as construction of the roadways within this area. So one key thing that we're doing here, you know, this is a fairly large phase that we're talking about, 69 units, and Eastbrook generally does not put that many units out for, you know, on the market at one given time. However, the utilities in this area are kind of necessitating that we construct this entire area area at once. Sanitary sewer for this entire phase comes from the west at Georgian Bay, and water main, of course, loops through, but they'd like to develop the eastern half of this section first, but they've got to construct all of the sanitary sewer to make that happen. So what they're actually proposing to do is to build the infrastructure for this entire phase, but they're only going to construct the roadway for the eastern half of this phase, and then the western half is going to be an emergency access only. So there will be a gravel roadway sufficient to meet the requirements for the fire access, and that will probably be restricted in some way, so it's not going to be a public access. It's going to be available for emergency access only while the eastern half is being built out. And then later on, they will make the western half available for sale and build the roadway in there. So they're going to base the road and then top the road within 2 years, just as we've done in prior phases of the development. But they're going to actually build the roadways kind of in 2 sections, if that makes sense. So that really highlights all the issues I wanted to point out, and I'm available for any questions. Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I just want to talk about the road situation again. On the resolution, in the resolution, in the second paragraph, it says, and affirm a deviation from the private street. Is that what you just talked about? When they say a deviation from the 2-year period, what do they mean by that?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So, let me see if I can actually get to that.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: It's the second paragraph of the resolution. And it says, and affirm a deviation from the private street paving requirement associated with 8-2, et cetera, et cetera.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah, so the deviation, actually, is that typically, your requirements would dictate that the base course and top course of pavement be installed prior to obtaining building permits and building any homes. What Eastbrook likes to do And there's a lot of good reasons for this, and this is kind of the precedent that has been set out at Makatawa Legends, is that we actually put a thicker base course down than what is required. Normally, we'd have to have an inch and a half thick base course and an inch and a half thick top. We're proposing to do a 2-inch base, but then we're allowed to defer the paving of the top course until within a period of 2 years after obtaining the first building permit. And what that does is it allows a lot of the construction traffic for the construction of the homes themselves to be on that base course rather than on the top.

JAvUAR8x9to.summary:
- Stephen Willard presented public comment on enforcement of noise and parking ordinances, proposing stricter enforcement of illegal exhaust modifications and overnight parking bans; township confirmed ongoing meetings with sheriff's department to improve code enforcement coordination
- McKenna Associates presented the comprehensive master plan housing study update, recommending distribution for 42-day public comment period; plan projects 42,000-45,000 township residents and 3,000-6,000 new housing units by 2040, with sufficient zoned land capacity through 2040
- First reading of zoning text amendments: wetland mitigation bank establishment in agricultural districts (minimum 10 acres, designed to allow wetland credit creation and sales within watershed) and front yard definitions clarifying that US-31, M-196, and N-21 shall be considered streets
- Approved charitable gaming license recognition for Champion Gymnastics USA Booster Club (unanimous); approved MCI Metro Access Transmission Services LLC right-of-way permit under Metro Act (unanimous); authorized refunding bond resolution for water treatment capacity improvements, projecting $880,000 in savings (6-0 with 1 absent)
- Approved depositories for fiscal year 2026 (Charles Schwab, Macatawa Bank/WindTrust); approved 2026 board meeting schedule with twice-monthly June meetings required for CIP review and adoption
- Reappointed Construction Board of Appeals members (Balkan, Brink, Harper for 2-year terms), Robert DeVries to Zoning Board of Appeals, and Doug Becker to Planning Commission (all unanimous); disagreement between Trustee DeYoung and Community Development Director Broersma regarding selective enforcement of parking/driveway ordinances at 610 North Shore Drive (unresolved)

highlights.quote: It's time we stop judging noise by its source and start judging by its effect. When enforcement is lax, the problem compounds. What starts as an isolated nuisance quickly becomes the new normal.
Housing is going up faster than population. Household sizes are declining. We have literally fewer people living in each individual housing unit. There's 0.2 of a person that has to live somewhere.

We are experiencing a market failure. It's the— it's like it— I can't rent it out because it's too expensive for anyone to rent out, but I have to rent it at that price because it cost me that much to build, right?
We have enough land zoned for residential through 2040. We don't need to talk about converting agricultural land. We have enough.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I'm writing today I'm here today as a longtime resident who cares deeply about the safety, character, and long-term value of our neighborhood. Over the past several years, I've watched a troubling trend take root, one that is eroding the quality of life for residents who simply want to enjoy their homes in peace. Loud vehicles, modified exhausts, and revving engines have become a persistent plague in our area. Once one is tolerated, or two, more follow. We've grown so used to the noise that we've stopped questioning it. But imagine this: if someone stood outside every morning at 6:30 AM and blasted a modified air horn, we'd all agree it was unacceptable. This disruption, the stress, the resentment, it would be obvious. Yet when a car with an illegally modified exhaust makes the same level of noise at the same time, it's brushed off as just a car. The impact is the same. The only difference is that one form of noise is normalized while the other would trigger immediate action. It's time we stop judging noise by its source and start judging by its effect. It's a pattern we've seen before. When enforcement is lax, the problem compounds. What starts as an isolated nuisance quickly becomes the new normal. We need proactive enforcement of existing noise ordinance to prevent this from escalating further and to stop normalizing illegal exhaust. But enforcement alone isn't enough. It's not enough if the system itself is flawed. In many cases, loud vehicle violations result in repair and report tickets where the driver is supposed to fix the issue and show proof. Yet in practice, many pay the fine and skip the report. Then there's no follow-up, no verification, and no consequence for repeat offenders. It turns the ticket into a premium for an A small price to pay to keep disturbing the peace. I urge the township to close this loophole by requiring violations— verifications for repairs and tracking repeat violations. If someone receives multiple tickets for the same issue, the fines should escalate and enforcement should Intensify. Otherwise, we're just normalizing non-compliance and undermining the very ordinances meant to protect our community. The same goes for parking. Fifteen years ago, street parking in a neighborhood was rare. Today, it's the default. Cars line both sides of the street. In some cases, people are now parking directly in their front yards. It's not just unsightly; it's unsafe. In neighborhoods like mine that lack sidewalks, street parking forces pedestrians into the roadway.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: 3 minutes.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Right now, the township code technically prohibits all street parking, but that is impossible to enforce, and so it isn't followed. The result is a rule that exists on paper but not in practice. I propose a more realistic An effective alternative: amend the code to allow street parking in neighborhoods, but with a clear overnight parking ban. This would encourage residents to use their driveways, improve visibility and access, and resets the streets daily. It's also far easier to enforce, especially if signage is posted at. Neighborhood entrances. Let's restore the integrity of our neighborhood through consistent enforcement and thoughtful action. I believe we can make this place— we're proud— this a place we're proud to call home, but it starts with leadership and follow-through. Thank you for your time and commitment to our community. And as a gesture of faith, the light on my road is out. If you guys have heard my message. Fix my light.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Might help if I have batteries in it. All right, uh, good evening everyone. It's, it's good to be back for this presentation. Um, I don't have to go into a ton of detail about this process. I think we've talked about it already, but, uh, obviously, uh, the township is going through an update to the master plan, the comprehensive plan. To confirm or to figure out whether you are on track in terms of the amount of housing that you are planning for within this plan and giving the Planning Commission the tools to approve appropriate housing within the community. You see the goals of this process on the screen, which we've talked about before. Tonight, as we move into the distribution of the So, as we move through the document and move closer to the finish line, I want to focus my presentation on some questions that came up last time and then resulted in some edits to the report as far as what you have in front of you compared to what you had last time I was here. First of all, there's an ask to add some 2000 Census data into the report for context, so I'm going to present some of that tonight. We're going to talk about more about our methodology for determining in-migration. And the rate at which that's happening. And the question of why does housing demand grow faster than population, which is an interesting one. You think those two things are tied together, and they are, but it's not a one-to-one relationship, and we'll talk about why. So to get into those, first on the 2000 census, we do have that number from— for the township. It's not available for the trade area because that's a customized geography, and the data for the system we use for that does not go back that far. For the township, we can say we had about, you know, 28,000, almost 29,000 people. That grew to 35,000 and change by 2010. Growth was slower in the 2010 to 2020 decade. We knew that. And then has sped back up again if you're taking it kind of on an annual basis in the 2020s. The trade area, you know, your broader housing market that you are a part of, has grown slightly faster than you guys. through that period, but not dramatically so. I did note the— your population growth, 2000 to 2010, you see on the bottom, you added 25% more people almost. So it was very dramatic during that decade. Slowed down, seems to be speeding back up. That's kind of the trend that we've seen. Now, how do we project population growth? I wanted to dive into this in a little detail because I know there are questions about it, and I want to make sure that you're comfortable with how we got these numbers. So what we do is what's called a cohort component analysis. And we divide the population into these 10-year age cohorts. And the nice thing about age cohorts is that we can kind of predict their behavior, right? We have data that tells us how likely they are to have kids at a certain age, how likely people are to die at a certain age, how likely they are to be homeowners at a certain age, how likely they are to be the head of their household at a certain age. And all this, of course, is statistical data, so it's not saying anything about any individual, but it gives you sort of a—

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: A sense.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Actuarial prediction about what people will do at different ages. And once we've divided our age groups up, then we can advance people through their lives over the course of time and have some statistical analysis of what their lives might look like and how that might impact the housing demand. So in order to illustrate how this works, I've created a fake township here. This township has 900 people in it and has exactly, as of 2010, It's had exactly 100 in each of these 10-year age groups. So 0 to 9, 100 people, all the way up to 80-plus-year-olds, 100 people. If in our hypothetical township nobody moved in or out for the following 30 years up to 2040, what would the population look like? Well, if we apply a birth rate and a death rate that looks like the rest of the state of Michigan, what ends up happening here is the population Sort of waffles, but generally kind of declines. And the reason for that is that in general, the birth rate isn't quite matching the death rate throughout. This is statewide data; we don't really have local data on that. But the point here is that to illustrate how we do this, you see that when we go from 2010 to 2020, a couple people get taken out of each cage category, and that unfortunately would be people who pass away statistically. Right, so we lose about 1 to 2% of our 30 and 40-year-olds and a higher percentage as people get older. And then we add in the babies. So it looks like we would have had 120 babies born in that 2010 here in our hypothetical township. Now, this kind of thing illustrates why migration is so important as far as this analysis goes though, because this is all great, but this place is flatlining because nobody's moving in or out, right? So there is no— increase or decrease really. You lose 10 people over 30 years. So say in our hypothetical township we took a look at the actual census data for 2020, and instead of 887 people as this model projected with nobody moving in and out, say they had 950. Well, what happened there? Well, it means that a net of 63 people moved in. Maybe people moved out, people moved in. On balance, 63 extra people were there That our births and deaths model didn't predict. So they must have moved in. So that's where we get that migration— 7.1% more people than we thought there were going to be. So then if we just applied that to our previous population, you see now the population grows. And we can then project that out and say, hey, looking forward, we're going to have more people. So that's the analysis we did on your much more complicated numbers that aren't all nice round 100s. And we get these numbers. So, as requested, we did go back to 2000 to take a look at this. And with that fast population growth in the 2000s, obviously, you had higher in-migration, 8.4%. The 2010s, we know the first half of that decade, the economy was rough, people weren't moving to Michigan. And we've talked about this before, under 1% net in-migration. In the past several years, and this is based on your building permit data, So it's based on, you know, what has been built in this township, local data, not census data. You've got about a 6.3% migration rate. So then what did we do in the actual document as discussed before? We took a look at that 0.6% and projected that out because we do want to have that in there. Is it likely we're going to have another bad decade? I don't know, probably not. But if you stood here— if I stood here in 2005, I probably wouldn't have told you the economy was going to collapse in 2008. Right. God forbid we have another Great Recession, but we kind of have to analyze that downside risk. More likely is what you've seen as this 6.3% is likely to continue probably in that range of 5, 6, 7%. We did not rerun the numbers for 8.4% just because that's so long ago that it really doesn't feel like it's relevant to projecting out. I mean, what was going on in 2003 is not really instructive for 2033. That said, if you're around 6% now, you could absolutely be at 8% going forward. That's why the top end— that's why everything we present is ranges, and you can think of that as being sort of the top end.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yes. So in order to do that, we do back out to that annual, right? So We look at what does the— what is our population projected to 2030? And then we look at that census number that we have for '23, and then we back out, okay, how— what would our population have had to do to get to what we thought the 2030 was going to be? What does that predict it would have been in '23? And we compare those 2 together.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Make sense? So this gives you— and you've seen this slide before— You know, kind of the bottom line, right? 2040, Holland Township, what are we looking at? Between 42,000 and 45,000 people, which is about 3,000 to 6,000 new people, and your broader region of having about 10,000 to 25,000 more people. And what does that mean for housing growth? You can see it in the right 2 columns, which segues me into the why does housing grow faster than population question.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: You see the 2 left columns, that's your population. Under the lower growth scenario, you're still going up about 17.2%. Under the higher growth scenario, you're going to go up about 28.3%. But your housing demand is going to grow over 20% or even closer, maybe 36%. So there's— all the numbers are going up. Housing is going up faster than population. So why is that? There's a number of reasons for that. The simple one-sentence answer is housing— or excuse me, household sizes are declining. So we have literally fewer people living in each individual housing unit. There's reasons for this which I'm going to get into, but this is sort of the baseline math of it all is in 1990 you had 2.66 people living in every home. Today you have 2.43 and falling. And that 0.2 of a person has to live somewhere. And thus, we have more housing demand than we would have had for the same number of people 30 years ago. Why is that? Well, some of this is demographics. And I mentioned we do a lot of this analysis based on age because age is so predictive of housing. And these are a couple good examples of how you can get housing demand growth without population growth. For instance, say you have some kids at Hope College and they have their— there are 4 of them, they all live together, they share a house. And then they all graduate. And then they all move into their own place because now they have a job and they don't have to share student housing anymore. And that's now 4 housing units for 4 people, whereas before you had 1 housing unit for 4 people. No population growth, 3 extra housing units demanded. Another example is a family. Parents, mom, dad, 3 kids. 5 people living in a house. The kids grow up. They turn 18. They turn 22. They move out. Parents are still living in that house. Now it's 2 people in that house, and the 3 kids who are now adults, they're all living in their own house. Now we have 4 housing units for 5 people as opposed to 1 housing unit for 5 people. Once again, population doesn't grow, but housing demand does. Another aspect of this is that you're— as noted, your population growth is driven by people moving here. Babies being born helps, obviously. That helps population growth as well, but your population growth A lot of it is driven by people moving here, and if someone moves here, they need a housing unit today. Right? Someone moves here from Chicago; they need somewhere to live in Holland Township now. A baby who is born tonight doesn't need their own housing unit for 20 years. So if your growth is being driven by babies and families having more kids, you're not going to get the housing growth isn't going to grow faster than population as much as if your housing growth is being more. Excuse me, your population growth being driven by people moving here who all then immediately need their own housing unit. And what's notable here is that— and this is broad national, international trends— birth rate is declining, right? When we run these analyses, the birth rate we used to use a decade ago was 600 births over 10 years for every 1,000 women of childbearing age. That's the average. That was the formula. We've recently changed it, including for this study, to 520, which is more reflective of today's birth rate. So birth rates are going down. Meanwhile, as we know, people are moving here. People want to come to West Michigan. People are moving in. So that creates a bigger delta between those 2 factors of population growth than you might otherwise have and causes your housing demand to rise faster. Just to give some age, some examples, and these were inserted into the report since the last time we presented here. So just to give an example, so the folks born between 1960 and 1969 in our model, so right now these people are between 56 and 65 years old. In 2040, they'll be between 71 and 80 years old. There will be about 300 fewer of them In Holland Township, because unfortunately, people pass away, right? Even with people moving in and out, we still project that there will be 300 fewer people born between 1960 and 1969 living here in 2040 than there are right now. However, that age cohort will actually need 60 more housing units than they need right now, even though there will be 300 fewer of them. And the reason for that is that this is unfortunately also a prime age for divorce.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Right, in your 50s. It is also an age as people get older when they start needing assisted living. And then you might have husband and wife and they're in neighboring assisted living units. That's still 2 housing units. So you start to see the housing demand per person go up for this group as they get older, even though there's going to be fewer of them. Another example. So this is folks born between 2000 and 2009. They're currently between 16 and 25 years old. In 2040, they'll be between 31 and 40 years old. There will be 650 more of them in 2040 compared to 2020. That's people moving in. We don't create new 30-year-olds out of thin air. That means they move in. But they're going to need almost 3,400 more housing units than that age cohort needs today. And that's starting with those 16, 17, 18-year-olds who are still living at home, right? They're not in the system at all as far as needing housing. By the time they're in their 30s, they're better move out of their parents' place and they're going to need housing. Meanwhile, again, comparing, you know, the college kids who live in big groups, they're going to start moving out into their own places. The 30 to 40-year-old— I'm sorry, Gretchen, I'll give you one second. The 34-year-old generation or 34-year-old group is also a group that more— is more statistically likely to live alone than other groups. They're old enough to afford their own place but maybe young enough not to be married yet. All of that leads to this group, Like really shooting up in housing demand. So that's just a couple examples of how this works. I'm sorry, Gretchen.

_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So Michigan is sort of average in this regard, right? There are states where it's lower and states where it's higher. We do try in these analyses to Because it's a demand model, we do try to use broader geographies for this kind of thing because we want to tease out what people— what people in the broader market are trying to do, right? But I can definitely get that number for Holland Township and make sure it's in the document. All right, so what do we need in terms of housing? And these numbers you've seen before, about 3,000 to 5,000 new owner-occupied and about 800 to 1,200 new rental units. We compared that— oh, sorry, this gives you your pace of housing construction, which again, we've talked about previously, and is kind of roughly on par with what you need on an annual basis. So it's kind of a keep it up and you'll keep up type of thing. This breaks that down in terms of year things were built and what type of housing was built. The Planning Commission noted that there has been, if you look at it year by year, a bit of a shift away from single-family, which means that there is potentially a need for that to correct itself in the future years to keep the ratio that we talked about here correct. And so there are some planning commissioners that are talking about potentially, you know, looking at some master plan changes, even though this process has Currently not recommended any, but they're thinking about that kind of thing. Over at the Planning Commission, the germs of that are starting to come out. But also, this could just be market dynamics or coincidence. So we'll see what that looks like, but that is something the Planning Commission pointed out, so I wanted to bring it to you guys. These graphs show that if you continue to build housing in the township at the current rate, that you will keep up with demand generally, In the highest migration scenarios, you do need to pick up the pace a little bit. But if you look at the lowest demand scenarios, which is the orange line, if supply— the gray line, the supply line on these graphs goes up at the linear rate based on what you are currently building, and you'll see that you basically fall between the low and high demand scenarios by 2040. So we think roughly you are on pace. This compares your housing demand to the housing capacity, which is if all of the land that's not currently developed was developed as described in the master plan to its highest density allowable. That puts you at just under 26,000 housing units. The total we're saying is demanded— we're seeing is demanded out to 2040 is like 18,000 to 21,000, which you're well within. So we find your— future land use map in your comprehensive plan to be planning for sufficient housing, which is why our recommendation is to not change it at this time. Though, like I said, the Planning Commission following this process may start talking about a couple of sites where they might want to start encouraging single-family a little more. So, as I said, our overall recommendation is to stick to this plan, at least generally. Your current rate of housing growth is sufficient, but it's important to note that it shouldn't slow down. So the Planning Commission should stay busy. There should still be developers at this podium wanting to build things. Those things should be consistent with your master plan. As long as they are, they should be approved, the housing should be built, and everything will stay on track. So where are we at right now? We've done steps 1 and 2 on this slide, which is incorporating that housing study into the whole comprehensive plan document, which is why you have the whole plan in front of you, and the housing study is just an extra chapter sitting at the end. That gives context to the rest of it. The Planning Commission has voted to recommend that you distribute this plan. Just as a reminder what we mean by distribution, it is for an amendment, a 42-day process where the public is given an opportunity to comment and the plan is distributed to neighboring communities and other entities for their comments as well, leading up to a public hearing by the Planning Commission, which is noted as step 5 there, and then back to this board for final adoption. So with that, I can take any questions.

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: E

One is we see it in developers pushing for higher densities and sort of quick turnaround profits like high-density condos, right? Right. I want to sell as many as possible and get out of it. So we're seeing a little bit of that. But if we're trying to encourage things, developers will call it the gap, right? They'll call it the— it's the gap between how much they can make and how much this is going to cost to build. And in order to fill that gap, they do frequently come looking for help. Obviously, there's a number of programs through MSHDA and MEDC. They're not very popular right now, but they have programs for housing that help fill that gap.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Well, that's a significant income gap. It is, yes.

Because they're way over the income caps for things like homeownership assistance and that kind of thing. And frankly, on a local level, it is hard to do the demand-side stuff, right? A lot of it is supply-side. You know, I think there are a number of communities that are really embracing the state's new housing brownfield program. I think you guys are under the brownfield authority from the county, right?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: We have our own.
