Using this housing brownfield tool, which, as I understand it, is very lucrative for developers. It uses the state's brownfield tax increment financing system that was already in place and then basically opens the door to use it for housing in all sorts of scenarios, including, as I understand it, scenarios where there actually isn't any environmental remediation to do. It's just simply to help pay for the housing. And there are communities out there that have local brownfields like you guys do that have opened that door wide. They've passed resolutions at the elected level saying we want the Brownfield Authority to approve for housing that meets these criteria. That's one option. Other places have said— We have one in the works right now. Okay. Good, good.

To do something that eases the financial burden that a lot of young 20- and 30-somethings face when it comes to the cost of housing, trying to get a down payment together. A lot of them have student loans and all this kind of thing. There is certainly— there are some options. A lot of them do not currently exist, however.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yeah.

Finding that first home, a first home that you that you can own, that you can build equity in, that can become a second home and a third home, and then someone else's first home again. Right? So I think that is that that getting into homeownership is the problem, and that's going to help. So both on your sort of planning and zoning side, hey, we need townhouses, right? We need condominiums. We need Smaller single-family homes that are going to sell for a little bit less so that that young professional can buy them and get in and start building equity. Because if you don't build equity, you're never going to get this down payment for the more expensive forever home, right? And that's that is a product that is underrepresented in the market because developers are going to be are going to go out and say, "I need to either build single-family and sell it for us." much as I can, or I need as much density as I can. And so coming in the middle and saying what we want you to do is sort of medium density for sale at a lower price point, that's something where a developer is going to say, how are you helping me make money on that, right? And then so that's where the proposals come in.

Like, there's a, there's a mismatch. There's a market failure going on. _utterances.text: So in both of your presentations, you're basically warning, right, that we have a shortage problem of both rentals and single-family homes, correct? And we need to have 16,000 units more by 2040. Was that— is that right?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: No, it's not that high.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Here's—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: it doesn't—

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I'm not even—

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: yeah, it's, it's more like, all right, 6,000.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So here's my question: or what, right? What happens when we don't build these units? And part of the reason I'm saying this is I think we— I see all this green land that we want to keep as agricultural. Well, for us to keep building units, we have to not have agricultural anymore. And I think as a township, we need to And what do we want? And what is— all I hear is like, beware, beware, you're short. What happens when we don't build these units at all? Because we're just really happy with how many people we have in Holland Township. And like this gentleman here, we don't have places for all the vehicles. The roads are horrible by my office. Culver's and Menards, I can't move pulling out of my office anymore. I don't go toward the highway.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: That's true.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: All the roads get overwhelmed with another 20,000 units or whatever we're going to do, right? And I just wonder, what's the alternative if we don't—

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: what happens? So the problem that Trustee Malachy pointed out gets worse and worse, basically, is that the costs shoot up. And Treasurer Bush pointed out that there's— that you get more and more people that can't afford the housing that they want.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So what is it— what does a municipality like the City of Holland, who really doesn't They don't have any more land for growth. How are they surviving?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So they have designated land for increased density. Now, it's not the whole city, but they picked the spots where they felt like they had sufficient infrastructure, and that land is now zoned for higher densities than it was previously. In some cases, that means taking down what's there now, which might just be like a strip mall or something, and then That's one of the things they've done is if you go down Waverly Road through the city, much of that is now zoned so you can put housing there. It hasn't necessarily happened yet, but that used to be a commercial zone, right? Commercial only. Now it is a mixed-use zone in which housing can be built. They've done a lot of that kind of thing. They also, of course, have their downtown. Their downtown is designed to be high density. It has been for 150 years or whatever. And that's— or more than that.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: You know, putting in condos and apartments downtown is part of their strategy as well. For you guys, you don't have those options. I will say, to your point about—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Why don't we have those options?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: We don't— well, actually, you know what, I say that and I probably shouldn't have said it that way, because you do have the Federal District and there's allowable density there, and that is right next to downtown Holland, so it's very appropriate there. You have the Beachwood District where you've got some opportunities your master plan calls for, though I don't know that they've really come to fruition. You have the mall area, which can support some density because it has lots of infrastructure. So there are spots where you can do that, absolutely. And I apologize for downplaying that, because you definitely have them. I do want to say that if we look at that future land use map and you look at the big— the green area in the northeast, but then if we compare it to the zoning map, you will see a lot more green. And where we see green on that zoning map that is not green on that future land use map, The your stated policy is that that should be developed for housing, and when we say your plan calls for enough housing, what we mean is what's green on that map on the left turns yellow or orange over time as it gets rezoned and built on, and that so long as it is green or excuse me, so long as it's yellow or orange on the map on the right or purple to Treasurer Bush's point with mixed use, then we consider that. If it's green on the map on the right, then we don't consider that appropriate. And when we ran the numbers, we did not count the green. We didn't count the agricultural area. In fact, there's a little footnote there saying you may be able to get some more housing in that area just on, like, rural lot splits. But we did not calculate that because it's actually not your intent to use that area to fill housing need. That area was during the 2020— Master plan process, the boundaries of that green area in the far northeast and the far southeast were chosen specifically. And they go back further, too. They go back to previous master plans as well, that you kind of said, look, this is the point at which the township, as far as we can foresee, will stop growing. But anything sort of south of this point is open for growth. To your last point about infrastructure, that's also why we do this. So knowing you have this kind of coming, and so working with the Road Commission, working with MDOT, working with the MACS, working with the BPW, and making sure your infrastructure is in place. Or even with the developers. We just talked about developer costs, but sometimes the developers are the ones bringing the traffic and they have to provide the upgrades, right? So there's going to be— all of this is going to need to be kind of happen simultaneously. But I do want to lastly say something about the scale, which is right around, we think, sort of that 4,000 to 7,000 housing unit range. Numbers like 16,000 and 20,000 I know have been said in this room. That's not what we're finding. We're finding you need more, but it's not on the level of double what you have now or something of that nature. So I hope that answered your question.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I still just want to go back to What happens when we run out of room? And I realize that's a long ways out. I think that's the reason I use the City of Holland as an example. I think they have a— they just have to go up. And if you're doing a study for them, are you either way going to say, you guys need to build more space, you have to have higher density and start tearing single-family down and build multi-family up, or whatever that's going to be? But is it— if you're not growing, are you dying? Is that what the—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: No.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yes, to an extent. I think that in this environment for you guys, it really isn't about dying. People want to live here.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So they're trying to come in, right? And if they don't— if you guys don't have housing for them, they'll go to Park Township, they'll go to Zeeland Township, they'll go to Hudsonville, right? They'll drive longer distances, creating more traffic is actually the biggest— is one of the bigger concerns there. But someone will take them because they want to move to West Michigan.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I like that.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: That's great.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah, it's kind of an interesting perspective to spin off of that. You say that there's more supply and more—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: there's more—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: less supply, more demand, assessed values go— our tax values go up. So you make more money by having the same infrastructure and you're able to take care of your people even better.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Proposal A has something to say about that, but yes. Right? There is a— well, there are some— part of the reason we have market failure, right, is there are some perverse incentives. If you are an existing homeowner—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: It just interests me.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yeah, but if you're an existing homeowner, there is all sorts of incentive to not see other things built because your home goes up in value. And that is one of the issues we have.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And I'm—

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: well, obviously, that's something we should admit, you know?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Kind of. So instead of trying to accommodate growth, to what extent should a township try to control growth? Does that make sense? And I don't see that in here at all.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Is—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: maybe it makes sense not to simply allow the township to grow that much. Maybe we like it being the size it is. And should policies be adopted to perpetuate what seems like a livable situation?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yes. What—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I haven't heard anything about that. And I also— I'm wondering how employment matters in these equations. So the equations about who's coming in, that seems to be driven by something, and I'm guessing it's employment, right? And that seems like super important. It seems like the driving factor. And to anticipate, is employment going to increase? Is there an increase in employment that is going to support this projection or not? So that's a couple of questions. One is, should we think about controlling growth instead of accommodating it? Maybe we don't want that. And secondly, you know, how does employment factor into this? It seems like the biggest factor and it doesn't seem like it's mentioned.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So to the controlling growth, I think that's a great question. The Comprehensive Plan as currently drafted contains growth management, right? That's why there is an entire— what is that, 3 to 4 square miles of ag land preserved on that map.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Mm-hmm.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: But also, it's why there are varying levels of housing density planned for and that they're specifically called out in the plan as this is an area for lower density, more along the lines of single family, and this is maybe more of your mixed-use areas that could really go up in density. And it's not just blanketing the whole township with as much density as could possibly happen, right? That is— the intent is to create what we call the transect, where the center of the region, the city of Holland, is the highest density. And then since you have some areas in that immediate vicinity, they are going to be relatively high density. And as you go further out, you go lower and lower density. And that's kind of— you can see even the intensity of the color on the map.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: It gets brighter and bolder as you get closer to US 31 and lighter as you go away from it. And that is reflective of the actual policy there. Now, as with all things, you can dial it up and dial it back. I mean, our recommendation, having done this report, is if you just simply follow what's on that map, then you will kind of stay in parity with demand. Now, you could dial it back. You would then see costs, but also values, go up, right? And you could also dial it up and be like, hey, we want more and more density, and then you would see, you know, housing costs probably go down, but you'd see infrastructure costs go up, right? So this is the type of decision you guys have to make as elected officials. Uh, your question about employment, um, yes, it is one of the factors. We're seeing significant investment by companies, especially on the south side of Holland in the industrial area. But we are also seeing— and this is not as pronounced as it was in, say, 2021, 2022, right after COVID— but we are seeing the remote workers here too. This is a place where you can live, have a job that's in Chicago, be there once or twice a month, and work— otherwise work at home. And actually what we've seen happen with that over the few years since that kind of initially started is that that becomes the Holland office.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: And then these people have office space and 3 or 4 more employees, and they have an office in the Loop or whatever, but they now have a branch office in Holland because someone moved here during the pandemic or something. So we are seeing that. Now that's anecdotal. I don't have like numbers on that right now, but I do have anecdotal reports of that kind of thing happening, and it is one of the drivers of West Michigan's economic growth. Ultimately, this is a nice place to live. People want to live here, and they will— they do follow jobs, but also jobs follow that. Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chris. Lots of information. Randy, thank you for being here tonight.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: You got any comments?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, just a couple things, Dave, just to make it simple. We have enough land zoned for residential through 2040. We don't need to talk about converting agricultural land. We have enough. I'm not sure you answered that directly, but that is the answer.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thank you for that.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I do have a question, um, as far as what, what direction are we heading with the Planning Commission as far as, uh, residential versus apartments?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Because I've— yeah, are you going to get into that?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: All right.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Our plan is to— first of all, we want to get this released and published so developers have it in their hands. Then we want to invite developers in to talk about what can we do about single-family owner-occupied. What can we do to help you? If you listen to MacIntyre Legends, townhouses, high-end townhouses, are a very hot Well, we're not really zoned for that. So, should we adjust some of our zoning to maybe pursue more of that? But, that's what we want to do when this is released, so we can have those dialogues. Because, we have enough apartments coming in at us. We're not really concerned about that. We're worried about owner-occupied people Vince is talking about. And then, in terms of employment, We're trying to be very protective of our industrial and commercial property to support further employment, not just storage units. Gretchen and Corey can talk about— we just put some stipulations on that. But that's what we're planning to do as a planning commission, is to see what are things we could do through zoning changes to help the developers owner-occupy That's kind of the push right now, is more—

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: that's really what I wanted to do.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: If you really dig into the detail, that's what we need more.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I agree. Thanks for the input, Randy, because that is a good segue into actually taking action on getting this approved so that they can go to the next step and talk to developers. And areas in zoning and everything else. I'd look for a motion to approve the distribution of the master plan for public comment.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'll support it. And supported. All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any opposed, same sign. Thanks again, Chris.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Thank you, board members. Probably won't see you, so happy holidays.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Thank you, Chris. We we do appreciate all of your work.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, very interesting. We're going to get the introduction and first reading of the zoning ordinance text amendment, wetland mitigation and front yard. What's this document, Steve or Corey?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Someone want to introduce it for the first reading?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I'll introduce it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay, any other feedback on that?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So yes, I can provide a brief overview of that if you'd like. Um, so wetland mitigation bank, not sure if everybody's familiar with what those are. There is an existing one in the area by the Alcoa Discovery Center, but basically, as you can see on the screen here, uh, it's controlled, uh, by EGLE Part 303, uh, and it It is a tool to replace wetlands or a tool to establish wetlands for anticipated or future disturbance. So the establishment of new wetlands areas or banks in advance of anticipated losses. Wetlands established in a mitigation bank provide credits which can be bought and sold on the open market to assist with development. So a mitigation credit is generally in a unit of 1 acre of created or restored wetland, and a developer may buy those credits. And the reason this has come up to us is we have a developer with approximately a 54-acre site, was unable to get that rezoned for multifamily housing has now looked at the fact that there is a drain corridor there, some floodplain, et cetera. Would a wetland bank make sense? This developer, I think, would be able to use his own bank to support purchase of other marginal properties and displace wetlands there to create credits on this new site. So mitigation credits must be secured within the same watershed. As a wetland impact. However, if no mitigation credits are available in the same watershed, then credits may be sought within the larger ecoregion. And the next couple slides kind of show you that. So, light area is the mitigation area for the Makatawa Watershed. So, as you can see, it goes up to— you know, touches West Olive.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: It goes almost out to Hudsonville. Ironically, it's almost similar to the trade area that we talked about within our housing study. In the case— and this would be a rare case, but there are situations where credits could be bought for areas in the unusual-shaped areas numbered 1 and 2. The first one is the Jamestown And the second one is the South Lake Michigan ecoregion, going all the way past Muskegon, out to the outskirts of Grand Rapids, and further south off that map. Now, again, I've got to stress that that is— would be a very unique situation and unusual, I think, for it to go that far, but it is a possibility. So what we're looking for tonight, With the Planning Commission's recommendation to bring this to you is to establish a use, and this was discussed again with our attorney, to establish the wetland mitigation definition that you see on here, to establish that the wetland mitigation bank use will be added to the chart of uses, schedule of uses for the agricultural district.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: And the reason we did agricultural district is partly because of this map here. There will be very select few sites within the township where it would make sense to put one of these. It would need to be agriculture, and then through EGLE's program, you also have to have the history of hydric soils. So the gold blotches that you see there on your screen, those are those hydric soils. You combine that with the agriculture, it starts to limit where these could be. We don't want to take industrial land out of production, commercial land, or residential land, per our master plan, out of production and the potential tax revenue and jobs and families that could be here. So— With those parameters, we then set up a Section 9.28 wetland mitigation bank. Explains the purpose. Minimum area needs to be at least 10 acres. There's the state regulations that all these banks must follow, and one provision that the Planning Commission put in here was that the easement shall not prohibit improvements such as non-motorized trails, boardwalks, overlooks, and other similar amenities accessible to the general public unless authorized by the Planning Commission. So what this text is doing is keeping the door open for a large wetland bank to be used for a park of some sort, a very passive park. There would never be tennis courts, pickleball, basketball, volleyball, soccer fields, or anything like that. It'd be very low-impact use. The state generally dissuades that, but if we have a developer who sees that, for instance, if they were going to into a residential neighborhood next door and they see it as amenity or a selling point to also have a wetland mitigation bank and have a trail system, we don't want to stop them from being able to do that. So the other 2 points— access, we want to make sure that there's proper access from a county road, and then parking. That would be something to be talked about by the Planning Commission during the Yes. And that's part of the special land use process. If there was to be a trailhead, we'd want to have some parking. We're going to need some access because these items do need to be maintained in perpetuity. A conservation easement is placed over these. I'm not sure if you're familiar, behind Target, there's a large wetland cell system back there, and that has a conservation easement over it in perpetuity. So those are protected wetlands. Forever and ever. So that's— any questions on the wetland bank portion of the text amendment?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Can you go back to the map? So the question I have is that just— it's kind of overwhelming. I'm not 100% sure what really is going on here, but I'm assuming that you've got an opportunity of this, this 54 acres developer has wetlands he wants to build on it and he wants to mitigate somewhere else. He wants to use wetlands somewhere else to offset that building site. Is that what's going on? And then we're trying to identify where he could do that at.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Uh, these— the person in question that brought up this topic, he believed that it should be a use permitted by right in any agriculturally zoned property. Staff did not feel that way. Talked with the attorney, and because we didn't have it as a defined use, attorney was more than fine with us setting up a special land use permit for this. So the developer in question has a property, owns a property, is not looking for other properties. The property has some floodplain on it, about 24 acres, and that will be of— so there will be approximately 24 acres conservation easement in the end. The other remaining property will be developable at some point.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So if you did this text amendment, it allows him to use what he has on site to use that for the mitigation?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: His site falls in the ag zone and has some hydric soils, and it's— he's got a full designed plan. He was hoping it would already be approved, but it is not a permitted use by right in the township.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: He's going to sell opportunities.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: This isn't committed to building. He's selling it to other developers.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I see. So that's his business model, is I have wetlands available for sale, come see me.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: And yeah, he's— well, he's a developer himself, and I believe it will assist him in his developments. He can now search marginal properties that are maybe tough to develop. He now has an outlet to develop those sites wherever they may be and purchase credits from his own bank.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So the amount of wetland doesn't necessarily go away, right? If he's got 24— did he say 24 acres out of 54 or something like that? So when you're mitigating, you're going like wetland for wetland. He's just trying to consolidate his wetland, is that right, instead of having it all over his 54? He's trying to consolidate it. Is that the purpose?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Nope. The purpose is to create wetlands so there's a surplus. So he's going to make more wetlands than exist in nature right now. But as those wetlands in nature get disturbed due to development of some sort, those credits— so an acre over here gets disturbed, that developer can buy an acre of that excess credit here, get rid of their, their wetlands here, and know that that wetlands has been shifted to another spot in perpetuity.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So he's got like cheeseburgers, everyone knows he needs his Cheetos.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'm gonna buy them, you know.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I've got them, you're gonna come and see.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So you can— can you buy— is there a limit on how many acres they can buy? I mean, he only got 24, so he could only do it 24 times. Or if I wanted to— if I needed 5 acres of wetland, I could go over there and potentially buy 5 acres?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Potentially, yep. It'll all be depending on the type of credits needed. So some mitigation on that natural wetland may need to be mitigated at 1.5 to 1 instead of a 1 to 1 ratio. It could have floodplain, it could be forested. So EGLE will get into the limits of what can be bought and sold. I don't have much of one other than Outdoor Discovery Center is doubling their capacity.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: That's the point. They've already maxed out, so they're going to add.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's over by the airport.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yep, south by the airport.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay, we've had the first reading on that. I don't know if there's any other questions.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So then there's the, the other text amendments that are involved in this request as well, and this is spanning out of A challenging site up on 128th. There was a lawsuit involved with that. Township chose not to appeal the decision rather than— and then go forward with a text amendment to make it very clear that US 31, 196, and N21 should be considered streets to establish front yards. So the major change for the rest of the text amendment ordinance in front of you is shown here in red. It's modifying these 4 definitions to make it clear what a public right-of-way is and that front yards will be on those right-of-ways.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right. Thank you, Corey. We've got a consideration of the local government body resolution for charitable gaming licenses, right? Yep.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: What that is, we've done this before, it's recognizing the organization as a nonprofit. And this request from Champion Gymnastics USA Booster Club, they've submitted their request. They've also given me all the paperwork to prove that they are a nonprofit operating in the township, and this will allow them to conduct a bingo, raffle, or charity game. They still have to apply to the state, but this simply recognizes— we simply recognize they're a nonprofit doing business here. Any questions on that?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'll make a motion. I'll support it. And supported. All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any opposed? We got the resolution to authorize issuance of refunding bonds. Steve, you doing that one?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yeah, I think we want to hit the Metro Act first.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: We'll get a minute for that.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: In a second.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yep. I crossed that off while I was talking.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yeah, so we have an application from MCI Metro Access Transmission Services LLC, uh, to establish a right-of-way permit under the Metro Act. And, um, if you had a chance to take a look at, uh, the application, they provided all the information. This is, um, an application that's been vetted and approved by legal counsel, and they've provided all the information, uh, that we require, including, uh, where their lines are located or where they plan to place And that's Exhibit— the last page of that there. So this is one of the entities that has a little bit more in terms of miles in the township, but everything is in order, and staff recommends approval.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: And so, is there any construction that results from this?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Over time, as they seek to expand their network, they would look to— typically, they add on to where they already have lines existing.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Are these underground or above ground?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Usually underground. 99% of the time underground, yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Motion for support.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I support.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Supported. All in favor say aye. Aye. Any opposed? Now we're looking for the resolution to authorize issuance of refunding documents.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yes, as the COVID memo, uh, related to this agenda item indicates, uh, we were approached by Ottawa County who acts, Ottawa County Utilities, which is actually a subdivision of the Ottawa County Road Commission. And they represent us as well as other local units of government who purchase water from the City of Wyoming. And as the COVID memo indicates, there were some bonds that were issued back in 2007 to undertake capacity improvements at the City of Wyoming's treatment plant, and in 2015, the bond market was favorable in that they refunded those bonds to get a lower interest rate and had some savings back in 2015. As is noted there, those savings had a net present value at that time of 2015 of about 2.4 million. Bond counsel at the county, the county's financial advisor.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: The financial advisor says, you know, feels that the bond market is favorable again and that there may be an opportunity to realize some savings and is recommending that they proceed with another refund of these original 2007 bonds and then the 2015 issuance as well. Not quite the same savings are being projected as the COVID memo indicates there, but $880,000, you know, is still some considerable money. And so, they are recommending that we proceed with doing another refund, and that requires approval by all of the municipalities who are part of this bond issuance, including us. So, that's what's before you this evening. And in talking with Pat Staskiewicz from the— Ottawa County. County's utility department. Your authorization just provides the bond council with considering it. I mean, I think they're going to continue to monitor it. You know, the intent is to go out in January, but if market conditions change and they don't feel that they can realize the savings that they're estimating here, You know, they may pump the brakes on it, but if things hold, their intent is to proceed in January.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So in order to give them that opportunity, we need to pass this resolution, correct?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's supported. Anything supported? Mike, I'll do a roll call.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Sure. Mr. Bush?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Myself, yes.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Mr. Church, yes.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Mr. DeYoung, yes. Miss Malachy, yes. Mr. Tesla, yes. Okay, six in favor, zero opposed, one absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thank you, man. We got a consideration for approving the depositories for the fiscal year 2006. Send that to you, Vince.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah, this is just an annual thing that we do that we have the opportunity to find different investment options with some of these.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Organizations.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So, these are the banks that we use. Mainly, we use Charles Schwab, mainly, for most of the stuff, and WindTrust Bank, which is Macatawa Bank. So, Macatawa Bank is WindTrust. WindTrust isn't Macatawa, if that makes sense. So, I just need a motion. I'll make a motion to approve it.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I'll support it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And supported. All in favor say aye. Aye. Any opposed? Then we got the board meeting schedule for 2026. Had a chance to look at that executive committee. We looked at you know this coming year, and there's a few months where we scheduled just one board meeting. We canceled one this past a couple weeks ago. Just looking at basically when the volume of work.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I'd like to make one recommendation, is that the June 4th, that we remove it because it falls close to the heavy Memorial Day week. I'm proposing that we move it to later in June so the people can meet the meeting. So, I propose to modify this meeting schedule and removing the June 4th meeting.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: And only having one meeting a month?

_utterances.speaker: I

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: There was a reason. Did we have a reason?

_utterances.speaker: J

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, I think—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: let me—

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: we adopted at the second one, but I think the The main review of it is at the first meeting.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, I think so too.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: And we need to adopt it at the second meeting in June to set up the budget.

_utterances.speaker: I

_utterances.speaker: J

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: That's when we go through it in the whole CIP process, so we needed to have it, Vince.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay. All right. I'll just retract that then. I'll make a motion to make a motion to read.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All in favor say aye. Aye. Any opposed? Then, we get various appointments to a few different committees for terms that were going to expire by the end of the year. I sent these out in advance, but the Construction Board of Appeals. I don't know if they left last year at all, but we do need to have one in case there's questions from our building inspector that the developer would question them through the board for them to review. But Bob Balkan, Craig Brink, and Jack Harper have been on and said they are willing to continue.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Okay, I make a motion to reappoint them for 2-year terms. No, it's 2. Construction Board of Appeals is 2-year terms.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay, go ahead, go ahead. I'll support it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And it's supported. So all in favor say aye. Aye. Any opposed, same sign. Then we have the Zoning Board of Appeals. Robert DeVries is the only one that was going to expire at the end of this year. Elliot, you— he's on the board. You're on that board as well.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I like him a lot.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Oh yeah, just good input. Yeah, great. So looking for a motion to approve Robert DeVries.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I will move to approve.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Supported by Gretchen. All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Any opposed?

_utterances.speaker: A

Jf-2kUBtJJw.summary:
• **Citizen Comments**: Steve Willard raised persistent quality-of-life issues—illegal street parking and excessive vehicle noise (modified vehicles, loud mufflers)—citing safety hazards, emergency access obstruction, and lack of enforcement despite 12 calls to sheriff. Matt Furr voiced support for outdoor storm siren installation, emphasizing vulnerability of residents who miss digital alerts.
• **Pathway Maintenance Agreement**: Board approved updated intergovernmental agreement with Zeeland Charter Township for maintenance services (mowing, snow removal, tree trimming, minor repairs). Rate increased from ~$800 to higher amount; staff confirmed pricing reflects township's equipment and staffing to serve Zeeland's needs.
• **Outdoor Warning Sirens**: Board approved $47,000+ purchase of eight siren locations with coverage map. Single bid from Westshore Services (county monopoly provider); Dave DeYoung opposed lack of competing bids. System integrates with 911 dispatch for targeted activation by emergency location. Vote: 5 in favor, 2 opposed (DeYoung, Elliot).
• **Drain Maintenance (Drain 28)**: Joe Bush presented $47,000 resolution for maintenance work on Willowwood North drainage area (Eagle Crest Academy vicinity). Work needed because of high water table affecting condo units; requires expensive haul-out through residential backyards. Single bid from Volker Crane Services. Unanimously approved.
• **Financial Reports**: September building permits reached $10 million; sheriff calls up 1,550 (Aug); fire department 225 calls (Sept, mostly medical). State revenue sharing cut reduced from $300K to ~$100K. Building department returned $335,000 to general fund (post-recession recovery).
• **Administrative**: Parking lot renovation shifting to pavement removal phase; 120th entrance closed through October, using Garden Avenue access. Community grants awarded: $15K to Salvation Army for sidewalks, $20K+ to MRCA for roof repairs and kitchen training facility.

_utterances.text: Yep. So anyway, good evening, everyone. I'm here tonight to speak about two persistent issues affecting the safety and livability of our neighborhood: illegal parking. And excessive vehicle noise. These are separate problems, but both stem from a lack of consistent enforcement, and both have a real consequence for residents. First, parking violations. In our area, many residents are using public roadways as their personal permanent driveways. Vehicles are left parked for extended periods of time, sometimes days or weeks without movement. This practice not only violates township ordinance, but it creates safety concerns, especially during winter months. When snow falls, these vehicles obstruct the plowing operations, of course, especially reduce the visibility. You know, you got kids running around. There's no sidewalk toilet, by the way, and make it harder for emergency vehicles to service emergency vehicle services to navigate the residential streets. It's a hazard and is entirely preventable. I urge the township to enforce parking time limits and discourage long-term street parking or residential zones. Public roads are meant for transit, not for storage. Second one: excessive vehicle noise. The second issue is is chronic noise from modified or excessively loud vehicles, and I heard one just as I came in here. It's wah right by. These disturbances often occur late at night. Or during early morning hours, disrupting sleep and creating a hostile environment. I've submitted complaints and spoken with neighbors who share these concerns, but enforcement remains elusive. A deputy with the Ottawa County Sheriff's Office informed me that they cannot issue citations for loud vehicles because they lack decibel meters. This gap in enforcement not only allows a problem to persist, It emboldens those causing it. When violators know there's no consequences, the behavior escalates. I ask the township to explore solutions where through equipment acquisitions, ordinance updates, or even interagency coordination to ensure that noise violation can be addressed meaningfully. We do some tree and brush trimming. We'll do some minor repairs to components of their pathway system, you know, signs, potholes, things of that nature. I mean, when we talk about bridges, you know, we don't get into anything structural. If a particular plank has popped, You know, we'll come in and put that back in place and things of that nature. And then certainly we'll monitor the safety markings associated with the system as well. It's been through legal counsel, and Attorney Bolche is fine with it as well. It was just time to update things. So as I'm noting here, you know it has. The usual clauses in it regarding termination, regarding revisiting the rate that's being charged per mile, and so forth. _utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep. So as the current memo indicates, you know, there have been some discussion amongst us here, you know, after some weather events that occurred this summer. You know, fortunately, Holland Township largely was spared the worst brunt of those weather events that occurred earlier this year, but other communities not so much. Some of our neighbors sustained far greater damage, and of course that resulted in conversations in the community about the outdoor warning sirens. And even though we were spared the brunt of damage, I know I received several comments, questions, concerns from residents who realized that the township doesn't have outdoor warning sirens. And so staff was directed to inquire about this. That being, of course, being a safety issue, it was referred to Chief Kozel and the fire committee to look into this issue more, which they did and he did. And of course, we were not the only community doing that. Park Township was on a parallel path with us regarding that. So I know that Chief Kozel was talking to Chief Gamby about that as well. And Park Township, as well as, I believe, Laketown Township, as I was reminded, you know, did proceed with installation or have taken action to move towards installation of outdoor warning signs. Okay. Sirens, and so they were using the company West Shore Services, which is also the entity with the quote in front of you. They are really the provider of this equipment in Ottawa County. Of course, there's some benefits to that with one entity in the area taking care of numerous locations. That brings some uniformity and efficiency and cost savings when it comes time for. You know, the regular maintenance and other activities that come along with putting these in place. And so you'll see that a quote— Chief Kozel got a quote from Westshore, and they came back with what you see here: the placement of eight locations. And the last item in your packet was the coverage map of. Of where— when the sirens are located, where proposed. You know, I think Chief Kozel and the Fire Committee, when they looked at the proposal, you know, did acknowledge that we may do a little tweaking of these locations. We want to ensure that we're not duplicating area with our neighbors. So work with Chief Tibbetts in Zeeland, work with Chief Gamby, Park Township to make sure you know where they're placing them as well. But you know, in general, the locations, as you can see from the map, provide excellent coverage to essentially the entirety of Holland Township. And so that is what is quoted. One thing I do want to bring to your attention at the bottom of the first page of the quote in bold, that final paragraph talks about a cost to add the newly installed sirens to existing Ottawa County control points. And one thing that I learned when Chief Kozel was talking about this is this is not your mother's outdoor warning sirens. These are much more sophisticated. And by tying them in with those Ottawa County control points, dispatch can select a particular siren or sirens So, whereas in the past, when sirens went off, all of them in the entire jurisdiction sounded, now, if there's a particular incident in maybe the southeast portion of the township, only that siren would sound. If the danger is really only in one specific area, they can now be independently activated. As well as all of them going off all over, if if we include that. So this quote does take into account and provides for that coordination as well with central dispatch. So that was one interesting thing that I learned as well: that there may be an incident that doesn't require activation of the entire system. So just wanted to point that out as well. And as my cover memo indicates. You know, three of our four neighbors, so both cities as well as Park Township have now either had already installed them or are installing them. And so certainly there are coverage with these with this technology and and with this type of system all around us now.

Nut4f2RY20w.summary:
• Opened with Pledge of Allegiance and prayer; toured new fire truck acquired by township
• Citizen comments included superintendent of West Ottawa schools discussing bond renewal ($186M, zero increase) and county commissioner Jordan Jartsma providing state budget update (K-12 funding at $10,050 per pupil, road funding ~$1-1.5B, potential $376k cut to township revenue sharing)
• Sheriff's Office presented new organizational structure: Lieutenant Nick Knott introduced as new Watch Commander for District 3 (Holland, Park, Olive, Port Sheldon townships); 11 deputies assigned to Holland Township with discussion of need for additional personnel
• Approved motion (7-0) to award construction management services for public works building expansion/renovation to Lakewood Construction based on competitive bid analysis; project estimated $5.5-7M
• Administrative reports noted 6 simultaneous capital projects underway; Greg Mayberry consulting on 4 of them; discussed potential state revenue sharing cuts impacting township budget
• Adjourned meeting

highlights.quote: Both sides are pretty well set on a plan that would put the per-pupil funding at $10,050 per pupil...an increase...should put a lot of our school districts at ease.
The last analysis from the Michigan Township Association that I received indicated that the budget negotiations would result in a cut to revenue sharing for the township of about $376,000.
This is going to revolutionize the way that we police around here. This is something totally new for us. It's something that has been long overdue, I think.

In my opinion, we can never have enough...I would always appreciate a couple more...I have some ideas in my head on how I'd kind of like to break the township up into smaller parcels and patrol areas.
The township is growing and they are busting at the seams...expand the office area...new storage building on the open ground to the west.
If you do straight cost, Lakewood has the advantage just on apples to apples...gave Lakewood an advantage of $28,000 to $65,000.
We have six capital projects going simultaneously here. They're in different stages of the process...Greg is helping us out with 4 of them.

dave_segments.text: Yes.

tBML5b1Iw44.summary:
- **2026 Budget Work Session**: $16.8M general fund revenue (down $1M due to CDBG grant ending), millage rate held at 4.86 mills for 9th consecutive year, average homeowner levy $527 (up $9 from 2025)
- **Key Budget Items**: Fire department adding 3 full-time firefighters ($2.1M→$2.5M); code enforcement staffing increase; assessing department expansion to industry standards (+26%); community center transfer increased to $700k
- **Enterprise Funds**: Water purchase costs up 18.4% for 2026 due to third transmission main; sewer rates increasing 2.8%; rate study scheduled for 2026 to take effect 2027; $46M combined water/sewer fund balance projected to decline $1M
- **Capital Projects**: $1.5M parks transfer including $750k for Beechwood pickleball court reconstruction and $500k for Quincy Park improvements; no Building & Site capital projects scheduled for 2026
- **Board Concerns Raised**: Elliot questioned timing of water rate study (scheduled 2026, could happen sooner); questioned 22% fire budget increase with no corresponding police savings; asked about 18% water cost increases and adequacy of $46M fund balance
- **Public Comment**: Board member praised sound fiscal management and fund balances compared to other communities experiencing financial difficulties

-YAX5g4pQRw.summary:
- **Consent Agenda & Financials**: August disbursements of $3.9M approved, including $548K retainage release to GDK on community center construction (4 punch list items remaining), $373K for Helder Park parking lot resurfacing, and $390K to Apex for Hawthorne improvements
- **401(k) Plan Amendment**: Board unanimously approved (7-0) allowing participants aged 59½ to make annual in-service withdrawals of up to 90% of funds; discussion on township's 9.5% contribution being competitive with area municipalities
- **Zoning Ordinance Amendment**: First reading introduced for Butternut Drive property rezoning from C-2 commercial to R-2A residential; existing corner building will remain, new structure to be hidden behind berm and landscaping
- **Early Voting Agreement**: Approved multi-jurisdictional agreement between Holland and Park Townships to operate early voting site at community center; costs to be split based on registered voter percentage
- **Hawthorne Park O&M Agreement**: Unanimously approved operational/maintenance agreement mirroring 2008 existing arrangement; township responsible for park maintenance while county manages surrounding trailway
- **Administrative Updates**: 2026 budget review completed; sheriff seeking access to vacant commercial properties for canine unit training; broadband project underway with Holland Township as high priority

_utterances.text: Then item number 6 is consideration of the resolution pertaining to amending the 401 plan. Steve, you want to talk about that one a little bit?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: So the township provides a pension plan. We are a defined contribution organization, so we'll make a contribution into Every participant's account who is working at the township. The 401 is a plan; it's a tax-deferred plan generally under the IRS code for public sector organizations. Ours is managed; the plan administrator is a company out of Kalamazoo, and we're invested with John Hancock. So participants, employees seem to like it. But one thing they did request. Our plan currently does not allow for an in-service withdrawal from the plan until one of 3 conditions occurs: the participant reaches the age of 65, they separate from service, or they retire. And we've received requests from employees who are participants nearing retirement age that would like to make an in-service withdrawal from the plan. So what this resolution states then is to amend our 401 plan to allow a participant to make an in-service withdrawal at the age of 59 and a half, which is what's required by law. And if you had a chance to read the resolution, it would then allow a participant to make one withdrawal annually, and they can do so— they can withdraw up to 90% of their funds. They have to leave 10% of their holdings in the plan.

Fortunately, the existing Hawthorne Park also is a joint A venture between Ottawa County and Holland Township, and there was an agreement put in place in 2008, as the governmental memo indicates, for that park as well, or the improvements that were done at that time. So this mirrors that type of arrangement. _utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: F

And it's basically all the types of activities you would expect: you know taking care of debris and litter waste removal, cleaning of the restrooms and mowing and. So forth. So that's what set forth the agreement states that just like the existing Hawthorne Park, the township will be responsible for undertaking those duties, which puts us in the position of keeping this park at the same standards as all our other parks. I'm not saying Ottawa County Parks does not do a good job. I'm not saying that at all. But this does allow us to keep it. as the way and to the standard we expect our parks to be. So, again, it's been reviewed and, again, mirrors that 2008 agreement, and staff recommends your approval.

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Correct. So the exhibit on the last page shows the area for which we're responsible. And that's essentially that parcel that the town— the county purchased, upon which we are placing the improvements, have a license agreement that's in place already. Yes, that's a good point to make out, because obviously the Hawthorne Park improvements are part of the larger Middle Mac Trailway. So it goes on for another 2 miles beyond here. We have no obligations or responsibilities Related to the the trailway through the remainder of Holland Township and through Zeeland Township, that's a separate agreement. It's referenced in this, but it doesn't. We are not a party to that or have any obligations under that.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Good point.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any opposed? Same sign. Unanimous. Okay. Steve, some administrative updates.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, you know certainly. Earlier this evening, we had our 2026 budget review meeting. Certainly, if you have any questions that pop up between now and our next meeting, when we will be seeking— staff will be seeking approval of the resolution that is at the front there of your budget book, you know, certainly reach out and let us know. I thought we had a great discussion tonight regarding that and feel very comfortable With the budget that has been put together. And then secondly, we got kind of an interesting request from our sheriff. So the sheriff is looking for lightly used, abandoned, vacant commercial properties which he can use to train the canine unit. So to keep the dog sharp, they need to move, keep moving to different training areas. And so, what would happen is if a space is made available to the sheriff, then the trainers can bring the dogs in, hide contraband, other things that the dogs are to find. And he likes to move those around; otherwise, the dogs kind of get stale if they keep going back to the same locations. So, he put out an appeal: if you know of a vacant commercial property that you believe the property owner would be willing, you know, please let me know. Or let the sheriff know they're looking for some new spots for the canine unit. That's all I've got.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Is that for an extended period of time then, or are they looking for just a short?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: It would be a relatively short period of time because again, once the dogs get familiar with the site, then it's not very useful as a training location. So yeah, it's it's not a long-term commitment. That's all I've got.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Dave, any committee updates?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Information?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Pass.

_utterances.speaker: E

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: H

You know, here it is, December, and I don't think they've been here this year yet, either one of them. I think that's kind of sad, really. We used to have a regular. And, they don't have to sit here every meeting, or once in a quarter, once every 6 months. Months maybe. We just haven't seen them.

_utterances.speaker: A

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: A

And, we're like, towards the high, upper end of the priority project on that. So, there may be some work going on in our community, helping with broadband in the, over the next year or so. Good for business. I don't know if it really affects homeowners, but businesses anyway. That's all I have. I need a motion to adjourn.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion, Mr. Chairman.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All in favor, say aye.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right, thank you.

_Pi7eFnA6Uk.summary:
• **Housing Analysis Presentation**: McKenna Associates presented comprehensive housing demand/supply analysis for Holland Charter Township through 2040, projecting 3,000-6,000 new residents and comparing against comprehensive plan capacity of ~10,000 units. Key finding: township has sufficient zoning capacity; no need to expand allowed densities.
• **Migration Rate Debate**: Significant board discussion over methodology for calculating 6.3% net migration rate (2020-2023) versus historical 0.6% (2010-2020). Trustees Church and DeYoung questioned data integrity and whether projections align with actual population growth figures.
• **Affordability Findings**: Analysis identified mismatch between housing supply and demand across income spectrum—insufficient luxury housing causing middle-income bidding competition, and inadequate affordable units for lower-income residents. Recommended diversifying housing types rather than reducing lot sizes.
• **Planning Commission Input**: Randy Kortering reported Planning Commission approved the analysis (reviewed 3 times) and plans to focus on owner-occupied housing barriers. Commission will explore incentives for townhouses and examine lot size/setback modifications with developers, while monitoring rental market adequacy.
• **Zoning Amendment Approved**: Resolution 3385 rezoning 120th Avenue property from agriculture to R-2A (medium density residential) passed 7-0.
• **Drain Projects Approved**: Craig's Hall Drain (new orphan drain) and Sapphire Lake Drain improvements both approved unanimously to address flooding and maintenance issues.

highlights.quote: So if we have 40,000 people in this township, you're telling me that 6% every year of that 40,000? No, not 6% of the 40,000. 6% of the growth. 6% of the new people.

Your future land use map and your comprehensive plan right now do not need to be adjusted to add more housing. I would potentially caution against adjusting them to reduce the amount of allowable housing, but that's ultimately your decision.

The top end, the 4 or 5 bedroom, 3, 4 bathroom, has a swimming pool, has some acreage, that housing unit, there's actually not enough of them. And so folks are buying the houses that are out there, and those houses are no longer available for the folks that could afford that level.

highlights.topic: All Housing is Good Housing Philosophy
highlights.quote: You should not feel pressure as a board to reject housing because it's going to be expensive on day one. It's brand new housing. But adding housing to the market creates more opportunities for everyone all the way down the stream.

What we want to do is focus more on owner-occupied because that seems to be the biggest challenge. We're putting up enough apartments, but we want to understand why the property on 100 Forest near our place, right, Hunter's Creek, 2 years it's been for sale, still not being developed.
I doubt the integrity of that report. I think they've got some problems in their spreadsheet. It just— the numbers don't add up. They don't add up in a couple ways, at least a couple ways.

I sure would like to see what that migration looks like, those numbers look like in previous decades. I mean, is it zero? Is it less? Is it increasing or is it not? I mean, how do you make a prediction without having some more data?

Not the cost of construction that would prohibit a development of a Hunter's Creek property. Yes, it is overpriced. It's not on the market currently. We would purchase that property if the seller would sell it to us.

They'd actually like to sell, but the tax implications keep them from doing it while they're alive. The tax implications are significant. And so, as morbid as it is to say, oftentimes it's, I have no choice but to pass away, give it to my kids, and let them deal with it from that standpoint.
You are really pretty much right on track... slightly ahead of the game in terms of how much housing you've been building on the rental side. And the homeownership side is basically right on track.

dave_segments.text: But if we have 40,000 people in this township, right, which I think we just crossed that bridge a little bit ago, you're telling me that 6— we're gaining 6% every year of that 40,000?

So if we have, and let's just say for ease of numbers, 300 people move in in a year, because that's kind of what we're saying through 2020 to '23, right? We've got 1,000 people more in that 3-year period. Is that about right?
Is it fair to say that 6.3% of those 300 are new people coming in?
Do you have any data before 2010 at all so we have one more decade to compare?
I'm very curious about that. Just, is it— was it 12% then?
Is that piece overpriced?
I thought one of the most telling bar graphs was the one with the supply and demand at 250 units, and we're staying right at pace, right?

That's how I feel about it. But I also think, what I was saying, is I sure would like to see what that migration looks like, those numbers look like in previous decades. I mean, is it zero? Is it less? Is it increasing or is it not? I mean, how do you make a prediction without having some more data?
It seems to me like we're doing well.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Thank you, board members. I'm happy to be here tonight to present the results of the housing analysis that we've conducted along with staff and the Planning Commission, as well as the discuss the recommendations that have come out of it with regard to the comprehensive plan, which are fairly simple, but we'll talk about it. Steve, are you going to advance the slides or—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, already.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Or is there a thing?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: On the side.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Her memoirs.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: She's writing a book.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Really?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: It's been a juicy one.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: So why was this analysis conducted? There's a number of different reasons. But as you know, the housing market is changing fast. And the housing market and your comprehensive plan go hand in hand. Because, as you know, that housing demand that you are confronted with constantly, where is it going to be built and what is it going to look like? That's governed by that comprehensive plan. A few specific items. We do have a comparison of rental versus homeownership in this analysis, both on the demand side and the supply side. We do project out into the future. You know, the lifetime lifespan of the comprehensive plan is is supposed to be twenty years. Obviously, it gets updated every five, but it's supposed to look out twenty years. So we're looking out twenty years as far as demand goes. We are also looking at affordability. You have a very interesting, if not uncommon, but very interesting affordability challenge here, and I'll get into that. And then we, as you. Probably know, back in 2022 we did something very similar to this, and so this is an update to that analysis. And so there are some comparisons to what we looked at back then. So what exactly did we do? What's our methodology here? We defined a housing market trade area, and I'll get into the details of that in a minute. But what is the purpose of that? Purpose of that is that when someone is looking for a home in the greater Holland area. They're not just going to look in Holland Charter Township. They probably even won't understand what the borders of Holland Charter Township are. They may have some idea of where the school districts are, but they're not going to stop at township borders. What they're going to look for is the house or condo or apartment or whatever that fits their needs, that is within a reasonable commute of their job. So we try to define what someone who's looking for housing here, what is the actual geographic area that they're looking in, and we compare that.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Those are the trends in that geography as well as those within the actual township. We also look at both supply and demand. And notes under there that we look at affordability as well as total demand, ownership demand, rental demand, and we'll talk about how we calculate that. We project that out into the future. And then that last one, comprehensive plan, we did also do a build-out analysis of the comprehensive plan, which is to say if every single thing that it's So what it says on the future land use map that you want to get built gets built. How much housing is that? And how does that compare to what future demand is?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Next.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: There. As I mentioned, the trade area. So this is defining that region in which someone who may find housing here in this community might— was probably looking for housing. We usually define that by commute time, and that is the logic of that is that this is the region within which people could move without dramatically changing their commute commute to work in terms of how long it takes. So what does that look like on a map? That orange blob is the trade area. As an interesting comparison to when we did this a couple years back, your commute time has not changed. Your average commute time is 17.3 minutes. That's the same as it was in 2022, which is kind of interesting. However, 17.3 minutes doesn't get you as far as it did in 2022 per this software. It's not a dramatic change. It's probably a difference of less than half a mile in any of those little appendages that come off down the roads. But it does mean that, unfortunately, direct comparisons between the 2022 version and this version of the analysis at the trade area level don't really work because this one is just slightly smaller. But I guess that does mean, hey, traffic is a little heavier as we have growth. 17-minute drive doesn't get you quite as far as it did I've seen it before. First sort of input to this analysis is population growth, right? We don't do just a linear analysis, but obviously what we're seeing in population in the past, or leading up to today, is very relevant. You see 2010, 2020, 2023 up there, '23 being the most recent year we have census estimates for, for the township, and the change. You guys obviously are a fast-growing community. Interestingly, the trade area is actually growing a little bit faster, not dramatically so, but a little bit faster overall. And then that last number is you plus the rest of the trade area. So we're talking 10 to 12% growth throughout. And, and we have no reason to believe that trend is going to slow down, though exactly what that growth looks like, we're we're gonna talk about. And that is the component of growth that is migration, right? As you know, population grows because people have kids and population grows because people move in. And one thing that we do with these analyses is try to figure out what proportion of your growth is coming from people moving in and what is the sort of ratio between people moving in and people moving out. From 2010 to 2020, Your net migration ratio, which is the, um, basically the difference between the percentage of people that moved in and the percentage of people moved out, was 0.6%. So you had a 0.6% more population in 2020 than you would have had if nobody had moved in or out and everyone had just had kids and then passed away as they did naturally, right? So that's the comparison that we make. Your population was 0.6% higher than than it would've been if nobody moved in or out. And then from 2020 to 2023, the pandemic and its aftermath, you were 6.3% higher than you would've been had no one moved in or out during that period. That's a pretty big jump. Now, we don't know which of those trends is gonna continue.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We don't—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: maybe it'll be somewhere in the middle. We don't know. We know that there was this influx of people. We know that in the previous decade, like much of Michigan, there wasn't a whole lot of moving in because, again, the first half of that decade was the recession, right? So looking forward, to be safe, we analyzed both of these numbers. So what you're going to see from this point forward in the analysis when we look at projecting demand forward is the high migration scenario and the low migration scenario. And so that way we'll have some preparation for what happens. Regardless of what happens. Now, what does that give us? We put these numbers into Hopper. By 2040, you are looking— again, there are ranges. The bottom of the range is the low migration scenario. The top of the range is the high migration scenario. So you could end up falling somewhere in that range. You're looking at 3,000 to 6,000 new residents in the next 15 years. So that'll take you to 42,000 to 45,000. And then the trade area around Holland around Charter Township you, that greater Holland area, is looking at 10,000 to 25,000 new residents. So that is growth. Now, switching gears to supply for a minute.

All right, so your current— uh, a special thanks at this point to Corey and Kate, especially Kate, who did quite a bit of work in pulling building permits that the township has issued over the last several years so we can get a really good understanding of where you're at with your housing supply. We have found that census estimates, uh, in the last several years on number of housing units in a community are To be frank, way off. They are simply not keeping pace. They did the census headcount in 2020, and then as you probably are aware, they do surveys every year to get estimates. They are not headcounts though, and so they do have that methodological flaw potentially. And in this community and several others that we've worked in, they're off. They say things like you've lost 200 housing units in 3 years. Not clearly not accurate. So we were able to get, thank you to your staff, local data. We now know how many housing units have been built since that census headcount in 2020, which we think we can trust. So what's great about that is we get really granular data. You can see that the number of housing units you had in 2023 was 15,161. Two years on, we're 15,729. So you are building, you know, almost 250 housing units per year going back to 2019. Prior to that, going back to 2010, you were building only 95 a year. So you have jumped in terms of how much housing is being built. That is also true in the broader trade area, though not as dramatically, right? They went from about 560 to almost 760. So that's an increase, but it's not a, you know, 2.5 times increase the way you have seen A housing unit can be defined as anything livable? Yes, it's anything that is legally a dwelling unit, right? So it has to meet the building code and zoning ordinance definitions of a dwelling unit, which means it has to have a kitchen, it has to have a bathroom, it has to have a sufficiently sized bedroom, all of that. That's a dwelling unit. So, yeah, it could be a— for the purpose of this analysis, we break it down into single-family homes, Duplexes, townhouses, and then we have a small multifamily and a large multifamily category. And maybe the next slide. Yeah, the next slide. So we break this down by type of housing unit here. And this is your current supply. I would caution— and we talked to the Planning Commission, we talked about this as well— I would caution you not to translate single-family as homeowner.

If you have a population growth of 1,063 people, the 63 are the people who moved in. That's the 6.3%. Did I do the math right there? So without—

It means, yeah, it means that if nobody had moved in and out of the township, You would have had 1,000 people of growth, just kids being born, right? But you didn't, you had 1,063. That's not the actual number, I'm just trying to make an easy math for myself. So the plus 6.3% is the 63 extra people who moved in. It's not 6.3% growth, it's increasing your growth by that much.

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: F

Each of those age cohorts has what we call a headship rate and a homeownership rate. Homeownership rate is pretty self-explanatory. It's the rate at which folks in that age group statewide— and we use the statewide group because we're trying to get at what people want, right? Otherwise, it's just circular. We just say in Holland Township, X percent of 20-somethings own— well, we already knew that from the ownership data on the supply side.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: We want to know how many 20-somethings want to own, whether they have successfully managed to do that or not, how many want to. So we take the statewide number so we try to get a little more of an average. Now, the headship rate, to explain what that is, that is the rate at which people in an age cohort are the head of a household. So basically, if you have 4 23-year-olds living together, that's a 25% headship rate. Because any one of them could be the head, but only one is, right? And then if one of them moves out on their own to their own place, now they're at 100%. Then they get married, now they're at 50%, right? And so throughout life, we see, as noted here, the headship rate for 20-somethings goes from about 39%. It peaks in people's 50s at 58%.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: And then starts to go down as folks move back in with their kids and stuff like that. So why do we need that number? That number is how we convert from population to number of needed housing units. So it's a very important percentage. It is a national percentage because once again, we are looking for what people want, not what they are doing. That person with that 23-year-old with 3 roommates, they may not want roommates. They may desperately want their own place. They don't have it right now, but they want it. So we are putting in the demand model that there's a housing unit out there that is needed, if that makes sense. So that's how we estimate demand. Then we project it out in the future. This gives a little example about how that works. 30 to 39-year-olds, just picked an age cohort. You have 4,896 of them in the township per census numbers. Their headship rate is 53— 54.3%.
