Randy's sitting here, and he— they need to know what our feeling is about this and what growth level we want to look at.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, that is exactly right. Because in a second here— yep, there it is. So this summarizes what those graphs show in numbers, which is to say demand for housing in the township is going to increase by 3,000 to 5,000 new owner-occupied units And 800 to 1,200 new rental units by 2040. This is— to that point, this is your comprehensive plan as it stands right now. We analyzed, again, with the help of your planning staff, your existing built fabric and available development sites, then compared them to your future land use map. and the density and type of housing that it calls for on those sites that are still available to build on. And I do want to make it very clear that these numbers are the maximum, right? A developer will come in— when a developer comes in, sometimes they will hit that absolute maximum that you say is allowed. You know, a lot of times they don't. But in order to understand Where we're at on a geographic basis relative to the numbers, we have to put a cap on it. So this is the maximum. But that is important because your comprehensive plan, as I said, very clearly lays out the housing density on these undevelopable— undeveloped, excuse me, parcels. Which is to say, your low-density residential category, you have 1,100 acres of available land.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: master plan for that. That's 5 units per acre maximum density. We take 80% of the available acreage because you have to build roads and stormwater management and other things of that nature. So you really— a developer isn't going to build on all of the land. Take that number, you multiply it by 5, you get 4,400 housing units. So that's 4,400 single-family homes at 5 per acre, right? 1/5 of an acre lots, approximately.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Medium density residential. That's going to be your townhouses, your duplexes, maybe your small apartment buildings. For that, we have 210 acres. 80% is 168, but that's 10 units per acre. You can build higher density there. So that's 1,600, almost 1,700 housing units. High density. That's your apartment buildings or condo buildings, but regardless, they're high density. They're up to 15 units per acre, and that's 1,800. Housing units. And then mixed use, you know, that's like the mall redevelopment or things of that nature. You know, that can go up to very high densities. That's about 2,200. What that gives you is about 10,000 housing units that if everything the comprehensive plan says is appropriate to build gets built, you've got 10,000 more housing units to build. That puts you at about 25,000, almost 26,000 total housing units.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Units compared— just by adding that number to what we have today. Now, we compare that to what we projected for demand. We projected that by 2030, we're going to need 17,000 to 18,500 in terms of total housing units. That's what's going to be demanded. And you're going to have a capacity of almost 26,000. So there's plenty of capacity.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Potentially, you could argue there's too much. I don't— I would not normally say 65 to 70% of capacity used is, okay, we need to dial back what we're allowing. Again, developers don't always build everything. It takes time, et cetera. But you can very clearly say, we don't need to add more land or more density at this point compared to what we have already laid out on the future land use map. I think that's very clear.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: By 2040, you're going to get a little closer, 70 to 80%. You're still well under that 26,000, at which point it's now 2040 and we can reevaluate.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Who knows what we'll be living in then?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yes, who knows what the world will look like at that point.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: We can reevaluate.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: But I think that that's why our number one takeaway from this is that your future land use map and your comprehensive plan right now Do not need to be adjusted to add more housing. I would potentially caution against adjusting them to reduce the amount of allowable housing, but that's ultimately your decision. But we are certainly recommending that we do not need to add more. I will say this, and I said this to Planning Commission: As you well know, probably too well know, when development comes in. You will likely be pressured by the developer to allow more housing or more of whatever they want to build than your comprehensive plan says is appropriate. And you may also be pressured by neighbors or other opposition to approving it at all, or ask you to reduce down below what your comprehensive plan says is appropriate. Our recommendation is to follow your comprehensive plan, which was thoughtfully put together with concerns like infrastructure in mind, with The character of the township in mind, with the culture of the township in mind. It was thoughtfully put together, and when land was designated for a certain type of development, that was not done randomly. So it should be followed. That's our contention. Now, as many of you are probably aware, HousingNext did just complete a study for the full Ottawa County. So obviously our study was your township with comparisons to that trade area based on where people commute—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: can commute to.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: And this is for the full county. So it's not necessarily an apples-to-apples comparison as far as geography, but obviously you are a portion of Ottawa County, so this study does apply to you. They say there were 5,700 housing units built in Ottawa County over the last 5 years, and about 14% of that was built here.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Excuse me.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: As of right now, you have 13% of the county's housing stock. So 14% is right around on target as far as your share of housing being built in the county. So I thought that was an interesting finding because it does mean that you are not sort of over— being over-relied upon to provide housing in the county. But you're also not falling— you're also not not doing your share, quote unquote. You are basically right on as far as your proportion of the new housing is approximately equal to Excuse me.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: You said earlier that you didn't agree with Housing Next's numbers.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: This is the one that we really— we came up with a different number. This is where we came up with a different— this, yes, this I— well, this is just simply data. This is simply building permits. There are 5,700 housing units that have been issued a building permit and constructed. This is not—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, this is not analysis. I'm also, just to make it clear, I'm not criticizing their methodology or saying they're wrong or really criticizing them in any way. I'm just saying the numbers we got and the numbers they got on this particular issue on this slide is different. They say the county as of today has a housing shortage of over 16,000 housing units. We found that you have a housing shortage of basically zero. Those 2 numbers don't jive. And—

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: We—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: our projection is that because we're going to have population growth and people want to live in Ottawa County and in the Holland area, you're going to have to keep building housing to keep up with that. But as of right this minute, the number of people looking for housing and the number of housing units are roughly the same. They're saying it's 16,000 short countywide. I'm not saying ours is right and theirs is wrong. Maybe the other way around. Those 2 numbers don't jive. So I did want to point that out.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: If it is true, somehow, that there's a shortage of 16,000 housing units countywide, but you guys are at parity, that's going to drive people to move here. Because other people— other places are 16,000 housing units short. People are looking for housing in, say, Allendale. They're not going to be able to find it because it doesn't exist, and they're going to come here. So I don't know that that's a real conclusion because it's really 2 different methodologies coming up with 2 different numbers, and so I think that as policymakers, you should just kind of put them in a hat and think about how you want to address that. But anyway, I did want to be frank about that number being different than ours. County housing demand by tenure. This is a calculation. As you see, we're in that 75-25-ish range, just like our number. So this one we are consistent with them on, and this feels quote unquote, truthy to me because this is where we get a lot. Most communities are between 70/30 and 75/25 as far as how many people want to own versus rent. So I think this is right, this seems right on track with what we've seen. It looks like it. They also see that affordability bulge in the middle. They also see a cluster of housing prices in the middle. As was pointed out, there may be a cultural component to that here in Ottawa County. But also, we see it all over the place. Almost everywhere has— housing is all priced in this range and people's incomes are like this. And if you're here, you can't afford anything. And if you're here, you're saving a whole bunch of money, but, you know, you maybe really wish you had, you know, a nicer house. So the opportunities for new construction, you know, luxury homes are out there. Uh, their housing study recommends that communities reevaluate their minimum lot sizes in order to accommodate the housing growth that is needed. We are not supportive of that recommendation. As I mentioned a couple minutes ago, our recommendation is that your comprehensive plan is on target and should be followed. So when you say we want 5 units per acre for single-family, we do not see a reason to increase that to 6, 7, 8 units per acre.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: It is in their report. It's not something that our report would suggest. Doesn't seem necessary to us.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: What would—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: what I would say in response to something like that is that you guys, as noted in that slide a couple minutes ago, your plan has a wide variety of housing, right? You got about 4,000 single-family units planned for, and then you got about 1,800 townhouse-type duplex types Then you have about 1,800 multifamily, and then you have about 2,000 mixed-use. So you've got a nice variety planned for. If developers follow that plan, your planning commission, and you guys follow the plan, you're going to get a nice variety of housing. You're not going to have to create smaller lots or something like that to do it. That's our recommendation. To sum up, we don't really know if this migration pattern is going to continue. I think To Trustee Malachi's point, you know, the recession was a long time ago and the data that comes from that is probably not relevant today. But to Trustee Church's point, we don't know that that top-end scenario is gonna continue and something that's more of a halfway point is probably more likely. Housing demand is growing for homeownership in particular.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Rental demand is also growing. Everything's growing as we move forward in time. As of right now, as noted, the amount of housing being built in this township is keeping pace with supply. It just has to keep doing that for 15 years. We, as I noted, our recommendation here is that while this analysis and any revisions that we make to it Yes. So, the supplemental plan, such as adding the 2000 data or something like that, or a more robust description of how the migration rate is calculated, is ultimately intended to be an addendum to your Master Plan. So, it would be adopted as that, and we will incorporate it into the book, the document that you have, which we obviously created several years ago. But we are not recommending any substantive policy changes to the Master Plan, to the Comprehensive It is, in our opinion, sufficient at this point. There were a couple points made about infrastructure tonight, and we would agree with that. The Comprehensive Plan takes infrastructure into account, but it also does assume that there's some level of infrastructure improvement and growth. So, water and sewer capacity, there are sewer lines and water lines that may have to be expanded. There are roads, you know, I know the MAX just went through a whole realignment of their transit service. That's probably something they're going to have to do on a regular basis to keep tabs with where housing's being built. All of that kind of stuff. Schools are going to have to keep pace with this kind of growth. Nice thing for schools is every time a kid goes there, they get more money. So schools love these reports, love the reports like Housing Next that say, like, hey, we need all this housing. They multiply everything by 10,000 and add it to their budget.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: But anyway, the third point I think is important. All housing is good housing. You may get advocacy from this podium in any number of directions when a housing development comes in, but one thing you may— you probably do hear is, hang on, is this brand new housing going to be affordable? And the answer is probably not. It's brand new housing. But adding housing to the market creates more opportunities for everyone all the way down the stream. That doesn't mean that we should ignore the lowest income. That doesn't mean that we should shunt aside those that need, you know, developer side or resident side subsidies. But it— you should not feel pressure as a board to reject housing because it's going to be expensive on day one. It's brand new housing. It costs a lot to build. Developers are going to have to charge the rents they have to charge. But by building it, the existing supply— again, Economics 101— supply goes up, prices, at least for housing, they don't usually go down, but the rate of increase decreases, right? So they don't go up as fast. And then finally, monitoring the market. We are very grateful to this township for having asked us to do this report. And having asked us to do it previously, we'd be very happy to do it again at some point. Even if you don't choose us, we'd recommend that you continue to get this kind of analysis done every few years so that you know what's happening out there in the market. Things may change, and you may want to adjust your comprehensive plan accordingly, even though at this point we're not necessarily recommending that. What happens next? As I mentioned, this will be inserted as a chapter into the comprehensive plan with any revisions asked for by the board. And then that will be taken through the comprehensive plan amendment process, which begins with the Planning Commission, includes a comment period, includes a public hearing, comes to you guys for final adoption. My understanding is Corey intends for that to happen through the fall, and we're here to support that process as needed. Yes, ma'am.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Can I just ask a quick question?

_utterances.speaker: G

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yes, so those count for our purposes as large multifamily, and they do count. I believe there is some discussion in the report about senior housing. You know, and our conclusion there is that senior housing is going to continue to be needed. Because of the way demographics work and where we're getting to as far as how old the baby boom generation is getting to, we are going to see probably a surge in senior housing demand over the next few years, and then it's going to level out pretty steadily and then actually decline. So there's that. If you want to look out for what's going to happen in sort of the medium to long term, you may end up with a, all right, we need senior housing, we build senior housing, We're happy to have senior housing, and now it's the 2040s, and what are we going to do with all the senior housing? We don't have as many seniors anymore. I wouldn't say that's a reason to reject senior housing at this point, but that's the trend that it's on.

_utterances.speaker: H

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: C

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Because we have people turning 18 or 22 or whatever age they are when they want their first home, right? So there are— it is more than a linear projection. And one of the aspects leading to additional housing Right. In addition to the population not just growing linearly but growing faster than that is that we are seeing a— you guys have a relatively young population that is going to increase in headship rate over the next 15 years. I make a lot of— I talk about this a lot, but you have 20- and 30-somethings that are going to become 40 and 50-somethings, and they're going to move out of that, you know, 4-roommate situation, and they're all going to have their own housing unit. Um, and that is then in addition to the people that are moving here, and addition, you know, so it's, it's, it's all coming together. And this is consistent with why we have a housing supply problem right now, even though the U.S. had its The lowest population growth at a decennial census in like 150 years percentage-wise at the 2020 census. How does that jive? It jives because household sizes are going down. People are having fewer kids. Seniors are moving in with their children less. 20-somethings are having fewer roommates. So there is a— the headship numbers are going up.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F

They're doing some trial runs.

They'd actually like to sell, but the tax implications keep them from doing it while they're alive. And so, as morbid as it is to say, oftentimes it's, I have no choice but to pass away, give it to my kids, and let them deal with it from that standpoint. So that's another barrier that we run into a lot when we have a ready, willing, and able seller, ready, willing, and able buyer, but we have that as our roadblock. And is that 2 years? _utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So my interpretation is slightly different than yours because of— it's not a one-for-one ratio. So, appreciate the questions.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: But yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I want to say about, you know, math, what did they say? Math doesn't lie, but liars use math or something like that.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: And I don't presume to understand actuarial data. That's the part that—

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Aside from the report, which was rather I'm not going to go into a lot of detail, and obviously there's some problems with it perhaps, but I've always had a problem with the numbers that Housing Next gives us, or that are projected by— Steve, you know who I'm talking about. Thousands and thousands of units we need, and it never seems to end. We keep building, and then they come back with another report that says another 10,000 are needed. I'm going, really? Give me a break.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I thought one of the The most telling bar graphs was the one with the supply and demand at 250 units, and we're staying right at pace, right? Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: In our township, it's good.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: That's how I feel about it. But I also think, what I was saying, is I sure would like to see what that migration looks like, those numbers look like in previous decades. I mean, is it zero? Is it less? Is it increasing or is it not? I mean, how do you make a prediction without having some more data?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I don't know.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: If we had a crystal ball, then we'd all be in really good shape.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: It seems to me like we're doing well.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: That's what I have.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Keep looking at that report, and if you have questions or things that we do need to follow up on, I'll gladly do that, as well as work with Randy on their perspective on what they're looking at as far as the master plan, and where they see maybe adjusting some things, if it makes sense to the current state in our economic area. So, that's all I have. Need a motion to adjourn?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So moved, Mr. Chairman.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Secretary, all in favor say aye.

VBV9N22t67U.summary:
- **Eagle Meadows Condominiums (PUD)**: Final approval for 32-unit condominium project on Cherry Street. Planning Commission unanimous approval. Units range $375,000-$475,000. Motion carried 6-0-1 with concerns addressed regarding drainage improvements and dust control.
- **Orphan Drains Discussion**: Joe Milanowski presented three problematic orphan/aging drain systems (Craig's Pond/Felt Street, Sapphire Lakes leaching systems, Pilgrim drain) requiring maintenance petitions and county drain establishment. To be brought to board next month.
- **2025 Pathway Repair**: Approved competitive bid from Site Works Solutions for 3.7 miles of pathway reconstruction at $582,480 ($641,000 with 10% contingency). Vote 6-0-1.
- **Township Hall Site Improvements**: Approved parking expansion and bus shelter project with bids totaling $814,394 including contingency. Vote 5-1-1 (DeYoung opposed).
- **Consent Agenda & Financial Reports**: July building permits strong at $13M+ construction value; fire department 247 calls; no budget amendments needed; June 30 financial report on track.
- **Other Approvals**: Code ordinance amendment second reading passed 6-0-1; zoning map amendment for 128th Street (R-2A) introduced first reading; poverty exemption guidelines updated.

_utterances.speaker: E

Within the past week, of course, we're not flooding now. And he says, "Hey, why it's dry? Maybe we can look into doing something in this location." Joe, do you have any input? And I said, "You know, this is an orphan drain that is on our is on our master list of the township wanting to establish as a county drain. So it's already on the list." I said, "Well, let me go meet you out there." I met with Jerry Omen, the road commission today, and Lucas Timmer at four o'clock, and the property owner. And the property owners that own— property owner, which is Cal Weiner's son Kelly, own these 4, which are quite a few units. They flood right here in that cul-de-sac. And the drain actually runs down the center of the road, and there's a catch basin here, and it shoots out that way to drain number 40. There's nothing to capture all the water, particularly over here in the road right-of-way. The cul-de-sac is, you know, it's got a crown in the middle of it, so the water doesn't make it to that catch basin. Being an orphan, I thought it'd be a good time to evaluate that one for those residents because they always, when it rains hard, like over an inch, which we haven't seen a lot of rain this year, but typically in the spring and the fall there's some flooding issues. And he's like, you know, maybe it'd be a good time to revisit this. And I said, hey, good timing because it's on our orphan drains list. So that's one of them that would be, you know, coming before the board. Um, my, my thinking when I was out here today— and I've been here twice— but today I was thinking, you know, they're willing to spend money in their own private area here to repave some of this, to shape it, to get some of that water to sheet flow to some sort of a catch basin to capture here. And then we'll just tie in, you know, dig a channel Through the pavement, cut out, do an open cut through here. Road Commission said they had no problem with it. Tie into that catch basin and then let it go down the drain. And then that water could escape. It gets, right now it just kind of, it just kind of hangs here. And the Road Commission agreed today, he's like, yeah, it's not going to get to that catch basin. We have to figure out something on this side of the cul-de-sac. If anybody drives through there, you'll see it when you drive around there. So, that's the first one. So, that one is, again, an orphan. It would be established as a county drain for the BOD and all the legal process. But, we would need a petition from the township first.

And then, several years before me, anything that was proposed, new development, would be a county drain moving forward. So, everything you guys have approved since I've been elected, since 2013, has been a county drain. Unless the developer says, I don't want it to be that, it's all private, it's all an association. Okay. There's guidelines in there too for the association and the bylaws and the restricted deeds and covenants of that association to take care of their own drainage at their own cost. Typically, sometimes that does come back to the township 20 years later and going, hey, you know, they don't want to take care of it because it's maintenance and they don't know how to do it. So they would rather have it be a county drain because then they know who to call when the politics on associations change hands. And things sometimes don't go quite right. And infrastructure fails. That's what happens when infrastructure ages, right, and needs replaced. Well, this is kind of— that's what that orphan drain is there.

Most likely, we don't get very, very few of you know they don't want it to become a county drain. There's very few of those, but it is so detailed in the restricted deeds and covenants that it protects the township and the county for liability, and they'd have to petition and go through all that. They know that, but typically. When it's recorded, we let the property owners and developer do what they so choose.

I like to have some sort of a jurisdiction over that pond because that is the flooding mechanism that's going to be the most impactful. That pond doesn't have maintenance or the outlaw, I mean the outfall. That water has to go somewhere, usually in a county drain. I like to at least get some kind of some kind of a ownership of at least the outlet, so at least I can give them provide them relief on those flooding situations because nobody's flooded until they're flooded, right? And nobody thinks it's going to happen to them. But if you just look around the country of all the rain events this year that we've missed, you know, Texas, Kentucky, Tennessee, North Carolina, Iowa, even Chicago, the west suburbs not too long ago. Even in Detroit just a couple weeks ago. You get these intense 5-inch rains in 2 hours. You're going to hope you have good drainage. So, but yeah, you're right. We try to push people, not push, that's not the right word. I don't want to push anybody, but try to say, hey, this is really the smart way to go. But, we're— I'm the big property rights guy, and they can do whatever they want on their property and develop it how they see fit, according to ordinances, zoning, and drain codes.

So that really buoys us in terms of year-to-date, certainly compared to last year. We're doing very well. So that's good to see. If you had a chance to take a look at some of the activity, you know, some work out on Bay Park Drive there at $1.4 million by Yard Games LLC, and then also some multifamily housing going in On Westwood Place as well, by Lakewood— or Lakeview Holland LLC as well. So, yeah, very pleased. I mean, you know, building department had said things had softened a bit, and we did have a few slower months, but a great July. And then also was the July activity report from the fire department. So, another busy month for them with 247 calls. Again, 70% of those being the medical response, but a smattering of others as well. So again, you know, kind of trending towards, you know, doubling of what we've seen in previous years in terms of activity.

_utterances.speaker: C

So we do striping across driveways and so forth on our 70-mile-plus pathway system. That came in at $34,000, and then that allocation for, uh, to the Outdoor Discovery Center for Project Clarity in the amount of $50,000, uh, posted, uh, this past month. Uh, so those are a couple of the larger ones. Uh, and then of course the, um, law enforcement, uh, monthly allocation of roughly $189,000. Uh, under Fund 213, um, we're of course wrapping up some of the, um, final expenditures non-related to The construction project, and I'll talk a little bit about that in my administrator's report as well. But had an allocation there of $14,736 for some refrigerators and freezers and ice makers that went in as we kind of wrapped up the the final equipment purchases for the community center. _utterances.text: Any opposed, same sign. Thank you. Then we're looking at the second reading of consideration for the amendment to the Code Ordinance. So that was a few verbal—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: actually, that was a missing page, correct? A couple missing sections.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, that was caught and needed to be updated. So for a motion to—

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Now support. And Mike, you want to do a roll call on that one?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yep. Mr. Tesla? Yep. Miss Malatki? Yes. Mr. DeYoung? Yes. Mr. Church? Yes. Mr. Nienhuis? Yes. Myself? Yes. So we have 6 in favor, 0 opposed, 1 absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We're looking for an introduction to the first reading for the zoning ordinance on map amendment 3385 at 128th, changing from A to R-2A. I think this one's been It's been on hold for quite a while, but now, finally, came to us.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, you know, I drove by it a couple of times, and it seems like kind of a small parcel, but whatever. You know, whatever works. I'm sure the developer knows that. It does fit in well with the neighbors, or the rest of the zoning is right there. Fire departments are multi-use.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Multi-units. But anyway, I think it goes back more than a year because I think I was on the Planning Commission when it initially came up. So I thought that was sort of—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: why did it—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: does anybody know why it went on in a holding pattern?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Is that part of your— yeah, that's it. Thomas asked for it to go on hold.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'm here to kill 2 birds with one stone tonight. So, um, so yeah, we, we originally presented it, I think, at the end of last year. December, uh, rings a bell. Originally presented it as an R-3 that we actually voluntarily withdrew because we weren't able to do it based on the single-family homes. So, we resubmitted at the R-2A, received Planning Commission approval earlier this year, and then just truly scheduling conflicts on getting back in here for this meeting. So, that's why it got postponed a couple times, and then, yeah, had to be here anyway. So—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay. Here we are.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: So what is the size then of that parcel?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Um, it's 4 acres.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Terry, were you introducing the first reading?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I would certainly like to. Yeah, great.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Then we can continue the discussion. I didn't know which part of that time period it happened then, so no problem. Did you get your answer? Yeah. Oh yeah.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: All right, thanks.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Is it consistent with the master plan?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yes. Yep. Yeah, look at the map there. It's going to fit in with the area across the street. Then we got the resolution pertaining to the adoption of poverty exemption Guidelines. Have that packet in front of you. That's just updating the dollar amounts. They get adjusted, I don't know if it's every year or every other year, something like that, from Tyler. So, that's the new formula that we need to submit to the— I believe it goes to the state. Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Do we have any choice in the matter?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Is that mandated by the state that we submit some type of level?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: And that the—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: is the level itself mandated? It's the commonly accepted.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, it's commonly accepted. There's some guideline, the federal poverty guidelines. Federal guideline.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We're simply complying with that.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, on the second page it just says that Required local governing bodies have to adopt a manual, guidelines.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So, looking for a motion to have those accepted.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'll make a motion to accept those.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Second that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: And, seconded, Elliot. So, all in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Any opposed, same sign. All right, thank you, Dennis. We've got the financial report from June 30th that's put together in the packet.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yep, just a couple highlights there from Finance Director Smith's cover memo. You know, first of all, everything is tracking to date, so there are no amendments being proposed for any of the various funds. As his cover memo indicates, in the general fund, You know, we have about 99.5% of tax revenues collected at this time, which equates to about $9.1 million. And you can take a look at some of those numbers on page 7. Likewise, our state shared revenues, our second largest revenue source, we're at 48% of budget, which is pretty good when we're, you know, June 30th is 50% through our fiscal year. So to be at 48% is good to see.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: And that equates to about $2.6 million. That can also be seen on page 7 there at the top on the revenues. Interest, you know, continues. The market is holding strong, so you'll see our interest earnings there. You'll see notes there in the 3rd bullet on item number 1. Recreation Fund, their charges for services to date is at 58%. That, of course, the Recreation Department does receive some support from the general fund, as noted in that 2nd bullet there, and that's about in the amount of $160,000. A little more detail on the recreation fund can be found on page 9. Building Department, as I mentioned, things were a little bit soft, a little bit slower earlier on in the year. So revenues as it relates to licenses and permits lagging a little bit at 43%, but still, as he notes there, a little bit ahead for the same period last year. So— That's where things at. Of course, July is really going to help that number with a strong July that we had. You know the rest of the expenditures there, as well as his notes regarding the enterprise funds. Everything is on track. You know at at the time that this was done, water sales were about 40% of budget. But as noted there, those tend to lag a little bit until we get into the summer months, and we're going to have a very strong. July and August for that. So we'll be right at budget or better regarding that. So again, just to reiterate, no budget amendments proposed for this quarter.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: All right, thank you, Steve. Then we've got the consideration of appointment to replace the Brownfield Development Authority members and Did I miss that page?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: There wasn't. We had an email from the Brownfield Authority, and it was recommended that Jim Stroop would be— who is retiring— would be replaced by Randy Quartering, and that Doug Kuiper, who retired as finance director, would be replaced with Warren Smith.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: The current finance director?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: The current finance director. And Mr. Stroop's term ended December 31st, 2027. So Randy would go through that, and Doug's term ended December 31st, 2025. So that's how long Warren's term would go. Okay. Okay. Thank you, man. So I'll move to unless somebody has questions. I'll make a motion to. Appoint those two to the Brown Cove Authority.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I'll support that, Mike. Mr.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Chairman, thank you. All in favor say aye. Aye. Any opposed, same sign. Thank you, Mike. Then we got consideration for operational and capital improvement expenditures. First one is the 2025 pathway repair.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah, as my cover memo indicates, we received very competitive bids for this. Very pleased with where we came in. In fact, it felt like the good old days. So we had 3.7 miles of pathway reconstruction. Now, this is not resurfacing. This is reconstruction. So they're removing the pavement, replacing the base, repairing the base. So generally, it runs a little bit more expensive. Okay. But it was one of these situations where I think the contractor has been having good weather, and so they had a little, maybe a little flexibility in their schedule. We also, as part of this process, since it was 3.7 miles, gave the contractor the option to do work, you know, beginning upon the notice to proceed, but giving them the springtime to complete any work or do a portion of the work As well. So it could be a situation where, you know, they had a little gap in their schedule now, knowing that, well, if we don't get all $3.7 million done this fall, we still have some time in the spring. So, you know, very pleased. Site Works Solutions, which was the low bidder at $582,480, is known to the township, done a number of projects for us. I think did site— some excavation work for us as part of the community center, as I recall. Yes. But well known, has long history with the township, great contractor. So very pleased with the results from this bid process. So again, the staff is seeking approval for that amount of $582,000 plus 10% contingency for an amount not to exceed $641,000.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Sections, will they leave anything unfinished for a period of time?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: They'll finish a section, they may wait before they begin the next section, but there will not be something left undone?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Correct. Yeah, that would be bad for our snowplows.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: So yeah, well, in using a couple of those on a regular basis, it's certainly a welcome sight that they're being replaced or refurbished.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yes, yeah, they're due.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Oh man.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah, they're due.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I would just like to make a note of appreciation here because I really enjoy seeing 6 different estimates here. I think it's critical, and I'm saying thank you for doing the work on that.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Obviously, if you only get a couple—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I mean, we're almost double from the high end to the low end here, right? We're only getting a couple estimates. Very likely, potentially leaving money on the table. And thank you, whoever's in charge of doing that, Steve or whoever's doing that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I appreciate seeing that many estimates. Thank you. I'll support. All in favor say oh. We need a roll call here. You bet, Chuck. Dollar amount here.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: You bet, Chuck. Mr. Church. Yes. Mr. Nienhuis. Yeah. Mr. DeYoung? Yes. Mr. Tesla? Yes. Myself? Yes. Miss Malachy? Yes. Okay, 6 in favor, 0 opposed, 1 absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: All right, thank you very much. Then we've got the Township Hall site improvements, and I think Greg Mayberry here is here to help this guy through the details a little bit.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Hopefully. Uh, Greg Mayberry, owner rep for the township. Steve, do you want to pull up that? Yes. We had a project that GDK was running that included the Helder Park Drive and parking lot resurfacing. Those bids came in at the last meeting, was approved by the board, and that work is complete and will be open come this, this coming Monday. We had additional work with GDK to Improve the parking, expand the parking to the north, and provide a parking space for 8 vehicles for the assessors for the township. The initial budget was approved in scope. We're coming tonight with bids for you to review and approve. We had competitive bids for most divisions, which was nice. And you have those in your packet, from concrete, masonry, through here. The 2 areas where we had only one bid, multiple contractors, trade contractors were asked to bid, but we did not get multiple bids. Overeisel was the only bid for the rough carpentry, and that's mostly materials. They did takeoffs of the drawings. And And GDK reviewed those takeoffs to make sure they had the right number of linear board feet and so forth specced for that, and they felt that that was a reasonable number. The other single bid was distinctive for the general trades, and that is the constructing part of the rough carpentry. And they asked other trade contractors to bid, but they declined. And when I spoke with GDK, they felt that the tightness of the schedule was the issue for the other trade contractors in that we're starting this right away and we want to be finished by the end of the year. So we want— obviously the paving asphalt part has to be done before the plants close. But they felt comfortable with Distinctive and Distinctive would be one of the companies that they would have chosen. To do the work. And again, I have confidence that GDK is going to monitor that work and make sure that all the charges that come through are fair and equitable for both the trade contractor and the township. But other than those 2 single bid amounts, we had competitive bids for everything else. There was one area where the materials that were bid for I just want to make sure I quote this correctly. The metal wall and roof panels, there were 2 low bids, but they did not have the spec materials, so those were disqualified, and we went with only those bids that used the specified materials. I'm open to any other questions on the bidding process. Before you, you have the summary Where the construction total is $627,000 with a 10% contingency, followed by general conditions, design fee, management fee, and the construction total is $814,394. Knowing that there was some concern about the cost of the parking garage at the last meeting, I just wanted to point out that the construction for the garage component, construction only, is $360,000. Adding a 10% contingency, a portion of the general conditions, the total of the design fees that are shown, part of Driesinga's civil engineering and the CM fee for that portion, we come up with $452,000 for the total all-in cost for those 8 bays, which is $56,500 per bay, just so you have that number in front of you. And it's surprising, I know there was concern about spending that much money before.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Where is the— Greg, where is the bus garage in here? Is that included in here somewhere? Or not the garage.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Bus shelter.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Bus shelter.

_utterances.speaker: J

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, that's something we've been wanting to have on here for a long time. Good to see it kind of take place.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: If there's any questions about the design, Again, we're utilizing the former Township Hall space for expanded parking, relocating the drop box and the security and lighting for that. Water retention will be between the bus shelter and the parking lot, so we'll have more of a swale in that area. That leaves the property to the northeast still available for future use by the township. We have maintained the parking and have increased the number of parking spaces. That was a concern when we were redoing more green space near the entry. We want to, as part of the project, have it phased so we maintain ADA parking for the Township Hall during business hours and also to minimize the work That will take place during election and tax collection times. So if the board approves this tonight, we'll have a kickoff meeting and start the phasing for this, but we'll notify the trade contractors so they can gear up because we're going to get running pretty quick here.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I see this mansard roof.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: On the garage there.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Is that just a flat roof?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: It's, um, it's meant to be—

It's just that front look has that with a slope to the back, and we're not having gutters on that. We're having a drip area so that water would run off and collect. That was the simplest roof structure we could Come up with other than flat, and flat doesn't sometimes work as well.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Just a gabled roof wouldn't make sense?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: You could do a gabled roof, but—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'm not a big flat roof guy because, no, for one thing, they don't last half as long.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Well, this will drain rather nicely because it has that— it's a like a 4 in 12 pitch look, so you're only seeing this side, so it has the effect of a mansard. Yeah, but it's not Flat and then mansard on the other side, as this roof is.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: But that, that 4:12 pitch will be a rubber roof and not a shingled roof, correct?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: No, it'll be a metal roof.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Metal roof.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Metal to match what we have here.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: To match the township.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Oh yeah, it makes for good drainage because everything— 90% of the roof goes off the back.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yeah, I understand. Yeah, yeah, I think the The goal—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: You gave us the year of— I mean, kind of expanded the life expectancy as well.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: I think the township was looking for an aesthetic to match the township building and not have kind of an oddball standalone building.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Anyone else agree? All right, looking for a motion to approve. Awarding the bids associated at the cost shown at $814,000, which includes the 10% contingency on there.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I would make that motion. Mr. Chairman?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I'll support it.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Support. No objection. We'll have another roll call vote.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Miss Malachy.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Myself, yes. Mr. DeYoung?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: No.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Mr. Neenhuys?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Mr. TeSlaa?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Mr. Church?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay, we have 5 in favor, 1 opposed, 1 absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: All right, thank you, Mike. Then we're up to administrative reports.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yes, as I alluded earlier when we were talking about that expenditure for the Freezers and refrigerators and so forth. That's part of something that is called the FFE, or the Furniture, Fixtures, and Equipment budget. And as we were putting it, uh, the project together for the community center, certainly the construction side of that was done with the aid of the architects and GDK and so forth, but they don't get involved with the FFE budget. So I And I turned to our consultant here and said, Greg, you've done a lot of these. And he put pen to paper and thought about it a little bit and the project and so forth and came up with a budget. And now that the dust has settled and the equipment has been purchased and the furniture has been purchased, he was off by $2,000. So he hit that one right on the head.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Which way? Up or down?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Down.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Oh.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Down. He was under. Yeah, but did a great job, you know, just thinking through, hey, what are we going to need here, and put pen to paper and so forth. And now that it's all in, uh, so again, really appreciate Greg, all your assistance in making that project so successful. That's all I have.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Okay, I will just say thank you too.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Great work. Yes. I have not seen the completed project.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Oh, you'd be in good shape to go there now.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, get me all over that place. Don't lift anything too heavy.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: You're right. In your back.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Mike?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Successful election on Tuesday for our 270 voters. We had 1 person come in here to vote, and we had 7 absentee ballots. So, it was a Rather long day.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I'll tell Mayor Buck that we got some voters for their election over in Holland. Terry?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: No, I just want to mention, I love talking about the Parks and Recreation. I don't know if you've ever witnessed the sprinkler event that we hold there in the summer. It's fantastic. It's so well received, with just hundreds and hundreds of kids. And, of course, we give them all And then, there's firefighters involved, and they actually get into a little race with these kids. And, it's just unbelievable, the atmosphere that's out there. But, it's good to see. Great to see.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: How many kids would you estimate were out there?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Oh, I don't know. Probably 300.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: And, it's a very low-cost event.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Very. And, you turn on the sprinklers.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: It's our own sprinklers.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah. A little water.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Was that on Terry Nienhuis Drive?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: So what is it? It's just at the, um, it's Quincy Park.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep. And the soccer field. Yep.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Just turn them on, turn on the sprinklers.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: It's unbelievable. You, you got to check it out sometime.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah. Anyway, thanks. All right, Gretchen, anything?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Um, so the housing study was done, um, and then they're going to go over it with us at our next meeting. So I believe that's on the 12th. I'll be able to report back on that and I'll give everybody a copy. I can send it to them. That should be very interesting.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Good, thanks. Elliot?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Nothing.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Nothing? I just had a quick update. I went to the LAO Lunch and Learn this week. That's Latin American United for Progress. They just have a fantastic bunch of young Latin American women, because I think it was like 12 out of 13 staff were women. And they gave a presentation of how they are there to support the immigrants in the area, how they're there to support kids in the area, make sure they're getting the right education. And now, as all those kids are getting older, to make sure they're in the right preschool program, technical training, college entrance exams are getting done. One note I took that they had, the kids that applied for college this year, had $800,000 in grants.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Hmm.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So, that was really good. 33 students that were going to college. I forgot the number of high school students that were going on for just technical training, but they support them in that. The other very interesting stat was that of the immigrants in our area, 30% of them are— have master's, doctor's, or PhD-level education in their home countries. Wow. And that's their other initiative, is to make sure that their English speaking is good, and then to try to help them get into that field of work, rather than basically hourly type positions. But some of them are fleeing home countries, other ones have just gone through the process of coming to the U.S. And yeah, it was just a really encouraging luncheon that was very informative. So.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Glad you went to that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah. Yeah, I know that You know, we give them money to do some of that work, and so, I thought we'd get a little bit more detailed information on that. That's all I have, so, look for a motion to adjourn.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Oh, we got one more, Mike?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Did I miss one?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: No, one minute, then I can make it 8 o'clock.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: The meeting adjourned.

4g7v0TVK17A.summary:
- **IFEC Transfer Approved**: ADAC Electronics acquired EBW Electronics; board approved transfer of Industrial Facilities Exemption Certificate for real property ($3,425,250) for remaining 3 years. Expected to add 20+ employees and at least double production volume. Estimated tax benefit ~$60,000 over 2 years.
- **Audit Presentation (FY 2024)**: Clean audit opinion with no compliance issues. Two findings noted: (1) material weakness in financial statement preparation due to large journal entries found during audit (expected to resolve in 2025); (2) federal award finding regarding undocumented vendor debarment checks via SAM.gov (no noncompliance detected). Auditor praised strong internal controls and high fund balance (~$29M unassigned in general fund).
- **CIP 2026 Approved (5-1)**: Total changes of ~$500k across multiple projects including east wing renovations (+$100k), parking lot/garage (+$175k), pickleball lighting (+$77k), and new SCADA improvements. One trustee (DeYoung) opposed due to concerns about garage expense.
- **Construction & Capital Projects Approved**: (1) Lakewood Construction selected for town hall east wing CM services ($12,100); (2) Helder Park parking lot resurfacing ($386,727, must complete by Aug 11); (3) Pump Station 2 complete reconstruction ($2,650,000, Allied Mechanical Services).
- **Site Plan Approvals**: Silverwater Phase 4 (38 units, 30 permit cap before 136th connection) and River Hills land division both approved.
- **Community Center Grand Opening**: Tentatively scheduled Sept 18-20; staff tours underway; positive community response and media coverage (Fox News featured).

highlights.quote: our goal is another 20 or so employees at least...our current plan is to increase the volume in that facility and hopefully need to expand
there was a material weakness...this relates to the financial statement prep...we had a few large journal entries, which is something that we expect here...Warren is planning on cleaning that up

there wasn't formal written policies to do the procurement work, certain procurement procedures...in order to spend federal dollars, you have to check and see if a vendor is suspended or debarred, and none of that was documented
