_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I pledge allegiance to the flag of the United States of America and to the Republic for which it stands, one nation under God, indivisible, with liberty and justice for all. Shall we pray? Our Father in heaven, Lord, we thank you for this beautiful day that you again give to us, and we thank you for the opportunity as citizens to gather here and as a as a body to make deliberations on the business at hand. We ask for your guidance in that and that we might do that fairly and justly and to the benefit of our, our citizens here. And Lord, we pray for safety on all the first responders in our area as well as servicemen and women around the world, that you would keep them under your hand and guidance. In Jesus' name, amen.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Amen.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We do have an open mic for public comment if there is any. Most people are here for business.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We have one observer.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay, they're all set. Okay. And we have a public hearing for the related resolution. On the IFPC transfer of EDW Electronics to ADAC Electronics, LLC. Would you like to call the public hearing, or just read through those?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, I'm going to just say—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Open the public hearing.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'm going to open the public hearing, and this is for real estate in the real amount of $3,425,250.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: For construction costs. We don't have any personal property at this time. And the district was established on December 15th, 1994. And for the request of 3 years that's remaining on this IFEPC for the real property. And I think we have—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Was it Ken?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Here.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: You want to give us an update on what's happening over there? Sure, will do.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Good evening, my name is Ken Vermeulen. I'm with the Honigman Law Firm representing ADAC. ADAC is a Tier 1 automotive supplier. We're here to talk— discuss the transfer of the IFT from EVW that was granted about 8 years ago or so to ADAC for the remaining couple of years. Just for background, for about 20 years, DBW Electronics manufactured various electronic parts, including printed circuit board assemblies, or PCBAs as they're known, primarily for use in the auto industry. One of their— at a facility located at 13110 Ransom Street, that property was owned by DBW Properties. Who then leased the property to EBW Electronics at a related party lease. ADAC is a long-term customer of EBW. They were one of their primary suppliers of PCBAs. And recently this past spring, EBW has acquired EBW— or ADAC has acquired EBW as part of a vertical integration and hopes to now move more of its DCBA supply to its own production rather than from third-party vendors. So over the last decade or so, EBW has had 4 IFTs granted to it. The first one expired, I believe, in 2024, last year. 2 more expire this year, and so there's no reason to transfer those. The one remaining expires in 2027, I believe. As to the real property, and that's the one that we're here to talk about. That IFT originally applied to both real property and personal property, but again, with the changes in personal property taxes lately, that's no longer relevant. So we're here only to talk about the real property transfer for, for 2 remaining years. With me tonight is Jennifer Williamson, who is the Chief Financial Officer, and And Executive Vice President of ADAC. If you have any questions for her or I with respect to the application or the company's operations, we'd be happy to answer them.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Thanks, Ken.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Questions from the board?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So is this actually a transfer? It's not a new IFT?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: It is not a new IFT. It's just— it's simply a transfer of the existing IFT for the remaining, I guess, 2 more years. It's this year and then 2 more.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So apparently you're the biggest in Germany. Is that your company? How big are you guys in Europe?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: In Europe?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: You're like the biggest auto supplier in Europe? Is that the case? You have like millions of employees?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: No, no, no, no.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So we used to have a partnership with a company called Witte, W-I-T-T-E, which if you're looking something up, and we had a joint venture with them and it was called VAST. And they're the ones who have the presence in Europe. And we sold our interest in VAST to VITA in 2023. So they're still partners of ours. So for example, if we said we want a program and we needed production in China, they have a China production. We would say, can you make our parts for that program in China on our behalf? Likewise, they would say, can you make our parts in the US for you? very little of that anymore. Automakers no longer really do global programs because vehicles end up slightly different in China, in Europe, and in the US. So, they do less of it, but that's what you're looking at is that vast relationship that we sold our interest in. Stratech was the third partner in that, and they also sold their interest. Both of them— both of us sold to VITA.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So, ADAC is headquartered in Grand Rapids and primarily West Michigan presence.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Do you have 5 facilities in West Michigan?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: There is a headquarters and an innovation center in Grand Rapids, a manufacturing facility in Saarenac, and 3 manufacturing facilities in Muskegon. And then, we just acquired the assets of EVW, and now we have a manufacturing facility here in Holland, now called ADAC Electronics.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Are you publicly traded?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: No.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: You're privately held?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Where do the owners live, out of curiosity?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: East Grand Rapids, I think.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Oh, so local?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah. Oh, that's good.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, you're welcome.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Anything else?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So I don't know if you were here— I don't know if you were here 10 years ago when we probably granted this to you initially, I would imagine, right in that range. But, um, usually we ask for kind of a how many employees are you, uh, intending to— how many positions are you intending to create with this, with what you're doing with the, um, addition or whatnot, right? I guess my question is Where were you 10 years ago and where are you now? Are you growing?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I would say EBW, again, EBW was maybe a little bit overly optimistic on their— I think business wasn't quite as what they had hoped over those 10 years. But certainly now with ADAC's infusion of both capital and its Control— I mean, it's its largest customer, right? So now we know we're bringing more business to the, to the EBW production facility. So there certainly will be an increase in production because we're going to be buying more product from them and not from third parties. So our goal is another 20 or so employees at least.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah, at least. Our expectation based where we are today is to at least double the business from today. From 10 years ago, I can't say exactly what that would be.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: But our current plan is to increase the volume in that facility and hopefully need to expand.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I was going to say, can you fit it inside the facility that's there, or—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Today—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: If you grow that much, will they have to expand the facility itself in the future?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Today, the current plan is we can— we think we can, depending on equipment, double our volume out of the existing facility. But then we've got the big sign on the back wall that says, help us break down this wall, because our plan is to expand back into— I call it the field, but, you know, there's a field back there. The plan is to expand that way when we need to expand. Currently, we're about 50% utilized on equipment, so we can still fill up the other 50% and/or change out some equipment. Some are a little bit old, and you can get higher volume equipment today. So replace with new lines and be able to increase utilization that way too. But then when we need to, we plan to expand the facility.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Great.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any other questions?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So, what's the total amount of the benefit that you're deriving from this?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Over the next 2 years, probably in the city of $60,000.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So, you know, it is not a—

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: it's not a huge benefit. It wouldn't make or break the deal, but obviously, uh, you know, we'd like to take advantage of it if we could. It's about $30,000 a year. Of course, you're asking a lawyer to do like accounting, so my numbers are off. I apologize, but I think that's about right.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Maybe the CFO has better numbers, but I think the middle is about right. All right, we'll close the public hearing.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And for a motion for the request from ADAC for an industrial facilities exemption certificate in the amount of $3,425,000. Transfer— transfer EDW to ADC for the remaining 3 years that are left on that IFEC.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I'll support it.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: All those in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any opposed? Same sign. All right, thank you. Then we've got the consent agenda items.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Steve?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, no communications this evening. A few operational reports for your review, the first one being the June building permit activity. Strong April— I'm sorry, strong June, $6.8 million. And if you had a chance to take a look at the detail, that was really buoyed by some expansion work that's going to be done by the animal clinic up the street here, as well as NextGen Industries at 13023 Griesinger Drive. And if that address sounds familiar, it's because that's one of the parcels that was sold to NextGen ownership a couple years back. So it's nice to see them Uh, putting something up on that property and creating jobs with it. So happy to answer any questions regarding the building report. If none, the June Fire Department activity report had total calls in June of 225. And if you had a chance to take a look at the year-to-date and compare those to the annual totals from '24 and '23, You're starting to really get a good sense of how much busier the department is. You know, considering that for the first 6 months of this calendar year, you know, we're sitting at about 1,370 calls, you know, compared to 1,399 in '24 and 1,038 in 2023. So we've just about achieved or have surpassed, depending on what year you're looking at, those annual totals. And I think we're very much on track here to see, you know, somewhere between 2,500 and 2,600 calls responded to in this fiscal year. Happy to answer any questions regarding the fire department. If not, there's also the sheriff's report.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Isn't that nice?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, good, good. And then the sheriff's is a twofer this month, so both May and June are in there. And if you took a look at That first page there at the top, the graphic shows that for May we're at 1,431 calls. My math says that we're down about 6% compared to the same month last year. Likewise with June, down about 8% at 1,426. Some other great information there you can take a look at, but I would direct your attention to the bottom of the first page where the Sheriff's Department is going to have a Cops and Cones Event at Captain Sunday, but they put it on August 7th on a meeting night. So, but you can stop by before the meeting starts. It's running from 5 to 7. You know, it's great to see the sheriff.

_utterances.speaker: N
_utterances.text: They've been moving around the county doing these events, and it's neat to see that they're doing the next one in the township.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: That's all I have for operational reports.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So we got the approval of the minutes from June 2025, June 19th.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yep. Got that one yet?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Warren's going to handle that one.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Warren's covering that part.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Got the financial transactions.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Yes, yes.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I knew Warren was here for a couple things tonight.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: That's right, right.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: We're gonna make them work. We got them here.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay, so as far as the total disbursements for the month of June, it just exceeded $5 million. If you compare our totals to the previous year, general fund was almost the same, just slightly different.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Building and site was down a little bit, and that is mostly the community center being built and the cost associated with that. The water and sewer construction is up considerably, and that's due to the Pump 31 station, and that also impacts the sewer operating. Water operating is up somewhat. We are seeing some increased costs in some of the utility bills. And some of the bigger bills that we had during the month of June, we had in the general fund, we had Ottawa County Treasurer for the May policing services Yes. For $181,000. We had $571,000 in Fund 405 for the community center. We had $164,000 in the water fund for painting hydrants. We had— the biggest one is $1,390,000 to request foods for CBG grant. And that is funds that we got from the state and we just transferred it back over to them. We also had Pump Station 31 project for $477,000. Ottawa County Public Utilities for May sewer is $262,000. We had Apex for the Middle Macatawa Trail Improvement Project for $240,000, and Ottawa County Public Utilities for May water Thank you, Warren.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So we got the minutes from the June 19th meeting. If there's any updates on that, otherwise look for a motion to approve the consent agenda.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Vince, all support? All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any opposed?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Same sign.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And then we've got the audit presentations, so I'll let Vince introduce you.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Hi, this is Mike.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Go for it.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Thanks for that. Uh, yeah, Mike Vredeveld. I'm a partner with Vredeveld Hefner LLC. Um, led field work this year for the audit, so I'll just give a pretty brief report. This is as of December 31st, 2024, so the numbers are a little bit old at this point. Um, staff were very responsive and, uh, quick in getting us exactly what we needed, so the audit process went very smoothly. This year, which we really appreciate. So on page 1, and I think Warren's going to kind of try to walk through this with us here. So the first 2 paragraphs of this audit report here are a good clean audit opinion. That's exactly what you're looking for when you have an audit done. Nothing else to point out there. The next section, which is going to be on page 5, is going to be management's discussion and analysis. I'm not going to get too much into this here, but if you're going to read one thing out of this report, this is what it should be. It's a very high-level summary put together by management, and yeah, really good information, and just, yeah, kind of a big summary of what happened during the year. The basic financial statements start on page 11. So the first one you're going to see here is what's called the government-wide level. This is a little bit more of a long-term outlook versus some of the shorter-term kind of fund-level accounting. So you'll see some things on there like your capital assets, bonds payable on there that you might not typically see. If we go down to page 14, this is where we're going to see the governmental funds balance sheet. We did have 2 major funds this year. There's the general fund and then the building and site bond. Historically, that fund has been non-major, which means it's shoved way in the back of the report, but the community center's all running through there, so that kind of bumped up the activity a fair bit there.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Near the bottom of this page in the general fund column, and I'm still on page 14, so you'll see $518,000 that says assigned for subsequent year expenditures. That is a budgeted use of fund balance as part of the 2025 budget, and then you will see the ending general fund unassigned fund balance was just about $29 million there. Page 16 is going to be the income statement. At this level, you'll see there was a good amount of federal funding this year. That was about $1.2 million in CDBG money, which is part of what Warren was already talking about a little bit. That ties into that request foods. And then $4 million of that was ARPA, which was all transferred out, which you'll see further down this page in the big transfer out number. That was all sent over to the building and site fund in order to do that project. I'm continuing to go down. I'm just— or, oh, I wanted to point out on page 19, this is the income statement for the enterprise funds, which is the sewer water and then the water and sewer construction fund. All 3 of those had increases in net position this year, pretty significant increases, which is great. And then the footnotes, I'm not going to get into too much detail there. They start on page 23. But footnote 1 here just kind of goes over some basic accounting policies, a little bit of information on that. I did want to point out the investment note on page 30. So you can see there's a little chart here, right there, where you can see— I just wanted to point out that some of these investments listed out, their average maturity in years is quite long. There's some of them that are Over 20 years long, so just be aware that some of that money is not quite available. It's not as liquid as some of the other stuff, but there is plenty that is available. You know, fairly quickly there, and then the chart below that also shows the ratings of the investments, and they're all very highly rated. Um, I think I'll point out the bottom of page 34. This is your future debt payments, just so you can kind of see what's coming up in the next. 5 years and then kind of big chunks thereafter. I always like to point that out just so you kind of know what's coming. And now we're going to jump all the way near the end of the report to the single audit section. Let me get a page for you, Warren. Page 61. So because there was a large amount of federal funding this year that was spent, we did have a single audit. which is a separate compliance audit over the spending of the federal dollars. So, this first report here is a report on internal control, and then we did note that there was the material weakness. I think this has been there historically, and we do— we worked with Warren this year, and we do expect that to probably go away next year. The next page, or page 63, excuse me, this is our report on compliance, and this is a clean report here. We did not have any compliance issues. That we noted. And the actual schedule of expenditures of federal awards is on page 67. We did audit both of these as major programs this year for a total of about $5.3 million. They were both over the federally set threshold, so we did look at all of that. Page 69 is our summary of results. So, you'll see we did, that yes box is checked there for the material weakness, and then there are some audit findings. We'll get into that in a minute here. But the 2024-001 in Section 2, this relates to the financial statement prep. That's just there because we had a few large journal entries, which is something that we expect here. We've kind of done it historically. But Warren is planning on cleaning that up from our understanding, which will be great.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: The next page is a federal award finding, and this has— there wasn't formal written policies to do the procurement work, certain procurement procedures that have to be done. None of the— so in order to spend federal dollars, you have to check and see if a vendor is suspended or debarred, and none of that was documented. But we did look all of them up and There was no issues with it. So again, we have a finding, but no noncompliance resulting from that. And that is all that I have, and I'm happy to answer any questions that I can. Sorry if that went a little long.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I know that you had a team of four people here took over a conference room for about a week.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, probably. Yep. And then some of them were kind of in and out and on and off the job at times.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Appreciate the time that you spent and the detail.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any other questions?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I just wondered, did all the board members get this?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So, I don't have one.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I think in the future, all the board members should get a hard copy, just like this is.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay, everyone was given a PDF copy.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I know, but this is a lot easier to read through and make notes on.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Okay, all right.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, you can retro on the hard copy, right?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yeah, I don't need one now, but in the future, if we could do it this way so that each board member has a chance to read it page by page rather than going online.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I have a quick question. Yeah, so we hear so much about fraud, waste, and abuse in our federal government. Do you guys look at that?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yes, so part of the single audit, when we're looking at compliance, is we're picking a sample of transactions, which we assess based on the risk, mainly looking at internal controls over those invoices, making sure they're reviewed by somebody who's familiar with the requirements of the grant. So, we do it sample-based, and that is where we would note any of that if we did, but all the expenditures that we looked at were quite reasonable.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So, of the expenditures that you looked at, is there like a bottom line number, like we're not going to look at it and waste our time over $2,000 or anything like that? Is there a cutoff on what you're looking at as those samples?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: To some degree there is. We pick our sample based on a full population, and then we don't pick only high dollar amounts. We get the full population, and I don't want to use the word random, But random-ish, we select, you know, if the population's a certain size, we have guidance that says how many we need to pick. So we pick them and then we look at it. And then if we, you know, have some issues, then that increases our risk and that's when we would look at some more. And if we don't, then, then we assume it's good.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Can I chime in on that a little bit too, Dave?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Please.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Recommendation last year, what Jackie does is the cashier accountant cashier. She gives me a report every 2 weeks. It goes to the clerk and myself, and we randomly pick one and we work it back to make sure that there's no fraud there. So it might say like Fred Smith Cleaners. So we'll look, who is this Fred Smith? And we get the audit, we see who signed up, go to the department that we tracked it back that. And then also, what, after Mike's recommendation with the finance director, we get every quarter journal entry where they Put in some type of warrant. We looked at one recently. It was something to do with maybe with the parts. We tracked, excuse me, tracked it back how it was in the system and how it was paid for, who ordered it. And so those are the two that Mike recommended before they came along. We didn't do that. And their recommendation, I think maybe last year.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, last year, the year before.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah. So that was your one of the fraud things that they.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, the best way to prevent it is with good internal controls, not by, not by us coming in once a year.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I totally agree.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: And so in your opinion, are the internal controls satisfactory?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay. Yep.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, we do look at that. There's good separation of duties between several staff kind of looking at everything. Invoices are all You know, reviewed and approved. And yeah, I mean, it's not impossible. You know, if two people work together, that's always you know kind of the biggest threat there.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: But so you mentioned a sample size. What is that sample size?

_utterances.speaker: F

_utterances.text: Forty. Forty out of That's on the single audit threshold.

I'm not an auditor, but I'm curious about your work, and, and so I don't expect you to gloss over it.

_utterances.text: The question is, is to understand what percentage of the transactions that the township has conducted Have been looked at. You talked about tracing back, true?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So I want to understand what it is you're tracing. Are you taking— you said single audit. Does that mean you're taking a transaction and tracing it back?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: No, no, no, no. So I'll start at the beginning. So the first thing that we do when we come in is we get what's called a trial balance from Warren. Which is detail of every single account.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: And what is in that account.

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: So, we use that to build the financial statements, which is going to be the balance sheet, which we looked at up there. So, on the balance sheet, we audit every number that's on there. We say, here's the number, what makes up this number? And Warren gets us detail, and then we do set some thresholds based on materiality. And then we look at, you know, invoices or receipts or checks or whatever we need to do to support that number. Like for capital assets, we look at invoices of expenditures to make sure that there's no capital assets that should be there that weren't capitalized. And then we're looking at the assets to make sure, you know, all the invoices are included in there, that the number is right. And then we're also looking at the useful life to make sure that depreciation is calculated accurately. So there's a lot of different things that we do.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: It's—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: sometimes it's process-based, sometimes we look at the activity in there, sometimes we audit with more internal controls to kind of assess our risk in that. Does that help at all?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Kind of. So it's not transactions that you're tracing. So you're looking at accounts and you're looking at debits and credits and whether those are substantiated by something?

_utterances.speaker: F

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

Transactions are not your direct focus, but you may investigate them ancillary to looking at an account?

You're referring to page 71, the letter from our finance director saying the responsibility of the township manager, the township treasurer, to adhere to the policy of documents review of the vendor through SAM.gov?

_utterances.text: I'm just—

_utterances.text: Okay.

So I would prefer to have that changed. I'm not sure about that, it really lies with your audit, but I just that was my question regarding the findings. So I'll shut up. How do we figure that out?

_utterances.text: Yeah.

And so that was already completed, and so it was something that was missed.

_utterances.text: Yeah, 100%. I'll take credit for missing that because I think when we talked about the ARPA money, we weren't sure how we were going to spend it. It's a new process for everyone, so I I will take responsibility for that. So, it's not on you, Warren, by any means.

So, no, mine's around the corner, kind of by the condos. Yeah, it was Genevieve.

It's an interesting building in that the masonry choice, an original build, creates some fun issues aesthetically, the fluted block behind you. So, it was— in taking that out and reconfiguring, Corey did a great job of giving some initial concepts for us to consider. We went out to 3 firms. TV Construction, GDK, and Lakewood Construction to ask for proposals for construction management services only to handle this particular project. We received 2 responses from Lakewood and from GDK, and you have some of the cost elements in front of you. Lakewood was more aggressive on this particular project than it has been Knowing that GDK has been successful in another project, so they came in low for construction management five percent versus five point eight. Their general conditions value was twelve one versus twenty six thousand. Their site supervision was twenty eight versus thirty three, and then they had some design AE design for Lakewood was five versus. 18 for GDK. GDK did a very thorough job of looking at the project, photo documenting every room, so I think that they understood some of the complexities of the project. I believe that Lakewood wanted the project, so we had— I had a post-bid conversation with both GDK and Lakewood to make sure they understood the project design fees, length of time for the construction and design were at variance. So I needed to understand why there were the differences, and I understood. And in my memo to, uh, Township Manager Steve Bulthuis, uh, GDK was relying on a particular architect for their work, and he did not have time. And so that extended the design time beyond and pushed the into 2026 and was much more expensive. Lakewood has in-house architects and is able to do things more quickly and have the design. They also tend to design with a design-build with the electrician and the HVAC groups doing a lot more of the design. So that helped cut down their costs. They also had a shorter project time but a longer construction time. And they're able to end on December 3rd. So even though their project time was shorter, their construction time was a majority of that, whereas GDK project time had a shorter construction time, but it was jammed in December and January. So looking at all the differences, and plus the fact that Lakewood was low on all 4 areas, recommend that we choose Lakewood for the construction management.

euS3ikxkkEU.summary:
- **Public Works Building Expansion Update**: Greg Mayberry presented a master plan proposal for the maintenance building at 207 West Lakewood, including acquisition of the Wiley's cleaning area (approximately 5 acres). Lakewood Construction proposed ~$20,000 for architectural design, preconstruction budgeting, site survey, geotechnical, and civil engineering services to optimize the 14,000 sq ft pre-engineered storage building and future expansion opportunities over 30 years.
- **Tax Foreclosure Avoidance Program**: Ottawa County Treasurer Cheryl Clark presented a new 5-year payment plan program (one of only 2 in Michigan) allowing delinquent taxpayers to avoid foreclosure by waiving interest and administrative fees. Requirements: principal residence exemption, current taxes must stay paid, monthly payments required. Benefits township by ensuring current taxes paid on schedule (September/February) rather than waiting for county settlement in April. Board unanimously approved participation resolution.
- **Westwood Estates Final Plat Approved**: 50-unit residential development off Ottegon/west of 96th Street approved 6-0 after Planning Commission and Road Commission approvals. Minor easement adjustments requested by Drain Commission for drainage access through Lot 48 and catch basin area—developer confirmed no impact on building envelopes. Project described as challenging with lengthy approval timeline (3-4 years).
- **CIP 2026-2031 Updates**: Three new allocations approved for consideration: $170,000 for pickleball court lighting upgrade at Helder Park (Parks Committee recommendation); $100,000 placeholder for phone system replacement (current 10+ year old system; Holland Board of Public Works ending support); $100,000 for outdoor emergency warning sirens evaluation following storm damage complaints from Walkazoo Woods residents.
- **Community Grants**: Resolution approved for appropriations totaling $31,000 supporting local agencies: Hospice of Holland ($2,500), REACH Recovery Program ($2,500), Holland Civic Theater ($500), Zeeland Historical Society ($5,000 operational + referred $15,000 CEP request), Outdoor Discovery Network ($3,000 invasive species control).
- **402 East 8th Street Property Purchase**: Board approved (6-0) purchase and sale agreement for former Lemon Fresh/dry cleaner building to implement 8th Street vision plan. Project includes environmental Phase 1 assessment (county brownfield funds available), contingent Phase 2 if needed. Strategy: acquire property, demolish deteriorated structure, develop parking to support future mixed-use residential/retail development. Purchase price based on assessor valuation; contingencies allow board to exit if cleanup costs prohibitive. New Master Plan Vision Fund Committee formed with Vince Bush, Dave DeYoung, Russ TeSlaa, and Randy Quartering (Planning Commission chair) to oversee project review.
- **Votes**: Board minutes approval (unanimous); tax foreclosure program resolution (unanimous); Westwood Estates plat (6-0); community grants resolution (unanimous); 402 East 8th Street purchase agreement (6-0); Master Plan Vision Fund Committee formation (unanimous with Jenny Hamzik requesting future committee notification process be clarified).

_utterances.text: That's right.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I drove here from Big Rapids.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Oh, wow, you did?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes, we're camping at Cran Hill Ranch in Rodney, Michigan, there by Big Rapids.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Thanks for making it work.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Well, thank you for allowing me to come and talk and— on this program that I'm unveiling. It is called the Tax Foreclosure Avoidance Payment Reduction Program. And this is something that I ran on, which was helping taxpayers avoid foreclosure. The law recently changed, and it is allowing county treasurers to enter into types— this type of a program with delinquent taxpayers. Normally, we would only be able to give a 1-year extension to somebody who has delinquent taxes and they are going to be facing foreclosure. So we call it forfeiture, is that year before foreclosure. So when they're in the forfeiture stage, we will give a 1-year extension to anybody who comes in and asks us. Basically, we— because we don't want to foreclose on anybody's home, so we will give them that 1-year extension. Well, with this change in the law, it is now allowing county treasurers to give a— to enter into a 5-year payment plan agreement. And what it does is It— this agreement waives the interest and the admin fees that the county charges. So they would only have to pay on their base tax, the tax that was turned over delinquent to us. There's also a caveat that they have to have their current taxes paid. They cannot go delinquent.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So, um, if somebody owes, uh, 2022 taxes, 2023 taxes, 2024 taxes, they cannot have their 2025 taxes come delinquent. Those are coming out in July, due in September, then the winter comes out in December and is due in February. They cannot let those taxes go delinquent, because that will stop the cycle of delinquency. By, by having this program and this agreement with the county treasurer, we are waiving those fees to help them get caught up and to also help them pay their current taxes and keep those current so that they don't come delinquent anymore. And now they're getting their delinquent taxes paid, and it all equals out. I mean, they will get caught up. You will see I handed out a spreadsheet, and on that spreadsheet, the one page says current taxes. So we have this taxpayers—they're not in Holland Township—they owe all the way back to 2020 taxes. So they will never, on the current program where they get one-year extension every year, they will never get their taxes caught up. They will always be delinquent. If you look at the second page, this is removing the interest and the fees and keeping their current taxes paid. They will have all of their taxes, their delinquent taxes that they have currently, will be paid by September 3rd, 2030. So you'll also notice, so on the first page, they've been making $250 a month payment to us. The second page shows that they're only going to be making a $100 payment to us. We're giving them that extra $150 a month to get their current taxes paid. So that way, um, you get your money sooner at the township, because otherwise you would have to wait until the county goes through the whole settlement process with Vince. Um, and knowing— I mean, we have to balance everything in the books, and then we don't pay out to Holland Township until April. Right. But by them paying their current taxes, you'll get your money before— you'll get it in September when the summer ones are due. You'll get it in February when the winter ones are due. So you wouldn't have to wait, you know, a whole other cycle. Well, in February, I mean, we would send it to you in April. So it would be a couple months later. But this program— They have to have a principal residence exemption on their property, so it can't be for just vacant property. It has to be for a home, and they have to have principal residence exemption. Like I said, it's a five-year program. If they fail to make a monthly payment, then the agreement's null and void. If they fail to keep their current taxes paid, the agreement's null and void. We do work with them though, so if they were to call us and say, hey, I'm really struggling this month, we would give them that month. But they have to have it all paid before the 5 years are up. So we can't do that all the time, and they know this because when we enter into the agreement, we're going to say, you only have This 5 years to get these paid, and we will tell them how much they have to pay every month to us to meet that deadline and get those paid.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So the advantage for the township is that current taxes continue to get paid on time?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And then to the county, you collect the back Yes. Taxes over a 5-year period of time. And then in a dual effort, everybody stays current. They have to make those payments timely.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: To keep this program going.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Mm-hmm.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So. Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Is this a program that's familiar with other county programs?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So Ingham County started it in January of this year. This law just changed, so we We are the only 2 counties in the state of Michigan at this time doing this program. Ingham County has way more people who will be applying for this program than what we have. Currently, we know of 6 property owners in the county that will qualify for this program, but we just did settle with Vince in March, so they have to have 2 years delinquent. Or 2 years of delinquency. So it could be 1 year of delinquency, 1 year of forfeiture. So with '24 taxes just coming over to our county in March, we can now take a look at everybody in Holland Township whose taxes came over delinquent and see if they also had 2023 taxes that were delinquent. And if they did, we can offer this program to them. The thing is, is that I cannot offer this program to anybody who lives in a township or a city that does not pay pass a resolution saying that you agree to participate in this program, even though it isn't going to cost you anything. The law says that I have to get resolutions from every township and city.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Go ahead.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, if we, um, pass the resolution, is it something we can opt out of if things are not going well?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah. Lifetime. No, it's not.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: No. It sounds like from what you're saying is that you're going to pay us whole and you're going to be on the hook to get their money.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So we don't really have a real downside, right? No. Township doesn't because you're going to pay us. Yeah. It's up to you to get their money. Yeah. And if the taxes go delinquent, then the agreement's null and void. Right. Right. So yeah, it seems like—

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: so you're going to front their taxes? We do now. Yeah. Actually, so when taxes go delinquent, we pay every taxing entity in April and make them all whole. We pay about $7.5 million out every year in April to all of the taxing entities to make them whole, and then we take on that debt and the responsibility for collecting that debt. So the county is going to be losing because we start charging 1% per month interest Until they get into forfeiture. Once they're in forfeiture, then they we start charging one and a half percent month a month interest. So we're not going to be charging them that interest. We also won't charge them our admin fee, which is a four percent admin fee. But we are going to withhold them from going into forfeiture. So all of the other fees that we normally add on when they come into forfeiture. Um, so we add on a $175 site visit fee. We also add on another fee for posting their— the notice in the paper that we're foreclosing on them. And, um, all these other fees that we have that we are required by law to add on, um, we won't be adding them on because we are not going to put them into forfeiture. We're not going to be expending that money either. We won't be contracting with the people to go out and post the notice on their homes or do the site visit or anything like that. So, um, it won't cost the county anything.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: So out of these 6 properties that you have, are any of them even marketable? Sometimes that's what I notice with the foreclosures, that they're— that their foreclosure sounds like, you know, 123 Any Street in downtown or in Holland Township is a great property. Well, these foreclosures, sometimes they're not really, and no one wants them.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Not necessarily, no, because a lot of times And this is shocking for a lot of people when I tell them this, but these are homes that are owned outright by the people. They don't have a mortgage on them.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: And because if they did have a mortgage on it, the mortgage company would pay their taxes. So these are homes that we could, the county could foreclose on for $3,000 of back taxes. Which is why I don't want to do that. I don't want to foreclose on anybody's home. I don't want anybody's house. If I could change the law, I would. But that's why I want to do this program, why I'm trying very hard to give taxpayers who are really struggling the opportunity to get their taxes paid and to keep their homes, to stay in their homes. Because I talk about this with my husband all the time, but this is like the best affordable housing out there. So to stay in your own house, right? I mean, so this is something that I'm very passionate about, and I really want to try to help the people who are struggling like this.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I think before you came into office—

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: There was, the state of Michigan, there was something that was passed where when the counties did the tax foreclosures on the homes, our office worked on some of those cases.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: the over the dollar amount that they sold for, county had to be reimbursed, but all the money had to go back to the person who lost the house.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Right. So that did—

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So that's still being litigated.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: It is, yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: And that's a big headache for you, isn't it?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes, it is. I understand. I see— We have been fighting those lawsuits for probably 10 years.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah. So in the old law, it said that we had to foreclose after 3 years, and we had to sell them at auction. And when we sold them at auction, it didn't say that we had to give it to the property owners. So no county in the state of Michigan did that.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: They held on to this money. And what was the benefit of having money that you make on a home at auction was that that money could be used for the county's general fund to help pay other bills, to help build a building or something like that. When we started seeing the effects of what was happening on homes being foreclosed on and people losing them for $3,000, the county treasurers in the state of Michigan got together and they changed the law so that now we have to pay out any surplus proceeds, they call it, anything over and above our fees that we have into the property and the delinquent taxes. They file a claim with us. They also submit that they have ownership rights to the property to the judge, the circuit court judge, and they decide— the judge decides how much of a percentage of the surplus proceeds they would get when we sell it at auction, if we sell it at auction for over and above what was— what the taxes were and what we had in fees.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Has the land bank signed off on it?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: The land— so this land bank does not have to sign off on this. This won't even affect—

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Because it's their funding stream, right? If they get a house, it goes to the land bank.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: We— so in Ottawa County, we haven't done that much.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: No, I know.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: But Dave, you've brought it up to the land bank, this?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: No, I have not.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Nope.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Curious. All right.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah. I mean, I am on the land bank. So, I mean, I have talked with some of the members about this program that I am offering. I've talked with our Brownfield Redevelopment Authority group because I'm also on there. Yeah. And a lot of people seem to think that they like this program because it is helping people. The land bank is still going to be able to get other properties. Really, the kind of properties that the land bank wants are the blighted ones, the ones that aren't any good. And, you know, that could still happen.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I get it.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: What— State Representative Brad Slaw, he had a program as well that he started several years ago. I believe it was If I'm— correct me if I'm wrong, because I don't remember exactly, but he and his staff, some of his staff, had gone and knocked on the doors of people that actually had been noticed. And, and it was usually veterans, the elderly, or people that didn't dare open the mail, go to the door, whatever.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: And he really made a conscious effort, and he put a program Is that still in effect?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Well, he didn't really put a program together. I actually worked for Brad. I was his chief deputy. Oh, really? Okay. Yeah. So I learned a lot from him, and because he was very good about going out. And so as soon as I became treasurer, that is the first thing that I did, because I was appointed and took office in January of '24. And we were starting— foreclosure happens the end of March. So I hit the ground running and started hitting all of these houses that were on the forfeiture list, getting ready to be foreclosed on and visited them. And I have to say, in 2024, we foreclosed on one house and one business only because those people did not get back to us. And I stopped at both of them, and they did not get a hold of us. There was a house that was— another house that was on the foreclosure list. I pulled them from foreclosure because I got a hold of them and found out that The lady who owned it was going through a divorce.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: And was in a depression and was not paying any of her bills, was not getting her daughter to school, was not making meals for her daughter, all of that stuff. So once I found that out, I got her help. I got her help through community mental health. And then we had a doctor write a letter for her, and I was able to pull her house from foreclosure. Foreclosure. So stuff like that, I, I try really hard to work with them in any way, and I have up until the day before foreclosure to pull anything from foreclosure, and I will do that if they come to see me.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Great. Yeah, thank you for that. Thank you for this program.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, thank you for explaining and going through. Thank you. Different options. And the sample resolution was on The back of your packet. I guess we could look for. We need to. You're looking for a motion from us to yes, to pass the resolution.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah. Yep.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And I think we can do that tonight. We want to consider a motion to approve what's written on the to participate in the tax foreclosure avoidance. payment reduction program in Ottawa County.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I'll support it. I mean, I'll make the motion. I'm sorry.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All support? All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any opposed, same sign.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Thank you so much. I appreciate it.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Have a good trip back.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, just be careful. Careful driving in your flip-flops.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes, I can do that.

And it's something that came up in between now and the Planning Commission approval.

_utterances.text: Did he sign? Do you have to do anything else, or can you do it on the side? He's going to sign after the meeting. Okay. All right. And then, we have the CIP improvement plan. Any other updates that you've gotten there, Steve?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, just a couple. Certainly wanted to check back in after the presentation of the '26 to '31 capital improvement program that Finance Director Smith and I did at the last meeting. Certainly welcome any comments that you might have. There are a couple as staff kind of continues to work with the document that have come forward. Just wanted to bring those to your attention. First of all, as a result of a Parks Committee meeting, a recommendation that came from them was to upgrade the lighting at the pickleball courts at Helder Park. So staff was directed to obtain Quotes for that, those came in with a replacement of all the lights as well as the poles at those court facilities came in at $170,000. So you'll see that added to the CIP for fiscal year 2026. Likewise, a project that I'd been working on with Deputy Clerk Sue Dahlman on the replacement or upgrade of our phone system. I picked that up again and wanted to get a number in there for fiscal year '26 as well. We've had the current phone system for a little over 10 years. It's been serving us well. We're not having any problems, but we don't want to press our luck. So, we're looking to put in a placeholder in 2026 for $100,000. And of course, we'll continue working on refining that. Currently with the Holland Board of Public Works, but they are going to be ending their arrangement with us for phone support. So we're also in the process of identifying a new entity that can help us out with that. And then lastly, as a result of some of the storms that we had that particularly hit hard Walkazoo Woods, we've received a lot of inquiries or comments from residents regarding outdoor warning sirens for severe weather. And so, while we evaluate the need for that and so forth, we did think it was prudent to proceed with putting $100,000 in the 26th program of the CIP. While we continue to work on that, we'll certainly work with Ottawa County Emergency Management regarding that for placement and coverage and so forth. But we did want to make that allocation as well. So those are three. revisions that you can anticipate seeing the next time you see the document.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: We don't have any alarms?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Currently, we don't have any outdoor emergency sirens, so when you hear them, that's coming from an adjoining unit of government.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: What do—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: can you explain the contract with Holland Board of Public Yes, so our current phone system is a result of a collaboration that was more than a decade old now, and it involved a number of either institutional or governmental entities. So there were some public school districts as part of it, public utilities, local units of government, and we banded together and used economies of scale to purchase this phone system. And it runs over the internet that is primarily operated by the Holland Board of Public Works, but other entities have a little bit of the fiber as well that the phone system rides on. So, that was done a little over a decade— about 12 years ago, that was put in place. And over time, different members of the coalition have left the coalition for various reasons. And one of those entities is going to be the Holland Board of Public Works.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: And they're leaving the coalition?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Right, they're leaving the coalition. They have embarked on a new phone system, far more sophisticated, that meets their business needs. And so, that impacts us in the township in a couple of ways. The most immediate is that when we set this collaboration up, Holland Board of Public Works did our support. For the phone system, so they were running the same system we were. That arrangement worked well. They no longer are. Staff has changed. There are fewer people at the Holland BPW that have familiarity with how our phone system certainly was installed and implemented, much less you know knowledge on how to troubleshoot and keep supporting it. So they've informed us, hey. You need to either find a new entity if you want to keep the system. You need to find someone who can do that. So that's the immediate task. But again, we haven't had any problems. It's a quality system. But the reality is it is getting older. Of course, the technology over a decade old is, you know, quite dated at this point. _utterances.speaker: A

Afki_PkvgIY.summary:
• **MW Watermark Industrial Facilities Exemption**: Approved unanimously for $6,186,319 in tax abatement over 12 years for manufacturing expansion (high bay facility). Company manufactures water/wastewater treatment equipment, currently employs 70 with plans to add 10 more.
• **Capital Improvement Plan (6-year)**: Presented FY2026-2031 expenditures including $4.5M (down from $9.4M as community center completes). Major projects: Pump Station 31 (total $24M with $20M grant), 120th Avenue sewer extension ($7M over 3 phases), parks improvements ($1.4M in 2026 including Quincy Park expansion, Peachwood pickleball renovation, Helder improvements).
• **Financial Reports**: New Finance Director Warren (from Plainfield Twp) presented May spending: sheriff patrol $189K, Vredeveld Hefner auditors $17K, water/sewer operations ongoing. Emphasized CIP numbers are draft/subject to change pending bids.
• **Elections**: Approved relocation of Precinct 6 from Fire Station 2 to Community Center (better space for growth, less disruption). Approved moving Precinct 1 to Township Hall for August 6 election (cost savings).
• **Parks/Facilities Approvals**: (1) Boys & Girls Club license agreement for July 17 fundraiser at Dutton Park with alcohol service; (2) Ransom Booster Station roof replacement ($39,870 + 10% contingency); (3) Shuffleboard courts refurbishment at Peachwood ($28,690 with potential $5K addition for pickleball court crack filling).
• **Notable Discussion**: Board members raised cost control concerns—requested Pryne & Newhoff competitive bidding analysis, discussed revenue opportunities for parks/community center, noted township population exceeded 40,500 (significant growth vs. neighbors).

And we said, well, at least could you go back and ask them if they can cut their price? Just by going back and asking, right, I think the price came down from $70,000 to $40,000.

We have over $350 million worth of investment in the ground or supporting the ground, and that does not include the water towers. I don't have— I don't remember what— I think that's another $200 million worth of investment in there.

highlights.topic: Shuffleboard as last major outdoor court in West Michigan
highlights.quote: We are the last outdoor public large bean ton left in West Michigan.
You can have wonderful facilities, but if someone comes in and you know, has a negative experience on the person side, they're not coming back, no matter how good the facilities are.
When you look at that number and you compare it to our neighbors... our rate of growth is— it's surprising how we're pulling away as we get farther into the decade.
It will give the precinct more space as it grows, which it's some phenomenal growth in Precinct 6. And also, it'll be less of a disruption now that we've added full-time firefighters to that station.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We have received the application for an industrial facilities exemption certificate from MW Watermark in the amount of $6,186,000. $6,319 for construction costs. And that is the total cost. There's no other monies involved in that. This district was established on October 3rd, 1996, and the request is for 12 years. So, do you have a representative here?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And we're going to officially open the public meeting, so.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I'm Christine Gethin. My husband and I own Watermark. Thank you, Mr. Chair, or Vice President. So we're applying for this abatement. We have an expansion going on, um, to— we had to create a high bay because we were building bigger equipment and we needed a 45-foot high bay instead of 25 feet.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Mm-hmm.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We couldn't load the equipment under our hook height. So that's why it's being built. Um, we're scheduled to be in it by October 1st.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We've been in business since October, and things are going well.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We currently employ 70 people. We're going to keep those people, and we're hoping to add about 10 more over the next couple years.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Great. What kind of things do you manufacture?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: We manufacture water and wastewater equipment, so we're an environmental company. We manufacture filter presses, Clarifiers, sludge dryers, polymarks. We manufacture our own filter cloths that go in them. We actually get the rolled goods and we produce everything at our facility.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: What's your shipping range?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Global. We have presses all over the world. But we mostly, mostly in the southern United States. We have them everywhere for all kinds of applications. Cruise ship on an island where it empties midway through, beer making, military, egg washing at Skipper Eggs, you know, just anything that you need to— you can't throw it in our environment. Wolverine really needed us a few years back. So, you know, You don't put any of where you can't get it in the groundwater. So that's what we do.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Is it pretty much— I'm sorry.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, go ahead.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Is it pretty much a custom shop then?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: It is.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Everything is made to order. Everything is. I mean, there's a similar footprint, but it might have air hydraulics, it might have manual, it might have a cloth washer, it might have a lot of bells and whistles. We build anything, the filter presses, In particular, our bread and butter. They can go from this size all the way up to the size of a semi-trailer, and that's called a trailer press. And then we're hoping to get into a little bit of even bigger that could be involved in mining, and that's— we just need more space. We've outgrown our space, but we have It goes around all different sizes, just for whatever their application is.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I just have one more. Do you know of any first responders that work at your place?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Not officially. I mean, we used to have actual first responders, but we have first responders that we have had trained. I mean, like, we've actually had people that were part of a fire department volunteer before. But we have first responders on site.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: We have a volunteer— Well, that's a question that we ask all of the IFC people that come in, is if you did have first responders working for you, would you let them go in case of an emergency? Would you let them go?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Oh, we would, we would, and we did in the past. We don't have a volunteer fireman anymore on staff, but we actually train our people, and we have first responders in our building for what would happen.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: So, did someone in your family start the business then, or what's the story? I see it's a family-owned business.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah, my husband did. Started it in '03, and it started as a parts business of aftermarket parts for this kind of equipment, because that's kind of his background. And he got busier after about 6 months and asked me to come join him, and 22 years later, here we are. So we've grown into— this is our 3rd facility. We started on Greenlee Street over by Donnelly Drive across from Best Buy in a little— and then we moved, uh, where, uh, JR Automation is. That's still our building. National Bulk rents from us.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Oh, okay.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: That was 10,000 square feet. And then we moved up the road to Charterhouse, 72,000.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: So you just filter out anything that really needs to be filtered?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Is really what you're doing?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yes, because it creates between the filter presses, And then you get a sludge, a cake.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Hmm.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: It dewater it, whatever you're trying to dewater, and then it drops the cake. And the water that comes out of it, you wouldn't want to drink it, but you can put it in the earth because it dewatered all the stuff out of it. So now it cuts down on people's disposal costs, getting rid of it. Sometimes the sludge that actually comes out of the filter press is the person's product. That's what they use. It's not always the other way around, you would think. So I mean, we— an example is military getting the oils and gases, and they used to put them in 55-gallon drums in Vancouver, and they were all over the side of the road, and then they started tossing them in the— in Colorado.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Well, they started getting an idea to clean it up, Now, not only did we clean up Colorado, but we've also cleaned up Vancouver. And so, it's just— because you don't have to throw barrels of crap on the sides of the road, you know what I mean? So, and that's, you know, so stuff like that you wouldn't even think about. And car washes, now that they're popping up because they reuse the water, so they don't have to pay for so much, and they're going through filter presses.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Is Quality or Tommy's one of your customers?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Not here locally, no.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C

Um, you know, there's a pretty big jump in calls for service. That 1,532 that you see there, my math is correct, is about a 19% increase over April of last year. And certainly you can look at the various components to that. I just wanted to point out a couple of things. On the, the front sheet there in the lower left-hand corner, the speed trailer deployment, and you see a speed trailer sitting there. You may recall that several years back the township purchased its own speed trailer, and so that has now been taken out of storage and charged and is ready for deployment. So certainly this is helpful should you receive comments or complaints from neighbors regarding speeding either in subdivisions or on roadways. Certainly those can be forwarded to me, or you can send them on to Sergeant Bennett. And it is popular, so if you call, it's not— doesn't mean that you're going to get the trailer showing up the next week, but you know, you'll certainly be placed on the list. One thing, if you are talking with someone, that the Sheriff's Office really does appreciate is if the complainant is willing to have the trailer on their property, because when they do, you know, bring it in, they have to put it somewhere safe, of course. And so often I have found when I've talked with people who have complained, they're motivated and they're often very willing to allow the trailer to be placed on their property as well. You know, typically it's a week. It might go a little bit longer, can, depending on how long the list is. And we paid for the upgrade, so it is collecting some speed data and time of day data as well to help enforcement see if there's a pattern at all that then possibly can be followed up with enforcement as well. So just wanted to point that out. And then Holland Township was the location of a high-visibility traffic enforcement effort on April 30th, and it's noted there on the first page as well. Happy to answer any questions regarding the, the report.

We trust what people are doing here, but as far as fully understanding it, it's not going to happen for me.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I'm just going to say, along that same line, I do think that we are Very interested in finding out if there are ways to eliminate wasteful spending. So I think we're gonna be hopefully looking at that a little closer down the road, but I do think that, you know, the whole DOJ thing makes a lot of sense to me.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Mm-hmm.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: And I think there's an opportunity there on the local level as well. So I'm with that line of questioning, and I wanna think, I wanna be, I want to have an open, open mind on that. So I agree.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right, thank you. Then the Part D is review of the board meeting minutes. And look for a motion to approve the consent agenda.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion to approve.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'll support it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Support it. All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any opposed, same sign. All right, unanimous. Warren, you ready to— you and Steve tag team a little bit on the CIP?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes, we are.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's right, looking for the 6-year capital improvement.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: So I'll lead off here and, and get us started. So you should have at your place setting a hard copy of what's on the monitor as well. So, as in past years, I'll talk a little bit about some of the general government items, and then Warren will talk about some of the public works projects. So if you had a chance to take a look at it, you may have noticed that in FY 2026, you know, the document contemplates expenditures of about $4.5 million, which is a pretty significant drop from the $9.4. And of course, that's as a result, as Warren said, we're winding down on expenditures around the community center. And so that won't be part of the fiscal picture for next year, for FY26. So that's the main reason for that. I always want to direct your attention in the document itself to page 2, which I always felt is kind of a summary of, you know, what is going on on the general government side. We'll go through a number of those items as it relates to FY26 as we go through the presentation here. So, but that dramatic drop in terms of expenditures anticipated in the capital improvement program is, is a result of finishing up the community center. So road construction, which you can find on page 8 of the document, you'll notice there that is estimated at $1 million for FY26.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'm sorry.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: It stays that way in '27 as well. Then I bumped it up to $1.1 million for '28 and '29, and then it goes up again to $1.2 million for '30 and '31, which is the out year of the CIP. As you may recall, there were a number of years that was much higher, around the $1.5 million. At that time, we were not only resurfacing subdivision roads, but we were also— Providing funding to the Road Commission to resurface local roads versus primary roads. And as the, you know, the millage that was voter approved— and I think the Road Commission has done an excellent job of being stewards of that millage— you know, a lot of those local Road Commission roads are getting done with millage money now. So I think we can really start to pull back and focus primarily on the subdivision roads, which is going to be the township's responsibility. And as I stated when I brought the recommended list of projects this year, you know, we're doing a good job of keeping up with those. I mean, the package this year was around $500,000, so we'll see what it comes back— that was the estimate. We'll see what the bid comes back with. So that $1 million that's anticipated there should be fine for this year's project. And I anticipate, you know, being able to focus on the subdivision roads. I think $1 million and then those—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Jim?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Gradual increases is going to be fine to cover what the focus is for the township as it relates to roads. On page 5, there is an overview of what we're intending to do with the non-motorized pathway network. So you'll notice when you take a look at it, the focus of the CIP is now almost exclusively on the existing system. As I've stated before, we're at about 72 miles. We have an aging system. I think we've talked a little bit when we're renewing the millage that we're getting a larger and larger percentage of our resurfacing projects are becoming reconstruction projects. We constructed them, we did one overlay, the engineers tell us you can't do a second overlay, so we need to rip out the pavement, repair the base. That's more expensive. And so this CIP really leans into preserving and enhance— or preserving and keeping in good shape the existing system. So you only see a few years out. I think it's out in 20— yes, in '29, you know, there is discussion about a new project up in the McIntowa Legends area. But otherwise, you'll see that it's all being— that $800,000 annually is being allocated to preservation of the existing system.

_utterances.text: No, that is a design. Those types of situations, and I'm, yeah, familiar with what you're talking about. That is a design challenge. Yeah, yeah, that is, that is tricky, definitely.
