And we'll again in the presentation come back to those in just a second. So talking about this, um, the community center, uh, if you haven't had a chance to get there recently, it looks really, really, really different than the last time we went through as a group. Want to stop by tonight after the meeting, happy to let you in there. But the athletic flooring is going in. And I'll tell you, that really changes the appearance of the place, as the photo here demonstrates. At the time this photo was taken, the pickleball courts in the upper right corner of this picture weren't installed or weren't painted in. They have been now, so there's more color on the western part of the athletic area as well. So it's really coming along well, as the slide indicates. You know, that project was Roughly $13.6 million. $12.9 million of that was on the construction side. The remainder was really FFE, furniture, fixtures, and equipment going into that. As Warren mentioned, it's coming along very nicely. We're looking at a midsummer opening for it, and we really are winding down on the, on the types of things that are, are going in place— athletic flooring, of course, some of the last items that go in. And we, you know, hope to be doing punch lists here at the end of this month, maybe the first part of next month. And after that, get it open. Happened to make an offer to another community center staff person today, which she accepted. So we're staffing up on that regard as well. And Ben Beekhuyzen, the community center director, has just done a fabulous job of going out and finding folks. That are— we're going to have to have in place. He comes from the retail world, and, you know, we're building this fantastic facility, and I'm thinking, well, everyone's going to love it. It's new, and we have all these amenities and so forth. And he's very good at reminding me that you can have wonderful facilities, but if someone comes in and, you know, has a negative experience on the person side, they're not coming back, no matter how good the facilities are. So he's very customer— oriented and focused in that regard and is building a team around him that shares that same philosophy. So that's where we're at with the community center. And then, as promised, um, you know, again, page 3 if you want to take a look at some of the specifics of the parks. But at Helder in '26, you may be able to visualize that we have netting at the softball outfields that protect, uh, hit balls from the adjoining homes, and that is getting quite old and needs to be replaced. So we're intending to do so next year at an estimated cost of $112,000. And we're also this year intending to get the middle— what I call middle and western lots of the 3 major lots at Helder, the middle one and the western one there, not associated with Cott but the main part of the park. Looking to get those done. The parks are so busy that we— it was a challenge to find a window when we could actually shut it down because the contractor, to get better bids, you know, we're doing the driveway and that middle and western lots. But we found a window, so we hope the contractor can do the work within that window, and we'll get that done. The estimate right now is $475,000. Over at Quincy, you'll start to see in '26 allocations towards a major $2.5 million investment in Quincy, which is going to result in the addition of a softball field, as well as, as well as having a permanent restroom on the eastern end of the park. We have one in the western end of the park adjacent to the Little League fields, but we'd like to get restrooms as well as water. into that eastern end where we have a lot of softball tournaments and so forth. Some additional parking when we do have tournaments— people are parking all over there, so we want to add some parking. And we're hoping to have some multi-purpose open space, uh, an enhanced— we have open space now, but it would be an enhanced space for various activities on the eastern end of the park as well, adjacent to the driveway off of Quincy. Again, $500,000 in '26, $1 million in '27, another million in '28, and then construction in '28 is the plan for that. Peachwood pickleball continues to be, you know, very popular, and our courts are getting quite deteriorated. In fact, when we converted the tennis courts to pickleball courts a couple years ago, the contractor was very transparent saying, you know, this is a couple-year fix. We'll skin them, we'll repair the cracks, we'll skin them, but this is not something that's going to last 10 years. They never said that. It's true, we're starting to see some cracking in there. At that time we said let's make this initial investment, we'll see what the response from the community is and from the public for the usage of them. Um, it's been, you know, strong. And so we are planning to move forward with the complete renovation of the existing pickleball courts and looking at the possibility of expansion of the courts as well. And '26, at a cost that's estimated at $750,000. Some other park equipment noted there. We're doing some resurfacing of the parking lot at the parks building itself. And looking at those numbers, again, it is a draft. Now that we've got better numbers on what the resurfacing at Helder's is going to be, we're going to have to make an adjustment to the surfacing of the Parks Building parking lot.

_utterances.speaker: D

We are always looking at surveillance upgrades and improvements. You know, we know we have gaps in our parks. Uh, that aren't, uh, being surveyed where we want to get surveillance in there. So there's an allocation for that as well, um, as part of the 2026 program. Page 10, you'll find where some expenditures for the fire department are going to be, and for 2026, those equal about $582,000. A couple of the major components of that $582,000 Is the acquisition of another medical response vehicle. You're seeing that we're running about 150 to 170 to 180 calls monthly. The fire department is tracking for us how many times those overlap, and the thinking is having a second medical response vehicle to handle that when we have those overlaps with the kind of numbers that we're seeing. Uh, is beneficial. So $104,000 has been set aside for that. The other large expenditure in '26 is $350,000 for dorms. So when we constructed the Public Safety Building on James Street, we put dormitories in there. That was forward-thinking on Chief Kozel's part because today we have a full-time department and we use them. When Station 1 on Riley was constructed A space was, um, constructed that was intended to be dormitories. Um, the assumption at the time that was built that there was only going to be one sex there, uh, we're likely going to have both sexes at the— that facility, and, and thus we need to do some renovations there to make that possible. So that is what that $350,000 is intended to do. Um, then we're envisioning that we will be splitting fire department personnel between the 2 facilities for better response. So like other cities with full-time fire departments have. So we just need to renovate the, the dorms space over at Station 1 to get ready for that in preparation. And then some miscellaneous HVAC equipment going on various uh, buildings in the amount of $78,300 for '26. Um, speaking of other renovations, uh, question on the park stuff. Yeah, you bet.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yeah, so, um, the Parks Department— so we, you know, Well, to back it up, we have a 5-year recreation plan. So the Parks Committee works with MCSA, our consultant, in the development of a 5-year recreation program. So that's, that's kind of the basis for that, where we think and talk about what we're going to do. And then that too is laid out by year, so we have some idea as to how projects will flow and when they'll happen. And then, so that is the base, and then staff takes that and then works with the Parks committee to say, okay, these are the next things around that, or other things that come forward from either users of the park or staff. Park staff come back and say, hey, you should really think about this or think about that. So that's another means in which items come to the Parks Committee. So then the Parks Committee goes through the CIP and the projects that we're talking about here. They may make some recommendations or adjustments that are reflected in, in here. So that's kind of the process.

H1zXJRuanuw.summary:
**Planning Commission Meeting – May 12, 2026**

- **Lantern Village PUD (Quincy Street)**: Preliminary Planned Unit Development for 60 rental townhomes and single-family homes. Commission determined the proposal does not meet PUD qualifying conditions; **tabled unanimously (7-0)** pending fire chief review of circulation/access concerns, water table analysis, parallel plan comparison, and redesign addressing density, parking, green space, and sidewalk connectivity issues.

- **Crossings Court Amendment**: Final development phase amendment approved unanimously (7-0) with completion deadline of June 24, 2028; referred to township board.

- **Chick-fil-A Drive-Thru Expansion (2332 North Park)**: Site plan approved pending conditions—Ottawa County water approval, landscaping plan with 24-inch shrubs, and recorded easement with Quality Car Wash (2352 North Park). Debate over 10-foot vs. 9-foot drive-through lane width deferred to staff/zoning administrator; motion carried (7-0).

- **Quality Car Wash Encroachment (2352 North Park)**: Site plan modification approved (7-0) with same conditions. PUD request withdrawn by applicant.

- **Battery Energy Storage System Ordinance**: Draft presented for commission feedback. Key provisions include: on-site systems permitted by right; off-site systems require special land use (A, I1, I2 zones); 100-foot setbacks, 5-acre minimum, 16-foot height limit; decommissioning bond required with four-year review cycle. Commission requested attorney review; scheduled for June 2 final check before public hearing.

- **Public Comment**: Steve Willard presented proposal to modify residential street parking ban; staff noted road authority jurisdiction.

3q1nBWk9wM0.summary:
• **Capstone Project Withdrawn**: The capstone development proposal was withdrawn and will not be discussed further; no public hearing was held.
• **352 Beline Road Zoning Amendment (R2 to R2A)**: Planning Commission unanimously recommended approval to change the transitional housing facility zoning from R2 (moderate density) to R2A (medium density residential), allowing flexibility for single-family homes, townhouses, or multifamily units. Motion passed 5-0.
• **Berrs Hardware Text Amendment (C1 Outdoor Displays)**: Motion to recommend approval of a text amendment allowing seasonal outdoor displays in C1 district (April 1–October 31) with special use permit requirement. Vote: 4-1 (Randy Cordering no). Staff raised concerns about loss of setback guardrails; commission noted they would add safeguards in future ordinance updates to maintain consistency across all C1 properties.
• **Crossings Court PUD Amendment**: Planning Commission recommended approval for contractor facility and warehouse uses on the east lot (subject to 2026 C2 ordinance standards, no residential dwellings, no hazardous material storage). Applicant withdrew the pending December 2025 application in favor of this revised approach. Vote: 5-0. Bill Sickle's historical argument about 2016 meeting minutes influenced discussion but commissioners emphasized expired PUDs must be re-evaluated under current zoning rules.
• **Meyer Gas Station Special Use Permit**: Planning Commission approved outdoor sales display permit (firewood, bottled water, windshield washer fluid, propane) with conditions: no merchandise at gas pump islands, no palletized displays, and parking blocks required near display areas. Vote: 5-0.
• **Housing & Development Discussion**: Commissioners discussed challenges attracting owner-occupied residential development; builders indicated infrastructure costs and land prices—not lot sizes—are primary barriers. No immediate action; planning to meet with community development director from Lakeshore Advantage.
• **Oversized Accessory Buildings**: Zoning Board of Appeals requested Planning Commission consider allowing larger detached accessory buildings on larger properties (sliding scale). Staff to analyze how many residential parcels over 2 acres exist to gauge impact; added to work queue for future consideration.
• **Battery Energy Storage Systems**: Commission voted to proceed with a local "incompatible/workable" ordinance rather than defaulting to state rules (PA 233), to enable more developer engagement and local control. Kate to draft ordinance with setback comparisons from other communities and input from Ottawa County Water Resource Commissioner; special meeting likely needed in May to thoroughly discuss.

- **Tulip Tower Approved**: Planning Commission approved a Special Land Use Permit for a 30-foot tower ride at Dutch Village (12350 James Street) as a one-for-one replacement of an existing ride. The ride features four chairs, is motor-assisted and manually powered, and will be the first Michigan installation of this German-manufactured attraction. Vote: 5-0 in favor (Randy Cordery, Leo Barahas, Angela Husman, Gretchen Malaki, Evan Sharp all yes).
- **Conditional Approval**: Approval stipulated that the owner must complete a maintenance agreement with Ottawa County Water Resources Commission within 30 days and that an existing pirate ship structure must obtain ZBA variance approval.
- **Katy Coaster Withdrawn**: Applicant amended request to remove the Katy Coaster (a refurbished pumper car coaster) from tonight's approval due to lack of detailed plans. Applicant will resubmit with better specifications for future approval.
- **Other Projects Deferred**: Outdoor Discovery Center water play attraction, gazebo, boathouse, and demonstration hall were discussed but applicant clarified only the tulip tower is being sought for approval tonight. Future proposals will require more detailed site plans with dimensions.
- **Timeline & Pressure**: Applicant emphasized urgent need (less than one month before opening day) which prompted special Wednesday 5:30 p.m. meeting. Staff noted fire chief raised access/egress concerns but acknowledged they are less relevant for a one-for-one ride swap rather than expansion.
- **Past Compliance**: Applicant addressing legacy issues including pirate ship setback violations from 8 years ago, now being cleaned up through ZBA process.

B0a_i3kPui8.summary:
• **Quincy Street Zoning Amendment (R2A)**: Planning Commission voted 5-0 to recommend approval of rezoning ~9 acres from Agricultural to R2A (medium density residential, 5-10 units/acre). Applicant Dan Larabel presented a proposed 60-unit single-family rental development with 1,600 sq ft units, garages, and pond frontage—lower density than the master plan's high-density designation. Public comment from neighboring property owners raised concerns about traffic (4-600 additional cars daily), wildlife impacts, trash in county drain, light/noise pollution, and proximity to property lines. Staff and commissioners discussed traffic management, potential roundabout at intersection, and deferred parking agreements.

• **Quincy Place Senior Living Phase 3 (Special Land Use Permit)**: Planning Commission voted 5-0 to approve expansion of 34 independent living units and 9 memory care units. Applicant noted high occupancy (near 100%), waiting lists for certain units, and planned demolition of underutilized dog park. Deferred parking agreement approved contingent on Ottawa County Drain Commissioner approval and agreement on parking trigger conditions.

• **Balman Building Townhomes (10th Unit Amendment)**: Planning Commission voted 5-0 to approve site plan amendment adding one unit (9 to 10 townhomes) with requirement for 5-6 foot green buffer strips with trees between driveway sections to reduce appearance of "massive concrete" and improve aesthetics.

• **R1 Housing Gap Discussion**: Multiple applicants and commissioners discussed shortage of low-density R1 housing (5 units/acre). Constraints identified: sewer/water availability and property availability; builders noted single-family rentals (1,600 sq ft detached/semi-detached) are more economical and market-ready than traditional R1. Housing study completed September 2025 shows 1,500 acres designated for R1 but minimal development.

• **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)**: Introductory discussion on utility-scale (50+ MW) and smaller BESS facilities. Staff presented framework following state CREOLE standards; commissioners emphasized need for clarity on setbacks, fire/thermal safety, utility interconnection, groundwater protection, and potential overlay district approach. Monitoring Port Sheldon Township and City of Holland parallel efforts.

• **Dave DeYoung**: No specific items relevant to Township Trustee Dave DeYoung were mentioned in this Planning Commission meeting transcript.

pA2je7rnvp8.summary:
**Planning Commission Meeting – February 3, 2026**

- **Uncle Jib Camper Rental (12330 James Street)**: Special land use permit approved 6–0 to allow up to 9 travel trailer rentals in a designated parking area (84 ft × 36 ft). Applicant clarified vetting process, insurance, and safety protocols. Staff noted prior public notice listed 6–9 units; motion locked approval at 9 to avoid a second hearing.

- **Green Mark Equipment (11483 Lakewood Boulevard)**: Special land use permit approved 6–0 with conditions: outdoor turf equipment display in "Area A" (front) limited to 7 ft height; stacked storage area capped at 8 ft height. SwGPWJxAtxY.summary:
**Holland Charter Township Planning Commission Meeting – January 6, 2026**

- **Officer Elections**: Randy Cordering elected Chairman; Jack Vander Mueller elected Vice Chairman (unanimous).

- **Comprehensive Plan Amendment**: Approved 7–0 to incorporate 2025 housing analysis as Chapter 8. No changes to land-use map; future discussions on infrastructure and alternative zoning approaches planned.

- **Chick-fil-A Preliminary PUD (Drive-Thru Expansion)**: **TABLED** 7–0. Applicant requested PUD approval for dual drive-thru lane. Commission expressed support for the project's benefits but raised concerns about: (1) whether PUD is the correct legal tool (suggested ZBA variance instead), (2) two separate property owners with unified control complications, (3) impact on Quality Car Wash's future operations if linked in one PUD, (4) insufficient demonstration of four required "recognizable benefits," and (5) staff attorney disagreement on standards. Commission requested: clarification that both owners understand implications, guidance from township attorney on managing multi-entity PUD, and consideration of ZBA alternative before resubmission.

- **Wetland Mitigation Bank (NW Quincy)**: Approved 7–0 for special land use permit (34 acres). Conditions: (1) gravel parking area for maintenance vehicles (off Quincy), (2) site plan showing pathways on BMS for potential future public use. Neighbors expressed drainage concerns; applicant confirmed coordination with EGLE and Ottawa County water resource commission.

- **Guardian Jiu-Jitsu Relocation**: Approved 7–0 special land use permit for martial arts training facility expansion (4,000 to 10,500 sq ft) within Holland Town Center (Gapsold location).

- **Sinclair Recreation Parking Lot & Fencing**: Approved 7–0 special land use permit for outdoor storage area (fenced, screened) on west side of building. Pickle ball court removed from plan per staff recommendation.

- **Notable Process Item**: Chick-fil-A discussion consumed ~2 hours; commission discussed potential time limits on future meetings but deferred decision.

4CUrZ-MwXr4.summary:
**Holland Charter Township Planning Commission Meeting – November 4, 2025**

- **Comprehensive Housing Study**: Commission voted unanimously (6-0) to recommend approval of the updated comprehensive plan incorporating a new housing analysis chapter. The study confirms the township's current plan is adequate to meet projected housing demand through 2040 (42,000–45,000 residents), provided the township maintains approximately 250 housing units per year of construction. Staff noted the need for ongoing dialogue with developers about housing types (e.g., single-family homes vs. rentals) and potential zoning adjustments to encourage ownership housing and address affordability gaps.

- **Emanuel Church Special Land Use & Expansion**: Commission approved three-phase expansion (pole barn, office/youth space addition, and picnic shelter with restrooms) with conditions: construction must begin within one year; staff to review parking layout and aesthetic details; outdoor activities/services permitted if non-disruptive to neighbors; temporary barriers required for outdoor sales areas. Fire code issue regarding Phase 3 (two-hour fire wall separation) was resolved by applicant.

- **Wetland Mitigation Banks & Front Yard Definition**: Commission unanimously recommended approval of zoning text amendments (Articles 9 and 22) to define wetland mitigation banks as a permitted use in agricultural districts and clarify front yard setback requirements (referenced to lot lines rather than adjacent buildings). EGLE (state) was consulted; language added to allow non-motorized trails and public amenities unless planning commission prohibits.

- **Lowe's Outdoor Display Expansion**: Approved outdoor sales/display of non-combustible items (mowers, tillers, grills, etc.) with explicit exclusion of side-by-side vehicles and golf carts (which would require separate vehicle sales permit). Parking and barriers to be reviewed by staff per existing standards.

- **Backyard Chickens Ordinance**: Commission recommended approval of text amendment allowing up to 6 chickens per household in R1 and R2 residential zones, with setback requirements matching accessory buildings (7 ft. from side lot lines, 5 ft. rear). Permit process details (terminology, inspection requirements, fees) left to township board/staff to finalize; no roosters permitted; indoor slaughtering only.

- **Mac Legends PUD Amendment & Other Business**: Approved PUD amendment for Phase 1 (expiration date December 31, 2033). Staff updates included upcoming Gearlings project review (bridge removal decision), Lemon Fresh building demolition funded by blight grant, and initial planning for mixed-use development on 128th Street.

- **Makatawa Legends PUD Amendment (Fairway View Phase 2)**: Approved unanimously. Adds one net lot (69 total), adjusts lot widths, creates road connection around pond south loop. Key condition: entire roadway paving required within two years of first building permit. Sidewalk relocation approved.
- **Salvation Army Special Land Use (104 Clover Avenue)**: Approved 7-0 with significant conditions after extended debate. Permits expanded services including community meal program (1.5 hrs/day, Mon-Fri), shower facilities (up to 8 hrs/week, no later than 5pm), food pantry. Conditions restrict overnight warming/cooling center (except by emergency manager request), no outdoor storage, limit services to 5pm, implement sidewalk on their property. Community raised safety and neighborhood impact concerns; countered by 21 support letters and strong community backing.
- **Advantage Marine Boat Display (483 Douglas)**: Approved unanimously. Allows outdoor boat display in southeast corner parking lot with conditions: maintain 26-foot passageway for fire access, boat drop-off/pickup limited to daytime hours (no 24-hour outdoor storage).
- **Contractor Facilities/Industrial Suites (High visibility corner near US-31)**: Approved 5-2 with restrictions. Two-phase 24,000 sq ft buildings housing up to 11 contractor suites. Conditions prohibit hazardous material storage, outdoor storage, overnight habitation, RVs, boats, and personal hobby shops (e.g., classic car mechanics). Intent is job creation, not recreational storage. Site plan approved pending staff review and county agency approvals, limited to Phase 1 only.
- **Administrative items**: Lowes negotiation reached middle ground with fire marshal; wetland mitigation bank and definition amendments moving to November; comprehensive plan with housing study incorporation scheduled for presentation November 4, with board acceptance target January 15.

wNyhMwJMoD8.summary:
• **Housing Study Presentation & Approval**: McKenna consultants presented comprehensive 2025 housing market analysis showing Holland Township is at supply-demand parity currently but faces potential shortage if migration rates increase; 70/30 ownership-to-rental split projected; demand for 3,000-5,000 new owner-occupied units and 800-1,200 rental units by 2040. Commission unanimously approved the housing study as presented.

• **Comprehensive Plan Intent to Plan Letter**: Commission authorized staff to draft and send intent-to-plan letter to surrounding municipalities and designated agencies, incorporating the housing study into the master plan update. Public hearing expected in November; board approval targeted by year-end.

• **Key Housing Issues Discussed**: Single-family home construction down 45% since 2019 (from 100 to 55 units/year) while rental units increased from 24 to 178; affordability crisis noted with only 35% of households able to afford new homes in 2025 vs. 70% in mid-2020s; commissioners discussed lot size reductions, accessory dwelling units (ADUs), and low-income housing options as potential policy responses.

• **Zoning & Development Decisions**: Approved rezoning of Butternut Drive property from C2 to R2A for 4-unit duplex; approved special use permit for outdoor equipment display at Rena Center; tabled Greenmark Equipment special use permit (outdoor storage/display) pending revised site plan addressing setback violations and sidewalk access concerns; approved contractor facility special use permit at 188 North Jefferson with 10-foot front yard requirement.

• **Quincy Place Senior Living Expansion**: Approved special use permit for 20 independent living duplex units (reduced from originally proposed 24 units); site plan conditionally approved pending water resource commissioner approval, fire truck turning radius verification, building setback adjustment to 25 feet from sidewalk, landscaping plan, and easement clarification for future cross-access to Beline.

• **Community Action House Site Plan**: Approved improvements to Riley Street facility including donation drop-off canopy, compactor/dumpster relocation to comply with 50-foot residential setback, parking layout revisions, landscaping and lighting plans required; facility will include retail, sorting/warehouse operations with minimal truck traffic (0-5 box truck pickups daily, larger truck twice weekly).

• **Other Notable Items**: Lakewood Place Apartments (multi-family development) showing strong progress with buildings framed and interior construction underway; text amendments pending on front yard definitions (US-31/196/Chicago Drive access issues) and wetland mitigation banking use; chicken keeping ordinance amendment tabled pending township board input on coop inspection and maintenance standards.

**June 3, 2025 Holland Charter Township Planning Commission Meeting**

- **188 North Jefferson Avenue Rezone (I2 to C2):** Approved conditional rezone from industrial to commercial with lot line adjustment. Building operator to restrict uses (no gas stations, car washes, drive-throughs). Lot split preserving adjacent residential house. Passed unanimously.

- **Comprehensive Plan Discussion:** Confirmed current plan remains adequate with focus on housing analysis incorporation (waiting for Ottawa County study due June 17). Future discussion needed on employment/industrial zoning opportunities. No Trustee Dave DeYoung attendance noted.

**Zoning Board of Appeals Meeting - April 28, 2026**

- **650 Riley Street (3D Action House)**: Board approved variance for electronic message board (ECM) on existing pylon sign with conditions: (1) eliminate illegal lower sign panels to reduce to 75.5 sq ft, (2) ECM cannot change more than once per 60 seconds, (3) ECM positioned on bottom section of sign, (4) all permits required, (5) inspection required for illumination compliance. - **167 Burke Avenue (Lloyd Sting)**: Board denied variance request for 24x36 pull barn (864 sq ft) exceeding detached building limits. Applicant cited wildlife damage from nearby development. Board found request did not meet variance standards—particularly noting applicant had alternative options under ordinance (expand existing garage to 832 sq ft or add 480 sq ft detached building). Vote: 5-0 denial. Discussion centered on precedent-setting and spirit of ordinance.

Purpose: reduce traffic stacking on North Park Drive and improve safety/operations. Votes: both 5-0 approval. Condition: Chick-fil-A responsible for maintaining buffer plantings. Staff and board noted unique circumstances—two property owners solving shared traffic problem, safety benefit, and denser (not sparse) landscape replacement.

• **Mason Street Barn Variance (Dave Timberman)**: ZBA approved two variances for a proposed 3,072 sq ft, 22'8" tall accessory building on an 11.76-acre property (two parcels to be combined). Ordinance allows maximum 720 sq ft and 16 ft height. Applicant intends mixed use: agricultural equipment storage (four tractors), hobby/recreation space, and potential recreational vehicle storage—declined to sign agricultural-only affidavit.
• **Vote & Conditions**: Both variances (size and height) passed unanimously (3-0). Conditions: (1) exterior siding/roofing must be complimentary to principal residence, reviewed by zoning administrator; (2) two parcels must be legally joined to create 11.76-acre parcel; (3) building placed exactly as shown on site plans.
• **Key Deliberation Points**: Board emphasized exceptional lot size (unusually large for township), topography/natural screening preventing visibility from adjacent properties, proportionality (barn ~1/3 size of 6,000-7,000 sq ft house), and strong neighbor support (closest neighbor Mark Brink called it "a good buffer"). No public objections received.
• **Precedent Consideration**: Board referenced prior 2017 approval for Meisner property (2,400 sq ft, 28 ft height, 5 acres) but noted that occurred under previous ordinance (pre-May 2018). Board directed staff to request Planning Commission review zoning ordinance to consider larger accessory buildings on larger parcels (sliding scale approach).
• **Tabled Items**: Two other agenda items (Chick-fil-A and car wash variances) remain tabled.
• **No Trustee Dave DeYoung involvement noted** in transcript.

**Zoning Board of Appeals Meeting – November 18, 2025**

- **Approved Minutes:** August 2025 minutes approved by roll call (3-1, one abstention).

- **Case 1: 2490 Van Drive (Jeremy Fog/Angel's Car Service) – PARTIALLY APPROVED**
  - **Item 1 (Sideyard Setback Variance):** APPROVED by roll call (3-1 no)
  - **Item 2 (Storage Sideyard Setback):** APPROVED by roll call (3-1 yes)
  - **Item 3 (Outdoor Storage Front Setback):** APPROVED with a 30-foot variance (setback reduced from 50 ft to 20 ft) by roll call (4-0)
  - **Item 4 (Vehicle Sales & Display Setback):** APPROVED with 10-foot front yard setback requirement and staff coordination on plantings, by roll call (4-0)
  - **Key Issues:** Two front yards (US 31 and Benomomen), existing building constraints, need to hide vehicle repair operations. Board deliberated extensively on appropriate setback relief vs. township aesthetic goals. Public comment from neighboring business owner (Marty Dehan, Quality Brake) arguing changing "goalpost" unfairly burdens existing businesses.

- **Case 2: Outdoor Storage Variance (Masonry Company) – DENIED**
  - **Request:** Variance to allow 20,000 sq ft outdoor storage (double the 10,000 sq ft maximum equal to building size)

  - **Alternative:** Board recommended applicant pursue zoning text amendment through Planning Commission rather than variance.

- **2026 Meeting Dates:** Approved by voice vote; meetings remain third Tuesday at 5:30 PM.

- **Dave DeYoung (Trustee):** Not mentioned in transcript; no specific actions attributed to him.

BikeH8SnMYU.summary:
• **Applebee's Signage Variance (3134 Beeline Road)**: The board voted to DENY a variance request for 259 square feet of additional wall signage (resulting in 358.8 sq ft total) that included apple-logo canopy awnings. The applicant argued the canopies were decorative architectural elements previously approved in 2013, but staff determined the logos classify them as signs exceeding township limits. The board noted Applebee's in nearby Granville successfully adapted to local ordinance by using plain red awnings instead. Board members cited concerns about setting a precedent for signage expansion along US-31 corridor.
• **Secondary Canopy Variance (Applebee's)**: The board voted to APPROVE a lesser motion allowing variances for only TWO specific canopy signs: the "Welcome Back" sign and "Car Side to Go" sign, as these dimensional variances fit within the 99.6 sq ft overall maximum. The nine apple-logo canopies were implicitly denied and cannot be installed.
• **Accessory Building Use Interpretation (Withdrawn)**: Staff requested ZBA interpretation of what constitutes "residential or living quarters" vs. "hobby or recreational space" in accessory buildings, citing increasing requests for sheds with kitchenettes, bathrooms, and sleeping areas. After extensive discussion, the board determined this is a policy/planning matter rather than a variance interpretation issue and voted to WITHDRAW the request, directing staff to bring the discussion to the Planning Commission for potential zoning ordinance amendments.
• **Key Discussion on Living Quarters Definition**: Board consensus emerged that accessory buildings with all four components (sleeping area, bathroom, kitchen/food prep, and living space) should be classified as living quarters and prohibited. The board referenced the example of a studio apartment as a benchmark—if a structure resembles one in layout and function, it qualifies as living quarters.

kHKEA9sbuDw.summary:
**Holland Charter Township Zoning Board of Appeals Meeting – June 24, 2025**

- **12330 James Street (Holland Town Center wayfinding signs)**: Approved variance for up to six oversized internal directional signs (exceeding the standard 4-square-foot limit). Motion passed 5–0 with conditions: (1) signs must be non-illuminated, (2) all tenants must receive equal lettering allocation with no double-stacking per entity, and (3) applicant must submit site plan for final location approval by township staff to ensure compliance with clear vision corners.

- **37 Scotch Drive (carport/garage setback variance)**: Variance request DENIED 5–0. Applicant sought approval to keep existing carport structure 1.7 feet from property line (instead of required 7 feet). Board found the structure violated character ordinance, was self-created without proper permits, and that alternatives exist (temporary solutions like tarping boat, building larger compliant garage). Staff noted carports are not permitted in residential districts; any approved structure must be fully enclosed garage.

- **Zoning Ordinance Review – Accessory Buildings & Living Quarters**: Tabled for further discussion. Board debated definitions of "residential quarters" and "living quarters" in accessory buildings. Key issue: whether structures with cooking, sanitation, and sleeping capability (e.g., golf simulators with kitchenettes, pool houses) constitute prohibited dwellings. Energy code implications for buildings with electrical service were discussed. Board noted concern about single-family neighborhoods converting to multi-tenant housing but acknowledged growing trend toward accessory dwelling units (ADUs). Staff to provide additional guidance; no formal interpretation adopted tonight.

- **No mention of Trustee Dave DeYoung** in this Zoning Board of Appeals transcript; he does not appear to have attended or participated.

dFNihAvD1eA.summary:
- **Item: Great Lakes Partners Lot Split Variance Request** – Applicant Bill Sickle presented a request for a variance to allow splitting approximately 2.47 acres (modified proposal) from an 80-acre farm parcel to sell the existing farmhouse while preserving farmland. The 5-acre minimum is required by ordinance; practical difficulty cited is a creek that meanders through the property, creating awkward configurations if complied with.
- **Key Discussion Points** – Board thoroughly debated competing interests: preserving farmland (applicant's intent) versus enforcing the 5-acre minimum zoning ordinance adopted 5 years ago. Alternative solutions raised included leasing the house, selling with a farming easement, or challenging the ordinance itself. Applicant argued the variance *preserves* farmland by keeping 77+ acres available for continuous farming rather than chunking off larger mixed-use parcels.
- **Standards Analysis** – Board reviewed all six variance standards. Consensus: (1) practical difficulty exists due to creek; (2) creek is not unique to most properties; (3) variance not strictly *necessary* (alternatives exist, though less ideal); (4) no substantial detriment to adjacent properties; (5) applicant created the problem by proposing split; (6) granting variance conflicts with ordinance's spirit of preserving low-density agricultural character.
- **Vote Results** – Motion to approve modified 2.2-acre variance (Ross De, seconded by Elliott Church) **failed 2-3** (De, Church yes; Mackey, De, Bander no). Motion to deny the request **passed 3-2** (Mackey, De, Bander yes; De, Church no). Final decision: **Variance DENIED**.
- **Notable Public Comment** – None; public hearing was closed in September.
- **Dave DeYoung Reference** – No mention of Trustee Dave DeYoung in this Zoning Board of Appeals meeting.

TiKsL_x_XUU.summary:
- Board approved resurfacing of Parks Department parking lot ($56,650 with 10% contingency) and pathway reconstruction project (not to exceed $1,135,000), both funded by voter-approved millage.
- Introduction and first reading of zoning text amendment for 352 Beeline Road from moderate density to residential R-2A, supporting transitional housing for women in crisis by My House Ministries.
- First reading of text amendment to allow seasonal outdoor display/sales in C-1 zones (April 1–October 31) subject to special land use approval, following Planning Commission recommendation (4-1 vote). Rivers Ace Hardware seeking to continue long-standing greenhouse and seasonal product sales.
- Board approved traffic control order converting West Street, Center Street, and East Street to one-way (west to east) to support parking lot design at former Lemon Fresh Cleaners property on 8th Street, advancing Federal District development vision.
- Approved vehicle purchase (2026 Chevrolet Trailblazer, $24,095) for assessing department to conduct 2,500+ annual site visits.
- Principles of Governance and Conduct Guidelines discussed but motion failed; board members expressed concerns about consensus requirement, time limits, and term 'collegial' as potentially limiting respectful disagreement.
- Extensive public comment and board discussion on Lawn Avenue road abandonment selective enforcement allegations; township attorney (Ron Bulthuis) clarified prior involvement ended before litigation, settlement resolved dispute, and board must comply with court order regarding parking and access.

dave_segments.topic: Dave's extensive Lawn Avenue statement part 1

dave_segments.text: Mr. Bulthuis, at the last board meeting regarding the Lawn Avenue Road abandonment legal update, you mentioned several things that caught my attention. You said it was a blast from the past for you. You stated that you were surprised the board was still talking about it... My question to you is this: Was it actually your first time looking at it?
dave_segments.topic: Lawn Avenue history and selective enforcement

dave_segments.text: Before that vote was taken, Mr. Church asked, if we approve and support the road abandonment, then Holland Township gets the property and we can make Dave do anything we want before we hand the land over to him, correct? The board agreed. Staff confirmed. Based on that recommendation, the board approved the abandonment, but with specific conditions.

After roughly a year of back-and-forth negotiations, thousands of dollars spent on legal fees, drafting easement language, engineering surveys, and continuous edits and revisions to both, you and Mr. Sickle came to an agreement on 4 separate easements that resolved all of the township's concerns.

Instead, staff recommended denial for an absolutely invalid reason. My family spent thousands upon thousands of dollars on attorneys, surveys, et cetera, to prepare the township request required easements, and staff still recommended denial because a distant neighbor didn't like it. That was the reason given.

The selective enforcement against my family over the last dozen years has been extreme. The selective enforcement alleged by Mr. Willard over the last 8 months has been extreme. The alleged disparate treatment of employees that we discussed during the January 16th, 2025 closed session deserves oversight and investigation by this board.

I am currently working with a team to develop a website where I will publish every email that I have ever sent to this board regarding selective enforcement, as well as every email moving forward. I will be posting video clips from every board meeting where I have discussed selective enforcement and the alleged disparate treatment of our employees.

You represent and have represented the township for many, many years. Could you explain what that means specifically? And what I'm trying to get to here is You obviously represent Mr. Tesla. Do you represent Mr. Church and Miss Malatki and everybody else here? Do you represent staff? Do you represent Mr. Boltice and Mr. Borsma? And finally, do you represent me?
Because I've been trying to get a meeting with you for. Well over 6 months, and it doesn't seem like that's going to happen.
I'm getting the sheriff stopping at my house to To make sure we don't park. I just had a note put on my car.

I am— I'm gonna— I'm gonna go ahead and bring those into the next meeting for you. You can take a peek at those.

_utterances.text: Good evening, Steve Willard, 3568 Jayden Court. Before my main topic, and actually that might even change after speaking with Russ before this, but I wanted to acknowledge something publicly. So Ottawa County Sheriff's Office has shown a meaningful shift And exhaust enforcement in Holland Township. FOIA data shows that 6 citations in 58 days compared to 4 for the prior 378 days. That's roughly a 10x increase across 6 different deputies. That is a coordinated effort, and it deserves to be set on the record. So to Lieutenant Knott, everybody here, thank you for that. I appreciate it. Now, I was going to take up this time to speak about overnight parking ban. It sounds like we have some movement in that direction with enforcement, but I'm just going to read through this real quick. You can pass it off whatever you want, but Section 3271 of the Township Code prohibits street parking. In practice, it's unenforceable because it's absolute. A blanket ban means every street in every neighborhood is technically in violation at any given moment. An ordinance that cannot be enforced uniformly is enforced selectively by definition. Any citation issued under the current code is a choice, not a standard. That's selective enforcement, and it creates legal exposure for the township. Meanwhile, residents are using this street as permanent parking because their garages are full of storage. A time-based overnight restriction, something like 2 to 6, is targeted, clear, and uniformly enforceable. The City of Holland does this, and we have the legal authority under Michigan Common Law 257.675, which grants local municipalities the ability regulate parking by ordinance regardless of roadway maintenance jurisdiction. I raised this at the March 12th Planning Commission meeting. The response from staff was that this is a board matter, so that's why we're— that's why I'm here tonight. So my, my ask, and I'm glad that Ron is here, um, direct the township attorney to prepare a legal opinion on the board's authority to adopt a time-based overnight parking restriction and place this on a future agenda for formal consideration.

_utterances.text: We have the, uh, review and approval of the board meeting minutes from April 16th, 2026. Any changes, updates on that? I always look for a motion to approve the consent agenda.

_utterances.speaker: B

Then a few of us were at the MTA conference earlier in April, and they were just talking about board meetings, efficiency, and also the governance of board meetings. Meetings and kind of realized that we had done this, I think, 7 years ago, Mike, and thought it, uh, we have some changes on, on the board and we should get this updated. So this is basically a repeat of what we did in 2021, but Holland Charter Township principle of governance to maintain the highest standards and traditions of Michigan townships, we embrace these principles to guide our stewardship, deliberations, and constitutional service as we commit to the safeguard of our community's health, safety, and general welfare. And then we pledge to insist on the highest standard of ethical conduct by all who act on behalf of the township, bring credit, honor, and dignity to our public office through the collegial board deliberations and diligent operate— responses to constituent concerns. Actively pursue education and knowledge and embrace best practices. Treat all persons with dignity, respect, and impartiality without prejudice or discrimination. Practice openness and transparency in our decisions. Thank you. Decisions and actions. Cooperate in all reasonable ways with other governmental entities and consider the impact our decisions may have outside of our township borders. Communicate with the public township issues, challenges, and successes, and welcome the active involvement of stakeholders to further the township's well-being. Strive to compliance with all state and federal statutes requirements, refuse to participate in any decisions or activities for personal gain at the expense of the best interest of the township, further an understanding of the obligations and responsibilities of American citizenship, democratic government, and freedom. These principles we pledge to our township, our state, and our country. So I just, uh, Part of the sessions that I was in, they also kind of talked in generalities on some of the conduct outline, and that's the second page. That's kind of a synopsis of some of this, but it goes a little bit further with some of the details. Respect to all persons in attendance. Refrain from interrupting the person given the floor to speak. Supervisor will acknowledge each person's turn on the floor. Each board person shall actively listen to the person talking. In essence, no side discussions while discussion is happening. Disagreements need to be constructive and to the point on task. Respect all viewpoints. Criticism— criticism should be private at the office or call for closed sessions to discuss personal issues. Citizen comments are limited 3 minutes and received as information, not debated. Board committee updates should be covered within 3 minutes, and any other board member comments or updates within 5 minutes. Consensus is the goal while maintaining consistent standards, but with a willingness to compromise based upon the merits of the issue presented. The board works to improve the quality of life in the community and encourages It is business to provide work opportunities for its citizens. So we had a couple of great sessions at the MTA conference, and the outcome of that, we look forward to basically posting that in our office and looking for the signatures of each person that's on the board, that we would adhere to those principles of governance.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Good evening. Good evening, and thank you for the opportunity to speak tonight. I'm requesting approval of a text amendment to Section 9.20 to allow seasonal outdoor displays and sales within the C-1 neighborhood commercially zoned district between April 1st and October 31st, subject to special land use approval. We are here tonight following the recommendation of approval from the Planning Commission, and I see We sincerely appreciate the time, consideration, and discussion that went into, into reviewing the request throughout the process. When we moved into this location following more than 50 years of Van Meer Hardware serving as a staple of the community, we understand— we understood the importance of continuing the tradition of a locally owned neighborhood hardware store that residents have relied on for generations. Since taking over this location, our goal has been to continue serving the community in the same spirit while investing in the property and operating reasonably as a small local business. As a part of the effort, the greenhouse structure that had been on the property for approximately 28 years was removed after we learned it did not meet current setback requirements and had significantly deteriorated, deteriorated beyond safe use. We, we removed the structure promptly to bring the property into compliance and continue improving the site. In 2025, during the first summer operating in this location, we placed a temporary greenhouse in the front parking lot to continue offering seasonal product— seasonal products sourced in part from other locally owned growers and small businesses within the surrounding community. However, it was cited by zoning and removed immediately. Since its removal, many members of the community have continued asking us to bring back the seasonal sales of flowers, greenhouse products, mulch, landscaping materials that has been long associated with the property and remained directly connected to the hardware, hardware retail operations. Under the current ordinance, outdoor displays and sales are limited to 2 7-day periods per calendar year. While the framework may work for temporary promotional events or yard sales, it does not reflect the realities of seasonal merchandising commonly associated with greenhouse and horticulture products. Importantly, the proposed amendment does not introduce a new principal land use and preserves all existing safety standards, parking requirements, pedestrian access requirements, and clear vision protections while continuing to require special land use approval. Our intention is to simply establish a clear and reasonable seasonal framework that allows small business like ours to continue serving community while operating in full compliance with the township regulations moving forward. Thank you again for your time and consideration. Thank you.

Did not get past Planning Commission? So, for short-term—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: from a zoning standpoint, we can't issue a special land use permit unless the zoning ordinance says that a special land use can be issued for the particular use in question. If we're just allowing them to do it without having the language in the ordinance saying you can get a special land use for this particular use, that's a variance that goes to the Zoning Board of Appeals and is only granted in very rare circumstances. So, uh, the proper way to do it would be to put the authority in the zoning ordinance, which opens it up to everybody, but it's done through a special land use so that conditions and factors can be considered and we can make sure it's not offensive to the neighborhood.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Well, and the building, I mean, they You know, it wasn't damaged that they had to take down. So if it was left up, really, it was— it had been there, right? Well, they had to take it down. So then when they put the temporary up and there was a little bit of space— Yeah.

And also, yes, it was deteriorating. Deteriorating, so we would have had to replace it anyway anyways, but we were not allowed to put it in the same spot because of setbacks. And so we tried to put it in the parking lot where we're wanting to put it now and what we're asking for, and it was immediately told— we were told to take it down because it— because of zoning.
