My one question about specialty land use approval is, is it revocable? Can you revoke that, and under what conditions? And should there be something in here about whether or not it's revocable? Are they permanent? In nature, or what happens? So, I think this use case sounds great. All right. What I'm trying to think about is, are there other use cases that you would have approved and then decide later this is creating some sort of a problem and you'd like to revoke that? Do we have the right to revoke it?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: We do. Any special land use is subject to conditions. One of the conditions from the Michigan Zoning Enabling Act is Compliance with all requirements of applicable laws, including the zoning ordinance. But we can establish specific conditions for any particular application, and a violation of a condition of approval gives us the ability to say, you're violating the condition of approval, we're going to take the approval away. We got to go through the same procedure, uh, a public hearing and all that, that we do to grant a special use, but we can go through—

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: that procedure, give the due process, and take away the special land use if the conditions are violated.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay, you're saying that the Planning Commission can establish other conditions if it sees fit?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: They routinely do. Whenever they consider special land use, they consider conditions for that special land use.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: For that land. So it's generally customized and they're accustomed to adding conditions.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Every C-1 would have to come forward with Their specific requirements.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: There are always the same conditions. You've got to comply with the application. You've got to comply with applicable laws and ordinances, especially the zoning ordinance. But then, the Planning Commission can establish specific conditions in addition to the always required ones for any particular special land use.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: And, a violation of any condition So, that's for the first reading, and next month we'll have the second and approval.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thank you. Then, we have the operational and capital improvement expenditures. Steve, you got that parking lot resurfacing? Building Department.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah, that's right. So, um, as part of the, um, CIP for this year, the 2026 to 2031 CIP, uh, does call for the resurfacing of the Parks Department, uh, parking lot out on Quincy there. So bids were solicited, 2 were received. You see the results there, uh, one from Tulip City Asphalt in the amount of $51,490, the other from Bruce's blacktop, $54,225. So you'll see that staff is requesting approval of that low bid plus a 10% contingency. There is a bit of volatility in the petroleum market right now. And so while the bid is there, there is certainly a situation where the contractor could come back and say they want to take a look at again. It wouldn't be significant. And we've talked to the low bidder. They've asked for $1,000 more. So the contingency certainly will, will cover that as well. But with that contingency, the amount being requested from staff is $56,650.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: So looking for a motion on the $56,650 for the parking lot resurface at Parks Department. I'll make the motion. I'll support it. Mike, can you do a roll call vote on this one?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Sure. Mr. Tesla? Yes. Miss Malatki? Yes. Mr. DeYoung? Yes. Mr. Church? Myself? Yes. Mr. Bush? Yes. Okay, we have 6 in favor, 0 opposed, 1 absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thanks, Mike. We got the pathway repair program.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: That's right. As the memo indicates, we continue to do our best to keep the 70-plus miles of pathway within the township in the best shape possible. As we've talked about before, the age of our system is now requiring us more and more often to do a reconstruct of segments, and as opposed to just another overlay. And that is certainly the case with all of these segments that are noted there. These are all going to be reconstruction. And they're noted there kind of from one end of the township to the other, about a little less than 4 miles in reconstruction this year. And then also, we do have some retaining walls in various locations. Those are primarily timber in nature and are starting to deteriorate to the point where Our engineer has recommended their replacement, so four of those have been identified: one for removal, the others for replacement. When we do replace them, we are not going to use timber again; we're going to be using block. So that'll be a much longer lasting. I think all the communities around us have adopted block retaining walls at this point. So that is our intent moving forward as well. Bids were solicited for that project; two were received. One, the low bid was from SiteWork Solutions in the amount of $943,675. The other was from Bob's Asphalt and Paving in the amount of $1,117,590. Staff is seeking authorization to award that bid to SiteWork Solutions as well as— Allocation for engineering and contingency, an amount not to exceed $1,135,000.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: So looking for a motion for the reconstruction of, uh, pathways not to exceed $1,135,000.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: This comes out of the, the millage, correct? The millage that's voted on by the public to review the public— yes, I'll make that motion.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Looking for support. I'll support. And Mike, can I do another roll call, please?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Mr. DeYoung? Yes. Mr. Church?

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Myself? Yes. Mr. Bush? Yes. Ms. Malachy? Yes. Mr. TeSlaa?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay, 6 in favor, 0 opposed, 1 absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: All right, thanks, Mike. Then we've got a vehicle purchase for the assessing department. Steve?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes, as the COVID memo indicates, the assessing department had placed in the current capital improvement program funding, or requested funding, for purchase of a vehicle. As the memo indicates, this is going to be used for various assessing activities, primarily to conduct site visits, as the assessing department endeavors to do a site review of 20% of the parcels annually. So that's approximately, for our township, about 2,500 site visits. So it's making— using the vehicle to take care of that work, as well as doing permit reviews and other business activities as well. So Tyler Wolf, the assessor, used the MyDeals program, and we've talked about that before, the statewide purchasing program. The specification was for a small SUV. Bids were received from the various dealers who were identified or selected under the MyDeals Statewide program, and you'll see that the low bid was for a 2026 Chevrolet Trailblazer from Berger Chevrolet in the amount of $24,095, and staff is seeking authorization to proceed with awarding that bid to Berger for that amount.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: So, looking for a motion to approve the 2026 Chevrolet Trailblazer I'll make the motion.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I'll support it.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: And, Michael, do one more roll call.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay. Mr. Bush?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Mr. TeSlaa?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Myself?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Mr. Church?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Ms. Malacki?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And, Mr. DeYoung? Yes. Okay, we have a six in favor, zero opposed, one absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thanks, Mike. We got the. You moved, don't you see? I thought you were gone. I thought we were gone. Administrative reports, Steve.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes, you may recall that last fall you authorized expenditures for the installation of emergency sirens throughout Holland Township. That was through a company called Westshore. And there were— that allocation was for 8 installation sites. Those have now been sited, and we're just about done with permitting. So these are the various locations within the township where those are going to be. Although I think it is also informative not only to look at the coverage map for the township sirens, But also have an understanding of where our neighbors have theirs, and most of them have them as well. So you'll certainly see that with the addition of the sirens in the township, we certainly have great coverage between both us and our neighbors as well. So just wanted to let you know these sirens are going to be placed on their own poles, and certainly as those stakes, those location and sighting stakes, were put in place. Residents did call and say, "Hey, what's what's going on here?" When we explained them to them what was happening, they were overwhelmingly supportive of this effort. So they're all going to be located on public property, typically within a road right-of-way. So we're securing all the permits from the Road Commission for that. The electrical contractor will be running power. To the sirens has submitted their permits, and Westshore has informed us that they intend to start installation early next month. That's all I have.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: If I can ask a question, I want to go back to River Hills. As I'm remembering, I believe when When you came to the zoning, you wanted to get this through for the holiday, memorial holiday. So I don't know what we can do, if there's anything we can do about this. As I was remembering, we had canceled one of our meetings, trustee meetings, and that would have allowed River Hills to have this amendment done, and then they would have been able to come back and been ready for the memorial weekend to be able to get their plants out. Um, I would have to go back and look at the minutes. There was something said that there could have been a special meeting that they could have called so that if we did approve it, that they could have— would they have the Right. So there was discussion about that today with that time. I don't think we ever anticipated our trustee needing to be the one that we didn't have. That's when they would have then brought it, it would have been presented here, and then at this meeting now, they— we hopefully would have approved it for them and they would have been able to get their plans up for the memorial. Weekend. That was an issue that we all had talked about, and I'm sorry that I didn't bring that up when she was speaking. And it's— I started to think about that because our meeting was canceled, our trustee meeting. Is there anything, Attorney Balti, that we could do right now since we're all here to maybe— if we would all agree upon Having a special meeting, so we don't.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: We can't do that, Gretchen, because we have to have the posting requirements prior to the meeting.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Because I think if we had a meeting, we hadn't canceled that.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Maybe she could have gone in and had called and asked.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: There still would have been a special use requirement, okay? Even after the text amendment was adopted. Okay.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I mean, because we were we. We tried to figure it out in our meeting, and I thought, I mean, it was pretty important that they did this. They wanted to kick off, as we all know, Memorial Weekend. Um, is there anything we can—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: The special use would go to the Planning Commission, not to the Township Board. That's correct, right? Yeah. Uh, have you applied to the Planning Commission for a special use?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Um, no, we cannot apply for the special use until this is voted on here at the board.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I also have another concern. We currently have a citation for having bulk goods in our parking lot, and it was having bulk goods like our— it's our mulch. The mulch is sitting— yeah, bulk goods. The mulch is sitting out in the parking lot, one pallet of each kind, and we have a zoning violation citation for that. And Kate White was able to push it back until the 26th, until after this meeting, to see if we could come to a resolution on what we can do moving forward. Otherwise, it will be very, very difficult for us to store any of our products that we currently have in stock.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I think we've set everything in motion to— that this will be approved.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, they gave him until the 26th. I just couldn't remember all the details. There was, there was another. So she's— you're worried about getting fined, right?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah, we're worried about getting fined. I mean, we'll pay it obviously, but we don't want to have a bad rapport with the township.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I understand. Okay, is there anything we can do?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Is that in front of district court or is that in front of the township's civil infraction?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Township civil infraction is, is what I understand of, of the citation that we received in the mail. We received one notice, um, and then we have received our second notice, and we've actively been working with Corey and Kate to—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: That's not going to set a precedent that says you can't do anything in the future, I'll say that. But you may be able to work out a further extension. That's, that's really up to the township, but I think—

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I think we can work on it.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: extension for that.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: So she would be okay with her—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Well, that takes care of— I don't think the citation— the citation can be delayed.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: How can we make sure that that's going to happen for these folks? Can we do anything with that tonight right here with this board, giving them an extension for another 30 days past the 26th? Or what's it— it's going to take longer than that.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Wait a second, I think I think you had some other creative ideas.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Well, I was going to move on to the, uh, the status, the bigger question. Okay. Um, so why don't you go ahead, Dave? The citation discussion now.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah, maybe you could direct the manager to meet with the— well, Russell.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, we'll talk to them, make sure that we— so they can talk with the code enforcement about extending that.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Lengthen the citation until approval has happened here for at least 30 days.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: In terms of Memorial Day weekend, we really, we really can't do anything officially because we've only had a first reading. We can't consider, um, adoption of the ordinance until after we've published a notice of first reading, and that's not good. So we're not going to get this done before Memorial Day. And even after that, there's an 8-day waiting period before the ordinance takes effect. And then there's a special use requirement that they have to get once the text amendment takes effect. So officially, we can't do anything about that.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Okay, maybe there was wrong information given. I just remember that— or maybe it was maybe We were misinterpreted, but I—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Maybe the Planning Commission can have a special meeting.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: But they can't really do anything until the test—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: For their approval process once it gets to them.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, they had started— I know that they had started this long before with anticipation because they have to buy— they buy their supplies, you know, ahead of time and everything, their stock and everything that they have to get in. So I know it wasn't anything on their end. that they were trying to do to circumvent, you know, anything. I'm just wondering if there's anything we can do to—

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So we had a first reading. Is there a second reading? Is that the process? So we got to go through another reading?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: The adoption is the second reading.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: All right. Is there any way to skip to that? We're all, I think, in agreement.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: He said we have— that's what they're saying. Okay, I'm sorry. Thank you. That's what I was saying.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: We have to publish a notice of a first reading before we can have the second reading. I understand. We have an 8-day waiting period after that second reading slash adoption when it takes effect, and then the Planning Commission could grant the special use.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Work with Corey, Steve, and Kate.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah, for now, if we could just have the extension on this ITA. I think we can still proceed with business as normal. At this point, we're not anticipating putting a greenhouse this year just because it really wouldn't even be worth it at this point for us. But hopefully we can at least keep our bulk goods in the parking lot and fork it so we can get plenty of machinery for that.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Okay. Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Dave, any updates?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Oh, I sure do. Thanks. First, I have a question for Mr. Bulthuis. You represent and have represented the township for many, many years. Could you explain what that means specifically? And what I'm trying to get to here is You obviously represent Mr. Tesla. Do you represent Mr. Church and Miss Malatki and everybody else here? Do you represent staff? Do you represent Mr. Boltice and Mr. Borsma? And finally, do you represent me? Because I'm just wondering if I called you to set up a meeting, is that something that we can do? Because I've been trying to get a meeting with you for. Well over 6 months, and it doesn't seem like that's going to happen. And I guess I'm just curious, what does that mean when to say you represent the township?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: In my mind, that means I represent the township, uh, which is governed by the township board. So I continue to answer my phone calls from the township. I continue to show up at meetings to which I'm invited unless and until there There's at least 4 members of the township board who vote I'm done, and then I stop representing the township. Um, yeah, I don't represent any individuals. I don't represent individual citizens. I don't represent individual board members. I don't represent individual staff members. I work with all of them.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: So it would be acceptable to say that you would work with me if I would want a meeting with you?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I work— I, I should add a sentence or two after that. Yes, I work with all the individuals involved within the parameters that the township board establishes. If the township board says, uh, you get your work and your assignments through the township superintendent, then that's what I do. I'm not saying that you don't work with the superintendent. If the township board either specifically or informally allows any township board member, any township staff member to contact me and expects me to answer them, I will. But it's up to the township board. I work within the parameters you establish. I've got some townships who say we don't want— we want to limit our concerns and our expenses, and all of your contacts will go through this person. And if you don't hear from this person, you don't do anything. And I've got others that say, if anybody calls you, answer the phone and do what they ask. So it depends what you want.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Well, let's talk about this township. What does this township— what, what's the policy for this township?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I don't— I'm not aware that the township board has ever established a limitation on me. I will also say that if I get a phone call from a staff member or a resident or a board member, which is not my— in the normal course of business, I would not do something without my contact being the superintendent, knowing this is what I'm doing unless you tell me not to.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: All right, thank you for that. I appreciate it. So I have a bit of a response here from your legal update at the last meeting that I'd like to read through. Mr. Bulthuis, at the last board meeting regarding the Lawn Avenue Road abandonment legal update, you mentioned several things that caught my attention. You said it was a blast from the past for you. You stated that you were surprised the board was still talking about it. You said you had to request copies of the lawsuit documents and that you reviewed the lawsuit with a fresh set of eyes, almost like it was your first time looking at it. My question to you is this: Was it actually your first time looking at it? Because according to my records and everything that I recall, you had very little to do with that lawsuit itself. I don't think you spent more than five minutes working on it because Bob Sullivan was the attorney who handled it. However, I do not. I do not doubt that you were surprised that we are still talking about it. Quite frankly, I'm surprised too. I imagine you were surprised a lawsuit was ever filed at all. I would assume you thought everything had already been resolved exactly the way it should have been after you prepared the documents to satisfy the township's conditions for the final abandonment. I imagine you assumed the road had been abandoned at that time, just like the other 222 documented road abandonments in Holland Township. I imagine that after all the work you actually did regarding this abandonment, you assume the adjoining property owners ended up with the land just like they should have. So no, I do not doubt your surprise, but that does lead me into the most important part of your presentation. It's not about what you said, it's about what you didn't say. You never said one word about everything that led up to the lawsuit, and that is the most important part. Because that lawsuit should have never been necessary. You never spoke about the work you personally did on this matter. If the township had simply done what you recommended at the time, the lawsuit never would have happened. Furthermore, it never should have happened. It only happened because of the continuous selective enforcement by our staff. Staff misguided and manipulated this board, recommending denial against the board's wishes for no valid reason. Mr. Bolche, while your summary, summary of the mediation was appreciated, it is also totally irrelevant because the lawsuits itself should have never happened. Every board member sitting here tonight knows it. That is why we are still talking about it. Staff went against board support and recommended denial, denial of the final road abandonment because one distant neighbor didn't like it. In doing so, Holland Charter Township landlocked my family's home. Our legal ingress and egress were effectively stolen from us. Then staff doubled down, harassing my family with citations, threatening letters, threatening emails, no parking signs being installed, concrete barriers, and even throwing our personal property into our yard while demanding that we stop using what was suddenly considered Holland Charter Township property. This happened despite the mediation settlement specifically ordering the township to treat us no differently than any other family regarding parking. Staff even violated that mediation agreement through selective enforcement against my family. Mr. Bolche, let me refresh your memory with the timeline of events from the point this board agreed to support the road abandonment. After years of going around in circles with possible solutions, the Holland Charter Township Board voted to approve the Lawn Avenue road abandonment. My predecessor on this board, Mr. Nykamp, asked a simple question that brought us there.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: What is the alternative?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Wouldn't it solve all the ongoing issues if we just abandoned the road? And of course it would have. So he recommended to the board that the road be abandoned. Before that vote was taken, Mr. Church asked, if we approve and support the road abandonment, then Holland Township gets the property and we can make Dave do anything we want before we hand the land over to him, correct? The board agreed. Staff confirmed. Based on that recommendation, the board approved the abandonment, but with specific conditions. The next step, of course, was getting the Road Commission to approve the abandonment. That would not have been possible without township support. Obviously, that support was given because the Road Commission approved the abandonment and quitclaimed the land to the township. Mr. Bulthuis, this is where I believe you became involved. You were tasked with working with my attorney, Bill Sickel, to hammer out the specific conditions required by the township. After roughly a year of back-and-forth negotiations, thousands of dollars spent on legal fees, drafting easement language, engineering surveys, and continuous edits and revisions to both, you and Mr. Sickle came to an agreement on 4 separate easements that resolved all of the township's concerns. Mr. Bulthuis, I can only assume you made some kind of a statement to the board or staff, whomever you were representing at that point. Obviously, it wasn't. It's time to turn the land over because at the last meeting you made very clear that you never said that. To quote you, you stated absolutely untrue. I had no discussions like that. I have never said that. I don't know where that came from, but I can tell you they are absolutely 100% not true. I do apologize for putting words in your mouth. However, these are the words that were relayed to me through Mr. Through my attorney, Mr. Sickel. Whatever statement was made, I imagine it must have been something along those lines. Perhaps all conditions have been met, or all easements have been finalized. Whatever the wording was, some kind of confirmation clearly occurred because the board was subsequently notified and the final abandonment of Lawn Avenue was placed on the agenda for final approval. From there, this should have been a straightforward process. This board should have simply voted to approve an already prepared quitclaim deed, just like we did at the last board meeting for Ottawa Street in Zeeland, and just like every other road abandonment that has ever happened in Highland Township. That is how this should have gone. And yes, it should have been done and over long ago. Instead, staff recommended denial for an absolutely invalid reason. My family spent thousands upon thousands of dollars on attorneys, surveys, et cetera, to prepare the township request required easements, and staff still recommended denial because a distant neighbor didn't like it. That was the reason given. And as we all now know, especially after the Culver's Road abandonment, it does not matter how many people oppose a road abandonment. This boardroom was packed with residents opposed to Culver's 15th Street abandonment. They even had legitimate concerns about traffic and congestion, yet the abandonment still happened. Exactly as it should have. We now know there were 20— there were 18 successful road abandonment completions in Holland Township before my arrival here. Every one of them ended the way they should have. The adjoining landowners received the property. Then came my family's request, the only road abandonment where Holland Township kept the land, despite, despite a written statement from the supervisor saying the township had no interest in keeping it. It remains the only unfinished road abandonment to this day. 3 more road abandonments have happened since I joined this board. All 3 went exactly the way they should have. The adjoining property owners received the land. Holland Township kept none of it, except ours, of course. This township effectively landlocked my family's property, taking away legal access to our home. Staff then followed that up with letters, emails, citations, and threats telling us that we were not allowed to use, use what was now considered township property. What choice did we have besides pursuing legal action? Do you really think we wanted that fight? Mr. Bulthuis, your trip down memory lane omitted the most important part— your part— and everything that happened before the lawsuit. The most important events occurred long before mediation. That lawsuit and mediation never would have happened if this abandonment had been handled like every other abandonment in Holland Township history. The lawsuit should never, ever have happened. It only occurred because of ongoing selective enforcement by township staff. Unfortunately, this board, including myself, places too much trust in unethical and perhaps even corrupt staff. We all trusted staff to do the right thing. Mr. Bolche, the reason we are still discussing this abandonment is exactly that. Everyone sitting here knows this abandonment was a complete hack job led by our staff. Every member of this board has recently expressed support for finalizing the abandonment of Lawn Avenue, and that support clearly existed long ago, long before any lawsuit. Every issue had already been resolved by you, Mr. Bulche. We all know this road should have been abandoned years ago. You were absolutely right about one thing. It is shocking that we are still talking about it, but it is not going to go away because it stands as the pinnacle example of selective enforcement against township residents by our staff. It is all connected. Mr. Bulthuis, prior to the last board meeting, I don't believe I had ever met you. Even seeing— I've seen your name on emails many times, and despite being opposing counsel, I have never had any animosity toward you. In fact, I've always respected you, and I still do. And admittedly, you were very hard on me during those easement negotiations, but you were also very fair and very detailed. I respect you enough that while township staff has burned through attorneys trying to find anyone willing to oppose this road abandonment, including anonymous attorneys claiming it's too complicated because easements don't run with the land, I have consistently supported your original work and encouraged this board to follow your legal guidance. I just never expected you to dismiss your own work. Unfortunately, I believe staff guided you away from everything that happened before this ridiculous and unnecessary lawsuit. My request to you is simple: go back further, review your own files, review the easements that you drafted for the Lawn Avenue abandonment. You yourself stated this is not a complicated abandonment, and I could not agree more. This should have been resolved years ago. Staff made it complicated because they simply do not like Dave DeYoung. They bent over backward to ensure my family did not receive the land despite every precedent ever established in Holland Charter Township. Unfortunately for staff, personal dislike does not justify selective enforcement. Holland Charter Township staff selectively enforced ordinances against my family again and again. First came the absurd— absurd incomplete abandonment of Lawn Avenue. Then came setback requirements for our storage shed that no other resident appears to have been forced to follow, even requiring my family to pay for a survey and to prove compliance. And then, of course, there was the selective enforcement of the no parking ordinance. That selective enforcement was so blatant that for the first 10 years we lived in our home, a neighbor parked a 30-foot RV directly in front of our home—not theirs, our home—for 10 straight years there were no citations, no warning signs, no sheriff visits, nothing. But the moment we parked in that same exact location, everything changed. Suddenly there were citations, threatening letters, sheriff visits, no parking signs, concrete barriers, and our personal property. Property being thrown into our yard, all for doing exactly what others had done for years without consequence. Meanwhile, no one else in the township appears to have had this ordinance enforced against them in the same way. In fact, we cannot even get the township to enforce the ordinance at the very same neighbor's home that repeatedly complained about us. Records confirm my family has requested equal enforcement at 610 North Shore Drive since 2011, yet the ordinance has never been enforced there, not once. So I ask again, how does this happen? We all know the answer: selective enforcement by township staff. You were right about one thing, Mr. Bolche. I am on this board because of the Lawn Avenue disaster. It is a premium example of selective enforcement by our staff. I had to run for office to protect my wife and daughters. I had to put my name on a ballot and be elected just to make the targeted harassment stop. And lo and behold, it stopped almost instantly. I hadn't even been sworn in yet. Would anyone care to explain why the harassment ended immediately after I was elected? I will. The answer is obvious: because it never should have been happening in the first place. We all know it. It is glaringly obvious at this point. So what does one do when you own— when your own township strips access to your home? What do you do when 3 attorneys tell you there's no realistic path to winning? You still have to try. You go to court because it's the only option left. The only reason we settled at that mediation was because we realized everyone was right. You cannot beat the government. They will not rule against themselves. The government does not rule against the government. So we settled instead of wasting even more money. In summary, the selective enforcement against my family over the last dozen years has been extreme. The selective enforcement alleged by Mr. Willard over the last 8 months has been extreme. The alleged disparate treatment of employees that we discussed during the January 16th, 2025 closed session deserves oversight and investigation by this board. Mr. Bulthuis, we have a major problem involving selective enforcement against residents and possible disparate treatment of employees by this staff. This board, myself included, is trying to determine how to properly address it. Quite frankly, I'm glad you are here because we could use some legal guidance on how to investigate these issues appropriately. Actions have consequences. This is about transparency and accountability. And to my fellow board members, Mr. Bolche is hired to protect the township. He is not elected by our citizens. We are. It is our responsibility to protect our citizens. We are responsible to provide them transparency, accountability, and oversight. It is our responsibility to ensure that selective enforcement against our residents never happens again. And that, in a nutshell, Mr. Bulthuis, is why this board continues discussing the botched Lawn Avenue Road abandonment. I believe the main reason you were invited to these meetings was to silence me, shut me down, and fearmonger me into to stopping my public discussion about selective enforcement in Holland Charter Township. Hence the pointed discussion about emails being sent to board members and possible Open Meetings Act concerns. In light of those concerns, I want to disclose to everyone that I am currently working with a team to develop a website where I will publish every email that I have ever sent to this board regarding selective enforcement, as well as every email moving forward. I will be posting video clips from every board meeting where I have discussed selective enforcement and the alleged disparate treatment of our employees. Additionally, all of these emails and videos will be shared on local social media platforms such as Holland Informed, Holland Is So Ghetto, and other local information sites. I am not interested in hiding anything. In fact, quite the opposite. I support full transparency, full exposure, and Full accountability. Our citizens deserve to know what has been happening regarding the treatment of residents and employees by township staff, and they soon will. My goal has always been full public transparency, not private undisclosed meetings. Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Wait, I'll pass.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Oh, on the finance side of things, we're. Our finance staff's working on some accounts payable, uh, makes— make it a little bit more efficient. Working with our deputy treasurer and our finance director and our cashiers of just trying to make sure we're doing things a little bit more with BS&A.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: That's all I got. Gretchen? Well, um, I think that this deserves what Dave has said. It deserves some conversation. I'd like to know how we can go about doing that. Does anybody have any ideas?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I think we've taken all the action that we can.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Attorney Bulthuis, is there any way that we can— what we could do to resolve these issues? I mean, could we maybe get back and have a meeting on it? What can we do? Because this has been going on a long time. Um, I, uh, I've looked into things and been objective, um, and I think it does deserve some, some attention here, some answers. Is there any— do you have any ideas?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Can we have a constructive conversation without—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Well, I think we can. I think a couple of things that ought to be said— well, there are several things that should be said. I kind of stopped taking notes because there was a lot of stuff being thrown at the wall. Number one, I'm glad you didn't adopt the principles of governance because I think there are some revisions that need to be made. When someone is pursuing an individual item, individual as to the family, individual as to property, I think a board member should leave the dais and sit in the audience and be a member of the public because there's a true conflict of interest. When someone is pursuing a personal matter as opposed to a township general interest matter, the person should not do that as a board member. That is inappropriate. It's a conflict of interest, and I think having a board member pursuing a personal matter sitting in the audience makes it clear that we're recognizing the conflict of interest and dealing with it appropriately. Secondly, I'm not sure what Mr. DeYoung was hearing from me last night. Month. But basically what I talked about with regard to Lawn Avenue was all before the litigation. That's when I was involved. I met with Mr. DeYoung multiple times then, and his attorney, and the neighbors, and the township staff. And that's what I reported on, that Sickel and I had multiple discussions about the Road Commission procedures And whether it's the proper procedure or not. Bill indicated he thought it was wrong. We talked with Randy Skipper, who represented the Road Commission, and said it was the right procedure and they were not going to change it. I reported on all this last month. This was all before the litigation. I did not start at the litigation. I was not involved in the litigation. My former partner handled the litigation. So it's true, I was not involved in the litigation, and I think I reported that as well last month. My point was the litigation ended the matter. The parties went to court, and it's not a case that the government won't rule against itself. The court is an independent third party of the governmental function of the— of this country. And the township and the court are not one governmental entity. And the parties, being the township and being the property owners, made a determination to settle the lawsuit. And what Mr. DeYoung is now asking is that you ignore the settlement that he made. That's what I object to, and that's what when I said I have never said since that time, since it was resolved, to go back and that this should be done and that the property should be deeded over. Actually, what what I said with regard to notices given to people when we are dealing with platted property and not proceeding. When the property owners are in disagreement, that was I just said that because that was a helpful discussion at that point on a different matter. But that's exactly what happened here. The property owners couldn't agree. There was going to be litigation one way or the other, and the township board as my minutes, my notes, and my file stop. With the determination of the township board to not vacate Lawn Avenue. I have no idea of the township board ever deciding to abandon Lawn Avenue and then somehow I stopped it or somebody stopped it. I don't know about that. I do not know about that. My, my indication, my file says we talked about it, we could not reach agreement among the, the neighbors, including Mr. DeYoung, but also the Walters family and others, could not reach an agreement. Staff recommended, because we couldn't reach an agreement, we couldn't redo the process with the Road Commission because the Road Commission said we did it properly. Uh, Sickles said, no, you didn't. We said, we're not going to— that's not our fight. Uh, when we couldn't reach agreement, the The board decided to not pursue the abandonment of Lawn Avenue. That's all I know. I have never known that the board took a different position. And then I thought— that's what I assumed was the reason why we ended up in litigation, because the board didn't agree to abandon Lawn Avenue, and therefore the DeYoung family brought litigation against the township, and that litigation was settled. And so that's why I can't understand why we're still talking about it, because we went to court and the court ruled through an agreement reached among the parties. So I really— I don't know what else to say about a lot of this stuff. I guess I wouldn't— I would need to have a slower recitation or maybe copies of the notes or the written comments or whatever the allegations are. But, but I did not— I don't believe I've ever written— I don't write easements as a general matter. I'm not a property lawyer. I give them to property lawyers to do. I'm a general governmental attorney. I don't dabble in easements unless they're very, very straightforward. Don't believe I ever drafted any easements. I don't believe I drafted the documents up to abandon the property, and they had it all keyed up, ready to go, and recommended it to the board. The board couldn't The, the property owners couldn't reach consensus, so it just never went farther than that. Um, and that's what I talked about last month. All of that was preceding the litigation. I'm accused of just going from litigation forward. Absolutely not. Uh, all of my information last month was before litigation, so I'm not sure where the disconnect was.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I, I appreciate you Thank you. And I didn't mean to put you on the spot, but I just was hoping maybe we could come with some clarity here. My parents and my grandparents owned property along that area, that Hope Platte in there. In fact, my grandfather helped develop it with the cows that were there many, many, many years ago. So when my I was first born, I my parents had a cottage over there. I'm familiar with where Mr. DeYoung lives because of that. I have there's old family friends that live over there. I understand you know what's going on. What is so abhorrent to me is that. If we all would drive over there and we saw this piece where it is, what Mr. Young is talking about, that piece does not benefit anybody whatsoever. So whatever neighbors are complaining, um, I'm familiar with the complaints that have been made, as everybody is here on this board. But who's ever complained? There's no one that would benefit from this property. It's Basically, Dad's right end ends right in there. Mr. DeYoung and his family cannot park their cars really in their driveway. It's in the right-of-way. It's been diced up and changed many times, and I understand that prior to Dave and Aaron purchasing the house. But if we were all to drive over there and, and not take everything that has happened I'm going to put it aside. But just if we— common sense says if we were all sitting there looking at it, it's— it makes sense. And I've heard this board multiple times, obviously what Dave is saying, abandoned the roads to property owners and whatnot because it's not of use to anybody else. It's properties I think we've all spent enough money and time and energy, and I know that everybody's sick of hearing this from Dave, and I'm sorry, Dave, but maybe we can all go over to Dave's house, have a meeting, and just see what he's talking about. Maybe if eyes, visual eyes— and Attorney Balchi, I, I know maybe you might disagree with me, I'm trying to offer a solution. I agree with you. That this is ridiculous. It is just so ridiculous that if you went over there, you would say this doesn't make sense. It doesn't make sense to have you put on the spot. And, and, and I know you're, you're gracious, and I appreciate that, but I don't think anybody likes to hear the frustration from Mr. DeYoung. I certainly don't like to see see his wife and children from a neighbor being aggressively approached, I wouldn't tolerate it. And I think that he— we're getting— and I think that's the neighbor that may be causing problems. But if we just put all that aside and just look at common sense, common sense and cooler minds will I mean, we just— if you look at it, there's nobody else that benefits from this property.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Let me ask, let me ask, you know, Chair, Mr. Supervisor, you should cut me off whenever you want to, but I, um—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: And Dave's not paying me anything. He's not taking me to lunch to talk. I'm just saying this is— he's a constituent and I'm asking for your grace here.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Well, I understand. So here, here's the problem for the township. As I indicated to you, when we have platted landowners in disagreement, we're not going to take a shortcut. We're not going to try and administratively do what the Land Division Act says is done through circuit court. We're not going to spend public money on something like that.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Mm-hmm.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So, uh, we couldn't reach consensus. We couldn't deal with it that way. It didn't work. Mr. DeYoung chose to go to court and settled the lawsuit and gave up his claims. And there's another property owner that says, if you now, Township, abandon the property, we're going to sue. We're— how can we—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Do we know who that resident is?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yeah, I think we do.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, we have connecting residents.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: But it doesn't matter. The property doesn't— it doesn't matter if, if we're going to We have to be in a dispute. There's a dispute among platted landowners. We don't need to be involved in that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I understand that.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Then why are we— wow. And now when we have— we would be doing something contrary to a court order.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yeah, I understand.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So, yeah, so our hands are tied. Now let me ask you, the court order says the township will not restrict the DeYoungs' use of Lawn Avenue to access their property. And I went through this court order last month.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: The court— the township will give DeYoung an access easement over Lawn Avenue. The township will remove barriers and signs adjacent to Lawn Avenue. The township will not enforce parking regulations against DeYoung differently from others. DeYoung may use Lawn Avenue in a way that does not interfere with any other person using Lawn Avenue, and all claims by DeYoung are dismissed with prejudice. That's the court order. That's what I reviewed last month. If we're not doing that court order, then holy cow, let's abide by the court order. But we're not going to just throw the court order away. Let's just abide by the court order.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Well, then everyone should. Why do I have— why do not this guy?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So we're restricting your use of Lawn Avenue to access—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: why do I have citations and letters from the township saying you said that you You said the unfair enforcement went away.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: You said it stopped.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Once I became an elected official, yeah.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Why did it take that long? Why— I—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I don't know. I don't know. So are we talking about past grievances? Are we talking about a person— the instant situation now? Has the matter been resolved at this point?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: No.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So you're still getting citations?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I'm getting the sheriff stopping at my house to To make sure we don't park. I just had a note put on my car.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: You were parked illegally, and you had a note put on your car.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Well, you tell me if I'm illegal. I'm parking where I'm allowed to park. I don't know. I don't either. That's what I'm talking about. It's called selective enforcement.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: So let's take. Let's just. He just said. Let's eliminate everything.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Should not be having that done.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: The second court order. Let's take the court order, comply with the court order, and we're done.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: And I think he is complying with the court order, and you are getting ticketed.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I haven't got a ticket. I've gotten a letter from the sheriff on my car.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Then I think that's a violation.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: That goes against the court order, correct? No, depending on where it's parked.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: It depends where it's parked.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I don't know.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I know, we can't— I'm sorry, I got you. We gotta go out there and measure it.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Okay, this This part's getting into the weeds.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I know it is, and it's why I'm saying I want clarity, because we're going to sit here at our next meetings over and over. Dave's going to be upset. If we can get some way—

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: My way is this. My way last month was this. My way this month is this.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Take the freaking court order and comply with it, and we're done.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: And if he is and he gets a note, what is— what would What would you suggest?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: If he gets a note, I'd say answer the question in the note. We all get questioned about our behavior from time to time. That's not unfair to be asked, are you in compliance? We're being told repeatedly we're in violation. Is that unfair? It's fair to ask the question.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: It's not fair to harass. So let's just— let's— if there are questions that come up, let's answer the questions, but let's take the court order, put it on the wall, and everybody comply with it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Agreed.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Elliot, so you're telling me though that you never prepared any easements to— that is that what you're saying?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: I don't believe—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: I am— I'm gonna— I'm gonna go ahead and bring those into the next meeting for you. You can take a peek at those.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Well, why don't you send them to me beforehand rather You know what, I'd like to set up a meeting with you is what I'd like to do, and I've been requesting one for a long time.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Mr. Supervisor, can I have a meeting with the attorney here? Do we have a policy that prevents that?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: If it's your personal wish, then you need to make it yourself. What are you talking about?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: If it's a township— This is a township thing because it's selective enforcement on our citizens. That's what it is.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: With regard to easements, selective enforcement?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: It's all of that.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Just dealing with compliance with the court order at this point.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So, Ron, the court order was between the township and Mr. DeYoung, right? It had nothing to do with the sheriff, the county?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: No.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So we're, we're abiding— our township is abiding by our part of the court order.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yeah, the lines between the County and the sheriff's department and the township might get a little blurred because we have sheriff's departments, sheriff deputies working by contract for us. So I can't say necessarily that a sheriff deputy is on his own or her own and that we have no connection. I don't know that. But in general, you're right. The sheriff's department was not a party to the lawsuit, and they're not bound by the court order. Bill Bill. do their enforcement thing, and we contract with them for enforcement time. We don't generally tell them specifically, go do this and go do that. They're— they, they do what they're supposed to do.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: If this was supposed to go through the court because it's a platted subdivision, why was Holland Township involved from the beginning? Why was Holland Township involved on this one and not on Oak South Oak.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Those are some legal questions that—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Not at this board I'm going to be able to answer today.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I understand.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: We're dealing with all kinds of, uh, of street violations, street vacations. We are routinely involved in street vacations and we'll address them, and if they go smoothly, we pretty much let them flow smoothly. If they're contested, we pretty much back away.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: So, for my update on other matters, yesterday I was at the Lakeshore Advantage fellows meeting. We were talking about kind of future plan and time investment. They gave some historical data, so I handed out the sheet where they compare our area to 5 other areas kind of in the Midwest, and look at data as far as income, median housing availability as well as education levels and some of the goals that we try to maintain for our area. And then today, the Community Foundation— Community Foundation— Holland Zeeland Community Foundation had their annual We had a wonderful luncheon and just talked about the success stories that they are able to help provide support for a number of young people going through education to improve themselves all the way up through college. And they have done very well in the last few years to really build their investments in order to continue To do that. Their other biggest areas of support are looking at affordable housing in different ways throughout the county, and just supporting different education programs to meet the needs of the children in school. So, that's all I have for tonight.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I'll look for a motion to adjourn. Adjourned. Thank you.

VXCS9jDXbnw.summary:
• **Meeting called to order at 5:30 PM** with all ZBA members present; April 28 meeting minutes approved via voice vote after discussion of stipulations regarding affidavit requirements.
• **First case (645 Douglas Avenue / 19 North Division):** Fab Leasing sought variance for depth-to-width ratio (5:1 vs. 4:1 required) to split off a residential house from commercial hardware store property. Board found exceptional circumstances due to unique historical situation (house existed ~80 years on commercial lot) and approved the variance with stipulations requiring barn removal and gravel driveway improvements.
• **Second case (10671 Chicago Drive):** Kevin Nash/FPH requested six variances to create flag lot with 22-foot easement (vs. 66-foot required) and reduced setbacks to develop rear residential lot. Public comment strongly opposed (neighbors Jerry Vandenberg, Katrina Perez, and Cecilia Garza cited privacy, wildlife habitat concerns, driveway proximity). Board denied variances 1, 2, 5, and 6; approved variances 3 and 4; and denied variance 6 on a separate vote—effectively killing the project.
• **Notable discussion:** Board member Jack Vandermillan noted the driveway would come within 5.2 feet of existing house, expressing concerns about neighbor impacts. Applicant emphasized tree replanting efforts and economic development benefits, but board consensus rejected the plan as contrary to ordinance spirit and harmful to adjacent properties.

the soon as they get word of all the approvals that the next call is to the excavator to take care of that
I will make a motion to approve as submitted, including the stipulations of the removal of the barn and for the gravel drive

basically that's the neighbor's driveway within 5t of my house front corner. I take a look at that. there.

Great horned owls have nested and raised young in this exact location for many years along with numerous other species. After monitoring this habitat for more than 50 years, I believe it would be irresponsible to disrupt such a rare natural setting within our community.

IUCyS4dhNFo.summary: The Holland Charter Township Planning Commission held a regular meeting on June 2, 2026. Two special land use permits were approved: (1) an indoor golf simulator facility (Back Nine Golf) at 12330 James Street, approved unanimously (4-0), with no exterior modifications and membership-based access; and (2) a residential construction contractor facility (Skippers Construction) at 2336 112th Avenue, approved unanimously (4-0) with conditions requiring a garbage container enclosure and approved front yard landscaping. The commission discussed a draft battery storage ordinance that received attorney comments on Friday; staff will review legal feedback before bringing it back for public hearing in approximately two months. Notable discussion included the lack of utility easements at the golf facility (being addressed separately) and dumpster screening requirements at the contractor facility. Staff provided an update on ongoing projects and future planning priorities, including potential data center ordinance work.

highlights.quote: I move to approve the special land use permit for an indoor commercial recreational facility consisting of an indoor golf simulator facility at 12330 James Street sweet B90
There will no be no sales for alcohol or any type of food on the premises
Last year they had 50 franchises that were represented. This year they had 540 represented

I'll move to approve the special land use permit for a contractor facility consisting of a residential construction contractor at 2336 112th Avenue where um an approved garbage container enclosure shall be installed and an approved front yard landscaping shall be included
However, the paved lake leading to the eastern pond has been removed and a dumpster pad has been installed without proper screening
We would like to move into this new building which is 50% larger than our existing space
Those easements have been drafted at the expense of the township. Uh just awaiting the property owner signature
We got those back Friday and staff still hasn't really dug deep into the comments, but there's a couple things that we'll need to research
we have not tackled that yet that's something the planning commission would like to perhaps do next
We have 22 employees right now and hope to continue to be a good service to our community

vyi2DEFOFmY.summary:
• **PUD Amendment Approval**: Board unanimously approved (6-0) a Planned Unit Development amendment allowing personal/recreational property storage and outdoor storage with screening at a contractor facility lot on a corner turnaround near railroad tracks.
• **Zoning Ordinance Text Amendment (Second Reading)**: Approved 7-0 to allow R-2 to R-2A rezoning at 352 B Line for Women in Transitions specialty housing facility.
• **Capital Improvement Plan (6-Year, $43M)**: Presented for feedback before July 16 vote; includes $9M in FY2027 spending covering road resurfacing, bike paths ($1.06M), park amenities ($2M including $1M to Quincy Park), Fire Station #1 expansion ($1M), Sheriff building expansion ($600K), drain/stormwater projects (~$1M annually due to 1990s subdivision infrastructure failures), and Public Works building renovation ($8.4M across 2026-2027).
• **Property Sale**: Approved 7-0 the sale of 25.24-acre Halisey/Ransom parcel to Grand Rapids Iron and Metal for $883,400 ($35,000/acre) for hybrid metal/paper/plastic recycling facility with ~10 on-site jobs, one shift operation, pending 130-day due diligence period.
• **Smart Zone/Surge Incubator Update**: 10-year milestone celebration; 52 member companies in 2026 raised $1.4M capital, 119 referrals provided; over 10 years: 150 companies supported, $75M capital raised; highlighted success stories including Reveland Sports (pickleball paddles with circular economy model) and other hardware/software ventures.
• **Designated Assessor Agreement**: Approved contingency interlocal agreement with Ottawa County naming Director of Equalization Brian Busker as backup assessor in unlikely event township assessing department becomes incapable.
• **Subdivision Infrastructure Crisis**: Lengthy discussion on stormwater system failures in 1990s-built subdivisions (Sleepy Hollow, Hunters Creek, expected Deerfield/Autumn Woods); infrastructure failing 10-20 years early despite 40-50 year design life; board members requested root cause analysis and Water Resource Commissioner presentation to understand material/engineering failures.
• **Independent Investigation Motion**: DeYoung moved (supported by Malatki, passed) for third-party investigation into selective enforcement, disparate treatment, and retaliation allegations involving parks director resignation and prior employee complaint settlement; Church questioned individual legal remedies availability; motion carried despite resistance from Supervisor TeSlaa.
• **Public Comment**: Karen Vaness presented 5+ minute concern about Vaughn Avenue abandonment affecting property access/easement claims involving Dave DeYoung; Steve Willard challenged $14,886 FOIA fee for 6-month code enforcement records (99x higher than sheriff's equivalent cost).

My great concern and worry is that if Dave DeYoung or anyone acquires this Holland Township property, when it gets abandoned, if it does, I will get blocked for ingress and egress to my garage because simply, uh, Dave DeYoung already got the easement, and I feel that he feels like it's his and has like ownership to the property. I've been informed that the Ottawa County Court has dismissed all claims with prejudice. However, it is continually being brought up and discussed, and if the Lawn Avenue abandonment is ever opened up again, I would strongly request to be notified so that my attorneys and myself be present at the board meetings. Because he keeps arguing back and forth with the court system, saying he and his family are unable to get to his property. However, Dave DeYoung is not landlocked, per my legal counsel in court. Back to the year of 2008, 5 years before my husband Don passed away, Dave DeYoung had already reset our boundary lines, adding our deeded property onto his warranty deed. And all was recorded at the Ottawa County Register of Deeds. My husband and I not being aware of all this transaction being done privately and secretly. Since then, being in Ottawa County Court with this matter, the court has ordered him to correct the legal descriptions, which has been done in the last 2 years by him. However, the claim of easement is still existing, the cloud's still on my title, and I'm questioning to myself, how can I sell my house with a cloud on my title? I don't know how I'm going to do this. Dave DeYoung was given a prescriptive easement for ingress and egress only, but the cloud is still on my title, and this is the reason I have taken the case to the Court of Appeals, and hopefully a decision to have the cloud removed so I am able in the future to sell my house with a clear title and not have any questions asked from any buyers that come around. All of this harassment has cost me tons, tens of thousands of dollars, not to mention all the stress and heartaches I've experienced from an individual who thinks to himself, me, myself, and I, which is so sad and really too bad what could be a friendly environment in the neighborhood. Don, my husband, and I Save our finances for the next chapter in our life, called a peaceful and happy retirement. But not true with someone resetting boundary lines and acquiring easements unknown to me, a Holland Township resident so many years. Again, please notify me if the Lawn Avenue banment reopens for any case or any reason. I don't want to be cut off. from entering my garage and living in my home, uh, because I fear this. Thank you for hearing my situation and understanding my unfortunate circumstances. I'm sorry to have said this, but the truth is the truth. Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I asked the township for 6 months of their code enforcement records. They said that'll be $14,886. That last one wasn't a joke. On May 9th, I filed a routine public records request with Holland Charter Township asking for 6 months of proactive residential code enforcement actions. Standard FOIA, standard public records data your staff already uses, already tracks, and already presented to this board at the February 5th meeting. in the form of statistics, complaint counts, and compliance rates. On June 2nd, the last possible day of their extension, the township response was a fee estimate of $14,886.88. For context, Ottawa County Sheriff's Office produced 3 separate FOIA responses spanning 14 months of countywide law enforcement records for a combined total of $51.98. The most I ever paid for a single FOIA from the sheriff was $33.15. Same public record, same Michigan FOIA law, 449 times the cost. And here's what makes this especially noticeable. The same office charged me $150, your office, for similar enforcement records in 2025, records I turned into a 24-page report and presented to this board in December. That was $150. 6 months later, so the same records category, the same requester, the same office— $14,886. Board members, I want to be fair tonight. There are 2 possible explanations for this. The first is a data infrastructure problem. If producing 6 months of code enforcement records genuinely requires 300 hours of staff labor, which is what $14,886 implies, at the township's own billing rate, then this board has a serious operational problem on its hands. Your code enforcement department cannot efficiently retrieve its own records. That is a management and technology failure that deserves immediate attention. The second explanation, I'll leave it to your judgment. What I will say is this: the Michigan FOIA exists so that residents can hold their government accountable. A fee that makes records financially inaccessible does not serve that purpose. And what that fee is, and when that fee is 99 times higher than what the same office charged the same resident 6 months earlier with no change in scope and no change in the law, someone on this board should want to know why. I have filed a fee challenge and I have requested a waiver on public interest grounds. I am making this Board, I am asking this board to direct staff to review the fee calculation methodology and ensure that FOIA requests from residents are processed at an actual cost, not at a price designed to discourage them.
