_utterances.text: Hello.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Hi, everyone. My name is Nora VandenBrink. I'm the Vice President of Entrepreneurship with Lakeshore Advantage. I want to first start by sharing our gratitude to Holland Charter Township for supporting the Smart Zone and making our work at the Surge Incubator Space possible. I have the pleasure of running the Surge Incubator Space and supporting the founders that call the first floor of the Next home to their business. And I'd love to take some time today to share an update of some of the progress we've made. We're at a really exciting milestone of celebrating 10 years of a Smart Zone thus far, and that term will continue until 2030. And so we have some great highlights to share of the impact of the Smart Zone thus far and are excited to see the opportunities for continued growth through the rest of the term. So we'll go ahead and go to the next slide. Some metrics to share. So far in 2026, we currently are housing 52 member companies as a part of the SURGE Incubator Program. With that, thus far this year, those companies have raised $1.4 million in follow-on capital to support the growth of their companies. And in support of both follow-on capital, business growth, and milestone achievement, we've made over 115— 119 to be exact— referrals for those member companies so far just this year. Those referrals are to potential investor capital partners, resource partners, partners for customer acquisition, as well as local corporate community and education partners to support the growth of the ventures who are calling SURGE home. But again, in celebrating 10 years, it's a big milestone for this resource as being a part of the community. Over the 10 years of the SmartZone, we've been able to support 150 unique companies. From concept to market and through stages of growth. And those companies, in calculating some of the metrics that we continue to track over the 10-year period, have raised $75 million in capital. That capital raised is inclusive of both dilutive and non-dilutive funding. A fun fact is, you know, as many of our founders who are pursuing venture capital are also going after revenue-based growth models, showing that there's opportunity for lots of different business models and types in our region.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: What is that, dilutive, non-dilutive?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Absolutely. So dilutive capital would be those who are taking equity, so traditional venture capital and angel investing, whereas non-dilutive capital would be that revenue-based growth, grant opportunities, and the like. So just different ways that they're essentially pooling in capital to help the growth of their company.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay, something for which they give up equity or don't.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: That's correct. Yes, correct. So to provide an overview of kind of what our operations provide for those founders. We have programming events, wraparound resources, as well as the physical space for those founders to call their first business address and a high-polished place for them to be able to launch and grow their companies. I'm going to provide a quick overview of each of these elements just to give you a snapshot of what's happening at SURJ across the calendar year. For programming, we're really happy to be able to have curated content that addresses business needs needs as they progress. These include Lunch and Learns, which are quarterly founder-to-founder conversations where we bring in seasoned founders who have had success and growth. They come back and are able to share their stories and answer questions for those who are earlier in their journey. While their business model and output might be different, there are a lot of similar lessons learned that they can provide to our founders. So those are really valuable conversations. We also have quarterly founder roundtables, where we have a facilitator that supports founder-to-founder discussions where each founder brings a milestone or problem area that they're working towards and have a combination of peer support and subject matter expertise to help them determine a path forward to achieve those milestones and goals. Progressing to the next slide, we have cohorted accelerator programs. These accelerator programs are typically 8 weeks in length and have small cohorted opportunities to focus on particular areas of growth for their business. We wrapped up our Capital Readiness Accelerator in March. That was an 8-week program that focused on educating our founders who are looking to raise capital to support the scale of their business, to educate themselves on the dilutive and non-dilutive pathways, the different avenues of capital that they could pursue to support the growth of their business, but also match their desire to either give away equity or or to maintain that and maintain both ownership and full control of their company. It was a really great output and helped set up some of the founders with success to determine what their path forward would be. We then will be launching a traction accelerator coming up this fall that's going to be focused on customer acquisition and milestone achievement for founders who are pursuing different milestones of growth, likely a little bit further along than concept phase. They'll have some some customer traction, some revenue, and they'll be focusing on growth milestones through that 8-week program. Other resources, the wraparound resources that we provide to our founders, we have an entrepreneur in residence who has had successful exits across a few tech startups, namely software. He has participated in a Y Combinator accelerator program in Silicon Valley and is now sharing his expertise with our founders through one-on-one appointments, consultation. Though his specialization is in software, he's incredible at supporting our product-focused founders as well, helping them think through go-to-market strategy, sales strategies, as well as growth strategies. So one-on-one consultation there is really valuable. The SMART Zone then also enables us to provide some non-dilutive capital or grant-focused funding towards our founders, the first being ESN funds, where we're able to allot up to $2,500 towards project-based work paid to a service provider for each of our companies, should they choose to convert on that in a year. That would be paid to the service provider. So, a marketing agency for supporting a marketing campaign, a development firm for their website, any of those business milestones would be eligible for that. And then, in partnership with the Michigan SBDC and Michigan Economic Development Corporation, we're also able to deploy business Accelerator Funds, or BAF funds, which are up to $75,000 towards a company, tranched to reflect particular milestones of growth. Again, paid directly to the service provider so that there isn't any tax offset to the founder themselves. It goes directly towards a milestone for growth. So, we're very appreciative to be able to partner on that opportunity to provide some early-stage capital to those founders. So to contextualize this, a lot of resources, it kind of paints the pictures of what happens at SURJ. I'd love to give you an overview of who's incubating at SURJ. The images and logos here give just a small overview of a handful of founders who are calling SURJ their business home. Swivel Serve is a kitchen appliance company, if you will. Their target are first-to-market product was the Swivel Serve, a compartmented container with a cutting board top, kind of a Pac-Man shape, if you will. And as you chop, you can then put your items into a segmented container and either present at a party or store it in your refrigerator. Though this is their first physical product, they are going to expand across other kitchen and home appliances and incorporate elements of digitization, software, and hardware. So they have some cool upcoming growth strategies ahead. Audio Radar, the middle top photo, is an incredible technology that enables deaf and hard-of-hearing gamers to see sound. So, picture a TV or one of the desktop monitors in front of you. Essentially, this company has created hardware that enables you to put lights across the perimeter of your screen, and then integrates into the gaming platform itself. So that it helps show you direction and proximity of sound. So, if you were to be deaf of heart or hard of hearing, the importance of knowing where the sounds in your periphery are coming from is an experience that you wouldn't have. But this would light up with different color gradients that help to signal to that gamer where the sounds are coming from, so what direction, and then the proximity as well, or kind of the magnitude of the sound itself. Really interesting product. Locavona then is in the top right. They are an incredible platform that is making interesting strides to support agribusiness in our region. We've got a lot of incredible farmers and purveyors in our region who maybe are having a harder time getting their goods to the end consumer. Locavona creates digital grocery ordering that's delivered to your door and focuses on hyper-localization of the goods that they sell. So, as you peruse their website, you can shop similar to a grocery store, progressing through aisles from produce to meat and dairy to pantry. And you will be able to see exactly the farm that that comes from. Their platform makes shopping easy, and then the delivery comes to your doorstep. G3 Acoustics is an interesting audio technology. They create high-performance sound mechanisms for sound systems. systems in yachts. They previously were an add-on to a yacht, but they've actually gotten in with their first OEM. They are working with Galleon Yachts out of Poland. So, an incredible story of a small Holland-developed technology that's now going to be in yachts globally.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: And then the one that was hooked up with Jon Bon Jovi?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: That's correct, yes. They were in partnership with Bon Jovi Technologies for aerospace consideration of creating a similar high-performance sound experience in airplane cabins. They are still exploring that avenue as another market opportunity, but have found largest traction in boat manufacturing. The last one I'll highlight then is Theraplaying. This is a music therapy that has improved outcomes for individuals with Parkinson's and other movement disorders. They also have a physical product called the StrumPro Perfect, which is a hardware component that can add to a guitar that helps to stabilize your strumming hand, which then paired with their Strum Perfect and their playing curriculum provides improved dexterity and fine motor movement for those who have movement disorders with their first go-to-market indication focusing on Parkinson's. They had a story in the Holland Sentinel about a week or so ago. And from that have had some really exciting inbounds from local patients who have an interest in utilizing their services. And the last I'll highlight is a hyper-localized Holland Charter Township success story. There was mention of pickleball earlier. And so to complement the new investment in lights at that local pickleball place, we can share with you then about Reveland Sports. It's It's a high-performance pickleball paddle company that has unique material that they have created, and they are working towards full circularity of their product. So to kind of paint the picture of how they have benefited from the SmartZone, they launched their concept in 2019. In 2020, they went through a cohorted accelerator program supported by SURGE. It was called our Boost Camp, which helped them with customer discovery and market validation. At that point, they converted on their first Business Accelerator Fund project at a value of $8,000. That project was helping them with marketing and product launch efforts. They then progressed and scaled over the course of 3 years. In 2023, returning for another Business Accelerator Fund project at a value of $32,000, which helped with website development and marketing improvements. In 2023, as well, they were a recipient of a Sustainable Business of the Year Award through the Michigan Sustainable Business Forum. Progressing then in 2024, they represented the Smart Zones of Michigan at South by Southwest, a really large innovation conference that happens in Austin, Texas, and had an opportunity to feature and showcase Michigan in that space. In 2024, they also were one of the first investments from the Michigan Outdoor Innovation Fund, which is based out of Traverse City. Before then progressing into 2025, they received a really exciting grant from EGLE, again, working towards the full circularity of their product to improve their material technology. So now when you purchase a Revlon Sports pickleball paddle, at the end of its life, if you really just you know, wins so many tournaments that it's falling apart, you can then return that paddle for it to be recycled into a new one in the future. So with this, you know, they have some pending patents. They're exploring opportunities to diversify into the materials landscape, which could have then great impact for industry out of Holland, being the focus of manufacturing, there being such a hotbed of furniture industry here. It's a lightweight material that has a lot of capacity to then pivot into other industry opportunities and other indications. So, that's just a quick little highlight of a Holland Charter Township story right in our backyard.

_utterances.text: Yeah, so Warren and I are going to tag team this evening. So, certainly, if you had a chance to review the COVID memo that Warren had provided, I think there are a couple really good reminders in that memo to keep in mind. You know, first of all, that the CIP is a financial plan, not a budgetary authorization to expend funds, and its primary purpose is to help prioritize projects as we look out. It has a 6-year time horizon and, you know, helps us identify what funding needs are going to be, you know, what our funding resources for it might be, whether that's going to be general Fund funds, or if it's going to be something from the water sewer side, or if there's going to be grants or other types of things, other revenue sources that we might pursue. It helps us do that, and I think the presentation you know goes through a number of other items that are in his cover memo, as well as a very nice summary page that he put together, which is the second page that was in your packet regarding that with. Looking at some of the various themes regarding it, you know, second bullet there under the general government operations, you know, estimated expenditures for projects for the next six years totaling $43 million, or an average of $7.2 million per year. We'll take a little bit more detail. Look at that in a little more detail in just a second. And then certainly for fiscal year 27, which will be. The primary focus of the presentation this evening, we're estimating expenditures at about $9 million. And then, there's a nice chart on the following page that kind of gives those expenditures for both the general fund, as well as the water and sewer operations, for your review, as well. And with that, we can start jumping into the presentation. If you want to advance the slide there. Yeah, so certainly as I walk through these, I'll note some of the other pages from the CIP for your reference as well. But as I just mentioned a moment ago, for 2027, staff is estimating expenditures of $9 million. You know, that's a bump up from the $6.4 million in fiscal year 2026. And I would direct your attention to page 2 of the CIP, that's a nice snapshot of the various funds and projects that we're going through and that I— you can reference as we're looking at the slides in the presentation here. So again, if you're looking on page 2, you find that column about in the center of the page that says 2027, and if you go down to the bottom, you'll see that expenditure of $9,007,000 and what all comprises that. And so, a couple things that highlights from that funding for 2027. First of all, road construction and resurfacing sitting at $1.1 million for 2027. We were very fortunate last year. Petroleum prices and bituminous asphalt prices very low. Not the case this year. A lot of volatility in the petroleum market. Asphalt prices were up, and we certainly saw that. But staff feels— still feels confident that $1.1 million is going to be enough to take care of not only the subdivision resurfacing program that is funded out of the CIP, but also the mowing expenditures, the dust control expenditures that we do from that as well. If you look at page 5, that gives an overview of what the expenditures are going to be for the Bike path or non-motorized pathway component for fiscal year 2027. That's $1,063,000. And if you take a look at page 5 and you look at the year 2027, you'll see that the entirety of that expenditure is for the reconstruction, resurfacing of the 70-mile-plus system that we have. So there are no new pathways that are Included in the 2027 program. That changes in other years as well. But again, if you refer to page 5, you can see the various segments that are going to be addressed over the course of the next several years. Page 3 gives you a good overview of what the park amenities and upgrades are going to be for 2027. That's almost $2 million. Later in the presentation, we'll get into a few more specifics regarding what those are. for, uh, 2027. Um, page 8 of the CIP gives a good overview of what the drain projects are going to be. And again, we'll, uh, get into this a little bit more later on in the presentation, but certainly that is a significant change, uh, from the last several years when generally in our CIP we had expenditures for, um, drains in the $300,000 to $400,000 You'll see in this one it's over a million now. And, you know, when Joe Bush came and spoke about a couple of projects that are coming up, like specifically we talked about Hunter's Creek, that's a major expenditure in the 2027 program. You know, his recommendation, backed by Prinda Newhoff, our engineer, is that this is what we're going to see going into the future, that we're going to have more and more instances of subdivisions' stormwater systems failing, and that those are going to have to be addressed again, built properly at the time they were constructed, of materials that were allowed at the time. But we have found that those systems are generally not holding up. Sleepy Hollow subdivision is one we're replacing here in 2026. And we're being told this is going to continue. It's just what subdivision is going to be next as we move forward. That's why you're going to see in the CIP that we're keeping drain expenditures around that million-dollar mark for the entirety of the CIP, because we believe that it's just going to be one subdivision after another that is going to need attention. So, with that good news— yeah, sure.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Sure, happy to speak. Good evening, my name's Frank Schueller. I'm the general manager at Grand Rapids Iron and Metal. Appreciate being invited. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you for being able to speak before the board and staff this evening. Appreciate your time. Grand Rapids Iron and Metal has been in the recycle business since about 1923, so we've been around a little bit of time. We consider ourselves more of a boutique recycler. We service quite a few of the small to moderate-sized producers of scrap, if you will— scrap metals, papers, and plastics. We— because we focus on the small to mid-sized businesses, we're able to provide a high level of service and treat them at a higher level of customer service than they might otherwise get. We have been operating in the metro Grand Rapids area, as I mentioned, since 1923. We operate 2 different sites now. We have a site in Wyoming, Michigan. That is our primary paper recycling facility. And then about 3 years ago, we opened a second facility on the south side of Grand Rapids, south of 76th Street on South Division. That particular yard is primarily a metal recycling yard. We've been looking at expanding our operation. We'd like to grow. Things have been going very well at both facilities, and we wanted to move into a different market that will provide some additional options for the businesses around that particular area. We came across the property on Halsey and have been considering that for a couple months now. We started having conversation with the township, and we're excited about the property. We think it's a good location for us. It's kind of off the beaten path And access is good. Size of the property is great. We like large, square, open land. Gives us a great deal of flexibility, and we would move on to that property and put in a facility for indoor processing. But we also have outdoor processing. We are a recycle facility. We are not a high-tech. White-collar company. We are a blue-collar operation, and, um, and there are some things that go along with that, right? And as we started talking with the township, we wanted to be clear as to we're a recycler. We make some noise, we're a little dirty, but we also like to be good neighbors and, and provide a good working environment and environment around our facility as well. So we're not here to twist anybody's arm. We think it's a good fit. We hope that you do too. And I'm happy to, to take an attempt at any questions that you might have.

Although we love them and care for them, they're just not our primary target market. So we do still have at our metal facility as well as our paper facility We call them vendors, producers of scrap, if you will, that come in with their own trucks, whether they be 53-foot dry van trailers, stake trucks, or just, you know, smaller utility trailers. They do come in, but we also operate our own fleet of trucks. Again, we're not a giant company. We currently run 5 semi-tractors. and a little over 100 53-foot dry van trailers. So, those would service all 3 of our facilities.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right, then we have the consideration of the interlocal or no? Is that the next one? Yep. Interlocal agreement for yeah Ottawa County. To approve the designated assessor.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yeah, so we passed a similar measure several years back. As the COVID memo indicates, you know, what this addresses is a highly, highly improbable situation, and that being the event that our assessing department becomes incapable of conducting its process of assessment that it would be turned over to the county and then it would be under the auspices of the designated assessor. So this puts in place or formalizes a process that we currently have with the county state, and it just says again, highly unlikely, but if our assessing department is unable to fulfill its duties, those duties will be done by the County Equalization Department following the process that is included in the interlocal agreement. If you had a chance to take a look at that, you'll notice that there's signatories of all the local units of Ottawa County are signed on to this as well. And the summary sheet, which was right behind the COVID memo, kind of talks about, you know, what is the process, what happens if they start to indicate trouble within a local Assessing department, you know the steps that the county equalization will take to try to correct that. But in the event they are unable to do so, then the assessing functions will be transferred to the county, and there's a cost associated with that that's noted there as well. Again, a very highly unlikely circumstance, but there is state law that we have this in place, and this is up for. renewal at this point. And again, the designated assessor would be the Director of Equalization, Brian Busker. And our assessor, Tyler Wolf, you know, certainly is well acquainted with Mr. Busker, has full confidence in him, and certainly is comfortable and recommends approval of it as well.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Thanks, Steve. I've also met Mr. Busker. Very, very appreciative of his Appreciative of his work. He works closely with our assessing department. I highly recommend this, this, and make a motion to approve if you're ready.

So if my math is right, that increase of 408 persons, um, is the largest of any local unit of government Numerically in Ottawa County, a couple of others that were fairly close: Grand Haven Township saw a numerical increase of 349. Our friends to the west had 104, and Jamestown was another one that had a larger increase of 250. But we continue to hold our place as the second largest unit of government population-wise. behind Georgetown Township, which had an estimate of 55,682.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: Tonight, I again want to talk about accountability. At our last meeting, I heard what essentially amounted to this argument: the selective enforcement isn't happening anymore, so everything is fine now. I respectfully disagree. If something improper happened involving selective enforcement, disparate treatment, retaliation, or abuse of authority, our responsibility as a board does not disappear simply because the conduct allegedly stopped, which it actually hasn't. This is not an oversight. Our job is not to ignore problems. Our job is to determine what happened, whether policies were followed, whether corrective action is needed, and how we prevent it from happening again. That is exactly why I continue to ask for an independent investigation. Citizens have come forward with claims of selective enforcement. Employees have come forward with claims of disparate and retaliatory treatment. Taxpayer dollars have been spent. Public trust has been damaged. Yet the proposed solution seems to be, let's move on. No findings, no transparency, no accountability, no assurance that it won't happen again. That brings me to the employee complaint that was discussed in closed session.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Okay, we've heard this before.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So do you have a point?

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: I do. Yes, I do. Yep. Because it happened in a closed session, are we simply never supposed to talk about it again? I said on January 16, 2025, that I was not willing to let this get swept under the rug, even if we approved the settlement. And I am still not willing. Did anyone investigate the allegations? Were employees interviewed? Were findings made? Was any disciplinary action taken? Were any policy changes implemented? What was the outcome? If this board cannot answer—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Trustee Dave?

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: A

The investigator shall be independent of township administration and provide a written report Of findings and recommendations to the board upon completion, this motion does not presume wrongdoing by any individual. It simply seeks an objective review of matters that have generated significant concern among citizens, employees, and members of this board. That's my motion. Thanks for the motion.

And we've had enough complaints that we should be looking into this and find out exactly what happened. If there's nothing to it, so be it. I'm okay with that. But I think we have enough going on, and I think we have enough complaints and people resigning for the way that they were treated as employees That we need to look into this, and if there's nothing there, we can we can be done with it. It doesn't take care of the issues that may have happened within our township to our employees and our citizens. And I believe that we owe it to our citizens to make sure that this is— if, if it is happening, it should not be happening, and we owe it to our citizens to make sure that it's not.

Xpe6THhRHDM.summary:
- **Citizen Comments & Public Concerns**: Dave DeYoung spoke as a private citizen regarding selective enforcement and targeted harassment of his family's home, criticizing township manager Steve Bulthuis's management of code enforcement. Multiple residents (Steve Willard, Tim Bastian, Susan Veltkamp) raised concerns about Sleepy Hollow subdivision stormwater drainage project costing ~$1.6M, with homeowners assessed ~$6,000 each (roughly 18-20% of total cost). Willard highlighted a governance gap: the township's code enforcement system cannot distinguish proactive inspections from complaint-driven ones without $30,000-35,000 annual manual review.
- **Stormwater Infrastructure Presentation**: Joe Bush (Water Resources Commissioner) and Ken Lass (Prein Newhoff) presented on failing plastic pipe infrastructure in 1990s subdivisions. Sleepy Hollow's smooth-lined corrugated plastic pipe (expected 50-100 year lifespan) is failing after ~35 years. The township is replacing it with concrete for durability. Project costs: County 30%, Road Commission ~15%, township ~20%, property owners ~18-20%. Trustees Gretchen Hamzik and Elliot Church requested research into potential product recalls, engineer-of-record identification, bedding installation verification, and root cause analysis during replacement.
- **Capital Improvement Plan (CIP) & Code Enforcement Modernization**: Dave DeYoung moved to address the code enforcement tracking gap via CIP improvements, requesting staff investigate software modifications (BS&A module) to enable automated tracking. Estimated cost TBD. Trustees approved direction to modernize records management as part of CIP.
- **Employee Benefits Approval (2 Motions)**: Lindsay Cate proposed updated employee benefits including vacation accrual starting after 90 days (max 240 hours/6 weeks), sick leave at hire, bereavement leave, MLK Day as paid holiday, HET pension eligibility after 6 months (not year-end), and short-term disability ($1,992.05/month). Motion 1 passed 6-0-1 (absent). Motion 2 (pension eligibility amendment) passed 6-0-1.
- **Land Division Approval**: Lucas Hulbert (Viking Construction Group) presented lot division for 69 Coolidge Avenue. Project vacates portion of Coolidge Road to install cul-de-sac, reconfigures lots 18-21 on east side. Motion passed unanimously.
- **Clover Street Walkway Project**: Staff recommended awarding construction contract to Site Work Solutions ($156,836 bid, $242,000 total with contingency & engineering). Project creates non-motorized pathway from 8th Street to Salvation Army Community Center. Salvation Army covers ~15% of costs; township covers ~85%. Motion passed 5-1-1 (Supervisor TeSlaa voted no).

highlights.quote: I spent years trying to be heard as a citizen and I was ignored. That's the reason I eventually ran for office in the first place, to bring attention to the selective enforcement happening in this township.

Your record management system cannot distinguish between a proactively initiated enforcement action and a complaint-driven one. There is no field, no filter, no query that separates them...determining whether code enforcement is being applied equitably across this township right now today would cost somewhere in the neighborhood of $30,000 to $35,000 per year.

The pipe that's there now didn't make it to its 50-year life...That was what was approved, and the engineers can talk about that...Back in the day, in the '80s and '90s...What was approved back then is what was put in. So the question was, how did this happen?

I would like to see their assessments get cut in half. That's what I'd like to see. At the very least, let's give these people some relief...I don't know. I just feel like this township's got a lot of money. If we can do something to help these people out, let's, let's do that.
Wayne County had a big problem with it...They actually did a lawsuit, and it was a product that would fail...Has this particular product been checked to see if there were any recalls on it?

I would like to know something about— I think the township attorney might want to weigh in on what is the statute of limitations for a manufacturing defect...if there's a manufacturing defect, the township's under at least as great a financial burden from this as the owners.

If the township is going to exist in perpetuity, I think we need to have a system in place so that we are recording exactly this type of thing. I think that's a good point, right? Who is responsible? Who did it? And what did they do?

Before I sign a pledge promising equal treatment, I'd like to see equal treatment actually practiced...Until we start enforcing our ordinances equally, until accountability applies to staff as much as it applies to elected officials...I will not be signing that document.

dave_segments.text: If you don't mind, let me, let me, let me go first here. I'd like to be back in the position actually and hear you from the trustee perspective.

I continue to hear conversation about policy, procedure, and precedent. You'd have to forgive me if I'm not following your policies and procedures exactly. I may not have done things exactly the way you'd like me to, but there hasn't been even one policy, one procedure, and certainly no precedent consistently followed at my family's home over the last 15 years.

You can call me a grandstander if you'd like. That's what people often do when they don't want to address the issue itself. I call it standing up for what I know is right. I call it standing up for myself, my wife, my daughters.

We're told it's about travel and spending more time elsewhere, and maybe that's true. But I think a lot of people are asking themselves whether this is perfect timing or whether it's someone trying to get out in front of growing questions and avoiding accountability. This resignation sure seems a bit rushed and premature to me.

When it comes to serving citizens with courtesy, fairness, transparency, and integrity, he has been an absolute atrocity. In fact, I would argue he has done an abysmal job at the most important part: treating citizens equally and serving the people who pay his salary.

So while this board may be accepting a resignation, as a taxpaying citizen of Holland Charter Township, my message is much simpler. Mr. Bulthuis, you're fired. Go on and get out of here. You're fired.

So this is not concrete pipe, this is plastic pipe that we're dealing with. And you mentioned that Sleepy Hollow is one example of this. Do we have a lot more examples of this? Is that a question over here?
And in your personal opinion, is concrete a better product than the plastic?

It really sucks for the residents, right? Because— and I think as somebody selling real estate, I know I'm selling— I always check on assessments, right?...I would like to see their assessments get cut in half. That's what I'd like to see. At the very least, let's give these people some relief.

I know developers want to use the most inexpensive product that they possibly can...How can we prevent this problem in the future? And is there some kind of a regulation that we need to put in place that they use a more premium product, whether it's concrete or whatever it is.

I just want to go back to what Mr. Willard was saying here about the, uh, code enforcement tracking gap, and I guess I'm— I'd like to know Why it's so hard to track the enforcement that's being done, and what do we need to do different to make that easier?

So, my question for you is, is this enough? And I just want to make sure we do get the best employees and that we do retain them...If this is what everybody else is doing, I think we need to step it up even more from here. That's what I think.
I, I know that this was kind of addressed when we moved that meal program over there, right?...Is that a fair percentage? I mean, should they be paying a little bit bigger chunk on that?

Before I sign a pledge promising equal treatment, I'd like to see equal treatment actually practiced. For more than 15 years, I've watched staff selectively enforce ordinances, target certain residents, and ignore violations by others, and dismiss legitimate concerns when those concerns become inconvenient.

Until we start enforcing our ordinances equally, until accountability applies to staff as much as it applies to elected officials, and until these principles are reflected in actions, I will not be signing that document.

You'd have to forgive me if I'm not following your policies and procedures exactly. But I think a lot of people are asking themselves whether this is perfect timing or whether it's someone trying to get out in front of growing questions and avoiding accountability. This resignation sure seems a bit rushed and premature to me. Now, Mr. Bulthuis may be very good at certain parts of the job, I will give him that. So while this board may be accepting a resignation, as a taxpaying citizen of Holland Charter Township, my message is much simpler. Mr. Bulthuis, you're fired. Go on and get out of here. There is no field, no filter, no query that separates them. Staff would have to manually open every single enforcement record one by one to identify which cases were self-initiated. From your own fee estimate, 622 enforcement records in 2026 so far, 30 minutes per record at the rate that your staff applied, determining whether code enforcement is being applied equitably across this township right now today would cost somewhere in the neighborhood of $30,000 to $35,000 per year. That's not my number, that's your math. What happens practically is this: the board cannot know without spending $30,000 a year whether your code enforcement program has been Deployed consistently and fairly across all residential neighborhoods. You cannot know whether certain areas are over-enforced, whether certain residents are treated differently, or whether staff is initiating inspections proactively at all. The system doesn't track it. I want to be direct: I'm not here to argue a fee dispute tonight. I'm here because that is a governance gap, and it belongs in front of this board. Tonight, you're discussing the six. We have a 6-year capital improvement plan. That plan is about identifying what's broken before it becomes a crisis and budgeting to fix it. The code enforcement tracking system has the same problem as any aging infrastructure. It's broken in a way that's invisible until somebody asks a question it can't answer. Would we have approved that? You know, the standards were different back then for plastic pipe in the road and backyards. Everything was different back 30 years ago, right? So that infrastructure is starting to fail today. Sleepy Hollow is one of them. So that, I can't answer why it didn't last 50 years. But again, it's plastic pipe. And who knows over the years—

When we go out and do a job and put it out for bid, we don't have the money. We have to go out and get a drain note. That's why we carry about 70, 80 drain notes a year. Year or more to go out and cover the cost. This construction is going to be starting the week after July 4th weekend. So it starts, there's going to be cost accumulated. So we had to go get it borrowing. And the lady mentioned about we had to pay within 30 days, because we have to know how much we're going to go get a borrowing on the market for a bond to pay for it. So it's not like we can't take really Partial payments at that time because we need to know how much we need to pay in full or not. And then we have to go borrow. Now you can pay any time after we go borrow because the interest, 6%, you know, I don't set that. The, the bond market, the market sets the cost, not me. And the county doesn't— we don't make money on this.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So follow through with that 6% because that was one of the questions the gentleman had. Where's the 6% go to the bond? It goes to the It's your cost of debt.

_utterances.speaker: B

_utterances.speaker: M

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: September. So July, August, September, you have a million-dollar project and they're going to get paid a million dollars. Well, that's got to come from somewhere. Somewhere. So the bond is actually— that's what covers it, is the payoffs. Because if people try to pay off, they don't pay interest. But I don't want to have to borrow more money because then I got to pay more interest on it. So that's why— so the county does not hold on to that interest. It's going for the bond, if that makes sense.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Can you follow through on 100% pie? So at 100%, so it sounds like they're paying 18% of it. I just assume that their math's correct.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I think you guys are paying 50%, I'm guessing. I didn't bring it with me because I wasn't prepared for that, but I think it's 50%. And then I'm pretty close, I bet, the county— and I told him this— the county and the county drain office and the Road Commission. Again, we're not the same, as you guys know, but in the public, we're not. We're 2 different funding bodies. PA-51 funds the Road Commission, and then for me, it's special assessments, and that's how we cover our cost. So the county Me, the drain office, or water resources, we paid on this particular project 15%, which is required by law because you pay what's in the road right-of-way. So the grass, 33 feet of centerline, and then the pavement. So the road commission and the county, me, we split that, which was about 30% of the cost. So 80% of the cost was covered.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Gotcha.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Which is kind of unusual compared to any other county. You go to any other county, you don't normally get that kind of relationship between municipalities and the Road Commission. Well, the Road Commission, that's a state law, so, and the county for 14A is in the road right-of-way. So 80% of that, which Jim's pretty much on, 18% is they have to cover. So hopefully that answers the 80, you know, we're probably in that ballpark, 50, 30, 20 is the 100% cost. Okay. But again, it's frustrating for me because number one, I wasn't here 30 years ago or 35 years ago when the pipe got put in. You know, this was an orphan drain several years ago because one of your board members happened to live in there. I think he still does live in there. And we became— it become a drain a few back in 2018, I believe. And then with all the sinkholes the lady represented and then all the water issues. That's when there's a petition, they end up fixing it, and then it becomes one of your guys' top priorities to fix it. That's why we're here today. One of the projects, we can go through a laundry list of what we're doing in your township, but we're doing it. I mean, we have 1,000 county buildings. That's how many we have. With a small staff, we do the best with what we can.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So is there, uh, like the gentleman asked, is there any chance of recapturing any improvements they've put in to help drain be more efficient?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: That typically you can't— I mean, where do you start and stop? But you can't really go and—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I mean, I don't know the answer. I'm just asking.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: No, I know. It sucks for the property owner because he didn't know probably when we're going to come in to fix it until recently. But there was a petition. I mean, this has been in the works for a little while, but I know these folks put property or money into their own property. And but there's nothing I can do to recapture and say we're going to reimburse you for that because you can't— legally, we can't do that. You know, the drain code, I have to go by drain code, which is state law. So it's not like there's a lot of flexibility.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So are you guys presenting together?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, they're going to be presenting their PowerPoint. That's probably— but I wanted to share those questions and any other questions. Okay, go ahead.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Um, I guess this might be a question for the Prandtl people. But, back then, back in the day then, I'm assuming then the answer is yes, as far as, did this pipe meet all state codes?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Absolutely.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Any code that there was out there, it met the code?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yep, and that's, you know, and today's materials are made a lot better than what they were back then. They were pretty cheap, but that's what people used. And, not just in Sleepy Hollow, not just in Ottawa County, it was everywhere. So, which is why we're having issues not just in Ottawa, but my colleagues across the state with failing infrastructure. If it's not concrete, even concrete fails, right? Concrete's supposed to last 7,500 years, but joints fall apart. The concrete, sometimes they don't get wrapped in between. They start getting sinkholes because it starts sucking sand under road. That's how you get sinkholes near catch basins, manholes, because those joints start. Keep shifting, moving. Something else that's happened over the years, when water table comes up, it'll pressure, it'll rise plastic pipe, right? It'll pressure underneath and it'll push it up, and then the water table goes down. Well, they'll start getting separation in those gaps and form those sinkholes in those gaps. Usually it's in the road because they're all put in a straight line, and then you'll see the sinkholes right where the joints are. That's what you see a lot of times.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: And that's just because failing infrastructure. It doesn't last forever. Yes, this pipe should have lasted probably 50 years, but— and you can't go back on the developer, because I guarantee you the developers that were back 35, 40 years ago, they're not around today.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: No.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: They're not even around. So to go after them, who are you going to go after, right?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Can I make a suggestion before we ask questions? Is that let you guys do their presentation, then we can kind of tag team it? Unless you guys want to do it differently.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I have a question maybe for you, Jim.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, go ahead. Go ahead, Corey.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: If you don't mind. So this is not concrete pipe, this is plastic pipe that we're dealing with. And you mentioned that Sleepy Hollow is one example of this. Do we have a lot more examples of this? Is that a question over here?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Probably not as bad as this particular subdivision, but there is other examples. What is unique in this one is there's plastic pipe in the road. Now, typically, plastic pipe is usually in the backyards.

_utterances.speaker: K
_utterances.text: In here?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: It was in the roads. Now, the Road Commission back then said, well, that's what they put in the roads. Now, they don't allow plastic pipe in the roads any longer. It is mandatory concrete. So, or triple-wall plastic, but it's triple-wall, which is just as hard as concrete. But typically concrete's almost cheaper than it is that triple-wall plastic pipe, because that stuff is pretty expensive. But, so yeah, to answer your question, Sleepy Hollow at this point in time today is one of several, but it's probably one of the worst ones because we're dealing with it in the road right away under the pavement.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I think Brian and Newhoff might have some answers. Can we— all right, let's just— can we let them talk and then we'll hold our— can we just hold our questions till they do their presentation and we'll have all the time you want? Yeah, I think he installed it.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: I'm kidding, that was Thanks for inviting us. I'm Ken Lass, with Bryan Newhoff. Lucas Timmer is here, also with Bryan Newhoff. So, what I want to do, I'll go through this rather quickly, because I know you've got a long agenda. But, here quickly, as a background, as Joe was mentioning, you do have a lot of projects that you have ongoing. I know that you see that in your CIP list. I guess the only reason I bring this forward is it just shows that the growth in the township that has occurred over the several decades, and there is a time where infrastructure needs to be replaced, things begin to fail, and that's why you're starting to see a long list of different projects. I also would note that some of the— a majority of these are due to enclosed pipe systems, ones that you're familiar with, subdivisions that we're talking about, but there are also some open drain projects that are ongoing too, which adds to your capital improvement list. So for stormwater issues, how do we even get notified of what's happening? Typically it's property owners that are calling us. And when they call us, or call the township, or call the Water Resource Commission, often, particularly Joe, will ask us to go out there, take a look at, see what we can identify, and find out what the issues are. And then we write up a report. On enclosed systems, more and more we're asking, and Joe particularly is in agreement with this, that we televise the enclosed systems. And that's really where we find a lot of our information nowadays. You can see so much more than what you can see from sometimes above ground. As already stated, typically what we find in these systems, there's 3 different types of material that we're finding in the stormwater systems. And that is Corrugated metal pipe, concrete, and then what Joe was referring to as plastic, which is called smooth-lined corrugated plastic pipe. Those are what we typically find. Site conditions and installation, how it's done, can affect how the pipe lasts as far as its longevity. And I'll get a little bit into that in the following slides. I also wanted to reference that this isn't just unique to the stormwater system. We work obviously with your utility systems too, and we see that in your utility systems as well, that there are sometimes materials that are trying to be competitive in the world and have been referenced from other communities as good materials back in the day and were approved. But we're finding out over time, sometimes some of those things don't last as long as we expect. And some of those examples just in, in the utility world Is the polybutylene water services. As you know, we've been doing a lot of replacements of those. PVC water main, and even one of the things in the PVC water main, they have changed the molecular structure of that over the years. So that's improved. I do know some communities still believe in it. There, back in the day, the problem was that it put a stress on it. If you tap it, it'll crack that pipe instantly. And then finally, in the sewer, the concrete sewers and manholes have been deteriorating due to hydrogen sulfide. And what the township has done to help some of that longevity and repairs has been lining those particular pipes. So there are some things in other areas too that we're seeing besides just stormwater. As Joe's noted too, I will note that Both of these, where we find all these materials that we've been using, have been approved, have been accepted in the industry as approvable materials. Specifically the stormwater, and I guess I want to address then particularly like the smooth-lined corrugated plastic pipe, that seems to be most of the question for the subdivisions. That was approved by the county, but the county doesn't approve it until Until MDOT approves it. Back in the day, there was a process where MDOT went through, did a lot of testing and test results, and until they approved something, the county would not approve something. So MDOT had approved the smooth-lined corrugated plastic pipe, then the county approved it after having conversations, I'm sure, with other communities and that as well, as to what to use. And therefore, that's how it got accepted into being used back in the '90s. And the developments. So, moving on, corrugated plastic, or corrugated metal pipe. As you can see here, the biggest issues that we're seeing, the longevity of a corrugated metal pipe is typically about 25 to 50 years. What we're finding there is that there's a reaction with the water and oxygen that starts corroding, let alone the abrasive materials of stone and sand Which is what's causing the corrosion typically on the bottom. So we're finding bottoms of these culverts fail on us. And that's what you see in some of those pictures. On the far right, we've had where we discovered probably some heavy equipment may have gone over the material. And you can see some dents inside the top there. The way corrugated metal pipe is put together, it's put together basically with some kind of straps, because they They don't have a place to hone into it. Sometimes those fail, the way the load is. And that's probably why you see a little bit of that separation in that pipe there. Concrete pipe. I think Joe has alluded to this too, but we feel the same way, that that's probably one of the best materials to use. The longevity expected of a concrete pipe is about 75 years. But there are issues with that too. The plus of concrete pipe is it does not need to be bedded as well. It needs to be bedded good. What I mean by that is when it's laid down into the trench, they have to put material near the side, some sand or stone near it, compact it to kind of get under that pipe and haunch support that pipe. Again, concrete's a lot stronger, so if there's, there's more room for error With regard to that. But what we're finding in concrete pipe, there are some cracks. We're not sure why or where all those go. Must have heavy equipment, again, is on those. Or perhaps when they were installed, maybe the contractor was a little bit more careless. They may have dropped somewhere, started the crack, and then happened. I will say that that is usually pretty rare when we TV goes compared to plastic pipe that we don't see near as many cracks. We're finding utilities are a problem. Directional drill is a big thing right now. And so that picture on the bottom is showing somewhere where we got directional drill. Then what happens is we get debris and etc. also hanging up on that, which, which contributes to flooding issues. The joints, part of what Joe said too, around manholes, And all that tend to deteriorate that concrete, so that affects. But also back in the early days, storm sewers were installed with what we call open joint pipes. And that means there was no gasket where the 2 joined together. And the reason for that was because the groundwater is rather high in this area. And what they were trying to do is keep the groundwater down. So they keep the joints open, groundwater flows through. What happens then when you've got open joints like that? Over time, you probably start pulling some sediment in, which starts causing these sinkholes as well. In addition, what do trees like to do? They like to find any openings they can. So tree roots and all that start to come into something like that. So those are some of the issues we're having with concrete storm sewers. And now smooth-lined corrugated plastic pipe. Their life expectancy The life expectancy is supposed to be 50 to 100 years, as I think acknowledged by some of the residents here as well. What we have been seeing though with smooth-lined corrugated plastic pipe, we think, is, was, was the installation done properly? So unlike storm, the concrete part, the smooth-lined corrugated plastic pipe has to have good bed support behind it. That means it has to be really compacted well, has to have the right material. And the Material right material is what? in there. There's been a lot of contractors, there's been a lot of different developers, there's been a lot of different engineers in this area. Can't say what happened, you know. I can't say that we had eyes on everything because that wasn't our responsibility, and nor was it the townships or the counties. But that could be a lot of the issue. Sometimes it's the COVID Sometimes I I can't prove this, but I believe because most of these we find in our subdivisions, probably with shallow cover on them, as homes are being built years later, equipment runs over not realizing there's a storm sewer system in there, and that heavy equipment can't handle that storm sewer. We're having the same thing with utility borings through some of these things. Again, the old pipe, the The very first part of these pipes went in, they went in also with gaskets and zip ties. That's how they were formed. Today, they have much more of a bell and spigot type form, so they definitely form much better. But back in the day, they had these gaskets, so some of those could be having some of the issues as well on there. We also have, we find often that there have been homeowners that like to have tapped the pipe on their own to get rid of their, some pumps or roof drains. And when they do that, those seals may not be as good. So again, we're introducing more silt into the pipes, encrustation starts to happen, it may even fracture the pipe in its own way. So those are some of the things that we're finding that maybe are reducing longevity of these 3 different pipe materials. And I will stop for questions, I just got a couple quick more notes here. Again, CIP, open drains, you're well aware of the issues there. Log jams, erosion that causes loss of land, etc. So those are some of your other projects that are going in the open drains. And then finally, I just want to— I don't know how many of you know this, but the history with the township was they did used to pick up all the drainage at large. And they did that until in 2008, 2009, 2013 particularly. We had at least those 3 years were what we consider more than a 100-year storm event. So we have heavy flooding in the township. And calls came in heavily to the township, to the Water Resource Commission about, hey, we need something to be done to get rid of this water. Well, to be honest, typically we don't even design systems for that big to convey storm sewers in subdivisions our size for what we call a 10-year storm event. But after that happened, and there were so many calls, so many issues that went on with all these large flooding events, I believe the, the township started looking at their policies. And what they wanted to try to do is say, hey, we want to have the property owners have a little bit of teeth in the game here. So they went to more of this process Where once a petition is prepared, they were going to pick up a significant amount, which they have been doing. You guys have been doing that way. But they also wanted to make sure the property owners wanted to have the project bad enough that they were going to put some teeth into the game. And that's why today some of those stuff's assessed. Which, to Joe's point too, is very typical across all the townships in the county. So, just wanted to give a little bit of history on that.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Go ahead, you had some questions, Gretchen?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Gretchen?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: I'm wondering if, um, all of these subdivisions, they're about the same age, aren't they?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: We had a lot of growth in the 1990s, so a lot of them would have had the same product.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: It's the same product, right?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Was the product ever recalled? Because I, I looked into it and there were several products that were recalled during that time.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, I, to be honest with you, I don't recall it ever being recalled. And I'll be honest, it's still being used today.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Wayne County had a big problem with it.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: And maybe they did as a county, but I will be honest, it's still being used today. I will say, so I do, I do drainage reviews for some counties as well, and a lot of individual sites will still want to do it. And I will often put my letter recommend that they consider an alternative to that because of what we're seeing, but it's still being used a lot today.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Has this particular product been checked to see if there were any recalls on it? Because it just seems ironic to me that during that time frame that all of these are failing, and these are not the oldest subdivisions in Ottawa County. No, they're not. And if it was the product that's defective, I understand the developers may not be around, but the product is, and there is the infrastructure that was put in by whoever laid the pipe down. They still have to be around. Is there anything we can do so that it— the burden doesn't fall so much on the county, the townships, and the homeowners? Has anybody checked into that? Because I have heard so many horror stories in other counties in Michigan that have had these problems.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Again, I'm not aware of any recall on there. I'm not saying there is or isn't. I have not heard of the—

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: So no one checked into that?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Well, again—

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: If it was a product defect, that's what I'm wondering.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Again, it's still listed on the MDOT's list of materials as well as the county's. So for that reason alone, I tend to believe that the recall Because if it was, I'm sure MDOT would be taking it off their list.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Anything else, Gretchen?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: I think it's worth being checked into.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Okay, we can do that.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Who manufactured it?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: But I will also tell you, from a contractor's perspective, I mean, they, they probably have agreements. Like, I know when the township has agreements with contractors, There's this 1-year warranty. That's what they have on there.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: But it's a product.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: It's a product that's failing.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: And if there's a 20 to 50-year— what was it— guarantee on it?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Well, most of these— is it a product that's failing or is it installation? Because again, I want to say—

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: I would like to know.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Well, again, I don't know how you're going to prove that because what I tend to believe Where the most failure is, is probably installation. Because I'm guessing we're finding that most of this crushing is in the backyards. And they're not getting enough bed around it or whatever.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: I think on Sleepy Hollow, it's right in the middle of the street. Is it not?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That one is, yes.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah. But we're dealing with it with Hunter's Creek too. And there it's all in the backyard. Because they have concrete in the street. And there's not the issues of concrete.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Okay, is there concrete in the street of Sleepy Hollow, or is it plastic?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: No, that is plastic.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Okay, that's my point. So Hunter's Creek has concrete, Sleepy Hollow does not, correct?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Okay, see the difference there? Yeah, we have, we have residents here that are upset. Yep. So it's, it's not— I'm not putting any blame on anybody. I'm saying let's help solve the problem, or at least get these questions answered for these homeowners. They have been They've been calling on us for 10-plus years is what I've been told. There's been an issue going on for 10 years.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: But what I would need correction for something like that, if I were looking into it from that perspective, is I would need to understand what are the densitor reports, what are the data reports, and I don't know what they are. We weren't responsible for that. That was—

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Right.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: The developer's engineer, there's a contractor. So where are those reports available 30, 35 years later? I don't know.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Well, the product's failing right now, and that subdivision is how old? 30 years old, right? Is it 30? Does anybody know?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: 35. And if this started 10 years ago, that's 25 years.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Okay, if I'm a homeowner, got 25 years, bought a lot in a subdivision, paid my assessments, paid my taxes, did all of that, and I start calling because The road's caving in in the middle of the road. I would be very upset if it took ten years after making multiple repairs on my own sub pump. Whatever happened, this gentleman spoke on to figure out that we have plastic pipe in the middle of the street and it's failing. And now we get a letter and we're told we have to pay X number of dollars. Could possibly maybe be a recall. If anybody's checked into that, I think it's worth checking into.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Can I speak up on that?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Sure.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: So, Ken is correct.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: A simple Google search shows it's not been recalled. This material is still being used by MDOT, so approved by MDOT. You can search Smoothline corrugated plastic pipe recall. There's nothing that shows up for that. So the material is still acceptable.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: I think there's also some confusion.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: So then, no one would check into it to see if actually that product— because Wayne County had this. I did a simple Google search, as you said.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Wayne County had several subdivisions.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.text: And they actually did a lawsuit, and it was a product that would fail.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: So you can call it the Smoothline, but it was the same type of piping.

_utterances.speaker: G

_utterances.speaker: J

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Can I, can I—
