_utterances.text: Can I, can I just butt in, Gretchen? So I think what Gretchen's asking is, can Prien Newhoff do a little research to see if there was a recall or something on that type of pipe and get back with us? Is that possible for you guys to do that?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: It's not your— I'm not saying it's anyone's fault here. I'm saying we have residents that are asking questions. They're upset. They've been upset for 10-plus years. They want to be heard and they would like some answers. And I think that we owe that to them.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Yeah, certainly. So, yeah, share with that on Wayne County. That'd be helpful if you could send that over to us.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any other questions, Gretchen, for him? I think Elliot has some too, and maybe Dave does too.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay. May I?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Go ahead, Elliot.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: All right then. Number one, I want to thank you for your long-term service on the Planning Commission. So your expertise here today is valuable, and your expertise on the Planning Commission for many years was very valuable to this township. So thank you for that. Um, and, and I think the same goes for Joe Bush, who's been serving the county for a long time, appears in front of this board on a regular basis, and it is my impression that you take—

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I do.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Extraordinary interest in the quality of the drains in the entire county. I have a hard time imagining someone with a greater passion for the smooth operation of that system. So I do appreciate those folks who are here for those reasons. I have a number of questions for both of you, but since Ken is up right now, I'll start with you. So there's a 1-year warranty, right? But at some point I heard that there was some representation that it should last 50 to 100 years. So where did that representation come from?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: So that will come from testing that the manufacturer provides, right? And what they will tell you, and they'll show you in your documents again, is that they, they will guarantee that based on how your installation is, and particularly how the bedding is put in.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: So that's where some of the failure's probably going to come from, if there was a challenge on the pipe, is to say— they'll come back and say, show me that you bedded it properly.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay. Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Are there instances where pipe of this type was installed 50 years ago and is still operating? Do we have positive results too, or are they all negative?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: To answer your question, from what I know, I don't know because I'm not aware of anything in the township, nor have I been here that long. I believe MDOT would be— would have some of those. I don't know where, um, but that would be my understanding.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Right. So this contention about the bedding mattering only makes sense if there are some positive results for the manufacturer to rely on. And so I, I think that that's an important technical question here. And I guess I, for one, would ask that you guys look into that. I mean, if there are positive results, then I think, okay, I think that that claim seems to hold up. But if we don't have any positive results, then I think the claim is false. Okay, if the claim is false, the manufacturer has a manufacturing defect. Right. And then I, I don't know what the statute of limitations is on that, but it probably has something to do with when it was discovered. All right. And if the manufacturer is still around and we've only just discovered this, I'm thinking there might be a claim. And so I think from a township perspective, right, I, I guess I would like to know something about— I think the township attorney might want to weigh in on what is the statute of limitations for a manufacturing defect. But I think that only comes after this question, which: are there any positive examples of success? If there are none, then I think the next question is concerning statute of limitations on manufacturing defect, because the township's, you know, under at least as great a financial burden from this as, as, as, as the owners. And I think the owners feel it more acutely, I understand. But I think we have an interest as a township at whole in looking into why are we spending a bunch of money on this if there's a manufacturing defect. So I really want to know the answer to that. That seems like a threshold question, right? And then I think, Steve, it's incumbent upon you to get that answer and then follow up with the township attorney if there's— if it's a negative result. None of this has lasted 50 years, you know, thumbs down for that product. And I think we need We need to see what— if there's legal recourse.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I'm a little bothered by our not knowing who is responsible for the bedding. Is that information available but we just haven't tried to uncover it? Or do we not know what— who did the work?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, we probably could dig deep into files to find out who did the work. I think the issue that you have on reporting and that, that has, like most things, most regulatory agencies and everything, things have improved that way as far as requirements of documentation and being there than it probably was 30 years, 35, 40 years ago. So I don't think that document is as available today for back then as documentation is prepared today on those things. Okay, so short answer, I, I doubt you're going to find it. That's my, my opinion.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: But you could look.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, you could look.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So I'm firmly in agreement with Vince, is that, that, uh, I would ask that you look and, and come back with an answer. You know, what, what can we know? It would be nice to know if there's any patterns here. If every drain system that is failing had bedding installed by a single contractor, I would really like to know that. And I'd like to know who. And I'd like to be sure we're not doing more business with them today. I think that to the extent that we can uncover that information, I think it's incumbent upon us to do this. Because they're going to spend a lot of money, we're going to spend a lot of money, the county's going to spend a lot of money. I think we need to understand to the extent we can Why? So, I would ask that you research it and provide the information to Steve. Did Prein Newhof do all the engineering on this or not?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: No, we didn't do any of that.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay. What was Prein Newhof's involvement with these projects? You keep tabs on it, in a sense, but—

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: So, back in that day, those days, we would probably do a plan review. I don't recall for sure enough, but, um, if we would inspect, but that would be strictly for the water and sanitary sewer.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: We would not be involved in the storm sewer streets.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay. All right. You wouldn't have engineered it, spec'd it?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, we may have done some plan reviews for drainage through Joe's office on some of those.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: But again, not inspection or any of that stuff for that. So the plan review would be looking at, does it meet the capacity that is required by their standards? And looking at cover on different things and materials was one at that point. So yes.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay. I, I think one thing that has disturbed me over and over again in my tenure here is our inability to look back. The township's going to last virtually forever, right? And these things are going to last 30 years, hopefully, or 50 years if that's how they're specced out. But— and I know it's— we always have the same impasse. Oh, you know, we don't have records, or 30 years ago, it's too long. No, I think if the township is going to exist in perpetuity, I think we need to have a system in place so that we are recording exactly this type Type of thing. I think that's a good of point. thing, right? Who is responsible? Who did it? And what did they do, right? If, if we're, if we're approving a drain system and it is incumbent upon us to pay for its replacement, whenever that's the case, I feel like we need to have good records. I know this won't impact us, you know, for 30 years, but in 30 years, I would hate to see the same problem, right? There'll be a set of people just like us here I don't want them to be experiencing— I think we do need to record that diligently whenever they or, or us or we collectively are going to have to bear the cost of replacement. It sounds very important and it seems very easy to undercut whatever good work might be if you know, right, that you're not involved anymore. I don't like that. I don't like that deal. I don't like it for the township, and I don't like it for its residents. So I would ask that this be on the agenda for a future meeting, that we get a report.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So can I clarify that?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Please.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So is there an opportunity with the new subdivisions that are going in that you can go from pipe to sub— you could track it back, who did what? The subdivisions, that data's got to be out there somewhere, wherever we're doing it in, over by 100th. There's a subdivision over there, Bauman Buildings is doing it. Who's putting in— who put in the sewer? Who put in the pipe? Who did this? We should be able to find that and at least start a record.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, we would be able to have that information. I guess what I'm hearing Trustee Church say is, though, is he looking for documentation of, okay, where are the density reports and when was what put in and all that stuff? And I don't have those records.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: But, but you're talking 35 years ago, right? But currently you'd have them.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: We have. So, from the township's perspective, if you're talking about the utilities, we've been hired to do part-time work. So, we are not out there every day, or I'm sorry, we're out there most every day when they're working, but not all day long. So, we would not necessarily see every bit of pipe going.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I get it. I think he's just— I'm not trying to talk for him, but I think he's trying to draw a thin line. Here's what we did, board the future, because we're not good. The township probably won't go anywhere. Here's what they did in the year 2026. Here's your line. You could find it if it happened. Go back to the record. And I can tell you, I can tell you today, is that what you're asking for, Elliot? Something like that?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Exactly.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: And I can tell you today, our people that are out there for water and sewer are noting in there what we call IDRs, individual data reports. They're saying compaction is being done, sand's in the backfill that's being dumped. We do not receive copies of those density reports. That is the responsibility of the contractor and/or engineer of record who designed that.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: But there's— it's out there.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So it would be real interesting to know who the engineer of record was for the projects. I think that's the source of information here. If there's an engineer of record and we can determine who they are for all of these things that are failing, I think that's a great Sorry to interrupt you. No, thank you for helping out. May I continue? I have a number of—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, I got these guys here.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's what they're here for.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Just briefly, you commented on deterioration due to hydrogen sulfide. What is the source of hydrogen sulfide, and when would it happen?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: So, when the sewage Is kind of released in a way such that air is brought to it. So, it can enter near a manhole, or a drop, or whatever. Those gases are given up, and that's what starts eating the pipe down there.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay, when it's aerated, so to speak.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah. So, it goes from an anaerobic condition to an aerobic condition.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: All right. Air is introduced into the system.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: All right. It's exposed to the air, like these gaps in the pipe that you're talking about where the joints are open instead of closed.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So it creates—

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: The conditions are creating this chemical that's corrosive.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, it wouldn't be so much joints there for wastewater, it would be more manholes and things like that.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay. Is that avoidable? So you mentioned gaskets in some cases, or maybe a different approach to sealing joints?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: For storm sewer.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: For storm sewer.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: It was typical not to have gaskets in those back in the day because they were trying to keep the groundwater down.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: What about now? Is it different?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: They all have gaskets.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: They all have gaskets.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: In fact, like I said, even, even the plastic pipe that's installed today, they all have gaskets that are being installed.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay. Yeah. All right. I'm really concerned, uh, that we, we do get reports back on these things. I, I I frankly don't know where things go, right? And I think somebody is tracking it. If government is good at one thing, it's process. But I think I want to be very careful that this comes in front of the board again and we get an update. And I would like to ask that it be on the agenda 2 months from today and that we get an update on who is the engineer of record, what have we been able to learn about who installed the bedding. I think these are the key questions technically.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Are you clear what Gretchen and Elliot want?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, we'll do as much digging as we can.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: How much time do you need to do it?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: What's that?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: How much time do you think you need to do it? 90 days?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: I think if you're asking just from 2 months from now, we may not have everything.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So in September? September meeting, would that be adequate? Dave, you got questions?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I do have a couple. I don't think—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'm sorry, were you done, Elliot?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I do have more, but I'm happy to see—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: No, I'm sorry.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Go ahead.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I was just trying to keep things rolling.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: And I apologize for taking a lot of time, but it's a rare occasion where we have a collection of—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: No, yeah, I thought you were done. Sorry.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: And a collection of concerned people here. So sorry, but I think it's important.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: No, yeah, do it. Yeah, do it.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Joe, who is the lender? So who's the— just out of curiosity, you said the Drain Commission wants you to determine what work needs to be done and what it will cost. You go seek a loan so you have the cash.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: You put it out for bid.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: You put it out for bid and you get whatever lender gives you the best rate.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Usually it's like a couple of these. We had 4 or 5 different lenders, mortgage companies, commercial banks. Usually the bigger ones. We don't get local people. Well, you're doing a bond, right?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: He's not doing a loan, he's doing a bond. He's issuing debt that someone's going to buy.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Yeah, they buy our bonds. Oh, I see. Yeah, so it's not like a— you're not going to get a bank loan from, uh, Macomb Bank. We actually go put it out for bid. This is all legal documents through our bond counsel. How big is your bond you're asking for? Well, this one was at one point— I don't know, after everybody paid off You guys, we got all the payoffs. I think we ended up with about a $300,000 bond, but it's a $1.5 million—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I'm going off my top of my head. Yeah, yeah, yeah. So it's substantial, substantial bond.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: All right, but because you guys paid off, the county paid off, everybody paid off, property owners paid off, that's why the 30-day notice, like, hey, we got to pay off, we got to put the bid out legally. This is all through drain code. It's not like I pick and choose when I want to do this. I have legal statutes to follow on my timelines. And this is why it takes— it's very complicated to do a project when you have all these projects with limited staff. Again, there's a lot of—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Go ahead, Elliot.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I want to reiterate that I, in, in my experience, okay, and I've been here for 10 years, maybe more, and you've come into— I think you're doing a great job.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: All right, I have been impressed every time at the passion you bring to this subject. And the detail, it's remarkable, right? I have a hard time imagining someone having a greater passion and investment of energy into it. And I know you go out and inspect these things yourself, okay? So I'm not doubting your commitment. But I think it's important to ask about some of these things. And so the lending, it's bid out, right? And you get the best interest rate you can. Right. All right. And that seems sensible from a treasurer standpoint? Oh yeah. Okay. So I think because of the county's rating.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: So I don't think these are full faith and credit through the county, which I had to go to the full board. Okay. To get full faith and credit, which is— gets for lower percentages. Yeah. To include it when I put it up for bid.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay. So you're doing your best, and I don't have any reason to doubt that. It seems like the process supports that. The other thing is notice period. So somebody brought up, and I think it Mrs.

_utterances.text: No, I just can I just say something about with Elliot? One one quick break or answer it. _utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I mean, I hope not, but I love my job.

_utterances.speaker: B

Commission and your community development and then the engineers, we're all on the same page that we inspect, we do have documentation, it's all in my files, we have as-builts, we keep very good track record of our drains and our developments, at least since I've been here and even a couple years prior to me getting elected. But the documentation and the tracking has been much better. But technology, and I hold the engineers accountable to make sure that— a lot of times we camera the system when it's after the installation to make sure there's not a failure that we don't see because it's underground. It's easy to see when it's above, but when it's below, you don't see it. So I do camera a lot of times to make sure that I trust them, but it's always trust and verify and make sure and document and check the box that it's completed. Properly, install it properly, the joints are together, you don't have gaps, you don't have crushed pipe. So, there's times that I do go and camera— well, it's a lot of times. I do go make sure that it is installed properly, because it is taxpayers' money. And, that's my money, your money. We all pay a lot of money in taxes. I want to make sure that the bottom line, when the buck stops here, for me to do the project, that I oversee it. And, oversee it to its end, with documentation. So, I appreciate Mr. Church's comments, because I think it's my job as an elected official to make sure that gets done. And that's only since I've been elected, so I can't speak 2012 prior, but I know it's a pretty good record. But, I even try to do it better.

_utterances.speaker: H

I want to know if there were any positive results that anyone can report on where this thing lasted 50 years or is, is still not problematic. I would like to know that there's a positive result. And then finally, related to these questions, if we're going to go in and repair these things, I think we have an opportunity to do root cause analysis, right? So if the engineer says, well, such and such bedding installed. If we're going to go dig it up, I think it's a question that can be answered, right? We can figure out if that's true. I think digging it up is, is the opportunity to learn that. We should document what we find. I think that should be part of this process of replacement. And is there some kind of a regulation that we need to put in place that they use a more premium product, whether it's concrete or whatever it is. I, I know it's a different pipe. I hear different plastic pipe. All the plastic pipe sounds like it's terrible to me. What can we do moving forward so that we are eliminating this problem and the product lasts for the 50, 75, 100 years for concrete, whatever it is? But how can— and I know it's not going to be perfect, but I'd sure like to get more than 35 years out of something that they said was going to be 50 to Understood.

_utterances.speaker: E

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: A couple of quick things on that I would say is, again, all the pipe that is used, particularly for stormwater, is analyzed through MDOT, which does a lot of testing and things like that. That comes cycling through. So none of it, of what Joe puts in, is put in unless MDOT has approved the same material. The other thing is you're always trying to also look for some ability to be competitive in some way. So again, using material that's approved, allowing different ones, because if you only allow one particular material always, your prices are going to skyrocket all over. So as a realtor, your costs are going to go up per house too because you're only using one material. Contractors, developers know that. Boom, the price goes up.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So it wasn't too unfamiliar to listen to all of that. I'm Lindsay Cate, and I currently am the payroll and benefits administrator for Holland Charter Township. So I'm super excited to be here to propose some updates to our employee benefits. Okay, these benefits are based on a Employee feedback, or these updates for the benefits are based on employee feedback and a review of comparable organizations. The updates are intended to support employee well-being and engagement, strengthen our ability to recruit and retain employees, while allowed to enhance service delivery to township residents. I will briefly walk through each of these proposed updates. The first one is an updated vacation schedule. The updated vacation schedule was developed after reviewing practices in comparable organizations, considering feedback from employees, and evaluating the township's operational needs. Essentially, an employee would earn vacation time after 90 days of employment, and they would reach their max at year of 240 hours or 6 weeks. Number 2, sick leave. Providing sick time at time of hire supports workplace health and well-being, aligns with practices in comparable organizations, and strengthens our ability to recruit employees. Therefore, sick time should be granted to all full-time employees at hire. Rather than after completion of the first year of service. Number three, bereavement leave. Employees would be entitled to bereavement leave for the death of immediate family members. This leave would be provided by the township and will not require the use of employees' sick leave. Number four, adding an additional pay. We are proposing to add Martin Luther King Day as that paid holiday. Number 5, the HET pension eligibility. Full-time employees shall be eligible to participate in the HET pension plan on the first day of the month following 6 months of continued service rather than the first day of the plan year. This would, depending on when an employee is hired, sometimes the waiting period before they can get into the plan is very long. So this change would allow them to be eligible sooner. Number 6, adding a short-term disability coverage would be part of the township's benefit package, providing this Adding short-term would provide partial income replacement for situations like maternity leave or recovery from surgery or any long-term illness or accident that's not work-related. Finally, the financial impact to adding the short-term disability. The monthly premium for the short-term coverage is $1,992.05. Adding this benefit to the township's existing long-term disability and life insurance package will result in a 5% rate reduction from our carrier. The combined monthly cost for all 3 will be $3,557.80. Financial Director Warren Smith has evaluated this proposed addition and determined that the impact for this year's budget would be minimal. In closing, These updates are intended to further develop our benefits program and represent continuous improvement. I respectfully request your approval, and I welcome any follow-up questions.

_utterances.text: We thank you for bringing this forward. I think you're representing employees and the idea of quality of life, and I appreciate that. When we, when we get a proposal, normally we know something about the overall impact on the budget, and what I don't see here is any quantification of what the effect is. Okay, so if 3 3 more of this are granted and, you know, 80 more of that. Essentially what the calculation that I think the township is making is, you know, money for work hours, right? How much money buys how many work hours? And, and I have a feeling that this will alter that ratio. And so it would be in line with, I think, every other proposal we receive to know something about the financial impact. And, and I— the only, the only word I see on that is the word minimal, right? Which doesn't seem like enough information for ordinary decision-making. I would not vote for anything where that was the only input I had. And so I think that this is a great start, but in my view, you know, more information is needed from a budgetary standpoint to understand what, what is the overall impact, right? How much do we spend on employment now? I'm cognizant of what Terry says, right, that people seem to be quite satisfied as it is. And things that I don't see in here but would like to know, who are we comparing to? This is important not just for vetting the idea but for supporting the idea. I think we need to understand what are the comparables here, what, what townships are those, um, and where does this fall. I need simply more information to know where does this fall within the range of, of, uh, of compensation plans or benefit plans. When the fire department comes to ask for a truck Right. We have some sense of what other townships are doing and what can— what, what's, what's the cost and how much are they paying. And here, I don't like making a decision on no quantitative basis. I feel like that is not in line with all the requirements we make for everyone else who comes in here, right? People come in and ask for a tax rebate and all these kind of things. I want to know what the impact is.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Do you want to speak on that, Lindsay? Because I know seen it. Um, sure, she has that, some of that information.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: The employees that would be eligible for the pension if we change the eligibility start date, I can, I can share that there would be 13 employees eligible. There would be 6 or 7 that would be eligible Eligible now and a few more in a couple months. And I know that total cost to the township would be roughly around $37,000. However, looking at what was budgeted for the pension for this year, with the staffing changes, there's actually room there. There's $39,000, so it would actually wash. So I did take a look at that financial impact.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So by changing the eligibility date for the pension, the financial impact is minimal. It would be a wash, essentially.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Okay. All right. Thank you for that information. I'm also interested in vacation leave. What just may be apparent to everyone else—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: No, she's got the answers.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: All right.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So, as a mere trustee, I don't see all the figures a lot, but I don't see them on this. I don't know what the length of service vacation leave allocation is now, and I don't understand ultimately how that affects the budget. Okay, I don't understand.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So, can she take that? She has that answer.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: I think, yeah.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: As far as how that affects the budget?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Well, hit her, what are you doing now? What are you changing?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Right now, the current vacation schedule is an employee does not receive vacation until their 1 year of service, so they enter in at 0 hours.

_utterances.speaker: G
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay, um, and they max out at 176 hours at 20 years.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Um, I looked at Plainfield Township, Georgetown Township, Park Township, the City of Holland, Allendale, and Saugatuck, and I did a comparison. And everybody does their paid time off a little different. Some are combined PTO, some are more of a hybrid. They're more of a PTO bank plus a sick time bank. We currently have three banks: vacation, sick, and personal. I reviewed their policies. I feel that what I presented to you is in line with. I can provide you specifics to that if you want.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Just need to know what it is that you're looking for and I can find it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Well, so she did the research and didn't make the document. I'll just be the first to admit I didn't put it in the document. I didn't have her put it in the document. I'll take the blame. Okay.

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: But the vacation was a big one.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Because they didn't get anything for a year.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, this, this—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: The office rate was negotiated upon hiring.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: That's right.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: So then it was an administrative thing that I have to follow as well. It's okay, our payroll system is set up at the zero to start, but then people are entering with a certain amount, and then it's just having to watch and adjust with every employee. So this would help Make administrative tracking easier.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Have one pot instead of 3 pots or 4 pots. Okay, that makes sense to me. Um, I think from an employer standpoint, the calculation is ultimately hours of work and money, right? And when we make adjustments to things like vacation and add sick leave and bereavement, I'm not saying those are unfair, and I'm not saying they're not I don't think it's uncompetitive. I think to be competitive is very important here, and, uh, and it's— to be fair is important as well. But I think the calculation from an employer standpoint is, is, is person-hours versus dollars in a year, right? And so I think the overall impact of this is to get less person-hours For the same amount of money, or maybe slightly more money. I think it would be—

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: That's what I think we were trying to do. I mean, understand, it's a small enough organization that if someone— if Lindsay goes on vacation, someone's going to cover her job. And it's not— she's going to be smart when she does it.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Oh, I see.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So it's not like dollar for dollar, like she takes off 8 hours and there's 8 hours gone out the window. Someone comes in and helps her do it. There are people cross-covered everywhere in all departments, so there's cross-coverage everywhere. Okay. So because if she leaves for a week, you know, Sheila might come in and say, I'll help you do what you need to do, or Tammy helps her do it, right? Mike knows better than me, but there's people that cross cover. So it's not a dollar-for-dollar thing, you know what I mean? It's because she's not leaving for 8 hours. She's not— we don't have to bring someone in to replace her, or someone else is losing productivity. They're just— so everybody's cross-trained. They're going to help her out because they know they're going to get help later.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I understand. That makes sense. Um, do we have part-time employees as well?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: How many do we have?

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: Well, it depends how far down the part-time list you want to go, right?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So I see that these, these, these things are for full-time employees.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yeah.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So part-time employees are not getting—

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: They're getting what portion of holiday pay? They're— it's prorated, isn't it? I thought their holiday pay was prorated.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: So how— I mean, what is—

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: what percentage— the part-time— all part-time employees, all employees actually, right, um, do earn the extra sick time. That's new. It was mandated from the state February of '24. Okay. So they earn 1 hour of sick time per 3 hours worked.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah. My basic question here is what percentage of our employees are Well, again, I mean, it comes down to classification.

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: So we have regular part-time employees that work a certain number of days every week. We have part-time employees, you know, at the community center that fluctuates. So election workers are part-time employees.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: How deep do you want to go is the question.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yeah, so I want to know the extent. So what I see is a categorical There's a difference in how we treat people who are full-time, and we're very concerned about how we're treating them, evidently.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Well, they want to be part-time, though. They don't— we've tried to get people to be full-time.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Really?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, they don't want to be full-time.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I want to know— I appreciate what you're asking.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, they don't want to be full-time.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I feel like this is the start of being able to do all of that.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Correct.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I think this opens the door.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: This is opening the door for a more complete review. These things that I have presented are things that I've heard about since I started here.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: And they're very outdated.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: I see.

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay, um, but I believe that this is a step in the right direction, and what you are asking is valid.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, they're good questions. That's why she knows it.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I'm concerned about the person who works 37 hours a week. Do we have any of those?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: I don't think so, but not on a regular basis.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Those are full. Yeah, 37 is full-time. Treated very unfairly, right, in every employment situation that I'm aware of, right? Because they're not getting any of these benefits, or maybe very few, or only the ones that are mandated by the state.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: But they have ability in their schedule. That's what they want. I'm seriously— there's people that I've tried to recruit as a full-time.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: What's our hours for full-time? 40?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Anything over 40 hours is full-time. Well, I mean, you have to be classified as full-time, but you can't work more— on an average, you can't work more than 30 hours a week otherwise you'd be classified as a full-timer and then you would have to be offered benefits.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Then you would have to. Okay. Well, then I'm concerned about the folks who are working 27 hours a week.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: You shouldn't be. They love it. That's what they want to do. I've tried to get them to be full-time. Mike has too. They don't want to be part-time.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: That's fair.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: They don't want it.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: From Steve's perspective, does this seem in line with the way the township's been run for a long time, or does this seem like a good idea?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Pretty much.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: We've always spent a lot of effort making sure that we were competitive out there. Okay. Because we never want to lose an employee that's been here for a while, that we've trained, and that they're doing their job in an excellent manner. So, yeah, that's pretty much how we've done it.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: And this is competitive?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: I would say yes.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yeah, we thought so.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: So, I'll keep this brief, but my comments go in line with what everybody's saying. So, my question for you is, is this enough? And I just want to make sure we do get the best employees and that we do retain them. So, if this is what everybody else is doing, I think we need to step it up even more. from here. That's what I think. And everybody gets a 10% raise. Okay, I'm just saying that for fun. But I want to make sure that our employees— everybody's at a 5. I want to be at a 7, right? I want to have the best employees. I want to attract the best employees. I want to keep them. So if this is just a start—

_utterances.speaker: L
_utterances.text: It is.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I would say open the door for even a better program than this.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: It's a start.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Get it going. Let's get the best people we can get.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Is that a motion?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: That's a motion.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: I have a question.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: What is overtime paid at? Is it paid after 40?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: It is.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Okay. So mostly full-time. So the hours of an average township employer— employee is 40 hours, or is it 30?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: 40, okay.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: So we have a motion on the table by Mr. DeYoung. Would anyone like to support it? Gretchen supports it. And then we'll do a roll call vote, Mr. Clerk. I think there'll be 2 motions that we need.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yeah. Um, yep. Okay, Mr. Bush? Yes. Myself? Yes. Mr. Nienice?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Yes. Mr. Church?

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yes. Mr. DeYoung? Yes. Miss Malatki? Yes. Okay, 6 in favor, 0 opposed. 1 absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: And then there'll be one more motion. I'll make it. The consideration to amend the organization's existing 401 plan, and the Class 2 participants shall enter the first day of the plan month following completion of their minimum service requirement. I'll make that motion.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I support it.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Supported by Gretchen. Mr., uh, roll call vote, please.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Ms. Malachy?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Mr. DeYoung?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Mr. Church?

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: Mr. Neenhuys?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Myself, yes. Mr. Bush?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Yes.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: 6 in favor, 0 opposed, 1 absent. Motion carried.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Thanks for coming in, Lindsay. Very good job. Thank you. Item number 9 is the consideration of operational capital improvement expenditure for the Clover Street walkway— sidewalk. Mr. Bulthuis?

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: Yes, as the memo accompanying this agenda item indicates, the community meals program that was served for a number of years at Western Theological Seminary is going to be relocated to Salvation Army Community Center, which is located in the township. So, in preparation for that and thinking about that particular service that's provided, you know, staff came to the conclusion that a number of patrons of that particular service are likely to come by means other than a vehicle. And so, currently, Clover Street has no sidewalk, no Non-motorized pathway as part of it. So the intent here is to provide a non-motorized pathway connection from 8th Street, which is where our existing pathway system is, up to the Salvation Army Community Center. So that was— what was the intent? We had Preiner Newhof go forth and design that up. And this is a collaborative project with Salvation Army, they will have a small portion of the project that will connect from the public sidewalk to their site, but it was cost beneficial to do it all as one project. So you noted here that the costs here about 85% are township costs; the remainder will be borne by the Salvation Army. Bids were solicited; three bids were received. You see the results there. Site Work Solutions was. low bid at $156,836. And, uh, the recommendation from staff is to award, uh, the work to Site Work Solutions for that amount plus a contingency and, uh, engineering cost for a total authorization of $242,000.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any questions for Mr. Bulthuis?

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: I, I do on that. I, I know that this was kind of addressed when we moved that meal program over there, right? With this, that was kind kind of a big to-do. Is that a fair percentage? I mean, should they be paying a little bit bigger chunk on that?

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Because I—

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Who? Salvation Army?

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Salvation Army is paying a percentage. Dave's inquiring whether they should pay more.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Well, I mean, this was all kind of talked about, like it was going to get busier over there, all that, right? If they moved that program over there.

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: So the cost split was based on, you know, the footage of them.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: All right.

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: Of the plan. So most of it was public sidewalk along Clover.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any other questions for Mr. Bulthuis?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Um, the engineering cost, is that— that's all the contingency is for what, roughly $80,000 more for that?

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: The difference between the $156,000 and the $242,000?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Yeah. What, what would we need a contingency for? I didn't see that large—

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: just wondering. Yeah, whenever you do a construction project, you start digging in there, we could find some bad soils, we could find unmarked utilities that didn't get marked. So you start digging, it's like, oh, all of a sudden we find a gas line there that some— or something of that nature. That addresses those types of situations.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Any other questions? Anyone like to make a motion?

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: I'll make that motion, Mr. Chairman.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Anyone like to support it?

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I'll support that.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Elliot? All in favor say aye.

_utterances.speaker: D
_utterances.text: Aye.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Opposed, same sign. I'll say no on this one. So after that— I'm in bad mood, bad mood. No, I'm not. Um, I really want to say no. Yep. Administrative reports and updates, Mr. Bulthuis.

_utterances.speaker: O
_utterances.text: You know, Mr. Nyborg has been waiting patiently for us over at the Public Works building for our I think that's it for our tour, so I don't have anything else this evening.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Oh, wow, I forgot about that.

_utterances.speaker: F
_utterances.text: Very quickly, it won't take long. I have an update on the principles of government— governance document that was brought up at the last meeting. I find the timing of this discussion very interesting. Apparently, the principles of governance document has gone unsigned for 6 years, yet now suddenly there's an urgent push to get the signatures. Seems like just another attempt to silence me. Let me be clear, I have absolutely no problem following rules, policies, procedures, or standards of conduct. What I do have a problem with is being expected to follow rules that others are allowed to ignore. This document talks about impartiality, transparency, ethical conduct, and appropriate responses to constituent concerns. Those are admirable principles. They're also the very principles I've been asking this township staff to follow for years. Before I sign a pledge promising equal treatment, I'd like to see equal treatment actually practiced. For more than 15 years, I've watched staff selectively enforce ordinances, target certain residents, and ignore violations by others, and dismiss legitimate concerns when those concerns become inconvenient. My family has lived through it firsthand. I've repeatedly asked for accountability, transparency, and an independent look at these issues. Instead, we're being asked to sign a piece of paper. Trustee Church and I were asked by the supervisor to suggest revised language to the Principles of Governance document that might be acceptable. Respectfully, I don't have any revisions. My concern isn't the wording. My concern is whether anyone intends to follow it. Because if ordinances are enforced against some residents but not others, if staff can operate without accountability, and if citizens raising concerns are treated as the problem instead of being heard, then this document is nothing more than words on a piece of paper. Until we start enforcing our ordinances equally, until accountability applies to staff as much as it applies to elected officials, and until these principles are reflected in actions, I will not be signing that document. And before I finish, one final question that I would like an answer to: Has this document been signed by township staff? If so, when and how often? Because in my opinion, everyone who represents Highland Charter Township should be willing to publicly commit to these principles. And if they haven't, maybe that explains some things all by itself. As a taxpayer, a resident, and an elected trustee, I believe accountability should come before signatures. Thank you, Mr.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: DeYoung. Mr. Clerk? Pass. Mr. Neenice?

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Pass. Ms. Gretchen? I don't know if everybody knows or not, but Kate White is going to be leaving the township. I think she took another job. I am sad that we're losing her. She is, I think, a wonderful employee. She She does her job very well, and I hope that everyone here will give her a phone call or let her know how much we appreciate her tenure here. And I wish her the best, and I hope we run into her where someone's maybe doing something that we could maybe say goodbye collectively. I don't know, but—

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Well, thanks for mentioning that.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Mr. Chair, I agree. And Mr. Clerk, we're going to tour the Public Works building at 207 West Lakewood Boulevard.

_utterances.speaker: C
_utterances.text: Yep.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: All right, we'll see you guys over there. Please go ahead. Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: My name is Jason Reen.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: I live at 664. What's your last name?

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I'm sorry, Reen.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Spell it. R-E-E-N. R-E-E-N.

_utterances.speaker: B
_utterances.text: Sorry. That's okay.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I'm trying to be a little bit respectful of your time here. One thing I wanted to mention, they did mention the utility lines being bored through some of these pipes was an issue in certain situations. I mean, as they said, though, we do have That in at least two cases were gas lines were put in through drain lines on Sleepy Hollow. I recognize that it's old; it was there was installation attempts with plastic issues and all those things. But I think it's worth emphasizing that the utilities were bored through there twice, and that's definitely at least part of the problem. I think that's where we're going. Second thing was the letters we got for the assessments. I checked with those. I was checking with the other two that were here. The letter. Were dated 5/26. I received mine on 5/29. So I just wanted to put that out there as— that's not you guys obviously doing that, that was the, that was the, you know, the county. But I think it's worth noting too that, you know, we had 2 weeks and a couple days, not 30 days or whatever it is. And the other thing is, just as a more personal note, I believe it was Joe that brought up that part of the change in 2013 was for Having skin in the game, having accountability, and all that stuff as part of the residents to be to make sure it's worth our time and our money to do these things. And we really want to have enough to pay for it. And I appreciate that completely. As a new homeowner, I was sleeping hell. It was my first house. I bought it about ten years ago.

_utterances.speaker: H
_utterances.text: I helped you move in. You did.

_utterances.speaker: M
_utterances.text: Yeah, you did actually help me move in.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Sorry to interrupt. I tried to cut off. It was while the process was undergoing. Going on for the orphan brain approvals and all that, I found out later on. I didn't know anything about it when I moved in. Even if I did have a letter, I wouldn't have understood. Just as like a personal comment on that note, it's as a new person in there, I would have had no idea that any of this had happened. When this all came up, my reaction was is kind of what probably I said before. I've learned a lot from these guys. They've been very helpful in explaining how things work. I don't agree with it, but I accept that that's how. They work, but just as a as an observation, it is is it's annoying. It sucks all the words that everybody used to have this come up out of nowhere. It'd be something that I had nothing to do with, no control over, no input on, no knowledge of. That's all I wanted to just kind of throw that out there as a as somebody who did move in more recently. I mean, it's it's one thing if you're in the in the neighborhood from the start and all these things, but as somebody who I had no idea that any of this was going on. So, as part of like the record-keeping process and all those things, it touches on the gas lines too. Somebody hopefully would have known that there was a problem there. I don't know how that all works, but I just wanted to mention that in terms of like the overall nature of all these things.

_utterances.speaker: A
_utterances.text: Well, thank you very much for staying here and speaking your mind on that.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: Thank you, I appreciate it.

_utterances.speaker: N
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: What was your address again?

_utterances.speaker: I
_utterances.text: Okay.

_utterances.speaker: J
_utterances.text: Thank you.

_utterances.speaker: E
_utterances.text: I'm sorry, I was talking very fast there.

_utterances.speaker: I
